Fig. 9.5

Buying center analysis

 summary.



Buying Center Participation4



The first step in the buying center analysis is to determine who in the customer’s

organization will be involved in the buying center. Sometimes the person can be

identified by asking a contact within the account. Often, however, identifying that

person can be a difficult task for several reasons:



•  Sometimes the buying center member might be the key contact but could

have little impact on the final decision or approval. These individuals may

try to block a supplier’s access to other buying center members.



•  Some important buyers are not official members of the buying center yet



might have significant influence on the decision-making process.



•  In some opportunities, there could be many buyers who work as teams or



committees within the buying center.



With a new project or customer, significant effort might be needed to avoid the

risk of not contacting an important buying center member. When the opportunity

is for an existing account or is a re-tender, identifying the buying center member

is simpler and the previous opportunity outcome analysis can be used to prepare

a  contacting  strategy.  The  best  situation  is  when  your  company  is  in  the  imple-

mentation stage of a project and is working with the account daily.



Several actions can improve your understanding of buying center factors. The first

is  having  an  ongoing  account  management  program  in  place,  and  the  second  is

pursuing the opportunity long before the procurement process begins and before

buying center members are required to restrict their discussions with suppliers.

See the sidebar “It is Confidential” for a tip on how to ask for who is doing the

buying.



All Strategy Depends on the Competition



323





Buying Center Roles5



In addition to project initiators and users of your products and services, there is

usually a buying center team or individuals who:



•  Evaluates options



•  Defines requirements



•  Evaluates offers



•  Makes the final selection decision



•  Negotiates



•  Have the final approval



Advisors on Technical, Financial, and Legal Issues



Afterwards, the operations team implements and reviews the performance of the

project. Buying center roles can be both official and unofficial. Typically, the pro-

curement  department  of  most  large  companies  is  responsible  for  evaluating  and

selecting suppliers from whom the company will purchase. This is common practice

when a vendor list has been established for specific services or products. Your team

should identify the specific role for each buying center member, as discussed below.



Initiator



The initiator is the high level person who sponsors the project. Typically, the ini-

tiator  is  an  individual  but  can  also  be  a  group  or  committee.  It  is  important  to

identify the initiator and maintain contact regularly as part of the account man-

agement process. It is good practice to contact initiators shortly after the account’s

annual  planning  process  and  discuss  any  changes  to  the  company’s  goals  and

strategies and new initiatives.  The initiator may not be part of the buying center,

but if their support was needed to influence others in the buying center, you need

to know what they believe is critical for the project to be successful.



Approver



The approver is the person or committee who approves the award of the contract

to  your  company.  In  some  public  sectors  or  government-owned  companies,  the

approver may be a person or department from a government agency who is respon-

sible for the expenditure of government funds. In these situations, the final con-

tract is signed by the department or company requesting the services only after

government review and approval. The approver for large sales is typically a staff

member  or  group  from  the  senior  levels  of  the  company.  Approvers  should  be

contacted according to the following guidelines:



•  By  someone  from  your  company’s  upper  management  who  is  not  directly

involved  in  day-to-day  sales  activities.  This  is  necessary  because,  in  some

procurement processes, communication between suppliers and buying center

members might be restricted. Restricted communication, however, typically

does not apply to upper management. Contacting approver-level buying cen-

ter members should also be part of the ongoing account management plan.



•  Contacting should be planned to take place in the later stages of the procure-

ment process. During the late procurement stages, your company is in the best

position to address issues that might block the award of the contract to you.



•  The  person  making  the  contact  must  ask  whether  the  approver  has  any

unresolved issues or concerns. The person making the contact should state

the corporate commitment to the account’s project and demonstrate man-

agement support.



324



CHAPTER 9



Strategic Sales Plan



u It is Confidential!



Unfortunately buying center

roles are not listed at the front

of the customer’s offices or on

their business cards. So how do

you find out who is in the buying

center? Many times students

will say, “When I've asked that

question, they tell me ’it’s confi-

dential!’” My comment is “Don’t

give up. You just need to ask the

question differently.”  Here are

some good tips for discovering

who is in the buying center:



•  Ask what process you have

to follow to make the expen-

diture.



•  Ask early in the opportunities

management process and

early in the “Evaluation of

Options” stage.



•  Use the Request for Proposal

form. Have it on the desk; let

the customer see what you

are recording and offer them

a copy at the start of the

meeting. Once you have the

process down, then ask who

is involved in each stage.



•  Then do your analysis, looking

at rank, technical experience,

and location. Fill in the boxes

with the roles.



•  Ask a mentor to validate your



analysis for you.



Don’t be surprised if they ask

you for a copy, as they might not

have anything like this.



•  Preparation  is  essential  before  visiting  with  the  approver.  The  executive

making the contact should be fully briefed on the status of the sales strategy

up to that point. An excellent tool for this briefing is the strategic sales plan.



•  The  sales  team  should  prepare  a  one-page  executive  summary  of  the  key

issues and business value provided by your solution for the executive to use

when  making  the  contact.  It  is  not  unusual  for  the  approver  to  express

negative reactions when huge price differences exist between your company

and competitor offers. Usually, approvers are not involved in the evaluation

of options stage and, therefore, might not understand your value. It is also

useful to provide the executive with a list of users and evaluators who have

validated the offer in case the approver wants to confer with them.



As discussed in Chapter 6 “Sales Activities,” it is important for the professional

salesperson  to  stay  focused  on  building  relationships  with  their  customers.  The

same is true for executives. There should be a managed program to ensure that

this is an ongoing business relationship (OBR) program for the executives. This

will  be  discussed  in  more  detail  in  Chapter  13  “Account  Management  Plan.”  To

ensure that your executives have access to initiators and approvers for an oppor-

tunity, don’t wait too late in the opportunity for the first time your executive meets

the customer’s executive. The optimal contact occurs three times. The first time

is  during  the  customer’s  project  sanction  stage  or  once  annually  after  the  cus-

tomer’s annually planning process. At this time your executive should be able to

obtain the information on the project solution worksheet and qualify the compel-

ling event.  For an example of how one sales team executed a flawless executive

contact program in support of a B2B opportunity and an example of a real execu-

tive summary used in an executive visit, see the Chapter Highlight 9.1: “But It

Is Only One Piece of Paper!”



Decision-Maker



This person or committee takes ultimate responsibility for the final selection of the

preferred supplier and forwards a recommendation to the approver. The decision-

maker  can  be  one  of  the  most  important  contacts  in  the  buying  center.  Most

decision-makers need information to justify their decision, and the best justifica-

tion is a clear communication of your value and the benefits of your proposal.



Preparation is also key to a successful meeting with the decision-maker. In getting

ready to contact the decision-maker, contact one of the lower-level buying center

members described below. Depending on the preferred practices of the account,

it might be wise for a high-level member of the sales team to contact the decision-

maker.  As  with  the  approver,  decision-makers  can  be  higher  level  management

and are best contacted by an account manager or by someone who has not been

directly  involved  with  lower-level  contacts  or  buying  center  members.  Always

consider these options when determining who is the most appropriate person to

contact key buying center members.



Evaluator



The evaluator is the person or team who evaluates competing offers from suppli-

ers. Evaluators can also be users of the products or services under consideration.

As  discussed  in  Chapter  4  “Opportunities  Management,”  evaluators  should  be

contacted  early  in  the  evaluation  of  options  phase  to  determine  the  evaluation

criteria and model used to evaluate competing bids. The sales team must be sure

the evaluator has enough information to be familiar with the technology to fairly

compare your solution to competitor offerings. A completed vulnerability analysis

is critical for planning effective strategies for contacting the evaluator.



All Strategy Depends on the Competition



325





Chapter Highlight 9.1:  But It is Only One Piece of Paper!



It was a really important tender, and Company A was evaluated as being worth more than double the competitor. A

mentor of Company A was confident due to the very expensive nature of the exploration program and the fact it was

also a tight schedule and risky as this project was not about cutting cost, but getting the job done. Despite the best

assurances from the mentor, Company A’s management was still very nervous about losing the work. The com-

petitor, after all, was also a very viable company; it was well-respected and had worked on projects elsewhere in the

world for the customer.



What was done, however, was part of the on-going business relationship contact program. Each

year, Company A’s executive would meet after the customer’s annual budgeting just to catch

up over lunch and enjoy some golf after lunch. At this year’s meeting, the customer’s executive

mentioned he was pretty happy that his major exploration program received funding from the

head  office  and  2  rank  wildcat  wells  would  be  drilled  within  2-year’s  time.  Immediately  after

lunch, Company A’s executive passed the information on to the sales team.



One year went by quickly, but in that time, the sales team had met all the key buying center

members and understood their requirements and critical success factors for the project. The

sales team made their first sales meeting visits supporting the customer’s key factors, and the

feedback was excellent. Most of the buying center members liked what they heard and were

supporters of the approach. When the bids were evaluated, the news of the more than double

the worth was a shock. Company A recommended their very best solution, and the main com-

petitor decided to go cheap. The sales team was confident the recommendation from everyone

would be to go with their solution. However, the final approval would come from the custom-

er’s executive that Company A’s executive had visited almost 18 months ago. What the sales

team requested was for their executive to visit the customer’s executive who would approve

and  ask  him  to  review  an  executive  summary  they  prepared  (upper  right).  It  listed  the  cus-

tomer’s compelling event on the front, the top two requirements (reduced rig costs, and  infor-

mation for the lease sale), and what everyone on the customer’s team agreed was the critical success factor:

advantage to make a leasing, sales strategy over their competitors. On the back for the executive summary was the

unique business value for Company A. When the company received the summary, he looked at it and, while holding

it like an accordion said, “But it is only one piece of paper.” The sales manager who had been the sales lead for this

project  said,  “The  executive  does  not  need  to  know  all  the  details,  just  that  his  team’s  key  issues  are  being

addressed and, with our advanced technology, they will reduce their rig costs by 20% so they can pay our invoice

from their savings.” The Company A executive thought about it for a while and said, “I like it. I will call him now and

set the appointment.” At the meeting over lunch, Company A took out the executive summary from his pocket of

his  jacket  and  reviewed  it  with  the  customer  executive.  His  feedback  was, “This  is  great;  looks  like  an  excellent

decision. I’ll sign the request as soon as I see it.” The executive then asked, “Do you have a few more copies? My

headquarters staff will visit next week, and I'm sure the topic of who will be doing the project will come up. This

would be nice to review with them.”



This is a very powerful example of what happens when you apply the professional sales consultative selling approach. First

the account team had an ongoing business relationship program in place, which meant Company A’s executive discovered

a compelling event early in the opportunity, as shown in the figure to the left; this represents Executive Contact No. 1.  Then

the  sales  team  used  this  early  advantage  to  uncover  the  requirements  and  critical  success  factors  from  the  customer’s

buying center members and propose to them the best solution given their project challenges. Then by focusing on what

was stated as important to the customer and the fact that Company A had a perfect solution after the proposal was submit-

ted, the Company A executive was able to

use  the  Pocket  Executive  Summary  to

review  the  benefits  and  unique  value  of

his  company’s  solution  and  review  that

with  the  customer  executive  during  the

resolution of concerns Executive Contact

No. 2. The last visit for the executives was

Executive  Contact  No.  3,  as  part  of  the

project  review.  Company  A’s  executive

was  correct—the  executive  summary

was one page with what he called a fancy

fold.  However,  you  can  make  your  own

Pocket  Executive  Summary  very  easily

using  a  PowerPoint  template  with  the

example shown here and instructions on

how to fold. The file is available from the

textbook website.



326



CHAPTER 9



Strategic Sales Plan



u Who Are the

Powerful Buyers?



Psychologists R.P. French and

Bertram Raven studied sourc-

es of power in organizations.

In their report entitled “The

Bases of Social Power,” they

identified five power bases:



•  Reward. Ability to provide

monetary, social, political,

or psychological rewards to

others for support of their

ideas.



•  Coercive. Ability to provide

monetary or other punish-

ments for not accepting

their ideas.



•  Attraction. Ability to  elicit

support for their opinions

because   people like them.



•  Expert. Ability to elicit sup-

port because of technical

expertise, either actual or

reputed.



•  Status. Ability to gain sup-

port derived from one’s

position of power in a com-

pany.



Rank or status is a good, but

not perfect, indicator of

power. Observed behaviors

are better predictors of deter-

mining who has the power.

Communica tions between

buying  center members can

often identify the powerful

members. When buying cen-

ter members use rational

arguments, they might be try-

ing to influence those with

more power. When communi-

cation is one-way, it could

indicate that the information

is going to the more powerful

member. Commonly, the

powerful member does not

attend a presentation or

negotiation because he or

she is confident others will

debrief him or her and will

not make a final decision

without his or her input.



Gatekeeper



The gatekeeper can play an unofficial role in the buying center and is  typically

considered  an  internal  expert  for  the  product  or  services  under  consideration.

Gatekeepers  are  important  because  they  can  block  any  solution  they  consider

unacceptable. Contacting gatekeepers early in the evaluation of options phase is

critical, and if gatekeepers have official responsibility in that role, they should be

included as part of the account management contacting program. Gatekeepers are

good candidates for visits to your research and engineering centers in support of

future projects.



User



The user actually uses the products or services under consideration. They might

be  selected  to  represent  and  communicate  other  users’  requirements  to  the

evaluators, and they typically provide the product or service specifications. Users

are  often  forgotten  when  pursuing  an  opportunity.  If  users  are  not  formally

included in the buying center or bid committee, you must search for and identify

the most authoritative users to understand their requirements.



Multiple and Other Roles



Buying  center  members  have  multiple  roles.  For  example,  the  buying  center

member might be a user-evaluator. This designation should be captured into the

SSP. Roles not included in this discussion should be added and a footnote included

to explain the role.



The level of impact buying center members have on the final decision cannot be

determined only by their role in the buying center. A number of factors determine

the degree to which an individual buying center member can influence the final

decision.



Influence



The  level  of  influence  is  dependent  on  the  individual  buying  center  members’

power base, as discussed in the “Who Are the Powerful Buyers?” sidebar, and the

level of importance the specific project has on the buying center members’ per-

sonal priorities (discussed below). Some buying center members might try to use

their  influence  to  actively  achieve  the  decision  they  want.  Others  could  not

actively try to influence the decision but, by their participation or presence, may

indirectly affect the decision of others around them. When the level of influence

is considered with other factors, it can be used to determine the level of impor-

tance the individual buying center member has in the total sales strategy.6



Below  are  three  options  for  describing  the  level  of  influence  a  buying  center

member can have:



•  High.  He  or  she  can  change  outcomes  and  decision  criteria,  and  others

might  try  to  ensure  that  high-level  influence  buying  center  members  are

satisfied with the outcome.



•  Medium.  He  or  she  can  affect  decisions  beyond  the  immediate  organiza-

tional  chain  of  command  and  department.  Others  might  look  to  them  for

their opinion or leadership.



•  Low.  He or she must follow the organizational chain of command to pass

along input regarding the purchase decision. These members do not affect

a decision unless they are directly included in the buying center and will

not affect other buying center members’ opinions.



All Strategy Depends on the Competition



327





The level of influence of a specific buying center member can be both good and bad

for your company, depending on the buying center member’s alignment to your com-

pany. A decision-making member who is not favorable to your company and has a high

level  of  influence  can  represent  the  single  most  significant  threat  to  winning  an

opportunity if not addressed successfully. However, if the same buying center member

prefers your company, your chances of winning the opportunity are much greater.



Alignment



There are many reasons why a buying center member might have a favorable or

negative bias toward your company. That bias could be the result of past experi-

ences with your company, experience with other suppliers, or perceptions regard-

ing how well the proposed solution satisfies the customer’s needs or requirements.

It is not uncommon for a customer who has a favorable alignment toward one or

more  of  your  company  divisions  to  have  a  negative  bias  against  other  divisions

from your company. One responsibility of the sales representative is to be aware

of the customer’s alignment toward your company.



If  the  sales  representative  is  uncertain  about  the  alignment  of  a  buying  center

member, he or she must not make an assumption about the alignment. It is best

to leave this section blank or mark it with a question mark. Levels for customer

alignment are listed below.



Mentor



A mentor is someone in the customer’s organization who actively participates with

your  company  to  assist  in  securing  the  opportunity.  A  mentor’s  assistance  can

range from:



•  Coaching and advising on appropriate contacting strategies,



•  Validating your interpretation of other buying center members’ alignments,



•  Clearly specifying project requirements



•  Explaining both formal and informal decision criteria



•  Arranging introductions



•  Advising about significant competitor actions, etc.



Your  chances  of  winning  greatly  improve  if  you  have  a  mentor  inside  the  cus-

tomer’s organization.



Supporter



A buying center member who is convinced to use your company in the final pur-

chase decision will support or recommend the selection of your company. A sup-

porter pays a premium for your company without having to go through a detailed

justification. For example, a customer might say, “Yes, we know your company is

more expensive, but you are worth the premium.” A supporter is someone who,

late in the evaluation of options and procurement stages, has no unresolved issues

and can be referenced to validate the value your solution provides. A “supporter”

is  a  supporter  of  only  one  company  for  each  procurement.  However,  the  same

person can also be a supporter of a different company for a different project or

for different products or services.



Neutral



A buying center member who is not biased or has no specific preference toward

one company will select the company that, in his or her opinion, makes the best

business  decision.  A  neutral  buyer  will  pay  a  premium  for  a  supplier  only  after



328



CHAPTER 9



Strategic Sales Plan



that supplier has motivated the neutral buyer to become a supporter by clearly

demonstrating  sufficient  added  value.  Typically,  a  neutral  buyer  will  not  state  a

preference until the value of the solution offered has been proven. In a competi-

tive  bid  procurement,  very  close  contact  must  be  maintained  with  the  neutral

buyer once the tender has been opened (early in the submission of proposal stage)

to  ensure  that  the  solution  costs  have  not  created  financial  drawbacks  that

require resolution. Once the proposals have been analyzed, a neutral buying cen-

ter  member  will  become  a  supporter  or  non-supporter  after  analyzing  the  solu-

tions offered.



If a buying center member shows indecision after the final proposals have been

evaluated, the sales representative should probe for customer concerns regarding

your solution. If the customer offers no concerns and maintains a neutral position,

you  cannot  depend  on  this  person  to  defend  any  challenges  to  selecting  your

company. In this case, the sales representative will need to solicit the support of

a supporter or mentor.



A  customer  can  also  maintain  a  neutral  position  to  prepare  for  a  competitive

negotiation. Methods of handling this situation are discussed in Chapter 12 “Col-

laborative Negotiations.” A neutral buyer’s buyer-readiness stage is either prefer-

ence. A neutral buyer might even have a preference for two or more suppliers if

two or more companies for the project are under consideration.



Non-Supporter



A non-supporter is a buying center member who is convinced to use one of the

competitors instead of your company. This customer might believe the competitor

has a better solution fit for the project requirements or the competitor has pro-

vided better products and services than your company in the past. The customer

might have unresolved negative reactions to your proposed solution. A non-sup-

porter should be identified early in the evaluation of options stage of the oppor-

tunities management process. The time between the evaluation of options and the

procurement phase can be used to convert the non-supporter to a supporter.



Sometimes,  however,  you  might  choose  to  neutralize  the  non-supporter  with  a

mentor or supporter of higher influence and role rather than invest the time and

effort to convince the non-supporter. This is dependent on the:



•  Level of influence the non-supporter has



•  Number  of  issues  that  need  to  be  resolved  in  order  to  secure  his  or  her



support



•  Amount of time you have after you determine someone is a non-supporter



Non-supporters should be contacted again once the implementation stage begins

in order to take steps to address their issues and gain their support.



When deciding how to approach the buyer, the sales representative must consider

where  the  non-supporter  is  in  relation  to  their  buyer-readiness  stage  with  the

competitor. To be considered a non-supporter, he or she must be at least at the

conviction stage for one a competitor.



Enemy



An enemy is a buying center member who actively tries to prevent your company

from winning the contract. Their efforts are not focused on assisting a competitor,

but only on blocking your company. A person can become an enemy for reasons

such as a history of poor performance by your company, personal reasons, etc. The

enemy label should only be used when there is no doubt that the customer has

clearly  taken  actions  in  the  past  or  with  the  current  opportunity  to  cause  your



All Strategy Depends on the Competition



329





Fig. 9.6

The buyer-readiness stage was intro-

duced in the marketing module. The steps

illustrate the progression of the buying-

readiness stages as the customer makes

a purchase decision. Considering the cus-

tomer’s buying stage helps to accurately

classify the customer’s alignment to your

company and the competitor.



PURCHASE CONTACT RECOMMENDS OR MAKES



THE DECISION  TO USE COMPANY A



CONVICTION CONTACT IS CONVINCED COMPANY A



IS THE BEST FOR  THEIR PROJECT



PREFERENCE



CONTACT PREFERS TO USE COMPANY

A AND ONE OR MORE COMPANIES



KNOWLEDGE



UNDERSTANDS THE BENEFITS

COMPANY A PROVIDES



LIKING



CONTACT LIKES OR HAS FAVORABLE

OPINION OF COMPANY A



AWARENESS



AWARE OF COMPANY A



company to lose. Caution must be used before applying the enemy label because

your approach is different than your approach to a non-supporter or neutral buy-

ing center member. By definition, a buying center member who is an enemy can-

not  be  converted  to  a  supporter;  as  such  the  strategy  is  not  to  spend  resources

and effort trying to convert the enemy, but to solicit the assistance of a higher-

level buying center member to neutralize the enemy.



Figure 9.6 illustrates the relationship between alignment and the buyer-readiness

stages. To summarize the alignment states of buyers:



•  Supporter is someone who is convinced your company is the best company



and will purchase from you.



•  Non-supporter is convinced a competitor is best and will purchase from that



competitor.



•  Preference is a buyer who is neutral to buying from two or more companies.



•  Mentor will try to help you to win.



•  Enemy does not care who wins as long as it is not you or your company.



Contact alignment can change over time as a result of your positive influence by

implementing a strategic sales plan. If the account is a first-time buyer from your

company,  many  of  the  buying  center  attributes  might  be  unknown  early  in  the

opportunities management process. As you pass into later stages of the evaluation

of options phase, you should have identified most of the buyer profiles and should

be  executing  tactics  to  better  position  your  company  and  your  solution  with  key

buying center members. This should help move buying center members from the

lower buyer-readiness stages to conviction by the onset of the procurement phase.



If  the  buyer  is  at  the  conviction  stage  at  the  start  of  the  procurement  process,

chances are the buyer will make a purchase decision in your favor. Typically, the last

hurdle between conviction and the purchase buyer-readiness stage is negotiation. A

convinced buyer is much easier to negotiate with than a non-convinced buyer.



At the end of the procurement stage and after you have been notified of the out-

come, the sales representative should complete the outcome analysis as described

earlier in Chapter 4 “Opportunities Management” and Chapter 6 “Sales Activities.”

Part of this analysis should include the final determination of the buying center

members’ alignment to your company and the competitor.



Outcome analysis is a valuable tool for preparing the implementation strategy. If

you are awarded the work, you should try to convert buying center members who



330



CHAPTER 9



Strategic Sales Plan



u Did Your

Customer Win?



Miller and Heiman, authors

and consultants, strongly sug-

gest that delivering corporate

results to customers is not

the entire measure of success

and the only ingredient of suc-

cessful strategic selling. They

suggest that you have to deliv-

er personal wins for key mem-

bers of the buying center.

They offer the following defini-

tions to illustrate the differ-

ence among results, wins and

win-results.



Selling is a profes sional, inter-

active process directed toward

demonstrating to all your buy-

ing center members how your

product or service serves their

individual self-interests.



Deliverable is a product or ser-

vice designed to improve or

fix one or more of the cus-

tomer’s business processes.



Process is an activity or series

of activities that converts

what exists now into some-

thing else.



Result is the measur able

impact a deliverable has on

one or more of the customer’s

processes. Results are objec-

tive and affect many people at

the same time.



Win is the fulfillment of a sub-

jective, personal promise

made to oneself to serve

one’s self-interest. Wins are

always different for different

people.



Win-result is an objective busi-

ness result that gives one or

more buying center members

a subjective, personal win.



are  non-supporters  during  the  implementation  stage.  If  your  company  was  not

awarded the work, you should determine why. Many customers will tell companies

who are not selected for the contract why they were not selected and why they

were not convinced. This is valuable information for preparing for the next oppor-

tunity as it tells you what you have to do to win support the next time.



Contact Priorities7



The contact priorities consist of two parts: Business priorities and personal pri-

orities. Both are very important as the contact is motivated to achieve their pri-

orities  and  they  will  be  motivated  to  support  you  winning  the  work  if  they  are

convinced they have a better chance of achieving their priorities with you or your

company than with a competitor. A complete understanding of the contact’s busi-

ness priorities and personal priorities provides the full picture of what is impor-

tant to individual buying center members.



Business Priorities



The business priorities consists of the buyer’s perception of:



•  Objective



•  Requirements



•  Critical success factors



•  Budget and contract holder



Objective



What does the contact state as the objective or the reason they are buying your

products or services?



Requirements



The buyer’s specific requirements for what they are purchasing from your company.



Critical Success Factors



What are the one or two factors the customer identifies as being most important

in order to achieve the objective?



Budget and Contract Holder



What is the budget allocated for what they are going to buy from your company?

Who will be the contract holder responsible for managing the budget and ensuring

that the customer does not go over budget?



Personal Priorities



The personal priorities consists of three components, as follows:



•  Buying factors



•  Concerns



•  Expectations



Miller and Heiman, in their book The New Strategic Selling, refer to this com-

ponent  as  win-results.  See  the  “Did  Your  Customer  Win?”  sidebar  for  further

discussion  of  the  win-result  definition.8  Examples  of  customers’  concerns  and

expectations are:



All Strategy Depends on the Competition



331





Buying Factors



The buying factors describe how the individual buyer evaluates competing alterna-

tives.  This  is  his  or  her  personal  business  decision  model.  The  most  commonly

stated buying factors expressed as a percentage are described on the next page.



Financial Aspects (%F)



How  important  are  the  financial  aspects  of  the  solution,  such  as  price  for  the

products or services used, contractual terms and conditions, maintenance costs,

etc.? Buyers having a high %F factor tend to draw conclusions from quoted prices

if they are not familiar enough with the solution to equate features and benefits

into a quantifiable dollar value. For non-technical buyers with a high %F factor,

the sales representative should prepare to address any financial drawbacks those

buyers have. The best time to do this is early in the evaluation of options stage

with  planned  marketing  and  sales  communication  programs  that  highlight  fea-

tures and benefits. Then during the evaluation of options stage, the sales repre-

sentative uses support documents that are validated by supporters in the buyers’

organization to gain the buyer’s conviction to use your company. The sales repre-

sentative might also have to use the outweigh method, as discussed in Chapter 8

“Handling Customer Objections.”



Technical Aspects (%T)



How important are the technical aspects of the solution? The types of technical

concerns are dependent upon the customer’s technology adoption profile. A cus-

tomer with a high %T factor might be concerned with the technical risks of a new

solution,  the  user’s  acceptance  of  the  solution,  the  quality  of  the  solution,  how

restrictive  the  solution  is,  how  dependent  the  solution  makes  the  buyer  on  one

supplier, and the complexity and effort required to keep the solution or products

running.  Buyers  with  high  %T  concerns  need  sufficient  demonstration  of  capa-

bilities  and  proofs  of  the  solution,  especially  if  the  proposed  technology  is  new.

When using a flanking competitive strategy, buyers with high influence based on

technical expertise most likely have to approve the alternative solution or have to

be convinced of the benefits to support changing their scope of work. This type of

proof should be provided early in the evaluation of options stage.



Service Aspects (%S)



How important are the service aspects of the solution? Buyers with high %S factors

are typically concerned with issues such as experience of service delivery person-

nel and quality of the service if the scope of work increases. A buyer with a high

%S  factor  might  be  concerned  with  the  ability  of  your  company  to  supply  addi-

tional products or services at the same level of quality. You should expect a buyer

to have a high %S when the cost of the products or services is minimal compared

to the total costs, but the level of service could significantly impact the project

budget or outcome. An example is a customer’s concern for lost operating time

due to equipment failures in a deepwater exploration environment where rig costs

are much higher than the typical services your company provides. In such situa-

tions,  the  demonstration  of  capabilities  with  case  histories,  reference  lists  and

letters, and operating statistics can play a very important role in preventing cus-

tomer objections such as skepticism or misunderstanding.



Added Value (%V)



How concerned is the buyer about the impact the solution has on project objectives

and total project costs? Buyers with a high concern for the added value of a solu-



332



CHAPTER 9



Strategic Sales Plan



tion typically consider all claimed benefits and value; however, they require a very

good  demonstration  of  capabilities.  Additional  value  may  be  derived  from  more

than product and service capabilities. All differentiating factors—such as service,

organization, support personnel, and business models—must be considered.



Other Factors



The previously discussed factors are the most common considerations buyers have

regarding proposed solutions. Other commonly considered factors are:



•  Quality (%Q)



•  Health, Safety, and Environment (%HSE)



•  Relationship (%R)—how important the level of relationship is



The buying factors should be described and listed as percentages totaling 100%.



Concerns



Examples of customer concerns are:



•  Concerns caused by previous bad experiences.



•  Concerns the supplier is not treating specific aspects with enough importance.



•  Concerns specific requirements might not be met, such as target completion dates.



•  Concerns for how influential or powerful members of the buying center will

view  the  outcomes  and  the  impact  they  can  have  on  the  buying  center

member’s career.



Expectations



Examples of customer expectations are:



•  High  expectations  when  individuals  are  behind  on  objectives  and  expect

this project to make up for short falls, even though this could be unrealistic.



•  Personal objectives for which bonuses are paid.



•  Key  success  factors  the  buyer  believes  will  contribute  to  job  success  and

lead to a promotion or recognition. For example, no problems will  be proof

they are an excellent manager.



As discussed earlier in Chapter 6 “Sales Activities,” relationship building is very

important for the professional salesperson. An important part of building relation-

ships with customers is the level of trust between you and them. Most buyers will

tell respected suppliers their concerns and expectations.



If you do not know the personal priorities, you should not guess. Later in the imple-

mentation process, or as part of the account management activities, sales representa-

tives should try to build relationships with the buying center members’ and discover

their  concerns  and  expectations.  These  factors  can  be  entered  into  the  personal

priorities section of the SSP. Even though this information will come after the award,

it can become valuable the next time you have an opportunity for the same client.

The  completed  SSP  can  be  reviewed  at  the  start  of  the  opportunities  management

process for future opportunities and is an excellent way to capture important informa-

tion about the buying center members and share the information with new sales team

members or other salespeople in different divisions in your company.



The client interview form request for proposal worksheet is used to interview each

buying center member to ask about their business and personal priorities. Figure

9.7 highlights where this information is recorded on the form. The green area is

for business priorities and the blue for personal priorities. For large sales you use

the request for proposal form to interview the members of the buying center early



All Strategy Depends on the Competition



333





Fig. 9.7

The Request for Proposal

worksheet is designed to assist

the salesperson in having a

focused and efficient discussion

regarding the opportunity.

Business priorities are included in

the green area and personal pri-

orities in the blue area.



in the “Evaluation of Options” and then return to hold sales meetings to show how

your company can support their needs. Some salespeople report feeling uncom-

fortable asking customers about their priorities connected to an opportunity, but

you  should  not.  Refer  back  to  Chapter  4  “Opportunities  Management”  Chapter

Highlight 4.1: “Be Like Nike and Just Do It!” As mentioned earlier, the salesperson

might feel uncomfortable because it is a new approach for them. The other reason

is they may feel like they are supposed to ask such questions as a supplier. To see

why some salespeople feel like this, see Chapter Highlight 9.2: “Just Take My

Order, Please.”



Adaptability and Social Style



The adaptability factor, as described by the contacts technology adoption profile

and social style, are also included in the SSP. The technology adaptability factor

was discussed in the Chapter 1 “Understanding the Role of Marketing & Sales,”

and social style was discussed in Chapter 5 “Communication Skills.” These factors,

in addition to role, influence, and alignment factors, assist you in determining the

best way to approach the individual buying center member and help you to align

effectively with the individual.



Coverage



The coverage factor is the level of contact made with the buying center member

regarding the opportunity. This factor helps you decide whether the level of con-

tact is sufficient. If it is determined to be insufficient, you must assign someone

to contact the buyer and ensure that your representative has a well-prepared sales

call plan, as discussed in Chapter 7 “Sales Call Skills.” Later in the tactics section,

this  information  is  used  to  determine  the  highest  priority  tactics.  Key  contacts

who have little or no contact with you are considered a weakness of the opportu-

nity. Ideally, this lack of contact should be resolved before you select your com-

petitive sales strategy and certainly by the time you enter into the procurement

stage. Here are some options for the time-spent factor:



•  In-depth. The contact has been contacted multiple times by your company

representatives  and  you  have  an  in-depth  understanding  of  the  contact’s

priorities for this opportunity, their alignment, and how they rank the for-

mal decision criteria. There should be no unresolved issues with this con-

tact. If in-depth contacting has been done and the buying center member

remains neutral, you should consider asking a supporter or mentor to con-



334



CHAPTER 9



Strategic Sales Plan



Chapter Highlight 9.2:  Just Take My Order, Please



Sometimes  salespeople  feel  awkward  asking  the  questions  recommended  when  taking  a  consultative  selling

approach. They feel better if they just take the customer’s order, give them a price and see who wins the work. Part

of the awkward feeling comes from inexperience at having these types of conversations, and another factor is the

perceived role that you have with the customer in this opportunity. To understand this, you need to know what the

customer expects (or your perception of this expectation) from you and your company regarding your input into the

purchasing  decision.  Sometimes  you  might  hear  people  say  it  is  who  you  know  that  counts  to  get  things  done.

However, in the sales world, what is more important than who you know is how you are known to them?9, 10, 11



One way to determine what a customer perceives or expects from your company is to identify how you participate

with that customer in the five stages of the project management process, as discussed in Chapter 2 “Value Drivers.”

In the figure at the bottom of this page, the supplier role is classified on three levels, as follows:



•  Trusted adviser



•  Problem solver (sometimes referred to as Technical Expert)



•  Vendor



The black line under each role indicates in which stages in the project life cycle your company participates. The white

curved line indicates the amount of your company’s involvement with the customer’s executive in each stage of the

project life cycle. Typically, executive involvement in the project life cycle is high in earlier stages and then gradually

decreases through each successive stage to procurement, when it is lowest. Executive involvement increases again

once  the  project  starts  and  is  high  again  when  the  results  are  reviewed. Your  company’s  contact  with  customer

management during the project stages is a good indicator of your role in the account.



Trusted Adviser

If the customer considers you a trusted adviser, you are viewed as a stakeholder. At this level, the customer believes

you are interested in optimizing the performance of their reservoirs. You have demonstrated your concern that they

achieve their goals and objectives. When viewed as a trusted adviser, you will be involved in all five stages of the

customer’s project life cycle.



Problem Solver

If the customer considers you a problem solver, you are viewed as technical specialists. This perception can apply

to specific products or ser vices, or can apply only to a specific situation, such as your expertise in offshore develop-

ment. When this is your role, you are involved in the later part of the project selection stage (2) and in the evaluation

of options stage (3) as well as on through the review of results stage (5).



Vendor

The account considers your company as a vendor if the only time you become involved in a project is when you

receive a tender to bid during the procurement process. If you win the contract, you participate in the execution. In

this  role  the  vendor  does  not  have  contact  at  the  executive  level. With  such  limited  exposure  to  the  customer’s

project life cycle, you cannot have much influence.

335







tact  the  individual  to  determine  why  he  or  she  is  still  undecided.  If  the

contact  is  a  non-supporter  after  in-depth  contact,  you  must  have  inside

support to overcome the negative impact the non-supporter has at decision

time. There may be unresolved issues you are not aware of, or the buying

center member may be a solid supporter or mentor of the competition.



•  General. Multiple contacts have been made with the individual you know

from previous opportunities or are working with on the current project, but

you have not talked to them specifically about this opportunity.



•  Brief. Few contacts have been made, with no significant impact on the buy-

ing process. If the buying center member is important to the decision-mak-

ing process, more contact is needed to determine the member’s alignment

toward your company and whether that member has any unresolved issues.



•  None. No contacts have been made. This is not a good position to be in with

anybody in the buying center. Changing this should be a priority for account

management.  If  it  is  early  in  the  opportunities  management  process,  the

first  contact  should  be  made  to  determine  as  many  of  the  buying  center

factors as possible and plan the relationship strategy.



Decision Criteria



The formal decision is used by the evaluator, the person with the responsibility to

evaluate the competitive offers. If this information is not included in the request

for proposal, it needs to be requested as part of the  customer interviews early in

the  opportunity  management  process.  The  Decision-Making  Process  section  is

included  in  the  Request  for  Proposal  worksheet  and  is  shown  in  Fig.  9.8.  The

question the salesperson asks is how will they evaluate the offers from their sup-

pliers and what is the evaluation model? The form has been designed so that after

the  meeting,  the  salesperson  can  complete  a  vulnerability  analysis  of  how  they

believe they will be evaluated compared to their competitors. Two common chal-

lenges salespeople encounter when asking about the decision criteria are that the

customer tells them this is confidential or that it will be only the price. To see how

to respond to these types of customer replies, see the sidebar “Off the Record.”



In addition to asking the evaluator about the formal decision criteria, the sales-

person  also  wants  to  ask  the  other  buying  center  members  how  they  rank  the

formal  decision  factors  from  most  to  least  important.  It  is  useful  to  know  who

ranks those factors you are strong in as high in case you need to leverage them to

secure the opportunity and to know who might support the competition for factors

on which they are evaluated as strong.

The formal decision criteria used in

the customer’s decision-making pro-

cess and how the factors are evalu-

ated in an evaluation model. In this

example Company A is vulnerable to

Competitor 1 on the price factor.

Company A will need to address this

vulnerability  as part of the actions

in the strategic sales plan. See

Chapter Highlight 9.3

for some options.

CHAPTER 9



Strategic Sales Plan



u Off the Record



Sometimes when salespeople

try to apply the consultative

selling approach, the coopera-

tion from the customer can be

less than encouraging; once

you know how to handle this, it

will not be an issue. Here are

two common situations and

two replies for uncooperative

customers.



Scenario 1

The customer is asked how

they will evaluate the proposals

from you suppliers. The custom-

er replies, “I'm sorry, that is

confidential. I cannot tell you

that.” The salesperson’s recom-

mended reply:  “I'm sorry to

hear that as I do value your

business and want to ensure

that I do my best job possible

to recommend a solution that is

exactly what you need. If I

know how you evaluate your

proposals, then I know what is

important to you. Can you give

me some guidance on where I

should focus?”



Scenario 2

The customer is asked the

same question and replies,

“That is easy. We look for the

lowest price and award.”  The

salesperson’s recommended

reply: “Of course price is always

important. If you don’t mind, I

would like to ask you a hypo-

thetical question.” Customer:

“Yes, go ahead.” Salesperson:

“How would you evaluate if the

prices were all the same?”

Customer: “That is a good

question. I guess we would

look at different factors.”

Salesperson: "Yes and those

factors would be?”



At this point, the customer is

talking about the other factors

in addition to price.



If  the  sales  team  finds  they  are  vulnerable  to  the  competition  on  any  of  the

formal  decision  criteria  shown  in  Fig.  9.9,  they  must  decide  what  to  do  to

address the vulnerability. For ideas on what to do, see Chapter Highlight 9.3:

“Vulnerabilities 1-2-3.”



Presenting the Buying Center Analysis



The buying center analysis consists of three parts:



•  Buying center organizational analysis



•  Decision criteria table



•



Priorities table



Buying Center Organizational Analysis



The best practice for presenting a completed buying center analysis is to build an

organizational analysis using a program such as PowerPoint, Excel, Visio, or your

company’s  CRM  system  if  this  functionality  is  included.  In  the  organizational

analysis,  show  each  factor  from  the  contact  profiling.  Figure  9.9  provides  an

example for a completed buying center analysis built in PowerPoint. In addition,

add to your buying center analysis lines of influence that exist between the buying

center members. Lines of influence are discussed below.



Lines of Influence



Lines of influence are relationships that exist between individual buying center

members. Four types of influence relationships are identified on the buying center

organizational analysis chart:



Direct Reporting



Direct reporting is shown according to the standard organizational chart.



Business Relationship



The business relationship describes a formal or informal relationship that exists

between two buying center members. The following are examples of formal busi-

ness relationships:



•  Dual reporting or functional reporting, which is part of the formal reporting

structure.  This  is  a  common  in  companies  with  a  matrix  structure.  For

example, a geologist located in Indonesia reports directly to the Indonesian

asset manager and has a dual report to the exploration manager in Houston

at headquarters.



•  Temporary reporting lines, which apply to a specific project for the evalua-

tor, who may belong to another department but for this project temporarily

reports to the decision-maker or approver in another department.



•  The  decision-maker  is  actually  a  committee,  but  one  person  has  been

selected  as  the  chairman  so  the  other  decision-makers  have  a  business

relationship with this person.



Examples of informal business relationships are:



•  An approver may have worked many years with someone who is now at a

lower  level,  but  still  seeks  their  opinion  regarding  issues  in  which  they

consider them an expert.



All Strategy Depends on the Competition



337





Chapter Highlight 9.3: Vulnerabilities As Easy As 1-2-3



Discovering  vulnerabilities  in  the  sales  world  is  not  a  bad  thing  provided  you

have time to address your vulnerabilities, which is the purpose of completing a

strategic  sales  plan.  Consolidate  your  conversations  with  your  customer  to

analyze the buying center requirements and complete your vulnerability analy-

sis  to  decide  the  action  needed.  If  you  are  applying  the  professional  sales

processes,  it  is  as  easy  as  1-2-3. The  actions  can  be  categorized  into  three

strategies using the example from Fig 9.7 to illustrate. In this example, there

is a vulnerability for Company A based on the price factor; however, Company

A is rated better than Competitor 1 for experience.



Strategy No. 1

Change the Customer’s Decision Criteria or Evaluation Model



By  showing  the  requirements  and  critical  success  factors  collected  to

understand  the  customer’s  needs,  you  can  suggest  other  factors  to  be

included in the decision criteria or have them change the relative ranking of

the importance of the different factors. In this example from Fig. 9.7, the

price and experience factors will balance out and the evaluation will be very

close. In this case, it would be best to recommend to the customer that,

to better assure achieving their objectives, they should include another fac-

tor. This  can  vary  from  opportunity  to  opportunity,  which  is  why  it  is  so

important to interview the members of the buying center for every oppor-

tunity to understand their business and personal priorities connected with

this opportunity. In this example, as the customer is very concerned about

price, the sales team should look for factors that have a significant impact

on  costs.  Perhaps  in-country  support  or  local  content  should  be  added,  and  now  the  analysis  would  show  that.

Company A has two significant strong evaluations compared to only one for the competitor.



Critical  Success  Factor  for  Strategy  1:   This  strategy  in  most  cases  will  require  that  you  have  completed  your

strategic sales plan before the customer has locked their evaluation model. Therefore, it is an option if you have time.



Strategy No. 2

Improve Your Evaluation

In  this  strategy,  you  focus  on  improving  your  evaluation,  which  in  most

cases means that you believe there is a customer objection causing your

evaluation  to  be  lower  than  what  it  should  be.  In  this  example,  the  cus-

tomer  has  evaluated  your  added  value  or  benefits  to  be  lower  than  the

competitor,  but  you  believe  you  have  significantly  more  value  than  the

competition. You believe this error is due to a misunderstanding. If you can

handle  the  misunderstanding,  then  the  evaluation  would  then  look  much

better for your company.



Critical Success Factor for Strategy 2:  In this strategy you need to rec-

ognize the error and be able to handle the customer’s objection.



Strategy No. 3

Diminish Competitor’s Evaluation

In this strategy you believe the competitor’s evaluation is too good. In this example, you believe their price evaluation

will appear cheap if the customer does not consider the extra mobilization time and costs as Competitor 1 is not in

the country where the program will be executed.



Critical Success Factor for Strategy 3: For this strategy to be successful, you need to know your competition’s

capabilities  and  have  better  inside  support  or  stronger  relationships  with

the people in the buying center than the competitor. If not, the customer

could call the competitor and tell the competitor they are very concerned

about them not having operations in the country and the extra costs that

will  be  created  for  the  customer. The  competition  can  now  correct  their

vulnerability. The other issue with this strategy is that you are not talking

about  your  company;  rather  you  are  talking  about  the  competition,  which

may not be appealing to the customer. If you are going to use this strategy,

it is done best through education of the customer and where you talk about

issues  such  as  mobilization  costs  and  how  your  company  addresses  that

issue for your customer.

Fig. 9.9

A completed buying center organization-

al analysis with explanation of entries

and level of influence.



•  When multiple companies are involved in a project or there are  government

approvals  required  for  a  project  to  proceed,  as  in  the  case  of  production

sharing contracts, there might be individuals who have informal relation-

ships and have agreed to cooperate to ensure approval for the project.



Personal Relationship



Personal relationships identify the everyday relationships between people that are

independent  of  business  issues.  Examples  are  spouses  and  friends  who  have

similar interests and hobbies, such as golfing and scuba diving, etc.



Potential Conflict



Relationships with potential conflict can exist for many reasons. The individuals

might be competing for a promotion and might not support each other because of

this rivalry. Or there may be long-standing misunderstandings between two buying

center  individuals.  Whatever  the  reason,  you  should  record  any  knowledge  of

potential conflict and take it into consideration when dealing with these buying

center members.



Decision Criteria Table



In this section, sales representatives should list the decision criteria used in the

formal decision-making process. These criteria should be entered for buying cen-

ter members who have high influence. Each decision criterion is ranked according

to  importance  for  the  buying  center  member  of  high  influence.  It  is  useful  to

capture this information in order to prepare a focused discussion when meeting

with the high influence buyers and ensuring that you discuss the issues and busi-

ness  and  personal  priorities  that  your  company  can  impact  with  a  successful

project execution. Fig. 9.10 on the next page is an example of how to present this

information in a table format.

Priorities Tables



The priorities tables consist of two tables:



•  Contact business priorities. (includes objective, budget, requirements, and



critical success factors)



•  Contact  personal  priorities.  (includes  buying  factors,  concerns,  and



expectations)



Both  tables  are  completed  by  copying  the  information  you  recorded  on  the

Request  for  Proposal  worksheet  during  your  interview  with  the  client  (see  Fig.

9.7).  Each  table  has  one  line  for  each  buying  center  member.  The  tables  are

included in the Strategic Sales Plan template (page 5) in Appendix B. This infor-

mation is very important for anyone who will contact the customers in support of

securing the opportunity. The priorities information allows each sales call to be

tailored to focus on the factors that are important to the contact. Making connec-

tions between your proposal and what is important to the contact is the best way

to motivate someone to become a Supporter or Mentor.



Competitive Analysis

In the competitive assessment section of the SSP, you describe your competitive

sales strategy. This description should include:



•  Competitors. Your most significant competitors.



•  Competitive  Sales  Strategy.  The  competitive  sales  strategies  your  com-



petitors will choose.



•  Strategy  description.  Briefly  describe  the  competitor’s  strategy  and

whether  this  strategy  has  been  used  elsewhere  in  the  world.  Check  your

company  CRM  database  for  recent  opportunities  that  involved  the  com-

petitor for both the same account and similar projects.



•  Solution proposed. What products and services will be included in the com-

petitor’s solution? Include any products or services considered to have exclu-

sive features and benefits, as entered in the solution section products applet.



•  Strengths. What are the strengths and unique business value of the com-

petitor’s  solution?  Strengths  include  technical  factors,  service,  relation-

ships, and experience. The financial aspects value is the proven financial

benefit of any strength. This section should be a summary of the strengths

and values entered in the individual buyer’s personal priorities section.



•  Weaknesses. These are the weaknesses from the buying center members’



point of view.



340



CHAPTER 9



Strategic Sales Plan















•  Alignment. List any supporters the customer has in the buying center.



•  Comments. Add any comments regarding the overall competitor strategy.



The next part of the SSP describes your proposed solution.



Proposed Solution

Deciding on your solution starts with client interviews with the members of the

customer’s buying center. In these first visits, the sales team should determine the

customer’s business and personal priorities regarding the opportunity and what

decision criteria and evaluation model will be used to select the winning solution.

The initial solution that best satisfies the customer’s requirements is then tested

using a vulnerability analysis by considering competitive offerings and the unique

strengths  and  value  of  your  solution.  The  initial  solution  might  be  altered  as  a

result of the vulnerability analysis. Each of the sections in this part of the strate-

gic  sales  plan  are  described  below.  Figure  9.11  shows  the  key  inputs  into  the

proposed solution decision.



Proposed Solution



In the proposed solution section, you describe the strategic and physical compo-

nents  of  your  solution  and  how  this  will  contribute  to  the  customer’s  project

objective  and  help  them  meet  their  compelling  event.  The  solution  description

should also include your expected revenues.



Solution Fit



A  very  important  part  of  your  competitive  sales  strategy  is  how  your  proposed

solution will be evaluated against the formal decision criteria compared to your

competitors. The analysis identifies the best to lowest overall evaluation scores

for  the  customer’s  evaluation  model.  This  analysis  is  one  of  the  two  required

inputs when selecting your competitive sales strategy as discussed in Chapter 4

“Opportunities  Management.”  Figure  9.12  shows  an  example  of  the  decision

criteria analysis included in the SSP. The evaluation model shown in Fig. 9.8 is

the multiple point criterion evaluation model as discussed in Chapter 4 “Oppor-

tunities Management.” In this example, Company A would be awarded the work

provided  their  offer  price  is  within  the  acceptable  premium  range  (normally

10%). Company A has a much better evaluation on the experience and quality

factors.



Fig. 9.11

Key inputs for deciding on the solution

to be proposed to the customer and

described in the Proposed Solution

section of the strategic sales plan.



Fig. 9.12

Formal decision criteria analysis

chart presented in the Strategic

Sales Plan report.



All Strategy Depends on the Competition



341





Strengths



Intangible benefits of your solution for the customer should be described in the

strengths section. Each stated strength should be connected to one or more of the

customer’s project requirements and should have been discussed with and agreed

to by the customer.



Weaknesses



The weaknesses section describes the vulnerabilities identified in the vulnerabil-

ity analysis. It also contains weaknesses or risks not included in the vulnerability

analysis,  such  as  your  management  concerns,  and  sales  and  marketing  issues

associated with your solution. Tactics to minimize your weaknesses are discussed

later in this chapter.



At  this  point  in  the  planning  process,  you  are  now  ready  to  evaluate  your  com-

petitive  position  for  your  proposed  solution  and  select  your  competitive  sales

strategy and tactics to execute  your strategy. You will evaluate your position using

the level 1 twenty-question assessment described in Chaper 6 “Sales Activities.”

A copy of the assessment questions are included in the appendix of this chapter.



The process of doing this evaluation has several benefits:



•



•



•



•



It demonstrates that it is not enough to know the formal decision factors

but you also need to know how these factors are measured and evaluated.

Once  you  understand  how  these  measured  and  evaluated  then  in  your

proposal  should  discuss  those  specifics  to  help  convince  your  customer

and make them confident in your proposed solution.



In  the  case  where  your  company  is  evaluated  second  or  lower,  you  can

show where you must focus in order to address your weaknesses by choos-

ing one of the vulnerability strategies, as discussed in Chapter Highlight

9.3: Vulnerabilities As Easy As 1-2-3.



For the example, in Fig. 9.9, if Company A were to lose even though the

sales team evaluated themselves as the best, then they know they either

made a mistake in their evaluation or the competitor chose a weak posi-

tion  competitive  sales  strategy  (e.g.,  defend  or  fragment)  and  was  suc-

cessful in motivating one of the high-influence buying center members to

have  the  award  decision  made  in  the  competitor’s  favor.  The  benefit  of

this albeit bad situation is that now the sales team knows they have an

error. They can use the outcome debrief with the customer to clarify why

they lost and factor this into their next opportunity.  It is bad to make a

mistake, but even worse to make the same mistake twice.



If  Company  A  does  win,  then  when  doing  the  outcome  debrief  with  the

customer  they  can  validate  their  analysis  with  feedback  from  the  cus-

tomer. Once they start the project or selling their products and services

to the customer, they can use the factors from the analysis as metrics to

confirm they are meeting the expectations of the customer.



Unique Business Value



The unique business value section states the total dollar value of the benefits to

the customer derived from the differentiated benefits of your solution. The unique

business value must be stated in terms of dollar benefits to the customer and must

clearly  differentiate  your  solution  from  the  competitor’s.  Differential  benefits

result from leveraging your organization, unique products and services, superior



342

Strategic Sales Plan



Fig. 9.13

Customer Value Analysis. The custom-

er value analysis shows, starting on

the  left: market  price for standard

products or services (green bar), dif-

value the competitor provides but your

solution does not (red bar), customer

the customer would pay for your solu-

which is market rate plus your share

of the differential value (blue bar), and

much premium over the market price

the market-based price is.



service,  personnel  expertise,  and  pricing  (either  lowest  price  or  best  value  for

money paid).



This  section  also  includes  the  validated  dollar  amount  for  each  differentiating

value component and is presented with a customer value analysis spreadsheet. An

example of a value analysis is shown in Fig. 9.13, and will be further described

in Chapter 11 “Pricing for Bidding.”



Your unique business value is what motivates the customer to pay a premium for

you over the competition. This is critical in a bidding environment when the cus-

tomer’s  procurement  process  awards  the  work  to  technically  qualified  bidders

and, therefore, award is based on the lowest priced bid. In this case, the added

value will be captured after the award. The specific strategy will be discussed later

in this chapter.



Opportunity Assessment

This section provides the analysis of the opportunity assessment, as shown Fig.

9.14.  Visually, the more blue color in your analysis, the stronger your competitive

position.  In  the  context  of  creating  and  presenting  a  strategic  sales  plan,  there

should be very few “?’s” in the assessment analysis at this time. If there are too
