Opportunity assessment analysis.

Factors evaluated as “+” are color-

coded blue, factors evaluated as “–”

are color-coded yellow, and factors

evaluated as “?” are color-coded red.

The Excel worksheet to generate the

assessment analysis can be down-

loaded from the textbook website.



All Strategy Depends on the Competition



343





Chapter Highlight 9.4: You Snooze, You Lose



Making Time Your Ally

Earlier you read the Chapter Highlight 9.1: “But it is Only One Piece of Paper!” which told the story of Company A

winning a big opportunity at more than double the price of the competition. The fact is that the salesperson, as soon

as they were told of the potential opportunity, immediately developed a client interview plan and contacted each

person in the customer’s buying center to determine their business and personal priorities. That took three months

to complete and after that, the sales team completed their analysis and had their strategic sales plan reviewed and

approved, which left them one year before the bid would be sent to bidders. In the twelve months before the bids

were  sent,  Company A  ran  a  demonstration  job  of  their  new  technology  on  the  customer’s  well  in

another field that was a success. This was six months before the bid was released. At the post-job

review  of  the  results,  the  chief  geologist  and  decision-maker  in  the  customer’s  buying  center



stated, “This is excellent. We will run this in our big exploration project.”



In total, the Company A sales team made seven sales calls to the customer to support their

proposed solution. The lead salesperson was in five of those sales meetings and, each time

he would sign in to the customer’s reception register for his meeting, he would check back

to see if the competitor’s salesperson had made a sales call to the customer since discover-

ing the opportunity. The first time the competitor’s salesperson visited was after receiving the

bid. Although the competitor’s salesperson was waiting for the bid to come out to go

and talk to the customer, Company A’s sales team was providing value. This is why the

opportunities management process has been made to look like a clock. Time and cus-

tomer’s opportunity, waits for no one. If you snooze you lose! To quote T. J. Wilson,

founder of IBM, one more time, “Make time your ally and time will make you.”



Level 2 Strategic Sales Plan Review



The professional salesperson understands that there is a time pressure to start execut-

ing your competitive sales strategy as soon as possible. The best way to ensure that

the  sales  team  does  the  first  client  interviews  as  soon  as

possible is for the sales manager and sales team to commit

to a date to have the Level 2 Strategic Sales Plan Review,

which  will  be  discussed  later  in  this  chapter.  At  the

review, the sales team will present their completed



The

Opportunities

Management Process

looks like the hands of a

clock. The sales team should

focus on point A, Evaluation

of Options. This is the time to

hold a Level 2 Strategic Sales

Plan Review—as soon as the

key buying center members

have been interviewed and the

competition analysis completed.



strategic sales plan. This date should be well before the bid is received, so

the  time  after  the  level  2  review  can  be  used  to  execute  the  competitive

sales  strategy.  In  large  sales,  the  amount  of  time  typically  needed  before

receiving the bid is six to twelve months. There are many factors that impact

how much time is needed, such as:



•  Other key opportunities for the sales team



•  Other key projects for the customer that need to be scheduled around



•  Holidays in between the review and receiving the bid



•  Location of the customer’s buying center members



•  Availability of the customer’s buying center members



•  The  level  of  complexity  of  the  sales  calls,  such  as  whether  demonstration  jobs  should  be  run  to  provide



proofs (as in the example above)



As shown above in the magnifying glass, the sales team needs to focus on point A. This is the date to have the

strategic sales plan reviewed and then begin to execute their competitive sales strategy. If the competition wants

to wait till the bid comes out, then let them sleep.



many “?’s” then choosing your competitive sales strategy at this time is very risky

as was described in Chapter 4 “Opportunity Management” and illustrated in Chap-

ter Highlight 4.2: “Tell Me Doctor, Do I Have an Opportunity?” Ideally at this time

the sales team will base their competitive strategy on the number of yellow and

blue factors for them versus the competition. The quality and confidence in select-

ing the correct strategy is directly a function of making time your advantage and

completing all the customer  interviews as quickly as possible after an opportunity



344



CHAPTER 9



Strategic Sales Plan



has been qualified. See Chapter Highlight 9.4: “You Snooze, You Lose” for what

happens when you are waiting for the bid to arrive.



Supporting Comments for Opportunity Assessment



In  this  section,  you  include  any  supporting  comments  that  add  insight  into  the

assessment for any of the twenty questions that have not been covered in any of

the sections of the plan to this point, such as:



•  Other opportunities or projects that might affect this assessment, such as



the competition losing a major bid that is currently in progress.



•  Recent developments that could affect the customer’s decision criteria. For

example, a war in the country where the customer is planning operations

or a merger of companies (either customers or competitors) and new gov-

ernment  regulations  could  have  negative  or  positive  implications  for  the

customer’s opportunity.



Is it Worth Winning



Each of the other three main assessment questions had supplementary informa-

tion  to  give  more  confidence  in  the  assessment.  For  the  “Is  It  Worth  Winning?”

assessment question, you provide additional insight with the pricing strategy table

and supporting comments for degree of risk and strategic value.



Pricing Strategy Table



The  pricing  strategy  table  is  a  summary,  not  a  detailed  analysis,  using  averages

and ratios from past projects provided by the operational staff or salespeople with

previous operations experience to develop a first estimate of the revenue, costs,

and  profitability.  In  Chapter  11  “Pricing  for  Bidding,”  a  detailed  discussion  will

expand how to complete a pricing strategy for large opportunities. An example of

a pricing strategy table is shown in Fig. 9.15.



Fig. 9.15

The pricing strategy table: is an estimate

of the opportunity revenue, costs, and

profitability. It also gives an indication of

the expected bid price and expected rev-

enue depending on the final competitive

sales strategy and bid analysis. The table

contains three parts: chart input, table,

and customer perspective gauges. The

table can be generated in Excel and will

be explained in detail in Chapter 11

“Pricing for Bidding.” An Excel pricing

strategy table can be downloaded from

the textbook website.



All Strategy Depends on the Competition



345





Supporting Comments for Degree of Risks and Strategic Value



In this section provide any comments to support the assessment for the questions

degree of risks and strategic value questions.



Degree of Risk



•  What are the levels of technical financial and other risks associated with



the project?



•  What are the risks of the customer not fulfilling the work scope, ending the



project early, or canceling the project?



•  What could cause your solution to fail?



•  What are the critical dependencies in delivering value to your customer?



•  How could the customer cause your solution to fail?



•  What is the impact on your business if the solution fails?



•  How much of this investment is at risk?



•  If the risk factor is high, what could be done to reduce it?



Strategic Value



•  To determine whether a project is strategic, you must determine whether

the  project  is  more  profitable  than  other  projects  of  the  same  contact

period  that  use  the  same  resources.  If  so,  why?  The  strategic  value  of  a

project  is  best  answered  by  upper  management.  In  your  additional  com-

ments, state which executive identified this opportunity as strategic.



•  Do long-term account factors, such as product development or competitive

issues, make winning this project strategic? To answer this question when

resources are limited, it is advisable to obtain upper management input.



Competitive Sales Strategy

In this section, describe your competitive sales strategy based on your analysis to

this point and then identify the tactics to you will use to execute your strategy. As



Fig. 9.16

Selection of the competitive

sales strategy is one of the

most important decisions in

the strategic sales plan. It

also represents the turning

point where knowledge is

translated into a winning

strategy and actions.



346



CHAPTER 9



Strategic Sales Plan



discussed in Chapter 4 “Opportunities Management,” your competitive sales strat-

egy is selected by considering your evaluation against the formal decision criteria

and whether you can change the customer’s scope of work. Before selecting your

strategy,  it  is  recommended  that  you  review  the  Competitive  Strategy  Decision

Process in Chapter 4. In this section, you present the competitive strategy table,

competitive strategy description, and critical success factors of your plan. Each

part is described below.



Competitive Strategy Table



The competitive strategy table is created using the assessment analysis worksheet

available  for  download  from  the  textbook  website.  Once  the  sales  team  has

decided on their competitive sales strategy, they select the sales strategy for them

and their competitors. Then the competitive strategy table is created. An example

table is shown in Fig. 9.16.



Based on the assessment analysis, the spreadsheet using logical tests will identify:



•  Holes.  This  is  where  an  assessment  question  for  the  given  competitive  sales

strategy should be “+” in your assessment but you have either a “?” or “-”.



•  Vulnerabilities.  This  is  where  an  assessment  question  for  a  given  sales

strategy is “-” for you, but the competition for that assessment question has

a  “+”;  therefore,  you  are  vulnerable  to  the  competition  for  that  question.

The specific detail for which of the twenty assessment questions have holes

or vulnerabilities leads to another spreadsheet generated automatically that

you can review.



At the bottom of the table is the competitive sales strategy matrix discussed in

detail in Chapter 4 “Opportunities Management.” The matrix has an added panel

to emphasize the minimum requirements when selecting a flanking or fragment

competitive sales strategy.



As shown in Fig 9.16, any time the salesperson picks a flanking or fragment com-

petitive sales strategy, three questions must be answered:



1.  Who will sign the change order?



2.  Why will the customer sign a change order?



3.  When will they sign the change order?



It  is  possible  that  these  questions  will  be  answered  once  the  competitive  sales

strategy is confirmed. This is acceptable as long as specific tactics are assigned

to someone on the sales team.



Competitive Strategy Description



In  this  section,  you  describe  your  competitive  sales  strategy.  In  addition,  you

highlight the following:



•  Reasons why you have chosen this strategy rather than other strategies



•  Priorities to execute the strategy



•  Risks associated with the chosen strategy



Critical Success Factors



In this section, list the critical success factors for your plan to succeed. Evaluate

the critical success factors from the point of view of the customer, your company,

and  the  competitor.  Address  any  significant  customer  concerns  or  issues  that

could prevent you from being awarded the work.



All Strategy Depends on the Competition



347





Chapter Highlight 9.5: The Best Defense is a Good Offense



Winning the Work through Competitive Sales Strategy Execution



The best defense is a good offense. There are a lot of people who believe

this, and the same advice is also good for a strategic sales plan. There are

five main sales tactics as described earlier throughout the textbook. Prove

value, retrieve missing information, insulate against the competition, mini-

mize  weaknesses,  and  emphasize  strengths  are  all  very  good  tactics.

However, the purpose of these tactics in large B2B sales is ultimately for

the customer to make the award decision when you are not in their office.

At that time, you want to be insulated against the competition—from their

last-minute desperation attempts to secure the work or from a non-support-

er or, worse yet, an enemy trying to have the work awarded to anyone but

your company. To be insulated against the competition, you need to have

proved value, minimized weakness, and emphasized strengths. If you are reading closely, you will have noticed that

the tactics are now in the past tense.



i n



Below is another illustration to explain an effective competitive sales strategy execution. In the diagram at the bot-

tom are the opportunities management process and the buyer-readiness stages. As described before, by using the

consultative selling approach and being engaged with the customer early in the process, you should be aligned to

what they are trying to do. Early in the

process  (shown  as  the  stages  shaded

yellow), you should be on the offensive

and  meet  with  all  the  buying  center

members  to  understand  what  the

enemy will use against you to stop you

from  being  selected;  you  should  also

be  trying  to  motivate  the  people  who

are  non-supporters  and  neutral.  But

there  comes  a  time  when  the  selling

stops,  as  shown  by  the  red  line. This

happens after you have submitted your

proposal and gone through the submis-

sion  of  proposal,  resolution  of  con-

cerns,  and  negotiations.  At  this  point,

you are in the monitoring stage and relying on your supporters and mentors to defend the decision to choose your

company. This time is shaded in red on the diagram. The emphasis makes you contact all the people in the buying

center regardless of their alignment. Too many salespeople tend to restrict their conversations to supporters and

mentors who are already convinced your company is the best for their opportunity. The problem with this is you are

not talking with the people who are not supporters; therefore, when it comes time to make the final buying decision

you may not have sufficient materials for your supporters and mentors to defend your award.



The other point to make here is beware of the enemy; too often salespeople are scared to talk to someone who

has been labeled as not liking your company or who prefers to use the competition or has never used your com-

pany.  This information might be accurate, but it does not warrant not talking to these people. If this is their opinion,

then it will take time to motivate them from enemy or non-supporter, so don’t put off contacting them as you will

need time to bring them to your side. A good tip for when you are transferred into a new sales territory or assigned

a new account is to ensure that you discuss with the outgoing salesperson the alignment of current customers and

prospects. For those identified as enemy, make it a point to schedule a visit with them at the very beginning of your

assignment. The reason for this is to meet them and explain that from your discussion with the outgoing salesper-

son, you understand they prefer to use other suppliers; you would like to understand why so you can try improve

future proposals to win their business. This achieves three results:



•



•



It demonstrates to the customer that they are important.



It allows the customer to express why they prefer the competitors.



•



It builds rapport with the customer.



All the above are positive results and one of these might be enough to get you back on the road to securing future

business from this customer. For a real example of this, review Chapter Highlight 4-6: “Making Incidental Decision

Criteria Crucial.”



348



CHAPTER 9



Strategic Sales Plan





The last part of the plan are the specific tactics that will be taken to execute the

competitive sales strategy.



Actions

This section consist of two tables: relationship strategies and other actions.



Relationship Strategies



There are three relationship strategies chosen depending on the customer’s buyer-

readiness stage, alignment, his or her role in the buying center, the level of influ-

ence, and timing. For a more graphical explanation of these factors see Chapter

Highlight  9.5:  “The  Best  Defense  is  a  Good  Offense.”  The  three  relationship

strategies12 are:



•  Leverage



•  Motivate



•  Neutralize



Leverage



A leverage relationship strategy means you want a buying center member to act

on behalf of your company inside the buying center to improve your chances of

securing the opportunity. To leverage a buyer, the buyer must:



•  Be a supporter.



•  Have medium or high influence.



•  Be prepared to act on your behalf.



•  Recognize  that  you  can  help  him  or  her  achieve  business  and  personal



priorities.



•  Know that the position will not damage his or her internal reputation.



Examples of leverage relationship strategy tactics are:



•



•



You ask the buying center member to act as a reference for your claims of

value to other members in the buying center who may be skeptical.



You  need  the  assistance  of  the  buying  center  member  to  neutralize

another  buying  center  member  who  is  a  non-supporter.  This  is  typically

done by defending your solution when the buying center convenes to make

the final purchase decision.



Motivate



A motivate relationship strategy is used to try to convince buying center members

who are neutral or non-supporters to become supporters. The strategy and tactics

used  to  motivate  the  buying  center  member  depend  on  factors  such  as  buyer-

readiness  stage,  alignment  to  your  company,  alignment  to  the  competitor,  role,

influence, and reaction to your sales plan tactics.  Motivation relationship actions

use  several  tactics  for  proving  value,  minimizing  weaknesses,  and  emphasizing

strengths.



It is important to consider the role of the buying center member you are trying to

motivate. If the buying center member is a decision-maker or approver, you must

be  sure  you  are  aligned  with  the  advisor  positions  (user,  evaluator,  and  gate-

keeper).  In  most  cases,  higher-level  roles  will  validate  any  supplier  claims  to

provide unique business benefits with their internal advisors. It is best to contact



All Strategy Depends on the Competition

u Best Practice—

Pre-Review Meeting

Preparation



If possible, the sales team

leader should send out the

SSP to the review team in

advance of the level 2 review.

This gives the review team

time to read the plan and

send clarification questions to

the sales team leader. The

sales team leader then

addresses these questions in

the presentation phase of the

review.



u Best Practice—

Presentation Phase



Before the presenting team

gives the presentation, the

reviewing team should review

the level 2 strategic sales plan

review form. On page 3 of the

form is a brief overview of the

level 2 review process. Before

beginning the pre sentation,

the moderator should briefly

review the process and ask if

there are any questions before

moving to the next section. If

there are no questions, both

teams should take a few min-

utes to review the discussion

skills that will be used  during

the review process, which are

found on page 2.



During the presentation, the

review team re cords all clarifi-

cation questions on page 2 of

the review form. A completed

level 2 review form is the

result of this section.



advisor roles first to motivate them to become supporters before trying to motivate

higher-level buying center role positions.



Neutralize



When  motivational  relationship  strategies  fail  to  convert  non-supporter  buying

center  members  or  you  have  enemies  in  the  buying  center,  you  must  defend

against their negative influence in the buying center. To neutralize a buying cen-

ter member, you must enlist someone inside the buying center to act on behalf of

your  company.  Neutralizing  relationship  strategies  should  be  used  only  after

efforts  to  motivate  the  buying  center  have  failed  and  your  supporter  or  mentor

considers  the  request  reasonable.  For  example,  sales  representatives  would  not

ask a supporter who is a user and a junior in the company to discuss the project

with an approver who is several levels above. Neutralizing actions include:



•  Supporters or mentors intervening with decision-makers and approvers



•  Supporters or mentors arranging meetings with other buying center mem-



bers who were blocked by non-supporters or enemies



•  Getting feedback or information withheld by non-supporters or enemies



•  Giving  information  to  higher-level  buying  center  members  that  was  not



passed on by non-supporters or enemies



•  Correcting wrong information given out by non-supporters and enemies.



When deciding the relationship strategy, it must have the corresponding tactics

that will be used to execute the relationship strategy. Relationship strategies and

all tactics must be evaluated and prioritized according to the value of improving

your  chances  of  winning  the  opportunity  compared  to  the  amount  of  time  and

resources required to execute the tactic.



Relationship Strategy Table



This table lists near-term relationship strategies. Each entry has the following:



•  Buying center member



•  Relationship strategy



•  Description or the tactics used to execute the strategy



•  Person responsible to complete the tactic



•  Target date for completion



Other Actions Table



In addition to the relationship strategies above, what other actions are needed for

you  to  win  this  opportunity?  Consider  actions  with  company  staff  beyond  the

immediate  sales  team.  Also  consider  any  executive  actions  that  must  be  made,

such as prioritization of limited resources or funding of start-up costs.



The appendix in this chapter includes a table of three pages of possible actions

that can be taken in every sales activity and stage of the opportunity management

process.



The strategic sales plan is an excellent process to follow for your B2B large sales

and the perfect tool to use to review your sales strategy. In the next section, the

Level 2 Strategic Sales Plan Review Process will be described.

CHAPTER 9



Strategic Sales Plan



u Best Practice—

Challenge Phase



After the questions have been

clarified, the review and sales

teams should review the chal-

lenges page of the review

form found on the cover page.

Once they have reviewed the

more frequent challenges,

they should reflect on the pre-

sentation from the sales team

and record specific challenges

on the challenge–test–improve

page, which is on page 8 of

the review form. The review

team should classify each

challenge from a customer,

management, or strategic plan

perspective in the appropriate

column on the challenge page

of the review form.



When the teams reconvene

and the review team members

present their challenges,

someone from the sales team

records the challenges and

clarifies and confirms, as need-

ed, to ensure that they com-

pletely understand the chal-

lenge.



When presenting challenges,

use the skill of triangulating

the concern. All challenges are

directed at the plan, not the

sales team, as you want

to improve the plan.



If you are not clear on this

point, note how the challeng-

es are presented for the cus-

tomer, your company’s man-

agement, and strategic sales

perspectives on the review

form. All challenges are direct-

ed at the plan. Although this

point may seem obvious,

experience has proven it is a

point often forgotten. The chal-

lenge session can become

more combative than con-

structive if the  triangulating

skill is not used.



Level 2 Strategic Sales Plan Review Process

The formal structure of the level 2 sales plan is designed to:



•  Leverage the account knowledge



•  Determine where the plan is most vulnerable



•  Reduce risk associated with the opportunity



•  Modify the plan to improve the probability of success



•  Ensure the tactics are agreed to and achievable by the responsible tactic



owner



A level 2 review results in a more achievable strategic sales plan that has been

presented, clarified, and critiqued from a strategic and tactical point of view. The

first level 2 review should take place before the competitive sales strategy is set

for the sales plan and after the initial coverage of the buying center and require-

ment analysis is completed (Fig. 9.16). Once the preliminary contact is finished,

the  first  pass  of  the  strategic  sales  plan  and  level  1  assessment  is  completed.

Then,  the  strategic  sales  plan  is  distributed  to  the  opportunity  stakeholders  in

preparation for the level 2 review. The second level 2 review is held prior to the

submission of the final proposal. In preparation for this level 2 review, the sales

team  should  complete  a  level  1  review  and  SWOT  analysis  up  to  this  point  to

ensure that there have been no significant changes in any area customer, com-

petitor, or internally that could impact your sales strategy. The last level 2 review

is completed as part of the negotiation strategy planning process.



Conducting a level 2 review requires a moderator (sales or marketing manager),

the sales team, and a review team. The level 2 review is conducted in four phases,

as described below. To facilitate the level 2 review process, use the strategic sales

plan level 2 review form included in the Appendix. In this section, there are “Best

Practice” sidebars for each one of the level 2 review steps collected from experts

who participated in hundreds of level 2 reviews. The first one is for pre-review

meeting preparation.



Presentation Phase



The sales team leader presents the highlights of the strategic sales plan. This can

be  done  via  a  PowerPoint  presentation  for  a  large  group  or  the  Strategic  Sales

Plan  report  for  a  small  group.  For  more  ideas  for  reviewing  the  strategic  sales

plan,  see  Chapter  Highlight  9.6:  “What  Size  Are  You?”  The  purpose  of  the

presentation is to give everyone a chance to listen to and think about the plan

strategically. Twenty-five minutes are allocated for the presentation, during which

time  the  presenting  team  presents  the  opportunity.  This  is  followed  by  a  ten

minute  clarification  question  period.  The  moderator  controls  the  timing  and

restricts all questions to clarification only.



Challenge Phase



After the clarification questions have been answered, the next phase of the level

2 review is the challenge phase, which consists of two parts. In the first part, the

sales team leaves the meeting room and prepares for challenges expected from

the review team. The review team prepares challenges for the sales team. This

section  lasts  fifteen  minutes.  Challenges  should  be  considered  from  three  per-

spectives, as described below.



Challenges from the customer’s perspective may include the following:



All Strategy Depends on the Competition



351





Chapter Highlight 9.6: What Size Are You?



One Size Does Not Fit All

The strategic sales plan does not come in one size fits all. There is actually a small, medium, and large size depend-

ing on your purpose and audience for the level 2 review.



•  Large: A fully documented analysis of your client interview sheets entered into a Word document that allows



you to add additional comments, as needed.



•  Medium: A PowerPoint presentation created from the client interview sheets and answering questions on



PowerPoint slides similar to the information contained in the Word document.



•  Small: A discussion referring to the client interview sheets.



Each of the different formats of the strategic sales plan allow you the flexibility to choose the best



format to have a fit-for-purpose review. The purpose of a level 2 review can be:



As a Coaching Tool. For new salespeople, the sales manager should review the

salesperson’s strategy for  his or her opportunities on

a regular basis and select one key opportunity

from the salesperson’s pipeline each month as

part  of  the  monthly  pipeline  review.  For  this

purpose, reviewing the salesperson’s strategy by

reviewing the client interview sheets, vulnerability

analysis, and level 1 assessment on paper provide for an efficient one-on-one level

2 review.



Sales Team Monthly Meeting. Every sales team should meet a minimum of

once a month to share marketplace developments, competitor intelligence, customer

developments, and insight about sales-related issues the team may like input on from

other colleagues. At these team meetings, it is a good practice to pick the team’s most

important opportunity and have the opportunity owner talk through the review from a

copy of the Strategic Sales Plan report, which can also be shared electronically before

the day of the meeting.



Formal Level 2 Review. When the opportunity is considered strategic and requires  an

executive-level  review,  the  Level  2  Review

PowerPoint template is a good choice to pres-

ent  the  plan  following  the  Level  2  Review

Process. To  add  the  key  tables  and  analysis  as

shown  in  this  chapter,  there  is  an  Excel  tem-

plate  that  can  be  used  to  easily  create  and

update the tables if needed. The structure of the presentation is made



to answer the five strategic questions of the level 2 review:



Is there an Opportunity? Can You Compete? Can You Win? Is it

Worth Winning? and What Next?



There are two key points or cautions to make regarding level 2 reviews.

Many companies will set specific criteria for when they will do a formal level 2 review, including an amount of rev-

enue the opportunity is expected to make, opportunity for a key account, new technology or product introduction,

or other strategic reasons why the opportunity is very visible.



These policies are excellent; however, the sales manager must make it very clear to their sales team that the crite-

ria has been set for when a formal level 2 review needs to be held with upper management of the company for their

approval. This does not mean the other opportunities that are not required to be reviewed with management are

ignored. All the professional sales processes apply conversations with the customer, competitor analysis, and mak-

ing the plan. The caution is salespeople may interpret the criteria level 2 reviews as the criteria for when they apply

the professional sales processes, which is wrong. You apply the professional sales processes on any opportunity you

are going to spend your time on.



The level 2 review is not a bid review; this is another process to be discussed later. The problem is sales teams get

confused and only do the level 2 review when they receive the bid, which is too late. If an opportunity has to have

a formal level 2 review with upper management, then be smart and welcome them to your sales team with their

first responsibility to participate in the level 2 review to get their approval for your sales strategy and assign them

one or two key actions to support your winning the opportunity.



352



CHAPTER 9



Strategic Sales Plan



u Best Practice—Test

and Improvement

Phase



After the challenges have been

presented, the review team and

sales team should review pages

5, 6, and 7 of the review form.

These pages include the most

common tactics for the different

stages of the opportunity man-

agement process. Using these

pages, they can compare which

tactics the sales team has

already included as part of their

plan. On the same page where

the reviewing team recorded

their challenges, they can also

add the suggested improve-

ment tactic and classify it

according to whether it is an

addition to the plan, or modifies

an existing tactic.



While preparing their own

improvement actions, the

sales team can also classify

the presented challenges to

the other category if they are

not going to modify the plan.



•  Has  the  plan  convinced  you  that  the  solution  is  appropriate  for  the  cus-



tomer’s technical requirements?



•  Has the plan convinced you that the proposed solution makes good business sense?



•  Does the solution align with the requirements and preferences of the users?



•  Has the plan addressed the buying center’s adaptability to change profile



so the buying center is confident with the solution?



•  Is the solution proven?



•  Has the plan created confidence in the solution being proposed?



•  Is the unique business value validated by key buying center members?



•  Have  the  required  buying  center  members  in  positions  of  authority  and



responsibility for project outcomes accepted the value proposition?



•  Is the customer’s effort justified to prove and implement the solution?



•  Can the buying center members reasonably devote the time required to the solution?



•  Have  the  consequences  of  a  failed  solution’s  impact  on  the  project  been



addressed by the plan?



•  Is there a better solution available?



•  Has the plan convinced the buying center members they can be sure they



are getting the best deal?



•  Is the time line acceptable?



•  Does the plan demonstrate that the solution will be ready in time and that



you can meet the deadline?



Challenges from your management perspective may include the following:



•  Has  the  plan  tested  short-term  and  long-term  revenue  projections  with



sensitivity analysis?



•  Does  the  plan  identify  all  risks,  and  have  the  risks  been  adequately



addressed?



•  Have the critical success factors been identified?



•  Does the level of profitability justify the amount of resources required for



the project?



Level 2

Strategic Sales Plan

Peer Review



Fig.

9.16

A com-

pleted buying

center analysis

with explanation of

entries and level of

influence.



•  Does the plan have input from the service delivery team?



•  Was the opportunity discovered early enough?



•    Can  the  plan  benefit  from  or  expand  the  scope  to  include



other segments, business lines or products and services?



•    Does  the  plan  under-  or  overestimate  the  competition’s

desire to win the project?



•  Does the plan include all probable competitors in the com-



petitive analysis?



•    Does  the  plan  adequately  consider  recent  similar  bids  and  current



market conditions?



•  If the plan identifies the opportunity as strategic, is this reflected in the tac-



tics by leveraging your executives’ high-level contacts?



•  Does  the  plan  represent  a  major  shift  in  the  normal  way  of  doing  business



with the account currently?



•  Can all plan tactics be realistically implemented?



All Strategy Depends on the Competition



353







u Best Practice—Final

Assessment Phase



At the end of the review pro-

cess, the individual reviewing

team members remove the

back page and give it to the

sales team for their records.

On this page are the challeng-

es and improvement tactics

as recommended by the indi-

vidual review team members

and the final assessment.

When completing the final

assessment, the review team

members should make a final

assessment of each of the

four main questions and then

give the opportunity sales plan

an overall percentage chance

of winning the opportunity.



Challenges from the strategic plan perspective may include the following:



•  Does the plan describe the opportunity profile from the customer’s perspective?



•  Is the compelling event adequately described in the plan?



•  Is  the  buying  center  description  complete?  Are  all  key  roles  identified  in



the plan?



•  Does the plan reference an adequate SWOT and CRM database search for



similar opportunities (scope, account, and competitor)?



•  Do the plan tactics improve your chances of winning?



•  Do  the  tactics  extend  into  the  late  procurement,  implementation,  and



review stages?



•  Are the number of competitor’s supporters accurate in the plan?



•  Are the organizational charts in the plan complete? Are all roles present?



•  Is there a tactic in the plan to address the unknowns in the assessment?



•  Does the plan have a tactic for dealing with the negative assessment items?



•  Does the plan adequately demonstrate that planned relationship strategies



are achievable?



•  Does the plan consider the risk to the individuals who are asked to act on



behalf of your company?



•  Have  the  plan’s  supporters  and  enemy  alignments  been  validated  with



actions by that individual?



•  Does  the  plan  substantiate  the  alignment  against  the  level  of  contacting



that has been done for supporters, mentors, and enemies?



•  Does the plan demonstrate you have a good understanding of the competition?



•  Have the plan tactic due dates been checked against the calendar?



In the second part of the challenge session, the sales team is brought back into

the meeting room, and the review team presents their challenges. They first pres-

ent the customer challenges, followed by your management challenges, and finally

the  strategic  sales  plan  challenges.  The  sales  team  clarifies  and  confirms,  as

necessary. This part of level 2 takes fifteen minutes.



Improvement Phase



The improvement phase consists of two sections. In the first section, the reviewing

team and sales team work separately to test the challenges presented and then,

for the more substantial challenges, recommend improvement tactics to address

the challenge. Improvement actions should be restricted to:



•  Adding a  tactic to address the concern;



•  Modifying a tactic to address the concern;



•  Acknowledging the concern and explaining how an existing tactic already



addresses the challenge; and



•  Acknowledging the concern and confirming why it is important to the per-

son  suggesting  it,  including  reasons  for  not  taking  additional  action  to

address  the  challenge.  Your  decision  not  to  address  the  challenge  can  be

for  reasons  such  as  time  constraints,  priorities,  or  a  belief  that  the  chal-

lenge is not a significant vulnerability.



In  the  second  section  of  the  improvement  phase,  the  sales  team  pre sents  their

improvement plan. This section is allotted ten minutes for the sales team to pres-



354



CHAPTER 9



Strategic Sales Plan



ent and five minutes for the reviewing team to add any constructive criticism in

response to the sales team’s improvement actions.



Decision Phase



In the final stage of the level 2 review process, the reviewing team members—

after  listening  to  the  sales  team—present  their  improvement  actions  and  com-

plete  an  opportunity  assessment  according  to  the  level  1  strategic  sales  plan

review discussed in Chapter 6 “Sales Activities” and included in the Appendix of

this chapter. In addition, an the bottom of the assessments page (page 8 of the

review form) the review team records what they believe is the sales team’s prob-

ability of winning the opportunity.



All Strategy Depends on the Competition



355





Summary of Chapter Objectives



1.  Build a strategic sales plan to organize, manage, and monitor the actions to secure an opportunity.



The  strategic  sales  plan  is  used  to  guide  the  pursuit  of  an  opportunity.  The  successful  plan  selects  strategic  actions

resulting from studying the customer’s project objectives, requirements, individual buying center members’ needs, and

competitive  environment.  The  successful  sales  plan  results  in  your  company  winning  the  project  and  exceeding  the

customer’s expectation during the implementation stage. To develop the plan, follow the outline presented in this chap-

ter  and  leverage  the  professional  sales  consultative  selling  approach,  processes,  and  tools  described  throughout  the

textbook. The strategic sales plan is used to communicate the plan to the key opportunity stakeholders in your organiza-

tion.



2.  Communicate the plan to the stakeholders in the customer and your company organizations.



The strategic sales plan has a two-level review process similar to the review process used in the need-satisfaction selling

process. The level 1 review is completed by the sales representative and sales team members several times throughout

the opportunity life cycle. This review identifies information gaps early in the opportunity management process and later

identifies where you are vulnerable to competitive threats. The level 1 review is meant to answer four important ques-

tions for any opportunity: Is there an opportunity? Can you compete? Can you win? and Is the opportunity worth winning?

If  any  of  these  questions  is  answered  “no,”  then  seriously  consider  a  disengage  strategy  for  the  opportunity.  In  most

opportunities, the assessment clearly shows where you are vulnerable and which actions and tactics are needed to be

executed to address the vulnerability.



In level 2 reviews, the strategic sales plan is reviewed by a review team and the plan is challenged, tested, and improved

in a methodical five-stage process. To facilitate the level 2 strategic sales plan review, a level 2 review form guides the

process. It assists the review team members in effectively applying discussion skills that focus the sales team on poten-

tial vulnerabilities and improvement actions. The level 2 review should be done at least two times. The first takes place

prior to selecting the competitive sales strategy for the late stages of the opportunity, and the second occurs before the

final sales proposal is submitted to the customer. It is suggested that for important opportunities, another level 2 review

be done as part of the negotiation strategy-setting meeting.



3.  Capture the knowledge and promises made during the pursuit phase of the opportunities management process



and deliver on those promises in the implementation phase.



The added benefit of doing the strategic sales plan is that it provides the basis from which to start the outcome analysis

after the purchase decision has been made by the buying center. Actions taken to achieve specific results can be mea-

sured for how effective they were and either used again in future opportunities if successful or modified if not success-

ful. A completed strategic sales plan and supporting documents such as account corporate profiles and client interview

forms are all pieces that, if done correctly and thoroughly, improve the salesperson’s ability to create a win–win proposal

that meets the business objectives of both your company and the customer, and can be used as a powerful training aid

for a new salespeople.



Applying the Concepts



1.  What is the difference between “Can you compete?” and “Can you win?” Give examples of where your company

was able to compete but not win. Give an example of where your company was able to win but not compete.



2.  Of all the assessment tools discussed in the textbook up to this point and used in creating the strategic sales plan,



which are the most important and critical to a successful sales plan?



3.  What guidelines should a sales representative follow when completing a strategic sales plan?



4.  In your opinion, do you think the strategic sales planning process makes you a better salesperson?



5.  Of all the aspects of the strategic sales planning processes, which two provide the most added value and which



two could be dropped?



356



CHAPTER 9



Strategic Sales Plan







6.  Discuss what a program would consist of that could be adopted for your sales team to implement the strategic

sales planning process. What would be the vision of the program, objectives, resources required, potential incen-

tives that could be offered, type of training required, and realistic completion schedule?



7.  Complete a strategic sales plan Word document for an upcoming opportunity or a previous opportunity that was

considered strategic. Analyze it from a strategic sales perspective. Follow this chapter and complete all the neces-

sary documents. Once you complete the strategic sales plan, notify your tutor or ask your sales manager to do a

level 2 review of the sales plan.



8.  Participate in a level 2 strategic sales plan review using the level 2 review forms.

Clarification Questions

C



l



Discussion Skills



Exploring Ideas



When you invite a suggestion . . . give a reaction.



When you make a suggestion . . . invite a reaction.



When you use/build on the ideas of others . . .

acknowledge the connection.



When you need to be innovative or imaginative . . .

temporarily alter restrictions.



Triangulating



Use a neutral tone.



Do not express your opinion.



Refer to the problem as the PLAN.



Constructive Criticism



Introduce the subject.



Give balanced feedback.



Specify what you want to see retained.



Specify what you want changed.



Explore next steps:



Invite ideas; offer responses.



Offer ideas; invite responses.



Summarize next steps you’ve agreed to.



Managing Differences



Defining the Difference



You’re not sure that a difference exists.



You don’t fully understand the difference.



State what’s important to you (what and why).



Clarify what’s important to the other person

(what and why).



Discussing the Difference



You fully understand the difference.



You can honestly consider alternatives.



Invite and respond to the customer’s ideas.



Offer ideas and invite the customer’s responses.



Concluding the Conversation—Agreement



When you have found an agreeable solution or

follow-up action:



Summarize the discussion.



List the action items to be done.



Check for feedback from the customer.



Concluding the Conversation—No Agreement



When there is a difference of opinion and you cannot honestly

consider alternatives or you cannot find  mutual agreement:



Acknowledge the customer’s right to differ.



Explain what you’ve decided and why.

Appendix A



Level 2 Strategic Sales Plan Review Form—Page 3



Level 2 Strategic Sales Plan Review Process



A level 2 sales plan review process is a formal,

structured review process designed to



•



Leverage the account knowledge



•  Determine where the plan is most  vulnerable



•  Reduce risk associated with the opportunity



•  Modify the plan to improve the  probability of



success



•  Ensure the tactics are agreed to and achiev-



able by the tactic owner.



Presentation



Best Practice



and a person to record  challenges and tactics.



•  Each work group appoints a spokes person



•  The moderator enforces the timing and pro-

vides 2-min warnings as the end of each

phase and section nears.



e

c

i

t

c

a

r

P



•  When presenting challenges, the reviewing

team uses the skill of triangulating the con-

cern, as discussed in the Discussion Skills

module. All challenges are directed at the

plan and not at the sales team. It is the plan

you want to improve since you are all on the

same team.  If unclear on this point, note

how the challenges are presented above for

the customer, your company's management

and the strategic sales perspectives. All chal-

lenges are directed at the plan.



t

s

e

B



If possible, the sales team leader sends the

SSP to the reviewing team in advance of the

level 2 reviews. By providing the plan, the

reviewing team has a chance to read the plan

and send clarification questions to the sales

team leader. The sales team leader can then

address these in the presentation phase as

part of the Opportunity presentation.



•



Part 1, 15-min plan presentation

The purpose of the presentation phase is to give

everyone a chance to listen and think about the

plan strategically.



Part 2, 10-min clarification question

period

The moderator controls the timing and types of

questions, restricting all questions to clarification

only.



Challenge



The challenge phase consists of two parts.



Test and Improve



Part 1, 15-min challenge preparation

The sales team leaves the meeting room and pre-

pares for challenges they expect from the review-

ing team. The reviewing team  prepares challenges

for the sales team.



Part 2, 15-min challenge delivery

The sales team returns to the meeting room, and

the reviewing team presents its challenges. The

sales team clarifies and confirms, as necessary.



s

s

e

c

o

r

P



Part 1, 15 min

In this section, the reviewing team and sales team

work separately to prepare improvement tactics in

response to the challenges presented.

Improvement actions should be restricted to



•  Adding a  tactic to address the concern



•  Modifying a tactic to address the concern



•  Acknowledging the concern and explaining



how an existing tactic already addresses the

challenge



•  Acknowledging the concern, confirming why

it is important to the person suggesting it,

and stating any reasons for not adding tactics

(such as time constraints and priorities) to

address the challenge; alternatively, stating

why the challenge is not believed to be a sig-

nificant vulnerability.



Part 2, 10 min

The sales team presents an improvement plan.



Part 3, 5 min

The reviewing team provides feedback and con-

structive criticism in response to the sales team’s

improvement actions.

Appendix B



Strategic Sales Plan–Page 9



Opportunity assessment is conducted by  answering  the  following  questions  and  assessing  a  negative  -,  question  mark  ?,  or,

positive + depending on the answer to the question.



Is There an Opportunity?



Question 1: Customer’s Application of Project



• What is the objective of the customer’s project?



• Do you understand the connection between the company’s vision, goals, and objectives and requirements for the project?



• If you can answer these questions, evaluate this as + to indicate it has been defined by the customer.



• If the customer has not defined the requirements, select – for undefined. If you do not know or understand the require-



ments, select the ? .



Question 2: Customer’s Business Profile



• What is the account condition, as described in the account corporate profile?



• If the account is in a strong position, select the + for strong. If not, select – for weak. If the business profile is unknown,



select the ? .



Question 3: Customer’s Financial Condition



• Customers in a strong financial condition are evaluated as +.



• For lesser known accounts, you should check with your controller for input on the customer’s financial condition and

payment  practices.  Your  financial  department  will  use  credit  ratings  and  other  financial  reports  to  determine  the

account’s financial condition. If the financial condition is weak, select the – option; if it is unknown, select the ? .



Question 4: Access to Funds



• Have funds been allocated to this project, and if they have, what is the budget?



• If not, what is the approval process, and what are the chances of approval for this project?



• Are other competing projects more likely to be selected before this project for the current fiscal year?



• If funds have been approved and you know the budgeted amount, select +. If not, select – , and if you do not know the



answer, select the ? .



Question 5: Compelling Event



• Why does the customer have to do the project and when will the customer do it?



• What are the expected outcomes and paybacks?



• What are the consequences if the project is delayed or accelerated?



• Who has the objective for implementing the project?



• Who is the sponsor of the project?



If you can answer these questions, the compelling event is defined as a +. If not defined, select – , and if you do not know the

answer, select the ? .



Can We Compete?



Question 6: Formal Decision Criteria



• A fully defined formal decision criterion meets the requirements described in the vulnerability analysis described in

Chapter 4. The customer must have a defined set of criteria ranked from critical to incidental and have an evaluation

model by which to evaluate the competing offers. You should know who has created the decision criteria. If approval

comes from departments outside the account (such as partners and government agencies), you must meet with them

and determine whether they have the same decision criteria.



374



CHAPTER 9



Strategic Sales Plan



Appendix B



Strategic Sales Plan–Page 10



• Do you know the process for approval in such situations? If you believe the customer (including external approvers)



has well-defined decision criteria, select +. If not, choose –, and if you do not know, evaluate the question as ?.



Question 7: Solution Fit



• Will you be evaluated as strong or weak against the customer’s decision criteria when compared to competitors?



• Can you do anything to improve your evaluation, such as redefining the decision criteria or addressing the customer’s



negative reactions, such as misunderstandings, skepticism, or drawbacks?



• How do the most influential buying center members rank our solution? If you believe you will be evaluated as strong,

select + . If weak, choose – . If you do not know or have not validated your expected ranking with someone in the buy-

ing center, select ? .



Question 8: Resource Requirements



• For you to secure and implement the project, will it require additional resources and investment?



• Are there other projects that could use the resources if you do not do the project?



• Must you make significant modifications to your solution to qualify?



• Must you invest significant time and effort to sufficiently analyze the risk and build a complete proposal that makes a

strong business case? Input from the business managers should be provided for large projects. If the resources required

are low, select + . If high, select – , and if you do not know, select ? .



Question 9: Current Relationship



• What is the status of your current relationship?



• Do you have an ongoing business relationship, and are you working with the customer on other projects?



• How well do you know the key buying center members?



• When completing this question, compare your relationship relative to the competitors. To answer this question, only

one company should be evaluated positively to reflect their competitive advantage. The company with a + will most

likely have information in advance of their competitors and could also be the company the customer will speak to last

before  making  their  final  decision.  For  the  company  with  the  strongest  relationship,  select  +  .  For  companies  with

weaker relationships, select – , and if you do not know, select ? .



Question 10: Unique Business Value



• What differential value does your solution bring to the customer’s project? To answer this question, you should complete



the customer value analysis and validate your differential value with appropriate buying center members.



• In which of the five common differentiating factors (product or services, service quality, company capabilities, service

and  support  personnel,  and  price)  do  you  provide  differentiated  value?  Will  your  value  be  evaluated  in  the  decision

criteria?



• If you have significant differential value that will give you a competitive advantage, select +. If you do not have differ-



ential value, select – , and if your differential value has not been validated, select ? .



Can We Win?



Question 11: Inside Buying Center Support



• Do you have mentors and supporters inside the buying center?



• Can they influence the purchase decision, and will they act on your behalf?



• Do the competitors have more inside support?



• You should be able to reference specific actions of the mentors or supporters to prove their support. If you

believe you have significant inside support that improves your chances of securing the opportunity over the

competitor, select + for strong,  – for weak, and ? if you do not know.



All Strategy Depends on the Competition



375





Appendix B



Strategic Sales Plan–Page 11



Question 12: Executive Credibility



• Do you know which executives have an interest in the project? Do your executives have on-going business



relationships with them?



• How do they know you if they are not a current account?



• Do the customer’s executives consider you a vendor, problem-solver, or trusted advisor?



• Can your executive make contact with the account executives easily during the resolution of concerns and

monitoring  stages  of  the  procurement  phase?  These  factors  were  explained  in  Chapter  4  “Opportunities

Management” in the procurement phase section. If you have an active account management program, you

should be in a good position relative to our competitors. If you are in a strong position, select + ; if in a

weaker position than the competitors, select – ; and if you do not know, select ? .



Question 13: Cultural Compatibility



•  Does  your  account  relationship  profile  indicate  a  good  match  between  what  the  account  wants  from  its



suppliers and what you provide? (This is discussed in Chapter 13 “Account Management Plan.”)



• Does your company have executive-to-executive level ongoing business relationships?



• Does the account consider you the best company to work on this project?



• If you are not well positioned, what would you have to do to gain a better position? Is there time? If corpo-



rate compatibility is good, select + ; if it is poor, select – ; if you do not know, select ? .



Question 14: Informal Decision Process



• What will be considered beyond the formal decision criteria, and who will contribute to the final purchase

decision? If external departments or government agencies have to give final approval, do you understand

their informal decision process?



•  Do  you  have  previous  experience  knowing  what  it  takes  to  get  approval  beyond  having  a  best-evaluated



solution?



• Have you had a previous experience with the account where an award or loss was turned around in the late



stages?



• What issues can be negotiated? If you are confident you understand the informal decision process and have

a plan to manage it, select + ; if you are not capable or do not have the right contacts and supporters, select

– ; if you do not know, select ? .



Question 15: Political Alignment



•  How  well  aligned  are  you  with  the  high-influence  buying  center  members?  Are  they  supporters  of  your



company?



• Do you know their business and personal agendas? Can they change the decision criteria after the evalua-



tor or decision-maker submits a recommendation?



• Will they act on your behalf?



• Do you have more influential supporters than the competitors?



• Who has the most powerful buying center member as their supporter?



• If external departments or government agencies have to give final approval, are you in alignment with their



powerful approvers?



If  you  believe  you  have  a  stronger  political  alignment  than  your  competitors,  select  +  ;  if  you  are  weaker,



select – ; if you do not know, select ? .



376



CHAPTER 9



Strategic Sales Plan



Appendix B



Strategic Sales Plan–Page 12



Is It Worth Winning?



Question 16: Short-Term Revenue



• Is the short-term revenue for this calendar year high or low?



• Once the contract is awarded, can you sell additional services or expand the work scope?



• Will there be other projects coming in the short term that would enable you to leverage the resources allo-



cated to this project?



• Will your equipment and staff be utilized at full capacity, or can you generate other revenue with the same



resources from other projects unrelated to this opportunity?



• Is there anything you could do to improve short-term revenue? If the resources for this project have a reason-

able probability of generating high short-term revenue, select + ; if low, select – ; and if you do not know,

select ? .



Question 17: Future Revenue



• Ask the same questions as the short-term revenue questions but now from the long-term perspective.



• What kind of year-on-year growth might you expect in future revenue streams?



• If long-term revenue-generating prospects are high, select + ; if low, select – ; and if you do not know, select ?.



Question 18: Profitability



• Is the expected profitability high or low?



• What would have to happen to increase profitability?



• Could you negotiate items that would improve profitability or lower your costs?



•  Does  the  level  of  expected  profitability  warrant  the  resources,  or  are  there  more  profitable  projects  that



could use the resources?



• Do you have contractual protection for the resources requested, but not used? If profitability prospects are



good, select + ; if they are not or are low, select – ; if you do not know, select ? .



Question 19: Degree of Risk



• What are the levels of technical, financial, and other risks associated with the project?



• What are the risks of the customer not fulfilling the work scope, ending the project early, or canceling the



project? How could you cause your solution to fail?



• What are the critical dependencies in delivering value to your customer?



• How could the customer cause your solution to fail?



• What is the impact on your business if the solution fails?



• How much investment do you need to make to start the project?



• How much of this investment is at risk? If the risk profile of the opportunity is low, select + ; if it is high,

select  –  ;  if  you  do  not  know,  select  ?  .  If  the  risk  factor  is  high,  what  could  you  or  the  customer  do  to

reduce it?



All Strategy Depends on the Competition



377





Appendix B



Question 20: Strategic Value



Strategic Sales Plan–Page 13



• To determine whether a project is strategic, you must determine whether the project is more profitable than



other projects of the same time period that use the same resources. If so, why?



• Do long-term account factors such as product development or competitive issues make winning this project

strategic? To answer this question when resources are limited, it is advisable to obtain upper management

input. The strategic value of a project is best answered by upper management (1. If the strategic value is

high, select + ; if it is low, select – ; if you do not know, select ? .



378



CHAPTER 9



Strategic Sales Plan



Appendix B



Notes:



Strategic Sales Plan–Page 14



All Strategy Depends on the Competition



379





Competitive

Bidding



Smart Business People



One of the main differences between small and large sales is the size of the deal,

as discussed in Chapter 7 “Sales Call Skills.” Not surprising, when companies are

making large financial commitments, they want to ensure that they get the best

return  for  their  money  spent.  In  order  for  large  organizations  to  control  their

expenditures and ensure that they are making the best choices, they create pro-

curement procedures and departments. The procurement departments enforce all

departments’ use of a well-defined tendering process for large expenditures. Two

underlying  strategies  of    procurement  are  (1)  the  more  you  buy  the  better  the

price  and  (2)  transfer  as  much  of  the  risk  to  the  supplier  as  possible.  As  such,

most companies now tender or request multiple suppliers to submit competitive

bids for large volumes of work with a well-defined scope of work, including con-

tract terms and conditions.



In this competitive bidding,  the professional salesperson must consider two things:



•  Competitive  bidding  is  a  selections  process  in  which  you  must  beat  the



competition.



•  There must be a plan for achieving the company’s target profitability.

This chapter will describe the specifics of what the bidding team  must do to beat

the competition and make their targeted profitability. It will also describe anti-

trust laws applicable to bidding. Competitive bidding requires a lot of work and

commitment from the bid team, but the prize makes it worth the effort. Success

in this stage of the opportunity management process requires that the salesperson

and their bid team be smart business people.



10



Chapter Objectives



After reading this chapter,

you will be able to:



n  Know how to manage

the tender workflow.



n  Perform a tender assess-



ment.



n  Know the components



of a bid strategy.



n  Understand the key fac-



tors for a comprehensive

tender review.



Competitive Bidding



381





In  today’s  B2B  marketplace,  the  professional  salesperson  must  be  very  good  at

responding to tenders with a competitive bid. Fortunately for you, by applying the

professional sales processes discussed in this textbook, you will be well positioned

for the bidding stage.  It is recommended before reading this chapter to review the

“Phase 3b: Procurement-Submission of Proposal Stage” as it describes the funda-

mentals  of  competitive  bidding  from  which  this  content  will  start  from.  As  dis-

cussed in Chapter 4 “Opportunities Management,” competitive bidding is a team

effort consisting of people from sales, marketing, contracts, legal, and operations.

The bid team will focus on six critical tasks, as follows:



•  Pre-tender documentation and information gathering



•  Management of the tender timeline



•  Tender workflow management



•  Tender document assessment



•  Bid strategy creation and execution



•  Post award contract handoff



Pre-Tender Documentation

and Information Gathering

The documentation and information required to answer the customer’s tender can

be classified in two categories:



•  Tender requirements



•  Bid strategy information



Tender Requirements



Documentation  required  by  the  customer  include  documents  that  must  be

included  in  your  tender  response  in  order  to  be  considered  compliant  by  the

customer. Examples of such documents are business licenses, permits, description

of facilities, information on project personnel such as education and certifications,

and company programs or standards such as health, safety, and environment as

well as training. The best way to know what will be required is to review a previous

tender to identify the required documents and then ask the person responsible

for sending the tender to suppliers if there will be any additional requirements

for this tender.  See the sidebar “Hello, You’re Here Early” for other questions to



u	Hello, You’re Here Early



If you ever had the

occasion of arriving

early to a party or

having your guests

arrive early, the

response “Hello,

you’re here early”



is a little shock and surprise. You may get

the same if you ask your customer about

an upcoming bid. But that is okay; like

arriving early to the party, you will get the

best seat, first drink, and tastiest snacks.



Explain to them you want to ensure that

you have all the needed documentation

and information so nothing is left out.

The key to getting useful information is



to have specific questions. To prepare

your questions, review the most recent

tender and debrief with the operations

team on how the project is going. If this

is the first time working with the

account, then ask the customer for a

previous tender. A short list of questions

to ask before you receive the tender are:



•  What are the required documents?

•  How much time will you have for

submission after receipt of the

tender?



•  How will supplier clarifications be



handled?



•  How will customer clarifications be



handled?



•  Will alternative proposals be



allowed?



•  Will the terms and conditions be



the same?



•  Will you be able to present your



proposal after submission?



By getting these administrative details

taken care of before the tender arrives,

you will have more time to focus on the

other more strategic issues of preparing

a winning bid.



Of course, like the party, if you arrive

early you may be asked to help set the

table. If the customer needs your help,

be happy to oblige.



382



CHAPTER 10  Competitive Bidding



ask before you receive the tender.  A best practice is to create a central database

where this type of commonly required information for bidding can be shared, and

kept up to date.



Bid Strategy Information



Information needed to support the bid strategy decision is critical to making the

best decisions regarding how to respond to the bid. If this type of information is

not  organized  before  receiving  the  tender,  then  chances  are  decisions  will  be

made  without  it.  The  information  needed  to  make  the  best  bid  strategy  can  be

categorized into:



•  Market factors



•  Pipeline factors



•  Opportunity Analyses



This  information  will  be  used  in  a  bid  review  worksheet  discussed  later  in  this

chapter. All this information should have been collected during the evaluation of

options  stage  of  the  opportunities  management  process.  In  general  the  market

factors can be categorized as having a positive, negative or neutral impact on your

chances of winning the tender. These first set of factors will be included with the

tender assessment factors when you are ready to create a bid strategy.  Tables 10.1

and  10.2  lists  each  factor  component,  a  description  of  the  component,  and  the

comments regarding the type of influence the factor can have on your bid strategy.



Managing the Tender Timeline

Proactive planning will help the bid team effectively manager the tender timeline.

There are several key dates the bid team needs to consider, as listed below:



•  Prequalification of bidders



•  Receipt of tender



•  Acknowledgment of tender



•  Bidder clarifications



•  Bid submission



•  Bid bond



•  Customer clarifications



•  Bidders’ presentations



•  Negotiations



•  Award notice



•  Contract signing



•  Bid validity



•  Performance bond



•  Job start



•  Performance review



•  Job end



•  Project review



The bid team must ensure that someone on the bid team is assigned responsibil-

ity for each of the key dates and corresponds with the customer.



Dates  such  as  bid  submission,  bid  validity  date,  and  job  start  can  be  strategic

dates, and requesting changes can become a competitive advantage. For example,

Timing



What effect will this project's timing have on

the bid? Is the start of the project good, poor,

or neutral?
