Opportunity analysis.



over your bid as close as possible up to the deadline for submission. Once you have

submitted your bid it is no longer in your control. There are a lot of tender time-

line dates and with all the other responsibilities the bid team members have it is

critical the tender timeline responsibilities are assigned as early as possible. One

of  the  obstacles  to  managing  the  tender  timeline  is  the  preparation  of  the  bid

document. Successful bid teams and competitive tendering experts follow a very

detailed tender workflow that will be discussed next.



Tender Workflow Management

When  salespeople  who  have  not  been  trained  on  the  sales  processes  are  ques-

tioned  about  how  they  feel  regarding  bidding  most  reply “there  is  not  enough

time—it is utter chaos. I would rather go to the dentist.” The good news is it does

not have to be like that according to David Pugh, author of the “Bidder Dozen,”1

a paper written for the Association of Proposal Management Professionals. The

main issue for most people with the responsibility to reply to a tender is there is

only  one  deadline,  which  is  the  date  and  time  they  must  submit  the  proposal.

According  to  Pugh,  this  is  their  first  mistake.  To  get  the  most  done  within  the

timeframe, before the bid team receives the tender to submit their bid, additional

deadlines must be established providing sufficient time for writing and reviewing

the bid before being submitted. According to Pugh, not enough time is given to

properly write the bid; as a result, bid teams too often are writing, editing, and

re-writing  up  until  the  time  the  bid  is  due.  For  a  real  example  of  how  one  bid

team  suffered  from  poor  tender  workflow  management,  see  Chapter  Highlight

10.1: You’ve Got To Be Joking!



Smart Business People



385







Chapter Highlight 10.1: You’ve Got to Be Joking!



Careful with the Wording

In a B2B tender reply, the bid team was working on finalizing their bid right up to the last minute of the last

day before the bid was due. Early the same morning, just before the bid was to be submitted, a salesperson

learned there was a very good chance of another project starting shortly after the award of this project. A bid

team member came up with a very good idea. He suggested they offer an additional 10% discount if there

was a second project at the same time as this bid. Everyone agreed. As the rest of the bid team finished

packaging the bid, the other team member wrote a cover letter that included what is shown below.



One week later, the bid team was celebrating the

award;  although,  they  were  awarded  one  project

and the other project was awarded to the competi-

tion.



One month later, the salesperson was asking the

customer  why  their  accounts  payable  short  paid

their invoice. His answer was:



Customer:    “You  offered  us  an  additional  10%

discount on your bid if there was a second project,

and there is, so we added the 10% discount and

paid the invoice.”



The salesperson was shocked and replied: “But

we  are  not  doing  that  project. You  gave  it  to  the

competition.”



Customer:   “Your offer did not stipulate we had to

give you the second project.”



Salesperson: “You’ve got to be joking.”



Customer: “No.”



Salesperson:    ”Our  invoice  was  $90,000,  and  you  paid  us



$80,000. How did they arrive at that amount?”



Customer:  “20% discount off $100,000 is $80,000.”



Salesperson, now looking sick:  “Our offer was an additional 10% dis-



count. The original was 10, adding 10% to this is 11%, not 20%.”



Customer: “Look, here is what I will do. But first let me tell you very honestly when we evaluated your offer

we knew there was going to be a second project. You were not the low bidder with your initial offer; however,

with your additional 10% discount, this made you the same as your competition, which is why we decided to

split the work. But if you want us to evaluate your offer at 11%, we can do that, but you know the result may

be different.”



Salesperson: “Don’t do anything now. Let me talk with my management.”



Customer:  “Okay. Let me know.”



Most salespeople would probably say the customer is taking advantage of a poorly written offer. The issue

is the bid team should never have  put themselves in such an exposed position. It is accurate to say that, for

this bid team, the only deadline was the bid submission. Had the cover letter been reviewed, the confusion

of the offer would have been discovered. In the end, the supplier decided to honor the contract and not risk

losing the work.



386



CHAPTER 10  Competitive Bidding



Fig. 10.1

Managing the Chaos! The bidding workflow.

Most bid teams focus on one date:

submission of proposal. This is a very impor-

tant date, but when bid teams only focus on

that date, a lot of stress and chaos can be

created. The way to improve the workflow is

to create two additional deadlines. The first

deadline is bid strategy review, held after the

bid team has used the first 25% of the work-

flow time to create the bid strategy. The bid

strategy is reviewed and the offer is locked,

leaving ideally 50% of the time for writing

the proposal. After the proposal is written,

the proposal is passed to the bid review

team for review and their recommendations

are passed to the bid team so they can

finalize the bid and submit the

proposal to the customer.



To fix this problem, Pugh recommends that the tender workflow be managed by

dividing the time the bid team has from receipt of the tender to the time of sub-

mitting the proposal to the customer into three stages. Each stage is allotted a

portion of the time in which specific tasks must be completed before deadlines in

the workflow leading up to submission of the proposal. The workflow is shown in

Fig. 10.1 and has two additional deadlines.



The first deadline is the bid strategy review. During this review the core bid team

will  review  their  bid  strategy,  which  will  be  discussed  later  in  this  chapter.  The

steps to be followed at the time of receiving a bid and orientation were explained

in Chapter 4 “Opportunities Management.” The tender document assessment creat-

ing the offer and proposal strategy will be discussed in detail later in this chapter.



The objective of the bid strategy meeting is to review the offer and proposal strat-

egy and agree to any follow-up actions, for the bid team can lock the offer, leaving

50%  of  the  remaining  time  to  write  the  proposal.  The  proposal  writing  must  be

completed by the second deadline, proposal review, so the review team has 25%

of the remaining time to review and make recommendations to the bid team for

revising and printing the proposal in time for the proposal to be submitted accord-

ing to the customer’s instructions.



The highest level manager who approves the bid strategy is the person who sets

the three deadlines not the bid team. The first two deadlines have to be viewed

as important as the last deadline, submission of proposal. The date for completion

of the final version of the proposal is when the approving manager receives a copy

for his or her signature before submission.



Tender Document Assessment

As shown in Fig. 10.1, the tender assessment is done as soon as possible once the

tender is received from the customer after the initial tender orientation process

is completed. It is the job of the core bid team to assess the tender document in

preparation for preparing a bid strategy to be presented to a reviewing team for

approval and locking the offer.



Smart business people know the devil is in the details. Preparing a proposal for a

large scope of work, for a long period of commitment, with many variables that

impact  your  profitability  demands  that  you  pay  attention  to  the  details.  Tender

assessment  is  detailed  work.  To  do  it  well,  use  a  four-factor  tender  assessment

model, as shown in Fig. 10.2.



Smart Business People



387





Fig. 10.2

Four-Factor Tender

Assessment Model.



During the tender assessment, answer questions about each factor to create a bid

that:



•  Is compliant



•  Provides you with the best chances to win the tender,



•  Achieves your targeted profit levels, and



•  Minimizes your risks.



Each  of  the  factors  shown  in  Fig.  10.2  will  now  be  discussed,  highlighting  key

issues the bid team needs to be aware of and how to use the analysis to create a

winning bid strategy.



Type of Tender



The  type  of  tender  will  influence  your  options  for  handling  tender  assessment

issues.  Deciding  on  the  type  of  tender  is  therefore  the  first  step  in  the  tender

assessment. The customer’s decision for which type of tender to use is influenced

by  what  type  of  relationship  the  customer  believes  is  needed  for  their  project.

Each of the types of relationships are explained in the Chapter Highlight 10.2:

You Are So Predictable.



For each type of tender, several factors will be discussed:



•  Supplier selection



•  Tender requirements



•  Customer buying behavior



•  Competitive selling strategy



•  Selling approach



For the purpose of this discussion, several key figures from earlier chapters are

repeated to make it easy for you to review.



Type 1 Tender



Type  1  Tender:  The  customer  is  most  concerned  about  selecting  the  best  partner.

Most often these types of tenders are evaluated using the most economically advan-

tageous model, as described earlier in Chapter 4 “Opportunities Management.” The

key difference between this evaluation model and other models is the winning sup-

plier can be more expensive than competitor alternatives as long as the difference

is economically justified. In Chapter 8 “Handling Customer Objections” Highlight 8.2:

“We Would Like to Use You But,” there were three examples of the winning supplier



388



CHAPTER 10  Competitive Bidding



Chapter Highlight 10.2: You Are So Predictable



In the book Rethinking the Sales Force,2 the authors suggest sell-

ers  today  need  multiple  sales  approaches  and  selling  models  to

meet the demands of today’s sophisticated customers. The key to

applying  multiple  sales  approaches  starts  with  recognizing  that

customers  have  different  needs  for  different  projects. This  is  a

message  that  has  been  emphasized  throughout  the  consultative

selling approach described in this textbook. There is no time when understanding the customers is more impor-

tant  than  when  preparing  a  bid  strategy. The  customer’s  needs  will  drive  what  type  of  relationship  the  cus-

tomer will have between them and their suppliers, and the seller should match their selling approach to be

aligned with the customer. Listed below are the three types of customer–seller relationships.



Enterprise Relationship: The project is strategic to the account. The planning horizon occurs well in advance of

the project, and the customer looks at not only product and service features but also the supplier’s corporate

capabilities and commitment to their project. For an opportunity to be considered an enterprise, the customer’s

executives should be in discussions with your executives about the project.



Consultative Relationship: The level of importance of the project and concern of the account for meeting their

objectives is high and the account searches for the best possible solution. This relationship is the most common

type in B2B large sales opportunities. For sellers with a wide portfolio, this type of relationship leverages their

differentiated products and services as well as specialized sales and support staff.



Transactional Relationship: For these projects, the main driver is to control costs and source the most cost-effec-

tive  and  efficient   suppliers.  Standardization  and  efficient  purchasing  strategies  are  other  areas  of  focus  for  the

account.



In  the  figure  to  the  right,  the  account’s  willingness  to

invest in the relationship with their suppliers is plotted

on the x-axis, and the y-axis is the supplier’s willingness

to  invest  in  the  relationship  with  their  accounts. The

figure in the center of the chart matches the best selling

approach based on the levels of willingness to invest by

the account and supplier as well as type of tender that

should  be  used  for  each  scenario.  The   bidirectional

arrow highlights the seller’s needs to adapt their selling

approach based on the customer’s level of willingness

to invest in their supplier relationship for each project.



When  there  is  a  mismatch  between  the  account’s

desired relationship and the seller’s selling approach,

the seller is vulnerable, as shown by the black areas

in the corners of the figure. The level of relationship is

project dependent. For one project, the customer may

want a more transactional approach, and others more consultative or enterprise. For example, if the account has

an  ongoing  field  development  that  is  considered  a  simple  transactional  project,  but  you  take  a  consultative

approach and recommend using more expensive, advanced products and services, you might be at risk of losing

the  project to a lower-cost transactional supplier because you are over-resourcing the project.



Alternatively, if the account is starting a major new offshore development, is looking for innovative ways to run

the project, and wants an enterprise relationship with their suppliers, but you do not take an enterprise selling

approach, you might be exposed to a competitor who recommends forming a strategic alliance.



For professional salespeople, and as discussed in this textbook, the consultative selling approach is always rec-

ommended in a B2B environment up to the time you select your competitive sales strategy. The objective is to

determine what is the best selling approach and competitive sales strategy to best position you when you receive

the customer’s tender. If you do not have the correct type of selling, you are in the black areas of the above plot

and will either lose the tender or win and not make your financial objectives. For establishing a competitive bid-

ding strategy, this is critical. The good news is, if you are managing your opportunity and engaged with the cus-

tomer in the early stages, then the correct approach is predictable.

being 150% to 200% more expensive than the competitor. These are examples of Type

1 Tenders using an economically justified evaluation model.



For a Type 1 Tender, the customer identifies qualified suppliers they prefer to work

with. They describe what is required for their project to be successful. The supplier

then has time to develop, present, and discuss their proposal. The customer does

this with each of their preferred suppliers and awards the supplier they have most

confidence in working with to make the project successful.



For this type of tender, the customer should exhibit a relationship buyer behavior

relationship, as shown in Fig. 10.3 (this was discussed in Chapter 1 “Understanding

the Role of Marketing and Sales.”) Your competitive sales strategy should be frontal,

as shown in Fig. 10.4 and discussed in Chapter 4 “Opportunities Management.”



In a Type 1 Tender, the customer will want to discuss their requirements early in

the opportunities management process, and you should take an enterprise selling

approach as discussed earlier in Chapter Highlight 10.2: “You Are So Predictable.”

The main requirement for an enterprise relationship is the supplier must be at

the  “trusted  adviser”  level,  as  shown  in  Fig.  10.5.  This  requires  your  company

executive  to  have  access  to  the  account’s  executive  throughout  the  account’s

project management process, as shown in Fig. 10.5 and discussed in Chapter 9

“Strategic Sales Plan.”



What can happen is you might become engaged early with the customer and the

customer might encourage you to bring your ideas and suggestions before receiv-

ing  the  tender.  You  might  interpret  this  behavior  as  a  relationship  buyer  and

determine that an enterprise approach is appropriate. The end result would be a

direct award or the tender being evaluated using the most advantageous model.

But occasionally what happens to the disappointment of the salesperson is that

the tender is sent out with all the suggestions made by the salesperson included,

and the evaluation model is priced low. What this means is the salesperson has

done a lot of free consulting for the customer. In a situation like this, when the

salesperson  believes  it  is  Type  1  Tender,  he  or  she  should  ask  the  executive  to

contact the customer’s executive to confirm that this is a Type 1 Tender. Of course

if your executive is contacting the customer’s executive as recommended in Chap-

ter  9  “Strategic  Sales  Plan,”  then  the  seller’s  executive  should  have  confirmed

what type of tender to expect early in the opportunity management process.



Depending  on  the  competitive  situation  and  which  competitors  are  invited  to

submit a proposal, the above situation could be a serious misinterpretation. If you

plot this scenario on the figure in Chapter Highlight 10.2: You Are So Predictable,

it would show your proposal to be over-resourcing your solution, and you would

be  vulnerable  and  most  likely  would  lose  because  you  treated  the  project  as  a

enterprise when the customer was looking for a transactional relationship.



Type 2 Tender



For a Type 2 Tender, the customer creates specifications, evaluates bidders techni-

cally and assigns a technical score, evaluates the financial package, and assigns a

financial score. The best total score earned for the technical and financial pack-

ages is awarded the work. These types of tenders are evaluated using one of sev-

eral models, such as multiple criterion points or inverse proportional as used by

the World Bank. For this type of tender, the customer’s buying center can choose

the best offer provided the price is within a premium of the lowest priced accept-

able competitor. The lowest priced acceptable offer is commonly referred to as L1.

For example, the buying center can choose a more expensive preferred supplier

provided their price is no more than 10% more expensive than the L1 offer.



Smart Business People

For this type of tender the customer should exhibit a value buyer behavior and

your competitive sales strategy can be a frontal or flanking approach depending

on  the  formal  decision  criteria  and  your  ability  to  change  the  scope  of  work  or

submit an alternative proposal. These considerations were discussed in detail in

Chapter 4 “Opportunities Management.” In this type of tender, the most important

consideration  is  knowing  the  formal  decision  criteria  and  having  a  completed

vulnerability analysis, as shown in Fig. 10.6 and discussed in Chapter 4 “Oppor-

tunities Management.”



You do not have to be evaluated the lowest price for a Type 2 Tender. However,

not submitting the lowest price and still winning means you must know the for-

mal decision criteria and how you will be evaluated. You must know what pre-

mium  the  customer  will  pay  and  design  your  solution  such  that  your  price  is

within that pricing premium above L1. For a Type 2 Tender, the salesperson must

balance the solution, the premium over L1, and the cost of their solution so that

they win the tender and make their company’s target margins.



The best selling approach for a Type 2 Tender, is consultative: engage early with

the  customer  in  the  evaluation  of  options  stage,  and  determine  their  project

requirements, budget, and decision criteria to choose their suppliers. For a review

of what you should do and ask during the early evaluation of options stage, see

Chapter 4 “Opportunities Management.”



Type 3 Tender



For a Type 3 Tender, the customer creates specifications, selects qualified bidders,

and sends the request for proposal to qualified bidders. The work is awarded to the

company evaluated L1. These types of tenders are most commonly evaluated using

a two-envelope system as described in Chapter 4 “Opportunities Management.”



Your competitive sales strategy should be frontal solution price. The most impor-

tant decision factor for the customer is price.



For Type 3 Tender you must have the lowest price for the tendered scope of work.

Therefore, the best sales approach is transactional. Your efforts are best focused on

how to submit the lowest cost proposal (L1) and still make your targeted margins.



Table 10.3 is a summary of the critical factors for each type of tender. Regards-

ingto the figure in Chapter Highlight: 10.2: “You Are So Predictable,” it is clear if

you identify the type of tender as Type 3 Transactional but it is a Type 2 Consulta-

tive or Type 1 Enterprise, you are vulnerable to under-resourcing your proposal.



392



CHAPTER 10  Competitive Bidding



Table 10.3

Type of tender and critical factors.



As discussed in this section, interpreting the type of tender incorrectly will most

certainly make you lose the tender. The best way to avoid this mistake is through

early discussions during the opportunities management process with the key buy-

ing center members.



Another issue to be aware of is when the customer uses the tendering process as

a  way  to  ensure  that  they  are  getting  the  supplier’s  best  prices  by  making  the

tender appear as a Type 3 when in fact it is Type 2 or Type 1. In this situation the

customer  is  intending  to  purchase  more  expensive  or  value-added  products  or

services but is attempting to use the tendering process to make price appear as

the most important factor. When this is the situation the bid team must have a

clear strategy of how to submit an L1 price but still achieve their targeted profit-

ability when the customer requests the more costly higher level and value-added

products and services. The key for this situation is knowing what the customer is

going to do once the tender has been awarded and what is important to the cus-

tomer. As mentioned earlier, the salesperson must realize the tendering process

is a selection process; it does not commit the customer to what they will purchase

during the implementation stage of the project.



A common question from salespeople is “If you have a tender and you’re not sure

what type of tender it is, what should you do?” In this situation you determine if

the customer will accept an alternative proposal; if yes, then make your primary

proposal  compliant  as  per  the  customer’s  tender  requirements  and  submit  an

alternative proposal that you believe is what they really want.



For example you’re not sure if the tender is a Type 3 or Type 2. In this situation

submit a Type 3 low price bid and submit an alternative with your more expensive

valued-added solution you believe the customer will buy.  In situations where the

customer will not accept an alternative, you will need to submit a low price offer

but at the same time have the ability to sell your value-added product and services

after winning the work. How to do this is the topic of the next section and “Creat-

ing a Bid Strategy” section later in this chapter. Checking what the customer has

done in previous similar tenders is a good indication of what the customer may

do on this project.



The next sections will cover the analysis of the tender requirements. Terms that

will be used frequently in this section are:



•  Total  Tender  Value  (TTV)—the  total  price  the  customer  will  evaluate  for



your offer.



•  Expected Value (EV)—the total amount the supplier expects to sell.
