u Worth Their Weight

in Gold



As you

review

a tender

docu-

ment,

the notes



you make can be worth their

weight in gold later.



In many cases the person or bid

team who prepares the bid will

not be one of the people invoic-

ing. At the time of invoicing, if

the customer questions any part

of the invoice, there needs to

be a logical explanation of why

the item being questioned was

invoiced in the way it was. Here

are a few tips to help you keep

your gold.



Once you receive the tender,

if it is not in electronic format,

have the document scanned

into a PDF.



If you have a shared repository,

have each of the people review

and add their notes to the same

document using the “Comments”

function.



For each note, highlight where

the note applies in the tender:



•  Describe the rationale for the



note.



•  Provide an example of how

the issue being highlighted

has been handled before.



If the customer questions how

an item is invoiced, then your

company representative will be

able to explain the rationale and

give an example of how a simi-

lar issue was handled in the

past. This should convince the

customer to accept the invoice.



Later in this chapter, the con-

tract handover process will be

discussed and one of the key

items in the handover are the

bid notes.



•  Market Rate—the TTV of lowest priced competitor that you must beat to



win the tender.



•  Price  book—the  full  price  of  a  product  or  service  with  0  discount.  Other

terms that are used interchangeably are reference price catalog and book

price.



In general for:



•  Type  1  tenders:  your  TTV  and  EV  should  be  the  same  as  for  this  type  of



tender you are recommending to the customer what they should do.



•  Type 2 tenders: you want to keep the TTV within the allowed pricing pre-



mium over the market rate and maximize your EV.



•  Type 3 tenders: you want the TTV to be slightly lower than the market rate



and maximize your EV.



You must do this like a smart business person and not have the customer believe

that you tricked them. If the customer thinks you tricked them, you will find it

very hard to sell again to that customer in the future or anyone they choose to tell

as they will not trust you. The best way to ensure that the customer knows what

your  bid  includes  is  always  state  explicitly  in  your  offer  what  you  are  providing

and everything else will be priced at price book. When the offer is not straight-

forward  it  is  the  responsibility  of  the  bid  team  to  ensure  that  they  have  docu-

mented bid notes. See the sidebar “Worth Their Weight in Gold.”



Getting the Type of Tender correct will determine if you win a tender. The next

three  tender  factors,  if  handled  correctly,  will  determine  if  your  company  will

make your profitability targets.



Work Scope



The work scope describes what the winning bidder will provide. The work scope

includes the following:



•  Volume of work



•  Product or services requested



•  How the work will be awarded



For each workscope factor there will be a short description and recommenda-

tions how to handle it to ensure that you win the tender and make your financial

objectives.



Volume of Work



When assessing the volume of work, you consider a number of factors, including

the following:



•  What is stated in the tender



•  What you believe is the real volume of work



•  What has happened in past projects



•  Other project information



With this information you determine if the volume of work is accurate, underesti-

mated, or overestimated.



Accurate Work Volume



What is requested in the scope of work you believe will be the actual volumes or quanti-

ties purchased. Discounts and pricing formats are created and tested using the stated

volume of work to win the work and achieve the targeted margins for the project.



394



CHAPTER 10  Competitive Bidding



and the customer is correct. This is

one reason you try to collect as

much information regarding the

opportunity before receiving the ten-

der. With the information in this

chapter, you should now be able to

ask better questions of your support-

ers and mentors, like what is the

minimum, most likely, and maximum

volumes they expect. The chance the



u Can’t See the Forest for

the Trees



You would think in the sophisticated

world of B2B marketing, when cus-

tomers prepare tenders worth millions

of dollars or more, the tender would

be perfect in every aspect. But that is

not the case many times. In this chap-

ter, reasons why this occurs for each

of the common issues have been list-

ed. However, there are two other fac-

tors you might want to consider.



Like the idiom “can’t see the forest for

the trees,” sometimes you might be

looking at the tender so closely that

you might miss the obvious.



First, maybe the bid team is wrong,



bid team is wrong is why you always

test your offer against the best case

and worst case scenarios.



The other option might be that the

competitor is coaching their mentors

on how to write the bid so they win.

This is why you need to consider your

competition and the relationships they

have with the customer’s organization;

maybe you cannot win.



If you see things wrong that you

would not expect from the customer,

then stop for a moment. Consider all

the facts and ask yourself is there a

chance you are wrong. Is it possible

you are missing something? Or is

the customer being coached by the

competitor?



Underestimated Work Volume



What is requested in the scope of work, you believe will be less than the actual

volumes or quantities that will be purchased. As mentioned earlier, none of the

issues discussed in the remaining tender assessment section should apply Type 1

Tenders as you are having open discussions with the customer and making recom-

mendations to address any inconsistencies you would discover in order to have the

best solution to help the customer achieve their objectives.



When the customer creates a Type 2 and 3 tender, they do so after they have dis-

cussed it with their suppliers, as is the case for Type 2 tenders. Or they will create

the tender document without any or minimal input from their suppliers for Type 3

tenders. There can be several reasons why the volume of work might be different

from what will actually happen. It could be due to the customer:



•  Being  optimistic  and  believing  they  can  do  with  less  of  the  products  and



services than they will need from your company.



•  Making incorrect calculations for what they need.



•  Having insufficient budget to purchase all of what they need.



•  Being sure how well your product or services will work and wanting to test



them before making the full commitment.



There  are  two  more  reasons  why  the  tender  requirements  could  be  wrong,  and

these apply to all the tender assessment factors discussed in this section. Go to

the “Can’t See the Forest for the Trees” sidebar to find out what these are.



During your customer interviews, before receiving the bid, you should have gained

some insights to help you answer the reason why the work is underestimated. At

this point in time, you now have the tender to bid and will use competitive bidding

strategies to beat the competition. You will leverage any tender discrepancies to

gain a competitive advantage to win the tender and make your financial objectives.



In Type 2 and 3 Tenders, there are three ways to handle the underestimated work

volume scenario:



•  Price book



•  Rebate



•  Package price



Smart Business People



395





u Oh, What is the Price

for That?



It is difficult and sometimes near

impossible to price everything

that will be needed for a project

or purchase to be included in the

request for proposal from the

customer. You should always

include a reference price cata-

logue for any product or services

not requested in the scope of

work. In your bid, there should

be a pricing note that all out-of-

scope products or services will

be charged as per the attached

reference price catalog included

with your bid.



When quoting prices for prod-

ucts and services in the tender

scope of work, you can state

the prices in your bid document

or state a discount that applies

to the reference price catalogue.



Table 10.4

Underestimated scope of work.

Price book option.



Price Book



The discount level for volume of work stated in the tender is set to win the tender,

and a lower discount is applied for the expected additional work typically charged

as per the price book without a discount. See the sidebar “Oh, What is the Price

for That?” This approach to pricing is effective when a specific item is underesti-

mated and makes up a small portion of the total invoice in the TTV, but in the real

scenario you believe there will be more of the product purchased. In Table 10.4,

Product C is a small portion in the TTV but larger in the EV. By not discounting

and  charging  the  price  book  for  Product  C,  the  bidder  benefits  from  the  actual

scenario when more of Product C is needed.



In this example, it might have been tempting and easier to offer a flat 10% dis-

count across all products. But the impact on the EV would be 3% lower revenues,

which  can  make  a  big  impact  on  your  profitability.  The  objective  of  the  tender

assessment  for  Type  2  and  3  Tenders  is  to  determine  your  target  TTV  and  then

identify how to maximize your EV. There will be situations in which the target TTV

will be below your target profitability. This is common when you have competitors

that have lower costs than your company. However, in those situations, rather than

bidding what you need to achieve your target profitability and losing the work, you

can  bid  a  winning  TTV  and  depend  on  your  pricing  strategy  addressing  tender

issues such as an underestimated scope of work to make an EV that will make your

targeted profitability objective. Later, in the “Creating a Bid Strategy” section of

this chapter, this will be discussed in more detail. As smart business people know,

every  penny  in  your  pocket  counts!  These  techniques  will  all  add  percentage

points to your bottom line.



Rebates



The rebate technique applies when your charges consists of product and service

charges and fixed charges, such as rentals or mobilization charges. To illustrate

this option, first you will see the traditional method of discounting and then how

to improve your EV by using a rebate. For example, the bid team expects the cus-

tomer to do $50,000 more than what was stated in the scope of work in the tender.

The bidder in this case included in their pricing $10,000 of monthly rentals. The

bidder’s target TTV to win the work was $100,000. Shown in Table 10.5 is TTV and

EV for this work using a flat 10% discount applied against the product and service

charges to arrive at the winning TTV of $100,000, and the EV is $145,000.



This is good, but can be better by using a rebate rather than a flat discount, as

shown in Table 10.6. In this scenario, the bidder offered a 10% rebate of product

and  service  charges  to  be  applied  to  the  rental  charges.  In  this  offer,  the  net

rental charge is $0 for the bid evaluation and the TTV is $100,000, which is needed

to win the tender the same as in the TTV flat discount option. However, for the EV

scenario,  revenue  for  the  flat  discount  was  $145,000;  in  the  rebate  option,  the

revenue increases to $150,000 and the discount drops to 6.3%. In this situation, it



396



CHAPTER 10  Competitive Bidding



Table 10.5

Underestimated scope of

work. Flat discount applied to

product and service charges.



is important to add a pricing note that rental charges will have the 10% applied

until  the  rental  charge  is  $0  and  rebates  are  applied  only  to  the  same  month’s

rental charges; unused portions are not carried forward.



Package Price



The last option for the underestimated scope of work is a package price that states

that  any  additional  quantities  or  services  will  be  charged  as  per  the  price  book.

This is a good option when the supplier can justify the package pricing based on

efficiencies or one-time benefits to them, such as using up stock before it is has to

be written off, and the timing of the project is very convenient, fitting well with the

availability of equipment and personnel. For example, the customer’s scope of work

requests ten products and ten days of a technician’s time to install the products.

However,  you  believe  the  customer  after  completing  the  first  ten  installs  will

request an additional five. For the bid your target TTV is $100,000 for the package,

which is a 10% discount off your book price. Your justification for offering this pack-

age price is the products the customer requested you have in country, which will

not be manufactured again and you are glad to sell them. Also, the technician is

currently in country, and you will not incur any additional travel costs as a result of

having to fly in the technician for this bid. In your pricing notes, you add that any

additional products or services will be as per the attached price book. When the

customer does call to order the additional five installs, you will then invoice these

at $110,000, which is according to the price book. This example raises a very good

question: How should the next five products and services be invoiced, book or not?

See the sidebar “To Book or Not to Book. That is the Question” for the answer.



Table 10.6

Underestimated scope of

work. Rebate applied to fixed

monthly rental charges.



Smart Business People



397





u  To Book or Not to

Book. That is the

Question.



This is the question you must

answer. Do you charge price

book/no discount or price book

with the same discount or

something different? The time

to answer this question is when

you prepare your bid and pre-

pare a convincing rationale for

why you are charging this way.

It would seem from the cus-

tomer’s perspective that, if they

ask you for a price for ten and

then buy more, they should get

the same or better price.  You

need to be prepared to explain

why this is not the case.



In the example, the timing of

this tender was near perfect. It

allowed inventory that would

soon be obsolete and the tech-

nician was already in the coun-

try, therefore, meaning no extra

travel costs. These are great

rationales for why the pricing

was a package price.



By writing your bids well and

including pricing notes to pro-

tect your position, you will have

the confidence to submit a bid

and know when the situation

evolves as you predict you will

be able to protect your margins.



In this example, you can offer

the customer a small discount

for the new products, provided

they pay any duties and trans-

portation to bring the next five

items into the country and pay

the travel costs to bring the

technician back to the country.



By ensuring that you have a

well-written offer and rationale,

you keep the power to negoti-

ate a good deal, like a smart

business person would.



Overestimated Work Volume



In this scenario, what is requested in the scope of work is what you believe will be

more than the actual volumes or quantities purchased. There can be several rea-

sons why the work volume is overestimated. The customer:



•  Wants to attract as many suppliers as possible with a bigger work volume.



•  Attempts to get better prices by offering a large work volume.



•  Is overly optimistic, thinking they can complete more work than possible.



•  Has made an incorrect calculation.



•  Wants to be certain they will have enough products and services.



•  Is  intending  to  split  the  work  amongst  two  or  more  suppliers.  Having  a



larger work volume helps them justify splitting the work.



As discussed in the underestimated work volume for Type 1 Tenders, if you dis-

cover this issue, discuss it with the customer. For Type 2 and 3 Tenders, the strat-

egy is to offer a high discount on the overestimated volume and a low discount on

the  probable  volume  of  work.  For  the  overstated  work  volume,  there  are  two

options for pricing:



•  Tiered discounts



•  Single unit flat pricing



Tiered Discounts



This option makes discounts conditional or tiered. Table 10.7 is an example from

a real tender the author bid in Malaysia. In this tender, the TTV was computed for

the standard flat discount and then the tiered discounts. Both calculate the same

value shown in the red cell, $8,500. In the EV scenario, the bid team expects the

customer to use 35 products, not 100 (shown in the orange cells). In this scenario

with the tiered discount, the discount drops to 8% and results in additional reve-

nue (shown in the yellow cells) of $3,220 – $2,975 = $245, or 8.2% more revenue.



Single Unit Flat Pricing



Single unit flat pricing is based on a invoicing per single measurable unit regard-

less  of  other  volumes  of  work  being  provided  or  ordered.  For  example,  a  single

unit could be per well, per month, per job, or other fixed amount that is relevant

for the tender. This type of pricing is effective for tenders in which the incremen-

tal  costs  to  deliver  the  work  are  low,  as  with  service  type  tenders  compared  to

products when a large portion of the costs are to produce each product. In the

example in Table  10.8,  the customer stated in the scope of work that they will

drill ten wells per month, which they want evaluated.



In this tender, there are very little incremental costs. The bid team believes this

volume is overestimated. Their EV is six wells per month. The target TTV to win

is a 20% discount from the bidder’s book price or $800,000 per month using the

TTV scope of work.  In this example, the bid team has made their offer a unit price

per month good for up to ten wells per month. This offer from the supplier’s point

of view is the book price or 0% discount. The customer will evaluate the offer at

$700,000 or an apparent 30% discount. This will be 8.75% lower than the market

rate of $800,000 and should entice the customer to accept what will appear as a

very  good  offer.  In  this  type  of  offer,  the  bid  team  must  state  clearly  what  is

included in their unit pricing. Everything else needed outside what is included in

the unit pricing will be priced at the price book. In this example, the bid team

stated  which  products  and  services  were  included  in  the  unit  pricing,  and  the

price was good for a maximum of ten wells per month.



398



CHAPTER 10  Competitive Bidding



Table 10.7

Overestimated scope of

work. Tiered discount.



For  all  these  recommendations,  there  are  a  number  of  questions  the  bid  team

must ask before committing to their strategy. One of the questions management

always has is “How sure are you that you will make this revenue?”  The best way to

answer this question is shown in the Chapter Highlight 10.3: I’m 90% Confident!



Products and Services



For this factor, you analyze what is asked for in the tender and what you believe

will actually be requested once you are awarded the work. Issues in this factor can

create large pricing differences between TTV and EV. If you identify these issues

and the competition does not, it will give you a significant competitive advantage.

When assessing what product and services are requested, you assess the tender

document to determine if there are:



•  Extraneous  products  or  services.  These are items that are requested in

the scope of work, but will not be purchased or required. In this situation,

the customer may be uncertain if they need the item but, to minimize any

risk of not having it if needed, they include the item in the tender. It could

also be a case of the customer not knowing they do not need the item.

•  Omitted products or services. These items will be required but have not

been  included  in  the  scope  of  work.  Most  of  the  time  when  you  discover

missing items in the tender, it is the result of a customer error—possibly

due to inexperience or a lack of attention when preparing the tender.

•  Implicit  products  or  services. These are items that are not requested in

the scope of work but it is implied the bidder will provide at no cost to the

customer. Of the three scenarios to be discussed in this section, the implicit

scenario  can  be  the  most  contentious  as  the  customer  assumes  you  will

provide the product or service without charging. Unlike the omitted items



Table 10.8

Overestimated scope

of work. Single unit

flat pricing.



Smart Business People



399





Chapter Highlight 10.3: I’m 90% Confident!



There are times when bid teams are questioned about how confident they are that their bid strategy will make

the projected revenue. Bidding for tendered work does not have a guaranteed outcome. It is a business invest-

ment that has risk and uncertainty. To provide management with a complete picture of the uncertainties and

risk for your bid strategy, use a Monte Carlo simulation to calculate the P90, P50, and P10 probabilities.



Modeling Risks and Uncertainty

A Monte Carlo simulation lets you see all the possible outcomes of your decisions and assess the impact of

risk, allowing for better decision-making under uncertainty. The technique was first used by scientists working

on the atom bomb; it was named for Monte Carlo, the Monaco resort town renowned for its casinos. Since

its introduction in World War II, the Monte Carlo simulation has been used by professionals in widely disparate

fields. Professionals in finance, project management, energy, manufacturing, engineering, research and devel-

opment, insurance, oil and gas, transportation, and the environment have all used the Monte Carlo simulation

to make better decisions.3



The Monte Carlo simulation is an alternative to both single-point (deterministic) estimation and the scenario

approach that presents the worst, most likely, and best-case scenarios. The Monte Carlo simulation begins

with a model (i.e., one or more equations, together with assumptions and logic relating the parameters in the

equations). Risk analysis is beneficial because there is uncertainty in the estimates of sales volumes and costs

and timing when bidding. Quantifying that uncertainty with ranges of possible values and associated proba-

bilities (i.e., with probability distributions) helps everyone understand the risks involved.



With  the  power  of  personal  computers,  today  and  a  multitude  of  plug-ins  for  programs  such  as  Microsoft

Excel, some of the most powerful modeling tools for modeling your offer can be easily applied.



Shown below is a simple revenue projection for a bid. In this tender, the request is for three products and a

technician to install the products. The customer has asked for a proposal for the quantities shown below. The

sales team has analyzed the bid and believes that products A and B are overestimated and product C and the

technician days to do the install are underestimated. The bid team has analyzed tenders from the last twelve

and found their range of winning prices. Using the average pricing, the team calculates the Total Tender Value

(TTV) of $4,930,000, which they believe will be a winning bid as they estimated the competition would bid

$5,000,000. Because of the scope of work issues, the bid team has calculated an Expected Value (EV) for the

bid of $5,310,000 by using the most likely quantities and average pricing to calculate the EV. The risk here is

that the EV could be a lot less or more. The best way to have a full understanding of all the possible outcomes

is to run a Monte Carlo simulation.



In the Monte Carlo simulation, the bid team builds a model, as shown below. A Monte Carlo plug-in for Excel

was used to run the simulation. The key step was replacing the single point estimates in the model (average

pricing)  with  a  triangular  value  function. The  triangular  value  function  uses  a  random  number  generator  to

sample values from the distribution of possible values estimated made by the bid team as entered into the

green shaded cells in the spreadsheet. These values are the minimum, most likely, and maximum quantities.

This technique is called the “expert opinion” and is used to define the probability distribution when there is a

lack of data to build the distribution model. The simulation is run 1,000 times. Each time the simulation is run,

the plug-in randomly samples a value from the given range storing that value in the cell and then any subse-

quent calculations are completed. Selected cell values—in this case, the Expected Value Revenue—are stored



400



CHAPTER 10  Competitive Bidding



Chapter Highlight 10.3: I’m 90% Confident! (cont.)



and plotted in a histogram, and the outcomes are ordered and summed to build the Percentile Distribution

table. From the outcomes, the bid team can then find the  P90, P50, and P10 values from the percentile dis-

tribution shown in the lower right-hand side of the figure above.



The P90 value shown on the graph above is the value at which, out of the 1,000 times the model was run,

there were 100 outcomes when 10% of the total were less than $4,443,274. From a business person’s per-

spective, you can say you are 90% confident you will not make less than that value. The value shown above

for the EV (expected value for revenue) is $5,310,000 (red shaded cell). The shaded area on the “Percentile

Distribution” table shows approximately where the value occurred. You can then extrapolate the exact percen-

tile, in this case, it was 84%, or 100–84 equals 16% of the values being equal to or greater than $5,310,000.

If the bid team was only to report this number, then the expected revenue from the bid is very optimistic.

Business people do not make financial decisions when there is only a 16% chance of an outcome occurring.

Most business people want to be at least 90% confident they will achieve their financial targets. The same

logic  is  applied  to  oil  company  reserves.  Oil  companies  cannot  book  reserves  (add  to  their  balance  sheet)

unless the reserves are in the P90 area of the reserve estimates. Governing bodies, where the oil companies

have been incorporated, ensure that reserve estimates pass a rigorous test using a Monte Carlo simulation.



The additional use of this type of analysis is to compare bids when a company must choose which tenders to

pursue. Having the Monte Carlo estimates of revenue and net profit, the company’s management can make the

best decision and then select the most attractive as measured by revenue and profit from the P90 estimates.



most customers will accept that they have to pay for those items. However,

for implicit items, they believe these are provided at no charge and to con-

vince them you will need to have a good rationale and example of where you

have charged for these items previously. There can be several reasons why

the customer thinks this way. These items have been included on previous

projects, so the customers assume this will continue. They might think it is

industry practice to provide such items. This issue was discussed earlier in

the sidebar “Worth Their Weight in Gold.”



Smart Business People



401





Extraneous Products and Services



For extraneous items, you apply a high discount on the extraneous items or include

these  as  part  of  the  package.  If  the  extraneous  items  or  services  are  not  readily

available, you will need to add a pricing note in the bid that a minimum amount

of time is required for pre-notification when the extraneous items are needed.



Omitted Products and Services



These  items  are  not  included  in  your  offer,  and  a  pricing  note  is  added  that  all

products  and  services  not  in  the  scope  of  work  will  be  charged  as  per  the

attached price book.



Implicit Products and Services



Services not specifically requested should be treated the same as omitted items.

To  avoid  difficulties  after  awarding  of  the  work,  you  must  clearly  state  what  is

included in your bid.



Table 10.9 demonstrates how the bidder can use these differences to their advan-

tage to submit a winning TTV with significantly better EV. In this example, there

is $100,000 of undiscounted confirmed products and services shown in the red cell

in  the  Flat  Discount  column.  Using  the  flat  discount  method  of  pricing,  these

products and services are added with the extraneous, omitted, and implied ser-

vices making the total $120,000. A discount of 17% is applied to bring the offer to

the targeted TTV of $100,000. However, the actual invoice will be less $91,300 as

the extraneous products and services will not be run.



The recommended way to handle this is shown in the EV Recommended column.

In this pricing method, extraneous items are included (no charge) in the pricing.

This is low risk to your profitability as the extraneous items will not be run. The

omitted and implied items are not quoted as these are not specifically requested

in the tender document and a pricing note must be added that all additional ser-

vices will be charged as per the reference price catalogue. The comparison of the

two techniques: flat discount and EV recommended, will earn the bidder 20% more

revenue.



It is a best practice before committing to a discounted offer to always check your

discounting method against best case and worst case scenarios. You want to be

confident that your discounting model achieves the desired results in both best

and worst cases. Too often salespeople forget to calculate the worst case scenario



Table 10.9

Overestimated scope of work.

Single unit flat pricing.



402



CHAPTER 10  Competitive Bidding



u  Best Case, Worst

Case



Always test

your discount

schemes

against best

case and worst case

scenarios.



For example, if you offer

the customer a 20% discount if

they buy five products each

time they place an order, the

profitability of delivering five

products at one time is much

better than five deliveries of sin-

gle products.



If the customer buys five prod-

ucts in each order, fantastic—

this is the best case.



But what happens if the cus-

tomer agrees, but only has you

deliver one or two products at a

time now? This is worst case.

What is the discount?



These types of scenarios need

to be considered and then

include any conditions that pro-

tects your profitability. In this

case, assuming the five prod-

ucts prices are close to the

same, then one offer that would

protect your profitability is

when the customers buy five

product in one delivery, with

the fifth product being free. If

the customer buys fewer than

five, then the discount is zero

and you protect your profitabili-

ty. Other options could be a

variable discount based on the

number of products purchased

per delivery. For example, 5%

for two, 10% for three,15% for

four, and 20% for five.



until it happens, and then it is too late to make any changes. See the sidebar “Best

Case, Worst Case” for another example of best case/worst case discounting.



Awards



The  award  factor  is  the  simplest  of  the  factors  to  analyze.  For  this  factor,  it  is

important to understand how the customer will award the work. You must look for

the  specific  clause  in  the  tender  document  that  states  how  the  customer  will

award the work. If the tender does not have a clause explaining how the work will

be awarded, then you must state what commitment you require as a condition of

your offer. The most common types of awards are as follows:



•  Single supplier



•  Split



•  Second supplier



•  Call-out or call-off



Single Supplier Award



The customer will award the work to a single supplier. Prices quoted are for the

stated scope of work. You must clearly state that the prices are for 100% of the

award.



Split Award



The work will be split among two or more companies. If you are bidding for a split

award,  you  must  confirm  whether  the  split  will  be  an  even  split  or  different  sized

packages of work will be awarded. In some tenders it is not unusual for the customer

to award work split by geography or award specific products or services to one com-

pany and other products and services to another supplier. You need to decide which

portion of the work is the most desirable and give your best offer for the desired pack-

age. Clearly state your pricing conditions for each package. Another tactic for ensur-

ing that you win the specific package you want is to add conditions or extra charges

to the packages you do not want, which will make the competitors’ offer more attrac-

tive. An example is geographic surcharges.



Second Supplier Award



For this award, the preferred supplier will be called upon first to supply the prod-

ucts or services, but in situations where the preferred supplier cannot supply the

products or services, the second supplier will be used.



If a second supplier award is an acceptable option, clearly state your pricing and

conditions if you are awarded as the second supplier. Use tiered discounts to pro-

tect your margins.



Call-Out or Call-Off Award



A call-out or call-off award is when the customer awards the work to the supplier

with  no  minimum  commitment  to  purchase.  This  type  of  award  can  be  used  in

conjunction with the single supplier award. In this scenario, the customer has a

single supplier or split award contracts for a committed portion of the work and

the  remaining  portion  as  a  call-out  contract.  The  concerns  with  these  types  of

contracts is that there is no minimum commitment. These should be treated like



Smart Business People



403





u	 Sole and Single

Supplier. What is the

Difference?



Below are the definitions of sin-

gle source and sole source sup-

pliers, taken from a procurement

website.4



Single Source

Procurement Definition



Single source purchasing refers

to purchases from one selected

supplier, even though there are

other suppliers that provide simi-

lar products. If your company

decides to buy only Dell comput-

ers, then that is single source

purchasing. However, unlike sole

source purchasing, you and your

company have a choice to switch

suppliers, but for strategic and

possibly cost reasons the com-

pany decides to use only a spe-

cific supplier.



Sole Source

Procurement Definition



Sole source procurement, how-

ever, refers to those purchases

where there’s only one supplier

that provides the product.

Usually these are unique prod-

ucts that you cannot find any-

where only through one supplier/

manufacturer.



So before you do anything else

when dealing and negotiating

with a sole or single source sup-

plier, understand exactly that—

namely whether you’re dealing

with a sole source or single

source.



split and second supplier. For this type of award, you must ensure that there is no

penalty if the supplier cannot provide the products or services at the time ordered.



For both the second supplier and call-out or call-off awards, you add a pricing note

for  a  minimum  time  for  prenotification  to  mobilize  or  order  the  products  and

services. You also include another pricing note for if the products or services are

canceled. After being called out, a cancellation fee will apply.



There is another type of award called sole award, which at times can be confused

with a single supplier award. To understand the difference, review the “Sole and

Single Supplier. What is the Difference?” sidebar.



Specifications



Specification issues are when the customer requests products or services in the

tender document that are not appropriate for their requirements as described in

the tender. The most common issues are as follows:



•  Under-specified



•  Overspecified



•  Unique products or services are considered commodities



Under-Specified



In most tenders you should not find under-specified specifications. If this is the

case, however, it may be due to several reasons, such as:



•  Attract many suppliers. By having the specifications under-specified, more

companies will be able to bid. The customer hopes this will make the bid-

ding more competitive.



•  Attempt to get the lowest price for value-added products and services.

Most suppliers will price value-added services based on the added value the

customer receives and not based on the supplier’s costs, which can be sig-

nificantly more than standard products and services. From the customer’s

perspective,  the  supplier  can  therefore  accept  less  for  their  value-added

products and services and, as the customer in their tender are offering a

specified volume of work, they will expect better prices. One way to do this

by negotiating with the supplier. The first step to a planned negotiation is

lowering the specification and having the prices for the lower specification

products and services. Now the customer knows exactly what is the refer-

ence price from which to start the negotiations for the higher specification

product and services they really need.



•  Copy–Paste. Staff on the customer’s tender team copy and paste specifica-

tions from a previous tender into the new tender that are not required. As

unbelievable as this may be for large B2B procurement, such mistakes can

happen. It is more likely when the customer is facing time pressure, such

as when project start deadlines are approaching quickly and the customer

has not sent out the tender document. Another example is when the cus-

tomer’s operations are shut down due to the lack of inventory of products

and services; they need to run their business and start the tendering pro-

cess quickly. This copy–paste type of mistake made recent headlines in the

Irish  Examiner:  “The  government  procurement  process  was  a  shambles,

with  untrained  staff  copying  and  pasting  tender  documents  with  no  real

understanding of what they were doing, according to Paul Quinn, the new

chief procurement officer.” 5



404



CHAPTER 10  Competitive Bidding



•  Lack  of  attention. Sometimes the differences between products and ser-

vices requested in the scope of work are very negligible and the number of

options for what the customer can request can be enormous. If the person

from  the  customer’s  tender  team  does  not  proof  their  tender  closely,  this

can result in the wrong specifications in the tender.



Guidelines of how to respond to an under specified scope of work depends on the

type of tender.



Type  3–Transaction  Tender.  Treat  the  under-specified  product  or  service  as

extraneous and handle as described in the extraneous product and service section,

and the required products and services that have not been specified as omitted

product and services. In this situation, you must clearly state the specifications of

the  products  and  services  you  are  quoting  in  your  bid  and  other  products  and

services available as per the attached price catalogue.



Type 2–Consultative Tender. Provide what the customer really requires provided

their  evaluation  criteria  will  give  your  solution  a  higher  technical  score  and  as

long as the pricing for the higher specification product and services is within the

premium allowed for the company with the best evaluated score. If the pricing for

what  the  customer  really  needs  makes  your  offer  price  too  high,  then  price  for

what  they  need  as  you  would  in  a  Type  3–Transactional  Tender  and  submit  an

alternative offer for what the customer really needs.



Type  1–Enterprise  Tender.  Discuss  with  the  customer  the  specification  dis-

crepancies  and  make  recommendations  for  what  they  should  purchase  based

on their needs.



Overspecified



It is more common that a customer might overspecify their requirements. Typical

reasons why customers overspecify are:



•  They want to be confident that all situations they might encounter can be

handled,  regardless  of  how  unlikely  the  situations.  For  example,  for  their

project, there are no requirements for continuous user support seven-days

a week 24, hours a day (7/24), but to be absolutely sure their users never

have to wait for support until the next day, 7/24 support is part of the tender

specifications.



•  Previous  bad  experiences  with  suppliers  not  meeting  specifications  and



incurring extra costs.



•  A preference for a competitor. Overspecifying and requesting  the preferred

company’s  unique  products  or  services  allow  the  customer  to  technically

disqualify competitors who do not have similar services.



•  Familiarity with only one company. When the customer creates the tender,

they use the specifications or nomenclature of the products and services of

the company they are familiar with or from the current supplier.



•  Unique  products  or  services  are  considered  commodities.  This  is  another

way the customer can use the tendering process to negotiate better pricing

as described earlier in the under-specified section. In situations when only

one  company  has  the  products  or  services  meeting  the  specifications

requested  in  the  tender  but  the  tender  is  sent  to  multiple  bidders,  the

customer has overspecified the products or services. In this case, the cus-

tomer might be attempting to negotiate a better price for the unique prod-

ucts or services. In this situation, the company with the unique product or

services is called a sole supplier (see the sidebar “Sole and Single Supplier.

What is the Difference?” Rather than make a sole award to that supplier,



Smart Business People



405





the customer might still go through the tendering process to have the sup-

plier  with  the  unique  services  think  their  products  or  services  are  com-

modities  that  all  companies  on  the  bidder’s  list  have  and  as  a  result  will

receive a better price by tendering.



Another  way  in  which  the  customer  might  treat  unique  products  or  services  as

commodities  is  by  asking  for  supplier’s  prices  for  a  specific  product  or  service

where they use a company’s nomenclature. For example, Apple receives a tender

for cell phones from an international company asking for prices for iPhone 6+ and

you as the salesperson for Apple know the tender has also been sent to several

other companies. This is not uncommon when the customer is only familiar with

one company. In this situation, the customer has commoditized the iPhone 6+.



Response to Overspecification



In a tender in which you have identified an overspecification issue, you must also

consider:



•  What type of tender is it? What tender response strategy will give you the



best evaluation?



•  What  have  you  been  able  to  achieve  with  your  sales  strategy?  How  many



people in the customer’s buying center are supporters and mentors?



•  Can you sell up after awarded the work during the implementation phase?



•  What will the competitor response be?



Once you have answered these questions, prepare your tender response. In gen-

eral, there are three options for handling an overspecified tender. The bidder can

choose one or more of the following options:



•  Define



•  Replace



•  Version



Define



In  a  define  response  to  an  overspecification,  you  state  what  you  will  provide  to

satisfy the project requirements. Some examples of a define strategy response are:



•  If a customer wants tools to be run over 350º F, but you know they will not

need this, you state you will provide tools rated to temperatures up to 350º F.



•  If a customer wants a delivery of pumps in two weeks, but you know that

they do not need delivery for a month, state your prices are for one-month

delivery once the order has been placed.



•  If a customer wants dedicated people or equipment, but they do not require

this level of service, state that you will provide services within 24 hours after

notice has been given.



When you define that you will provide something less than requested, you must

provide your rationale for doing so and the benefit to the customer.



Replace



A  replace  response  is  used  when  the  specifications  request  services  that  are

unique or that are not required. In this situation, offer a replacement product or

service, providing “like for like” when compared to the offerings of other competi-

tors. Some examples of replacement are:



•  Older generation products for unique products.



406



CHAPTER 10  Competitive Bidding



u	Doing Less with More.

Does that Make Sense?



In most business

situations doing

less with more

would be catego-

rized as wasteful. But

in the B2B environ-

ment, where there

are difficulties compar-

ing like-for-like products and ser-

vices, having the ability to do

less with more can be a strate-

gy for victory.



University of California at

Berkeley professors Carl Shapiro

and Hal Varian explain how a

“versioning” strategy can enable

a company to distinguish its

products from the competition

and protect its prices from col-

lapse. The technique is called

value subtraction.



Most versions are created by

subtracting value rather than by

adding.



•  The producer first invests

in developing the most

technologically advanced

version in order to have

a distinctive product that

appeals to the most

demanding and least price-

sensitive customers



•  The producer then



removes features or capa-

bilities to tailor the product

for less demanding cus-

tomers



•  As the low-end users’



needs advance, they can

purchase the higher-end

versions



•  Lower  level  service  options,  such  as  a  less  experienced  interpreter  for  a



senior interpreter.



As with the define option, you must state your rationale for replacing the product

or service and the benefit to the customer.



Versioning



Versioning  refers  to  providing  different  levels  of  product  capabilities  or  service

levels to be delivered with the same product or service. This strategy allows your

company  to  offer  a  lower  version  to  be  used  in  the  customer’s  bid  evaluation

model, and provides your company with the opportunity to sell up to the higher

value versions during the execution of the project. Smart companies selling in the

B2B  environment  design  their  products  and  services  using  a  technique  called

value  subtraction.6  See  the  sidebar  “Doing  Less  with  More.  Does  that  Make

Sense?”



Some examples of versioning include:



•  Airline seats available in first, business or economy class. Everyone boards

the same plane yet the airlines can charge more to passengers sitting in

first and business than they can people in economy. They do this by ver-

sioning.



•  IBM laser printer: One prints ten pages per minute (ppm) and another five

ppm.  The only difference between the two printers is the slower printer has

an extra chip on the motherboard to slow down the printer.



•  Answer Products, delivered in 24 hours or in one week: All the resources to

produce the products are the same, but the supplier can charge much more

for the 24-hour version by versioning with time. In a Type 3–Transactional

Tender, for example, this could be used to provide a low price for one-week

turnaround time and once the contract is awarded, sell the 24-hour turn-

around when needed.



There  are  two  other  considerations  when  considering  using  versioning  in  B2B

markets.



First, the bid team must prepare and coordinate with operations to provide the

lower version when the customer orders the lower version. If the customer discov-

ers the lower version only exists in the bid and is not operationally possible, then

the first time the customer orders the lower version product or service and they

receive the higher level version, it will be very difficult for you to sell the higher

level version. The customer will continue to order the lower version knowing they

will receive the higher level version.



Second,  the  creation  of  different  versions  must  be  controlled  by  headquarters.

Every version adds an extra level of complexity, and there can also be added costs

for modifications to be able to provide the lower level of products and services.

By keeping these decisions at headquarters, they are in a better position to know

what  other  locations  have  done  and  can  ensure  that  versioning  is  manageable

and profitable.



Guidelines of How to Respond



The general guidelines for responding to an overspecified scope of work are as

follows:



Type 3–Transaction Tender. Offer only what is needed for the customer’s real

needs. You choose one of the options for responding to an overspecified issue

(define,  replace,  or  version).  You  should  clearly  describe  what  your  company



Smart Business People



407





will and will not be providing, your rationale, and the benefit to the customer

of the version.



Type 2–Consultative Tender. Offer a solution that is optimized for the customer’s

evaluation model and requirements. Use one of the response options of the define,

replace, or versioning strategy to address the particular specification issues. You

might not need to match the competitor’s specifications if a better specification

will give you a better evaluation score and the premium for the better specifica-

tion is within the allowable premium over an L1 competitor. In this situation you

should discussed with your mentor’s within the customer’s buying center so they

can leverage your better solution to select your company for the work.



Type 1–Enterprise Tender. Discuss with the customer the specification discrep-

ancies  and make recommendations for what they should purchase based on their

needs.



Unique Product or Services are Considered Commodities. For the situation where

the customer is trying to commoditize your products and services, you will need to

take an extra step to validate your response to this overspecification issue. First,

you will issue a clarification question, asking the customer if competitors do not

have a product or service that meets the customer’s tender specification, will the

competitor be disqualified? In most cases, the customer will answer “no” because

if the answer is “yes,” the customer will lose any leverage to a receive a better price

from  your  company  and  will  have  to  enter  into  a  sole  source  contract  with  your

company. When they answer “no,” ask “How can they bid?” The answer has to be

“They are to bid a similar product or service.” Now you can choose one of the over-

specify options of define, replace, or version, whichever is best for your company

and not be disqualified. When the customer opens your offer, they might challenge

your offer and remind you that you were to price your unique product or service.

At that time, your answer is that you were instructed to bid a similar product or

service if your company did not have the exact product and service specified. So

to be fairly evaluated, you gave them a like-for-like product or service. In this situ-

ation, you will need to make your mentors fully aware of your response so they can

defend the decision to make a sole source contract.



Risks



Risk factors are grouped into two categories, as follows:



•  Contract

•  Business



Very  few  salespersons  have  legal  training.  Therefore,  a  good  place  to  start  to

understand risk is to understand some of the risks-related legal terms you will find

in contracts, and what these imply for the supplier. See Chapter Highlight 10.4:

“Explain This To Me One More Time.”



Contract Risks



The wording in a contract is an agreement between companies. How the contract is

written  and  what  is  said  in  the  contract  is  one  of  a  company’s  most  valued  assets

;without  a  valid  contract  a  company  cannot  invoice  their  customers.  In  B2B  sales

most tenders include the pro forma contract, which the winning bidder must sign.

This  section  is  meant  to  make  the  salesperson  aware  of  the  contract  risks.  Before

responding  to  any  tender,  the  salespeople  must  pass  a  copy  of  the  tender  and  pro

forma contract to the contracts manager. The remainder of this section will briefly

discuss the following:



408



CHAPTER 10  Competitive Bidding



Chapter Highlight 10.4: Explain This To Me One More Time



Key Legal Terminology Explained

1.  Indemnity: An indemnity is a contractual obligation to make good any loss, damage, or liability incurred by

another. This is usually done in the form of reimbursement from one party to the party that incurred the liability.



2.  Knock-for-Knock: Also known as the Mutual Hold Harmless principle. The client and your company are both

responsible for their people and property even if the other party was responsible for a relevant death, injury, or

damages.



3.  Deliberate Misconduct: Intentionally or deliberately causing loss or damage (i.e., with malice). Note:  When

someone  intends  misconduct,  use  the  term “deliberate  misconduct.” “Willfull  misconduct”  and “gross  negli-

gence” are a lot closer in meaning in some jurisdictions. You should be careful with these terms in contracts as

you might intend to be agreeing to accept liability for deliberate misconduct only, but then end up agreeing to

accept gross negligence. The danger is, if the court interprets the term in the context of your company’s opera-

tions, deliberate misconduct is unlikely to occur in a work environment. An example of deliberate misconduct: I

am driving my car in town, and I see the car of someone I have had an argument with in the past. I deliberately

reverse my car into the driver’s car.



4.  Gross Negligence: Acting recklessly with complete disregard for the consequences. A person’s conduct

is either:



i)  Reckless in the sense of not caring whether or not he/she commits a breach of duty, or



ii)  Undertaken with an actual appreciation of the risks involved (i.e., a serious disregard or indifference



to an obvious risk).



In the context of your company’s operations, gross negligence is less likely to occur. For your company to accept

liability for gross negligence, it should be i) be capped and ii) defined in the contract or by the governing law of

the contract. An example of gross negligence: I am driving my car along a stretch of road that I know has a high

number of pedestrians adjacent to it. The speed limit is 30 km/h. I decide to race a friend of mine, and I exceed

the speed limit along this stretch of road and reach 70 km/h when I lose control around a tight bend in the road.

As a result, I injure a pedestrian. I understood that if I lost control of the car I could injure pedestrians, yet I con-

tinued to speed with disregard for an obvious risk.



5.  Simple or Ordinary Negligence: The court would objectively ascertain how a reasonable person would have

acted in circumstances similar to the ones that gave rise to the injury, death, loss, or damages. In the context of

your  company’s  operations,  the  likelihood  exists  that  such  events  could  occur  relatively  easily. An  example  of

simple or ordinary negligence: I am driving on an open stretch of road and check a text message that comes

through on my phone while I am driving. As I look up, I see a deer standing in the middle of the road, and I swerve

to avoid colliding with it. As a result, the vehicle rolls over, and my passenger is injured. The reasonable person

would not be checking a text message while driving and might have been in a position to avoid the accident.



The  figure  to  the  right  highlights  each  level  of  negligence  in

terms  of  the  likelihood  of  occurring  and  the  relative  level  of

negligence. In plain English, if something bad happened and it

was  your  fault,  the  company  is  not  responsible  unless  you

were deliberately negligent.



6.    Consequential  Damages: This  is  a  loss  arising  from  the

results  of  damages  rather  than  from  the  damage  itself. The

loss must not be remote—that is, it must be the natural and

probable effect of the wrongful conduct. An example of con-

sequential damages: I own a champion racehorse. While at the

third-party racetrack stables, a piece of masonry from a poorly

maintained stable roof comes loose and injures the horse to

such  an  extent  that  its  racing  career  is  ended  prematurely.

Direct damages will be the vet’s bills for treating the horse and

nursing it back to health. Consequential damages are the loss

of winnings that the racehorse would have generated over the course of its racing career.



Smart Business People



409







•  Acceptable risks



•  Unacceptable risks



•  Indemnities



•  Contract termination



•  Contract term of extension



•  Validity of proposal



Acceptable Risks



Most companies accept reasonable contractual risks associated with their opera-

tions.  However,  onerous  liability  provisions  proposed  by  clients  cannot  be

accounted for by pricing or mitigated by risk financing programs. Such liabilities

are disproportionate to the value of the contracts in question and might represent

a material liability to your company and, in extreme cases, an unlimited liability.

Most companies accepts risks that they can manage and control.



Unacceptable Risks



What risks are generally unacceptable? Unacceptable risk is defined as one that

would do harm, fiscally or physically, to person(s), a business, or the environment.

Unacceptable risks are well known for each industry. Using the oilfield services

industry as an example, service companies work for the client who is the license

or  concession  holder  and  owner  of  the  well  design.  The  oil  company  has  direct

care and control over the integrity of the well and its other contractors. The risks

associated with the construction of a well cannot be accepted by a service com-

pany.  The service company’s gain for completing a job is the revenue from the job.

Large jobs can earn up to a few million dollars. On the other hand, if the well blows

out or even worse as in the case of BP Macondo, it can lead to the loss of lives, the

rig, local industries, and costs in the tens of billions of dollars. It does not make

sense for any service company to accept such huge liabilities.



Companies should have client contract standards (CCS) they enforce to keep and

manage the contractual risks at acceptable levels. See the sidebar “No Way—I’m

Not Taking That!” for the top ten unacceptable risks. Your company should insist

on full indemnity from the client for any damages listed in the top ten resulting

from your company’s actions.



Indemnities



Based on the Mutual Hold Harmless principle, each party is responsible for their

respective  people  and  property  even  if  the  other  party  was  at  the  cause  of  the

damage, loss, injury or death.



This principle is traditionally applied irrespective of cause, with an exception for

deliberate  misconduct.  See  Chapter  Highlight  10.4:  Explain  This  To  Me  One

More  Time for more discussion on misconduct. Mutual hold harmless is a good

business practice for both the customer and their suppliers. It lowers the cost of

risk management as each party knows what they are providing and what is at risk.



Contract Termination



Typically  referred  to  as  the  “mutual  30-day  out”  clause,  it  is  essential  that  your

company can exit from a non-profitable contract. Alternatively, it might be accept-

able to not terminate the contract, but have the right to refuse work.



u	No Way—I'm Not

Taking That



The top ten contract risks your

company should not accept

(for good reason, because if

they happen once, once it

could be the end of the com-

pany):



1.  Client’s personnel



2.  Client’s property



3.  Client’s other contractors’

personnel and property



4.  Third parties, including

rigs, platforms, produc-

tion facilities, and pipe-

lines



5.  Reservoir damage



6.  Blowouts, fires, and



explosions



7.  Pollution emanating from

below the rotary table



8.  Radioactive contamination



9.



Inaccuracy of well or seis-

mic data, or incorrect

interpretations or recom-

mendations related to

such



10. Consequential damages



All the above risks are both dis-

proportionate to your returns

and outside your management

and control.



410



CHAPTER 10  Competitive Bidding



Contract Term and Extension



Every  contract  should  clearly  reference  the  original  term,  along  with  extension

provisions.  Extension  is  always  upon  mutual  agreement  between  you  and  the

customer, and each party must have the contractual right to refuse an extension.

Extensions should be subject to pricing renegotiations.



Validity of Proposal



A  proposal  valid  for  an  unusually  long  time  or  a  proposal  with  no  validity  date

might oblige your company to accept an old pricing that was provided before and

now is not the pricing you want to apply. Every proposal must have a duration for

which  it  is  valid;  after  that  period,  the  offer  can  only  be  extended  with  written

notice from the bidder.



Business Risks



Business risks are contract terms and conditions that your pricing structure does not

account for; if these business risks are not addressed, they can result in poor profit-

ability or losses. It is the responsibility of the manager with the profitability objectives

to review all contracts along with their contracts department personnel. They review

the  commercial  conditions  present  in  each  contract  and  mitigate  any  commercial

risks or negotiate better terms with the client whenever possible. These types of busi-

ness risks are referred to as revenue leakage that can have a significant

negative affect on your revenue or costs once the work begins.



u  Damn, that Hurts!



A few small words like the ones below can

cost your companies millions or more. Use

a magnifying glass to make sure you don't

miss these.

Watch out for the “Risk” words—consult

Contract Manager:

“Contractor warrants ..……”

“Contractor shall be liable .……”

“Contractor shall indemnify …….”

“Contractor shall be responsible ….…”

“Contractor  shall  hold  harm-

less......”



All the sections of the contract

need to be reviewed closely to ensure

that any additional liabilities are considered

acceptable risks. Below are common sec-

tions making up a contract:



•  Indemnities and warranties

•  Environmental provisions

•  Customs issues

•  Pricing and payment terms

•  Intercoms

•  Penalties

•  Liquidated damages

•  Tax provisions

•  Insurance

•  Force majeure

•  Suspension/Termination



Operations  and  the  sales  group  in  conjunction  with  their  contracts

managers should create a business terms and conditions checklist. This

should be used by the bid team to assess the business risks in a client

contract. This saves valuable time during the tender assessment, and

provides more to prepare the bid strategy review presentation.



Some of the more common contractual issues leading to revenue leak-

age are listed below, and a few things to watch out for when reviewing

the tender are listed in the sidebar “Damn, that Hurts!”



Business risks are grouped into three categories:



•  Revenue



•  Costs



•  Efficiencies



Revenue Risks



Pricing. Pricing should never be fixed for longer than twelve months

from the start of contract. If client insists on longer, make sure that a

commodity cost increase is included, along with scope of work adjust-

ments, as per below.



Price changes. Document how a price change will be implemented.

It is best to state the timing for reviewing the pricing.



Pricing  discount  structure.  The  clearer  the  pricing  and  discount

structure, the easier it will be to minimize errors at invoicing, and to

prevent disputes.



Volume discounts. For large corporate accounts, always check with your

headquarters  team  to  determine  if  any  volume  discounts  apply.  If  so,

theirs  will  need  to  be  explained  in  your  bid  so  the  customer  includes

there in their bid analysis. You also need to understand the impact that

any corporate level discounts have on the overall profitability of the sale.



Smart Business People



411





Performance incentives. Performance incentives are mutually beneficial arrange-

ments  that  promote  efficiencies  and  savings  for  clients  and  your  company.  A

generic clause can be included in the contract, and both parties agree to the key

performance indicators once the work is awarded. The incentives must contain a

description  of  the  bonus  and  penalty  scheme  to  motivate  the  supplier  to  provide

exceptional service. The incentive scheme defines what will constitute penalties for

service quality (SQ) problems and what constitutes bonuses for SQ successes. There

should also be an agreement on the maximum penalty and bonus. Generally accept-

ed maximums are a percentage of the invoice.



Costs Risks



Commodity price increases.  Every contract should have a generic clause allowing

for  price  discussions  if  commodities  increase  by  more  than  5%.  Alternatively,  a

commodity cost indexation scheme or surcharges can be considered and agreed to

by both parties regarding the details of the index or surcharges.



Extreme  conditions.  When  extreme  environmental  or  operations,  conditions

exist, such as in the oilfield services industry, there can be extreme wellbore con-

ditions such as high temperatures and high pressures. These types of  operations

will raise the costs of equipment maintenance. In situations like this, when the

conditions exist, there should be a surcharge applied. This can be in your standard

price book or added as a pricing note.



Third-party charges. On average, third-party charges can amount to up to 3% of a

business’s total revenues. To reflect the financial costs and the risk that your com-

pany is taking, you need to ensure a fair recharge level. Therefore, the administra-

tion fee for reimbursables should be in line with your project’s financial objectives.



Force majeure.  State what rates will apply during force majeure such as standby

or  monthly  rentals.  Also  ensure  that  the  contract  states  what  constitutes  force

majeure  and  what  documentation  is  required  for  notification  force  majeure  is

declared and what notification is required to terminate force majeure status.



Efficiencies Risks



Late  notices.  Late  notices  or  a  lack  of  equipment  might  prevent  your  company

from accepting a job. Your company should not incur any penalties or fees, unless

the crew is dedicated with a financial commitment from client.



Standby charges. The rates for standby of crew and equipment should clearly be

set, along with the free allowance when applicable. When possible, standby should

not be discounted.



Delays. When caused by weather, client-related delays or inefficiencies, or other

causes beyond your company’s control, demurrage should be recharged, with the

same administration fee as for reimbursables.



Volume of work changes. In a tender scope of work, the mention of an expected

volume of work is not binding. Therefore, the discount structure should be tiered

to allow for a reversal of high discounts conceded in exchange for large volumes of

work or high market share as discussed earlier in the volume of work section.



Product and services changes.  Similarly, effective product and service mixes might

significantly differ from what was originally stated in the tender. To avoid margin

erosion caused by a bias towards low-margin jobs, the pricing structure should allow

for adjustment as necessary and discussed in the product and services section.



Minimum monthly based revenue.  The provision of dedicated resources (equip-

ment and/or personnel) that the client expects to be available on demand should

be charged with a guaranteed minimum revenue per month.



412



CHAPTER 10  Competitive Bidding



Cancellations. Although present in the price catalogues, it is advisable to reem-

phasize in the contract the charges for cancellations.



Table 10.10 is a summary of the tender document assessment, which includes a

brief description of each factor and what conditions will have positive, negative,

or neutral effects on your bid strategy.



The customer will normally schedule a meeting for bidders to ask clarifications

about the tender document after the bidders have had sufficient time to review

the tender document. For this meeting it is absolutely critical the bid team has

completed the tender document assessment. The clarification process allows the

bid team a very good opportunity to position themselves competitively in prepara-

tion for submitting their bid. Clarifications are like a game of chess—if you play

as described below.



Clarifications



In  the  tendering  process,  the  customer  will  generally  have  two  clarification

meetings:



•  The first occurs after the bidders receive the tender, normally after suffi-

cient time to allow the bidders to review the tender and prepare questions

regarding  the  tender  document,  award  process,  background  information,

and  timing.  These  clarification  meetings  can  be  held  in  various  formats:

formal  meetings,  teleconferences,  web  casts,  e-mails  etc.  In  most  cases,

after the clarification phase has ended the customer will send a summary

of the clarification questions and answers to all bidders.



•  The second time is after the customer receives the bids back from the bid-

ders and wants to clarify points in the bidders’ submission. The customer’s

clarification  typically  relate  to  the  technical  aspects  of  the  offer  and  to

clarify  the  pricing  applicability.  These  clarification  meetings  are  in  most

cases held one-on-one with the individual bidders in person, via teleconfer-

ences, or by e-mail.



Bidder’s Clarifications



There are three guidelines for bidder’s clarifications.



•  Do not ask a question you do not know the answer to.



•  Clarify to position against the competition.



•  If you must ask questions, ask last.



In  general  most  bidding  experts  will  say  not  to  ask  clarification  questions8

because  the  other  competitors  will  either  hear  your  question  and  answer  or  be

sent a copy.  Your hard work to carefully read and uncover ambiguity in the tender

document  should  not  be  shared  with  competitors.  In  competitive  bidding,  your

objective is to beat the competition.



Do Not Ask A Question in Which You Do Not Know The Answer To



As mentioned earlier, in preparation for receiving a tender you should engage with

the customer’s team well before the procurement phase. During the evaluation of

options, you should review a previous tender and prepare clarification questions

regarding that tender and if this tender will apply for the upcoming work. If you

do have questions regarding the new tender, use the other means listed below to

address your question.



Add a note to the bid. Rather than asking a clarification question, interpret the

tender clause, add a note to your bid, and clarify your interpretation of the clause.



Smart Business People

Factor



Description



Positive



Influence



Negative



Neutral



Depending on your competitive and cost position relative to your competition and the customer’s preferred

supplier relationship, the Type of Tender can be a positive, negative or neutral. Knowing the Type of Tender is

required in order to know how to prepare a bid response when issues are discovered in the tender.



What is the type of tender and how will it be

evaluated?



Type 1 Enterprise: Most economically advanta-

geous offer wins.



Type 2 Consultative: Best evaluation wins.



Type 3 Transactional: Cheapest price wins.

How accurate is the volume of work the cus-

tomer is describing in the tender? Is it over- or

underestimated or accurate?



For the products and services requested in the

scope of work, are there any services that are

extraneous, omitted, or implied?



How will the customer award the work? Will it be

a single supplier, split or call-off?



What are the specifications of the products and

services requested in the tender document? Are

the specifications over, under or accurate?



Customer treast your added-value products and

services as a commodity.



Most companies accept reasonable, contractual risks

associated with its operations. However, onerous liability

provisions proposed by clients cannot be accounted for by

pricing levels or mitigated by risk financing programs. Such

liabilities are disproportionate to the value of the contracts

in question and may represent a material liability to your

company and, in extreme cases, unlimited liability. Most

companies accept risks that they can manage and control.
