Type 1

Generally better for everyone

involved as there is a much more

open discussion and dialogue

on how to ensure that both

parties win. Type 1 will generally

favor larger, more diversified

companies.



Underestimated

Can provide the bidder an ad-

vantage over other competitors

who do not discover the scope of

work is underestimated.



Omitted

Omitted products or services from

a bidders’ perspective are positive.

This allows you to have lower

costs for the scope of work, and

you are confident the customer

will run the omitted products or

services once the project starts.



Extraneous

Extraneous products or services

are also positive for the bidders. If

you recognize this, you can include

the extraneous items as part of the

package at no cost. This allows

you to charge more for the services

that will be run but still keep the

overall evaluated price the same.



Single Supplier or 100%

Award

The best situation provided the

bidder can execute all the scope

of the work.



Under-specified

Can bid aggressively on the

under-specified products and

services sell up to better margin

products and services during

implementation of the project.



Clarification – Yes

Use a clarification question to

confirm suppliers not having an

equivalent of your added-value

product or service will be dis-

qualified. The best situation is the

customer replies yes, and then

you make sole award contract.



No Contract Risks

Contract terms and conditions

are compliant with suppliers’

contracting standard.



Type 2

Is better if the supplier has unique

value-added products or services.

Strong inside customer support

can play a significant role in Type

2 Tenders as mentors can argue

the case to use suppliers they are

aligned with.



Accurate

The scope of work is neutral to

all bidders. However, an accurate

scope of work is rare.



Accurate

Requested product and services

are accurate and, therefore, neutral

to all bidders. However, an accurate

scope of work is rare.



Type 3

Is better for companies that have

a cost advantage over their com-

petitors. However, companies that

compete on price must be aware

competitors with wider product and

service offerings may try to beat the

low cost provider by using a 2-stage

sales strategy of frontal price during

tendering and flanking and selling

up to their more profitable services

during the implemetation stage of

the project. Type 3 Tenders normally

have the least margin of the three

types of tenders.



Overestimated

Is risky when responding, especially

if one of the bidders does not rec-

ognize the situation and bids at very

low margins, expecting the volume

to create an acceptable cash flow.



Implied

These are items that are not

requested in the scope of work, but

it is implied the bidder will provide

at no cost to the customer. Of the

three scenarios discussed in this

section, the implicit scenario can

be the most contentious as the

customer assumes you will provide

the product or service without

charging.



2nd Supplier or Call-Off Contract

No commitment is made by the

customer. Bid team must ensure

minimum notice for mobilization or

delivery of products and no penalties

if available.



Overspecified

Will need to use one of the overspec-

ified responses of define, replace, or

version. This is negative as there is

always a chance of being disqualified

for not pricing specifically what was

in the tender.



Clarification – No

The customer replies that the

competitors will not be asked to

provide a similar product or service.

You then respond using one of the

overspecified responses of define,

replace, or version. This is negative

as there is always a chance of being

disqualified for not pricing specifi-

cally what was in the tender.



High Contract Risks

One or more clauses that will need

management exemptions if your com-

pany is to continue with the contract.

You will file an exception with your

offer and re-word the non-compliant

terms and risk being disqualified.



High Business Risks

One or more clauses that will need

management exemptions if your com-

pany is to continue with the contract.

You will file an exception with your

offer and re-word the non-compliant

terms and risk being disqualified.



Split Award

Bid team must ensure tiered pricing

based on volume work. Must not

assume 100% award.



Accurate

Accurate specifications are neutral

for all bidders provided they have

offerings that meet the customer’s

specifications.



No Commoditization



Acceptable Contract Risks

Any risks can be mitigated or ad-

ditional charges added to make the

additional risk acceptable.



Acceptable Business Risks

Any risks can be mitigated or ad-

ditional charges added to make the

additional risk acceptable.



Business and post-award risks are commercial

risks in client contracts that may affet your rev-

enue flow, such as penalties, liquidated damages,

standby charges, currency fluctuations and your

costs.



No Business Risks

Business terms and conditions

are compliant with suppliers’

contracting standard.



Table 10.10

Tender document assessment summary.



414



CHAPTER 10  Competitive Bidding















Most common clarifications deal with the customer’s specific requirements and

how  this  affects  pricing.  In  this  case,  add  a  note  to  your  pricing  tables.  This  is

directly  applicable  for  when  you  discover  any  of  the  tender  assessment  issues

dealing with the following:



•  Scope of work: overestimated, underestimated, and award



•  Product and services: extraneous, omitted, and implied



•  Specifications: under-specified and overspecified



•  Risk: contract and business risks



Never clarify any of the issues you discover. The only exception to the above is

if  you  believe  that  by  asking  the  clarification  question,  you  gain  a  competitive

advantage, such as when certain products or services are implied in the tender

but  not  specifically  stated  in  the  tender  document.  In  this  case  you  know  your

competitor cannot provide the products or services without incurring extra costs

that you will not  incur. For example, if it is implied the bidder will have back-up

equipment  for  the  main  operation  but  you  know  the  competition  does  not  have

the back-up equipment and you do, then you would clarify if back-up equipment

is needed. The customer will answer that back-up is needed, and now the compe-

tition must provide the back-up equipment at an extra cost to the competition and

customer.



Early in this chapter it was discussed how to use the clarification meeting to dis-

qualify a competitor when the customer was treating your unique product or ser-

vices as a commodity.  In this situation, if the customer answers “no” or “yes,” you

are in a better position to respond to their tender.



Submit an alternative. In a situation where the tender is possibly poorly written,

respond to the tender by ensuring that all ambiguities are covered with notes and

prices have been adjusted to protect your profitability. Then submit an alternative

and explain to the customer that your alternative is technically better and finan-

cially advantageous for them.



Review  previous  clarification  minutes. Check to see if your question has been

answered before or how your bid team members handled the issue in an earlier

tender.



Ask a supporter or mentor privately. In most cases you will still have access to your

supporters and mentors. You can ask them to clarify the tender issue for you.



Ask your question after the clarification meeting notes have been sent. If you

must ask a clarification question, then ask it after the customer has sent out the

minutes to the clarification meeting. The customer might not be happy, but it is

still better than helping the competition. In this situation, wait until you receive

the summary from the clarification meeting. You can always say the question came

to you after you saw the list of clarification questions. If you ask immediately after

the  clarification  meeting,  then  the  customer  most  likely  will  include  it  in  the

minutes of the meeting sent to everyone.



Clarify to Position Against the Competition



Any clarification question should be analyzed as to how you want the competition

to use the information. You can use clarification questions to mislead the competi-

tion and highlight their weaknesses. An example of misleading the competition is

when you want the competition to think you do not have the required equipment

in country for the tender. Your clarification question would be something like:



Salesperson:



“Can the bidder have the company’s customs department clear

equipment into the country for the project?”



Smart Business People



415





The expected answer from the customer will be:



Customer:



“No.  Bidders  are  responsible  for  custom’s  clearance  of  any

equipment into the country.”



The purpose of the question is to lead the competitors to believe you do not have

equipment  in  country  and,  therefore,  they  will  increase  their  prices,  making  it

easier for you to win.



To highlight the competition’s weakness, ask questions to clarify if the customer

will  give  preference  to  advantages  your  company  provides  and  the  competition

does not. For example, you might ask whether the customer will give preference

to companies with operating or manufacturing facilities in country. In this case,

you  have  a  facility  in  country,  but  the  competition  does  not.  You  know  the  cus-

tomer will say no. This is what you want the customer to say so the competition

will not be concerned and try to offset their apparent drawback by bidding lower.

The objective of asking this question is that it will make the customer aware of

your advantage and they will think about this afterwards. The benefit will be in

evaluations  that  are  close.  It  could  be  these  added  benefits  that  convince  the

customer to award the work to your company. If you do not know the answer or

how  the  customer  will  reply,  then  do  not  ask  the  question  before  you  find  the

answer outside of the clarification meeting.



If You Must Ask Questions, Ask Last



Ask  your  clarification  questions  last.  There  is  a  good  chance  other  bidders  will

have the same question, and you can let them ask; by waiting until the end of the

meeting, there is a good chance the answers to other clarification questions will

answer your question.



If you do not ask any clarification questions, the customer will not know if every-

thing is clear or you have not read the tender. You must ask several questions (e.g.,

on the meaning of abbreviations) to communicate to the customer that you have

read their document very closely. These types of questions demonstrate that you

have read the tender without passing any useful information to the competition.

Ensure that you demonstrate to the customer that you are excited about having

this opportunity to win their business.



The next clarification meeting will be after you have submitted the bid and the

customer has had time to review your offer. The customer clarification meeting

will discussed later in this chapter.



Bid Strategy Creation and Execution

Once the bid team has completed the review of the tender documents and con-

sidered  what  they  learned  from  the  clarification  meeting  they  will  build  a  bid

strategy.  The bid strategy considers what the sales team has been able to achieve

with  their  competitive  sales  strategy,  the  level  of  alignment  between  the  cus-

tomer’s buying center and your company, and the overall importance of this bid.

Fig. 10.7 shows the many factors influencing the bid strategy decision.



The  bid  team  will  present  their  bid  strategy  to  management  at  the  bid  strategy

review  meeting  for  their  feedback  and  approval.  Once  any  remaining  questions

from the bid strategy meeting are answered, the offer is locked and the bid written.



The bid strategy to be reviewed at the bid strategy review meeting consists of the

following:



•  Offer.  The  offer  includes  tender  assessment,  proposed  pricing,  terms  and



conditions, operational plan, and any required investments.



416



CHAPTER 10  Competitive Bidding



•  Proposal Strategy. What will be the key messages of the proposal? The proposal

should tell the story of why to choose your company and not the competition.

•  Proposal Outline. The purpose of the outline is to identify each part of the

proposal, what is required, who is assigned the responsibility of writing, and

the due date.



Offer



The offer is the critical part of the bid strategy. Influenced by all the factors shown

in Fig. 10.7, the offer clearly defines what your company will commit to providing

to the customer and at what price and terms and conditions. The offer meets the

customer’s  tender  requirements,  beats  the  competition,  and  achieves  your  tar-

geted financial objectives. The offer consists of three parts:



•  Deliverables.  What  will  you  sell  or  provide  to  the  customer  and  are  any



additional investments or other resource allocations needed?



•  Pricing. What is the the winning TTV?

•  Cash flow. When will the EV revenues occur?



Deliverables



The deliverables of the offer is what the company is committing to the customer.

This part of the offer is established by working closely with the operations team.

It is important to identify if the deliverables are standard products and services

that can be provided by the current infrastructure or whether there will be addi-

tional investments needed or reallocation of resources.



Pricing



Fig. 10.7

The bid strategy is a key milestone

in competitive bidding. There are many

factors that are required inputs to

create the best strategy.



For many managers, this section is the most important decision to be made for

the bid strategy. The first question the bid team must answer is what is the win-

ning TTV and what was their benchmark to establish the TTV? Depending on the

type  of  tender,  the  level  capabilities  of  your  competitors  and  level  of  rivalry



Smart Business People



417





between  you  and  your  competition  will  dictate  where  your  pricing  needs  to  be

relative to the competition. In some situations, to win you may need to bid a lower

price  than  your  targeted  margins  and  sell  more  once  awarded  the  tender.  The

strategy of  selling more is covered in the cash flow discussion of this section.



Your ability to identify correctly the level of pricing depends how accurate your

pricing benchmark is. This will be discussed in more detail in Chapter 11 “Pricing

for Bidding.” The more confidence management has in the benchmark, the more

confidence  they  will  have  in  the  bid  team’s  strategy.  When  management  is  not

confident  in  the  pricing  benchmark,  then  they  will  question  the  strategy  and

typically apply a safety factor to improve the chances of winning the work which

in most cases results in larger discounts.



Cash Flow



The offer must clearly identify where and when the EV revenues will be realized

and the associated costs. The cash flow must be completed in cooperation with

operations management. The operations team needs to take this information to

calculate the operating costs to provide the deliverables and calculate a financial

statement for the bid.



The source of your revenue is from the expected sales:



•  Products and services as requested in the tender scope of work and adjusted



using the bid team’s analysis of the tender document.



•  Additional  product  and  services  not  identified  in  the  tender  document

analysis. For example, the salesperson believes the customer will buy a new

product or higher level of service once the contract is awarded that was not

requested  in  the  tender  document.  Most  companies  have  a  name  for  this

type of sales: up-selling or cross-selling. Many times the owner of the oppor-

tunity  will  identify  this  possibility  during  the  opportunities  management

process and will execute a flanking competitive sales strategy. The various

sales strategies and how these relate to the different types of tenders was

discussed earlier in this chapter and in depth in Chapter 4 “Opportunities

Management” and Chapter 9 “Strategic Sales Plan.”



One  of  the  key  decisions  for  the  offer  is  the  level  of  discounting  that  will  be

required  to  beat  the  competition  and  secure  the  work  while  at  the  same  time

achieving your company’s financial objectives. This one decision impacts both the

TTV and EV. A tool to help you optimize your pricing is Solver, an add-in for Excel.

With Solver, when there is a tender factor such as the scope of work that is over-

stated or understated, you can optimize discounting, leveraging the discrepancy

between the actual scope of work and what you believe the real scope of work. By

using Solver you will be able to identify the correct level of discounting to give you

the lowest evaluated TTV and highest level of EV.



Using the example from Chapter Highlight 10.3: I’m 90% Confident shown in Table

10.11, the bid team for this example analyzed their pricing from the last twelve

months of winning tenders and used the average of the lowest and highest prices



Table 10.11

Tender analysis with minimum

and maximum pricing from win-

ning tenders added for the

requested products and services.



418



CHAPTER 10  Competitive Bidding



(shown in the far right-hand side of the table) for products A, B, C, and technician

days to calculate the following:



•  TTV of $4,930,000



•  EV of $5,310,000



•  P90 value of $4,443,274



This level of pricing was satisfactory as the pricing achieves the targeted level of

profit for this tender. The question is can the team do better by optimizing their

pricing and discounting levels?



Figure 10.8 shows the settings using Solver. The Solver Parameters screen, on the

far  left  of  the  figure,  shows  the  constraints  set  by  the  bid  team.  In  this  case,  the

objective was set to maximize EV highlighted in green. The program will run many

iterations to identify what values tested in the orange cells will give the highest value

of EV. The values in orange cells changed within the constraints of the Solver price

becoming greater than the minimum price but less than the maximum winning price,

and the bid offer TTV must be equal to $4,930,000. The team could have chosen to

make the Solver TTV less than $4,930,000, but that would not be recommended as

the team’s benchmarking analysis identified $4,930,000 as the winning TTV. In com-

petitive  bidding  you  want  to  be  at  the  target  but  no  lower  than  necessary,  as  the

lower you go the riskier it is for you to achieve your targeted profitability levels. Once

the  constraints  have  been  set,  clicking  the  Solve  button  in  Excel  will  provide  the

values for the orange cells that satisfy the constraints. In this example, the bid team

was able to optimize the pricing to increase the revenue by $610,000 or 10.3%.



However, as discussed in the same Chapter Highlight 10.3: I’m 90% Confident, the

EV of $5,310,000 is only one number out of many possible scenarios. Now that the

revenue  table  and  model  are  already  built,  it  is  easy  to  run  the  Monte  Carlo

analysis  to  have  a  more  robust  analysis  of  the  optimized  pricing.  Table  10.12

shows  the  EV  of  the  optimized  pricing  P90  value  increases  from  $4,443,274  to

$4,834,463, or 8.1%, which is significant. As a professional salesperson, this should

motivate you to analyze what the customer has requested in their tender. Discrep-

ancies from the real scope of work indicate gaps where you can gain a competitive

advantage. From a customer’s perspective, they see nothing different from what

you bid to what is charged during the project. Everyone is happy:



•  Customer has a low TTV.



Fig. 10.8

Solver constraint settings to

optimize pricing.



•  You win the work and make your bonus.

•  Your company management will make their revenue profit objectives.



Smart Business People



419





Table 10.12

Monte Carlo results for average pricing

and Solver optimized pricing.



All  the  tender  assessment  efforts  and  supporting  actions  will  add  percentage

points to the bottom line. The sidebar, the “Power of One” demonstrates what the

impact of reducing discounts by just 1% has on profitability. Every person on the

bid team needs to understand how significant each pricing decision for a bid has

on the company’s success. You want each person on the bid team to be thinking

like a smart business person.



Presenting Bid Strategy Information and Tender Document Assessment



The tables containing the bid strategy information (market factors, pipeline analy-

sis,  and  opportunity  assessment)  and  Table  10.10  Tender  Document  Assessment

Summary are presented in the bid strategy review meeting to support the bid strat-

egy discussion. Figure 10.9 is an example of how this information is presented.



The top left part of the page has a summary of the tender analysis, and the top

right part shows the offer summary including the Monte Carlo analysis for reve-



u	The Power of One 7



Pricing right is the fastest and most effective way for

managers to increase profits. Consider the average

income statement of an S&P 1500 company: A price

increase of 1%, if volumes remained stable, would

generate an 8% increase in operating profits—an

impact nearly 50% greater than that of a 1% fall in vari-

able costs, such as materials and direct labor, and more

than three times greater than the impact of a 1%

increase in volume.



Unfortunately, the sword of pricing cuts both ways.

A decrease of 1% in average prices has the opposite

effect, bringing down operating profits by that same

8%, if other factors remain steady.

Chapter Highlight 10.5: Made in the Shade



Presenting the tender analysis on one page can help immensely in understanding what the chances of securing the

tender and achieving your targeted profitability are. The tender analysis worksheet has been designed to show the

factors of market intelligence, pipeline analysis, opportunity assessment, and tender analysis. Each factor has three

options when evaluating:



Level 1 Green Positive: “Made in the

Shade.” This tender is yours; go relax in

the shade.



Level  2 Yellow  Neutral:  “Slowly  But

Surely.”  There  a  few  things  to  done

before you are a winner.



Level  3  Red  Negative:  “Damn  Near

Impossible.” You feel like the guy in jail with

the ice bag. Maybe this is a good time to

disengage and let the competition win?



The results from the analysis worksheet are presented on one page as shown in Fig. 10.9, and the factors are listed

in Tables 10.1. 10.2, and 10.8. The spider diagram plots all the factors according to the assessment; the more factors

around the center, the more confident the team should be to win the tender at their targeted profit. Shown below

are three real examples of spider diagrams for real tenders—from Thailand, Alaska, and Alberta. All were large B2B

contracts for two years and worth more than $1 million each.



Alaska

This analysis is for Company A and shows 13 of the

23 factors are at level 1 with only four at the level

3. This is truly a Level 1 “Made in the Shade” ten-

der of which the bid team was able to secure even

though  it  was  strategic  with  for  the  competition.

Because Company A was the incumbent, they had

a very good understanding of the customer’s busi-

ness  and  strong  relationships  as  shown  by  the

“Can You Win” being assessed as "Strong.”



Alberta



In this example, there are fewer level 1 factors and

more level 2 factors. The sales team for Company A

in this example did win. It was a very tough battle.

What the bid team did was leverage that the timing

was good, and the company had available resources.

As a result, the bid team bid slightly less than market

rate and secured the opportunity. They made their

targeted  profitability  by  not  including  the  implied

services  in  their  offer  price. This  allowed  them  to

bid slightly less than the competition and capture

the work.



Implied Services



Omitted Services



Extraneous Services



Overstated

Work



Understated

Work



422



CHAPTER 10  Competitive Bidding





Chapter Highlight 10.5: Made in the Shade (cont.)



Thailand

In this situation, there are a lot more level 3 assess-

ments (twelve level 3, six level 2, and five level 1)

than  the  first  two  examples. This  is  “Damn  Near

Impossible.”  This  analysis  was  reviewed  at

Company A’s regional office in Kuala Lumpur. The

black dots highlight some of the very critical fac-

tors.  First,  the  timing  was  not  good  because

immediately after this tender was another major

tender,  but  next  time  it  would  be  in  Malaysia

where  Company  A  was  incumbent.  Next  the

competitor  had  very  good  inside  support  from

the  customer’s  buying  center  members.  The

other  significant  factor  was  the  market  condi-

tion’s excess capacity, meaning if either Company

A or the competitor lost the work their people and

equipment would not be used elsewhere. Company

A’s  management,  however,  was  very  eager  to  win

this tender because the reported profitability the com-

petitor  was  earning  was  very  good  (highlighted  by  the

green dot). The award was to a single supplier and the busi-

ness risks were low as the customer chose to work in a win–win



Implied Services



Omitted Services



Extraneous Services



Overstated

Work



Understated

Work



approach with their contractors and the contracting policy was fair.



What happened was Company A tried to win by submitting a very low price, much lower than the market rate, so

the customer would not say no to them. When the customer received the very low price, they were surprised. The

customer asked the competitor to match Company A’s price or they would be forced to award to Company A. The

competitor did match Company A’s price and awarded the work. Company A lost, but this is not the worst part.  The

competitor now believed the price they were shown was the new market rate and for the next bid in Malaysia they

dropped their prices, not wanting to lose. In this case, Company A was asked to price match the much lower prices

of the competitor and they did. The unfortunate part of this is both companies ended up with the same amount of

work but now at much less profit. Company’s A analysis confirmed they could win the first two and would lose the

third tender. Their analysis told them to disengage from the third example, but they chose to “try to win,” which was

not very smart. Professional salespeople use these tools to make smart business people decisions.



nue and profitability. On the bottom of the summary is a radar graph that visually

shows of how difficult  it will be to win the tender. Chapter Highlight 10.5: Made

in the Shade explains how to interpret the results. The worksheet to enter all the

bid  strategy  factors  and  create  the  presentation  format  shown  in  Fig.  10.9  is

available for download from the textbook website.



Offer Summary—Revenue and Profit Projections



In  this  section  of  the  analysis  worksheet,  you  enter  your  revenue  and  profit

analysis for the tender. This section is completed with input from the bid team

member from operations.



Market Rate for Scope of Work. What is the market rate for the scope of work?

What will your most serious competitor submit for their offer? This is based on

your benchmark analysis and how the competitor will evaluate the tender. Will

they make the same assessment as you? Do you think they will identify extrane-

ous, omitted, and implied services? Will they interpret specifications differently

from you?  Will they expect to up-sell? What will their offer consist of for products,

services, and other deliverables?  The market rate of your most serious competi-



Smart Business People



423





tor will not only depend on what they are going to sell, but also what their costs

will be to service the work. All these factors need to considered as part of your

competitive analysis and benchmarking exercise.



Bid Evaluation Target (TTV). Depending on the type of tender, what you submit

as an offer and what you invoice can be different. In this cell, you enter what you

expect the customer to evaluate as your total offer price. This value is extremely

important if the customer wants to negotiate prices. Before starting the negotia-

tion, you should first confirm what your offer was evaluated at. If it is not what

you expected, then before starting to negotiate you need to understand where the

difference  is  coming  from.  You  discover  this  information  in  the  “Resolution  of

Concerns” procurement stage after you submit your proposal. There will be more

discussion on negotiations in Chapter 12 “Collaborative Negotiations.”



TTV Relative to Market. Where is your TTV compared to the market rate above,

the same or below. This cell is a visual flag. The color coding for above is red, same

is yellow, and below is green.  If a Type 3 Transactional Tender, the flag needs to

be  green.    If  not,  you  will  need  to  negotiate  to  win  the  work.    For  a  Type  2

Consultative  Tender,  it  is  okay  to  be  more  expensive  as  long  as  your  offer  is

within the acceptable premium allowed by the customer’s evaluation model.  As

a guideline for Type 2 Consultative Tenders, you need to be within 10% of L1; if

more than 10%, you will need to negotiate after you submit your offer.



TTV Gross Profit $: Gross profit is a finance term used by management to deter-

mine the profitability of a tender. The gross profit margin is how much cash the

opportunity will generate in the execution stage. To calculate the gross profit, you

need to know the TTV gross sales and the incremental cost of goods or services sold.



TTV Gross Profit $ = TTV Sales – Incremental Costs



In Chapter 11 “Pricing for Bids,” there will be much more detail on the financial

indicators discussed in this section.



TTV Net Profit %: The net profit of a tender is the difference between TTV sales

and  total  expenses  divided  by  TTV  sales.  Total  expenses  includes  in  addition  to

incremental costs, overhead, interest payments, and taxes. The net profit margin is

the amount of profit a business receives for each unit of sales. For example, if Net

Profit % is 10%, it means for every dollar of sales the company has ten cents left

over to grow the company or give a portion of the ten cents back to shareholders.



TTV Net Profit Target % = TTV Sales – TTV Total Expenses / TTV Sales



Expected  Value  (EV)  Revenue  Target:  This  is  the  bid  teams  estimate  for  the

expected revenue. This  should be supported with a summary analysis identifying

the revenue components. Revenue components can vary from tender to tender,

but should include as a minimum:



•  Base sales. What the customer has clearly requested in the tender.

•  Additional sales. Revenues resulting from identified tender issues such as

underestimated  scope  of  work,  omitted  or  implied  products  and  services,

under-specified product and services.



•  Sell-ups.  Additional  revenue  not  identified  in  the  tender.  The  sales  team

believes they can sell higher level products and services based on the cus-

tomer’s needs.



•  Cross-selling. Additional revenue from selling complimentary products and



sales not included in the tender and not already included in the EV.

The bid team will also need to explain differences between TTV and EV when EV

is  lower  than  TTV  due  to  such  tender  issues  as  overestimated  scope  of  work,

extraneous  products  and  services,  overspecified  products  and  services,  and



424



CHAPTER 10  Competitive Bidding



awards.  As  mentioned  earlier,  the  timing  of  revenue  flows  is  very  important.

Irregular revenue flows and how these impact cash flows need to be explained.



The EV revenue target factor has an associated Monte Carlo analysis, as discussed

earlier in this chapter. The Monte Carlo analysis provides a measure of the risks and

uncertainty  for  the  tender.  After  completing  the  Monte  Carlo  analysis,  enter  the

P90, P50, and P10 EV revenue projections. These factors will influence how manage-

ment views the attractiveness of the opportunity compared to other opportunities.

EV Gross Profit Target $: This is the same calculation as TTV gross profit except

for this factor you use the EV rather than the TTV revenues and  the incremental

costs associated with the EV sales. This is the gross profit the bid team is expect-

ing from this tender.



EV Gross Profit Target $ = EV Sales – Incremental Costs.



EV Net Profit Target %: This is the same calculation as TTV net profit except for this

factor you use the EV sales rather than the TTV sales and  the total costs associated

with the EV sales. This is the net profit the bid team is expecting from this tender.



EV Net Profit Target $ = EV Sales – EV Total Costs / EV Sales



The EV net profit target also has a Monte Carlo analysis values for P90, P50, and

P10 EV net profit projections. The EV revenue and net profit are what most com-

panies  use  when  reviewing  bids  to  decide  to  either  disengage  or  continue  and

submit a winning bid. The final offer can be what the bid team recommends or it

can be the bid team offer with adjustments from management.



Offer Summary–Strategy



In this section of the analysis worksheet, you enter your strategy summary.



Type of Tender: What approach and decision criteria will the people in the cus-

tomer’s buying center use to select their supplier? In particular, at this stage of

the opportunity, you should be focusing on the decision-makers and approvers.

Will they treat this tender like a:



•  Type 1 Enterprise Tender. The best overall solution and support.

•  Type 2 Consultative Tender. Best value for money spent and within their



premium allowed over L1.



•  Type 3 Transaction Tender. L1 wins.



To  support  your  analysis,  you  should  also  be  prepared  to  present  the  business  and

personal priorities of the people in the buying center and the formal decision criteria.



Strategy Description: What is your competitive sales strategy to secure this project?



•  Frontal. Are you telling the buying center you have the absolute best solu-



tion given the formal decision criteria?



•  Flanking. Are you  going to sell something different either as an alternative

proposal or after you win? This strategy is commonly known as “bid low and

sell up.”  This is common in Type 3 Transaction Tenders.

•  Fragment. Your offer is for only a portion of the project.

•  Defend. You know you will be evaluated as weak using the formal decision

criteria and are depending on the customers in the buying center who are

your supporters and mentors to defend the decision to award to you. This

is a common strategy for Type 3 Transactional Tenders when you are com-

peting  against  low  cost  competitors  who  will  be  cheaper,  but  you  have  a

portfolio of  value-added products and services the customer needs.



Smart Business People



425





It is important your competitive sales strategy and financial analysis match. For

example  if  you  are  using  a  fragment  strategy,  then  your  TTV  and  EV  estimates

need to reflect the financial for the portion of the work that you are targeting and

not the entire scope of work as described in the tender.



Negotiation  Position. Overall, when all factors are considered how would rate

your negotiation position. Do you have trades that will allow you to negotiate a

win-win agreement. The options are:



•  Strong.  You  have  several  trades  the  customer  wants  that  you  can  use  to



close the deal.



•  Limited. You have a few trades to close the deal, and



•  Weak.  You  do  not  have  sufficient  trades  or  support  within  in  the  buying



center to close the deal.



The topic of negotiations will be discussed in depth in Chapter 12 “Collaborative

Negotiations.”



Offer Summary—Critical Success Factors (CSF’s)



In this section of the analysis worksheet, you enter CSFs grouped by tactics, rela-

tionship strategies, or other actions that need to happen in order to secure the

project and achieve your financial objectives.



Tactics. What are your key tactics that are planned for this bid during the remain-

ing procurement phases? If you expect to negotiate you must include tactics to

support  your  negotiation  strategy.  If  your  strategy  includes  critical  items  that

must occur in the implementation, these should also be presented. Such tactics

might be needed in the implementation stage to:



•  Sell omitted, implied, or services that were under specification in the tender.



•  If your strategy to make your EV and EV net profit depend on you up-selling



or cross-selling, then it is important this be identified.



Each  tactic  is  a  sales  call  and  must  have  each  of  the  SMART  components  as

described in Chapter 7 “Sales Call Skills.”



Relationship Strategies. If you have a tactic identified how would you categorize

the relationship strategy for your tactics? Are you trying to leverage, motivate, or

neutralize someone?



Other  If  there  are  other  actions  needed  that  are  not  a  sales  call  then  identify

what the action is.



Comment  Boxes.  There  are  comment  boxes  for  the  offer  summary  and  overall

comments.  This is where you can add additional details to describe or explain

your analysis.



For  a  real  examples  of  bid  strategy  presentations,  see  Chapter  Highlight  10.4:

Made in the Shade.



After the bid strategy review meeting, the offer is locked and the final phase of

writing and reviews is completed as described in Chapter 6 “Sales Activities.”



Present  Proposals.  Once  the  bid  has  been  submitted  most  people  in  the  cus-

tomer’s buying center will assign sections to different reviewers and evaluators.

After the customer has had sufficient time to complete their reviews, then the

salesperson  visits  the  customer  as  per  the  opportunities  management  stage

“Resolution of Concerns.” At this time the salesperson wants to ensure that there

are no questions or concerns regarding their bid submission. Also at this time the

salesperson needs to confirm the price the customer evaluated for their offer. If

there are any discrepancies between what the bid team expected to be evaluated



426



CHAPTER 10  Competitive Bidding



at and how the customer evaluated the offer, the salesperson needs to understand

the  discrepancy  and  try  to  resolve  the  differences.  At  this  time  or  as  part  of  a

more formal tendering process of the customer, there can be a clarification meet-

ing with the bidders.



Customer Clarifications



Customer clarifications happen after they have reviewed your bid.  The main issue

here is there are genuine clarification questions that you should be prepared to

answer. However, in many situations the customer clarifications are the first round

of negotiations. In some companies, the tender team will only bring to a clarifica-

tion meeting the bidders being seriously considered (i.e., the “short list” compa-

nies, one of whom will be awarded the work). The difference between clarification

questions from the customer and negotiations is, in a clarification question, you

can answer without impacting your profitability or risk exposure. For example, a

legitimate clarification question is:



Customer:



“How many engineers are you including at the job site?”



Salesperson:



“Two.”



Keep  your  answers  short,  and  answer  only  the  question  being  asked.  If  the

customer wants more information they will ask another clarification question.

The  same  question  as  part  of  negotiations  is,  after  your  answer  of  “Two,”  the

customer asks:



Customer:



“Are these both senior engineers?”



Salesperson:



“No, one senior and one junior.”



Customer:



“We need two senior engineers. The other companies are

providing  senior  engineers,  and  you  must  also  provide  the

same.”



If the clarification question results in your profitability dropping or risk exposure

increasing, then this is considered negotiations. If you discover you might lose the

bid, this could be a time for you to negotiate or make an alternative offer. Both of

these  issues  and  negotiations  will  be  discussed  in  depth  in  Chapter  12  “Collab-

orative Negotiations.”



Clarification Meeting Best Practice



There  are  several  best  practices  for  both  bidder  and  customer  clarification

meetings:8



•  Always have two people at the clarification meeting: one person observing



and taking notes, and the other asking the questions.



•  Whenever the customer asks you a clarification question, probe to under-

stand their question, acknowledge by paraphrasing their question, and then

answer their question. After answering check with the customer to confirm

that their question has been answered.



•  Arrive early and depart last.



•  After the clarification meeting, if in the customer’s office, schedule a meet-

ing with your mentor or supporter to update them about the meeting and

ask for their feedback.



•  If clarifications meetings are scheduled with companies individually, ask to

go last. You will learn the most by going last, either for your bidder clarifica-

tion meeting or the customer’s clarification meeting.



Smart Business People



427





Post Award Contract Handoff

Once the work has been awarded and the contract signed, the bid team should

hold a handoff meeting to pass the signed contract to the operations team who

will execute and invoice the work. At this meeting, the bid team should clearly

document and explain the bid strategy and details of how items and specific situ-

ations  are  to  be  invoiced.  The  handover  meeting  should  include  the  person

assigned as the contract owner, who will have the responsibility to manage and

track  the  performance  of  the  contract  over  the  contract  period  and  arrange  a

mid-project  contract  review  to  audit  the  contract  performance.  The  purpose  of

these reviews is to compare actual invoiced revenues against expected revenues

and understand any differences, both good and bad. At this review, there should

be a detailed audit organized by the contract owner with people from the original

bid team, operations, and accounts receivables staff. The deliverable should be a

detailed  report  identifying  best  practices,  revenue  leakage,  and  recoverable

actions for the unrecovered monies. For a real example of the value of mid-con-

tract review audits, see the sidebar “That’s Another Business Unit.”



At the end of the project, the contract owner should create a report, including:



•  Actual performance total revenue and profitability.



•  How  much  revenue  was  invoiced  from  sources  identified  in  the  tender

assessment, such as scope of work being under stated, omitted, and implied

and under-specified products and services?



•  How much revenue was invoiced from up-selling and cross-selling and were



the expected up-selling and cross-selling levels achieved?



•  What percentage of the scope of work was completed?



•  Were  there  any  significant  issues  affecting  revenue  and  profitability  that

were  not  identified  during  the  tender  assessment  but  were  in  the  mid-

contract review?



This  analysis  should  be  done  and  reviewed  with  members  of  the  bid  team  and

filed with the end-of-project review described in Chapter 4 “Opportunities Man-

agement.”



u	That’s Another

Business Unit



A large multi-national company’s

Latin American division had

annual revenues of approxi-

mately $3,000,000,000 per

year. The executive, at the rec-

ommendation of the contracts

manager, decided to complete

detailed audits of the largest

contracts from each country in

its division. It was a huge

undertaking with large commit-

ments of people and time from

the contracts department,

sales, finance, and operations.

At the end of the initiative, the

company discovered some

shocking numbers. The good

news they found:



•  $80,000,000 of recover-

able revenues and third-

party charges that had not

been charged as per their

contracts.



•  Significant revenues that

were not recoverable and

were considered lost or

what some companies

refer to as red money.



Their recommendations were to

change existing procedures and

future contracts so that almost

none of the red money would

happen again. In the meantime,

they began to collect

$80,000,000.



When the president of the divi-

sion received the report, he

was thrilled with the impact

that recovering $80,000,000

would have on his profitability

because money was, as he

called it, “pure profit.” His divi-

sion consisted of five major

business units, and $80,000,000

profit in his words would be

“another business unit.”



Mid-contract reviews became a

standard practice.



428



CHAPTER 10  Competitive Bidding



Summary of Chapter Objectives



In this chapter, you have seen the competitive bidding methodology, processes, and tools. These will allow you to manage

effectively the tender workflow, assess a tender document, prepare a bid strategy, determine what to expect in the bid

strategy  review  meeting,  and  identify  post-award  activities  to  manage  the  contract.  The  implications  of  this  task  are

significant for every company. The result can range from making a profit to risking the very existence of the company.



1. Know how to manage the tender workflow.



Managing the tender workflow is the key activity in the opportunities management stage: 3b, submission of the proposal.

A key to success is starting to prepare for this stage by collecting the information you need to during this stage. Infor-

mation for the bid submission and competitive information about the market conditions, your pipeline, and what you

have  been  able  to  achieve  with  your  competitive  sales  strategy  is  required  before  receiving  the  tender.  The  tender

assessment quality is significantly improved by leveraging the information gained from the earlier stages and under-

standing of the customer requirements. All this pre-tender preparation allows you to focus on the main task of complet-

ing the tender assessment. There are a lot of activities during this stage, and it is critical that the bid team be organized

and assign a team member to each one of the key dates in the tender timeline. Once you have received the tender,

there is a tender orientation with the core bid team. The time the bid team has to work on the bid has three stages.

The first stage dedicated to creating the bid strategy and having the bid strategy approved is the bid strategy review

meeting. After this meeting, the offer is locked, and the bid team writes the offer. Writing the offer is the bulk of the

work for preparing a bid. Writing the offer needs to be completed by a specific date to give the proposal reviewers time

to read the proposal like a customer and provide feedback to improve the proposal. Once the proposal is revised, it is

then packaged and submitted to the customer. There are two clarification meetings. The bid team clarifies any issues

they have with the customer’s tender before they start to write the offer. The customer will have a clarification meeting

with the bid team after the bid is submitted. An important part of managing the tender workflow is doing a midterm

contract audit of the contract performance to ensure that all invoicing is done correctly, and additional sales are taking

place as planned. If there are any post award issues, these are documented and reviewed with the bid team and man-

agement as part of the ongoing improvement process.



2. Perform a tender assessment.



To assess the tender in a thorough and complete manner, use a four-factor tender assessment model. The first factor is

the type of tender. Next is the accuracy of the scope of work consisting of the volume of work, products and services,

and  how  the  contract  is  awarded.  The  third  factor  is  specifications,  and  last  is  the  risks  both  in  contract  terms  and

business risks.



3. Know the components of the bid strategy.



After the tender assessment, the bid team prepares their bid strategy, covering the offer, proposal strategy, and proposal

outline. In the bid strategy, the bid team must provide the details of issues discovered during the tender assessment and

how these will be addressed with the pricing strategy. The bidding strategy should also include the negotiation strategy

and other key activities to manage the progress of the bid through the customer’s procurement phase and post-award

actions. The other key components of the bid strategy is the overall proposal strategy and the individual components of

the proposal.



4. Understand the key factors for a comprehensive bid strategy review.



There is a lot of information to review at the bid strategy review meeting. As discussed in Chapter 9 “Strategic Sales

Plan” this is not the first review for important opportunities. The first review was the Level 2 Strategic Sales Plan Review,

where you become familiar with the opportunity for the reviewing managers to focus more on the specifics of the offer.

The main tool for presenting this critical information is the bid strategy worksheet, which organizes the critical strategy

information and creates a one-page review document. The review document includes all the tender assessment factors,

bid strategy, and the P90, P50, P10 estimates of expected revenue and net profitability. Any documentation to support

the bid strategy is also presented. This can be information to support how the bid team determined what a winning total

tender value (TTV), is how the expected value (EV) revenues will be achieved, and support information for other critical

tender assessment factors.



Smart Business People

assumptions. Once you have completed your analysis, see note 4 in the references.

Star  Oil  requests  a  competitive  offer  for  standard  cementing  services  for  the  development  phase  of  the  Nirwana  Gas

Field, located five kilometers south of Cirebon, Indonesia. The requested cementing services are described in the techni-

cal specifications section of this RFP. Three companies, your company, Competitor B, and Competitor C, are being con-

sidered for this RFP. Proposals are to be returned via courier no later than 12:00 p.m. (noon) on January 4, 2011, to Star

Oil’s Houston office. Proposals received after this date and time will be considered non-compliant. This RFP includes five

sections:

3.  Pricing

The selected bidders will be required to sign the contract included in Section 4. Terms and Conditions.



Section 2: Scope of the Work



The project has a possible scope of 100 wells to be drilled and completed, all to a TD of 3,000 meters, with maximum pay

sections of 100 meters. Three cementing operations are required per well, as described in the costing worksheet. Bidders

are expected to have cementing services available 24/7, including an assigned support and design engineer dedicated to

the Nirwana project.

is to include the stated discount to be applied to the bidder’s 2010 cementing services catalog price. Note: The same

discount  will  be  used  for  services  requested  during  the  project,  but  not  included  in  the  scope  of  work.  Bidders  must

include their 2010 cementing services catalog as part of their RFP submission.

CHAPTER 10  Competitive Bidding







Pricing for Bidding



The Moment of Truth



Pricing for bidding is the moment of truth as all the marketing and sales strategies

come into focus during the pricing decision.1 Calculating the price of your com-

pany’s  products  and  services  is  never  simple.  Rather,  making  a  pricing  decision

derives from striking a strategic balance among winning, profit objectives, and the

development of customer relationships in a competitive marketplace. This chapter

examines the basic and advanced pricing models used to arrive at the best price

for the products and services offered in your bids or sales proposals.



As mentioned at the start of Chapter 10 “Competitive Bidding,” one of the main

strategies  of  B2B  purchasing  is  “the  more  you  buy,  the  better  the  price.”  Your

customer offers large scopes of work with the objective of securing better pricing

than if they made many smaller purchases. One of the fundamentals of B2B pric-

ing for large and small sales is if the customer wants the price for one item, they

should pay as per your published price catalog. The inference is every B2B sales

organization must have a published price catalog they can reference when offering

a better price to the customer. The published price catalog must be adjusted annu-

ally  to  compensate  for  increasing  costs,  the  introduction  of  new  products  and

services,  changes  in  market  segmentation,  and  competitive  pressures.  All  the

discussions in this chapter are based upon having a reference price catalog from

which you start the pricing decision. For the remainder of this chapter, when the

term price is used, it is referring to the offer price for a competitive bid.



The pricing decision is a team effort led by the person responsible for the profit

objective (usually the service delivery or operations manager), who is assisted by

the  core  bid  team  members.  However,  the  preparation  for  the  pricing  decision

starts  well  before  the  receipt  of  the  tender  with  the  sales  team  collecting  the

market information and executing a competitive sales strategy to be in the best

position to submit a winning offer. The team must choose which pricing model is



11



Chapter Objectives



After reading this chapter, you

will be able to:



n  Describe the basic  pricing



model.



n  Understand when to use the

different pricing strategies.



n  Describe the advanced pric-



ing model.



n	Understand the  selection



process for selecting incen-

tive  models for the advanced

pricing model.



Pricing for Bidding



435





most appropriate for the market condition to arrive at the bid-offer price, which

is adjusted to fit the bid strategy and targeted total tender value (TTV).  Typically,

the bid price is part of an offer package, which encompasses deliverables, as well

as  terms  and  conditions  based  on  a  given  set  of  circumstances,  as  described  in

the  scope  of  work.  Often,  customers  wish  to  negotiate  parts  of  the  offer,  so  in

completing the analyses for preparing a pricing offer, the team must also prepare

the foundation for a negotiations strategy, which is discussed in the next chapter.



Pricing Factors

The pricing decision carries significant consequences. With the ideal price, you

can win the contract, meet the profit objectives set by the organization, and con-

tinue to develop the customer relationship. With the wrong price, several things

can happen. If the customer perceives your price as too high, you lose the work.

If the price is set too low, you might win the work but reduce your profit margin,

weaken  the  customer  relationship,  and  possibly  impact  your  competitors’  future

pricing strategies.



Setting  the  right  price  begins  with  the  high-level  marketing  decisions  with

regard to the 4 P’s of the marketing mix, as discussed in Chapter 1 “Understand-

ing the Role of Marketing and Sales.” During the product development process,

the  marketing  group  selects  the  marketing  mix  after  considering  the  market

needs and segmentation analysis. In support of the marketing mix decision, the

marketing groups provide a reference price and price list for use by the regional

teams to create a published price catalog to be included in the bid submission

(see the “Reference Price” sidebar).



Figure 11.1 shows the relationship among the reference price catalog, published

price catalog, and the bid offer price. Many factors are considered in the pricing

decision. These are grouped into five main areas for analysis:



u Reference Price



The headquarters’ marketing group sets the initial reference price for any new products and services and makes adjust-

ments to the reference price catalog on an annual basis. This is required in order to maintain a consistent product and ser-

vice line pricing and to remain competitive given the changing competitive environment. From the headquarters’ reference

price catalog, the regions then publish regional or country price catalogs to be distributed to customers.



•  Product and service line pricing establishes price steps between various products in a product line based on cost differenc-

es between the products, customer evaluations of different features, and competitors’ prices. A typical product line offering

is the multiple levels of technology of basic and advanced products and services.



Reference prices might also change as the products and services move along their product and service life cycle. Traditional

new product pricing strategies follow two  variations:



•  Market-skimming pricing: Setting a high price for new offerings to skim maximum revenues layer by layer from the market



segments willing to pay the higher price, such as the early adopters in visionary companies



•  Market-penetration pricing: Setting a low price for a new product in order to gain a higher market share



At the same time as setting the reference price, the marketing group sets guidance for price-adjustment strategies in the

regions. Such pricing strategies include:



•  Cash discounts for companies who pay their bills promptly.



•  Volume discounts for companies who buy large quantities or make long-term commitments. This is not the same as bid-



ding, which will be discussed in this chapter.



•  Segmented pricing where a product or service might be sold at different prices to different market segments,  usually



based on perceived value for example land pricing and offshore pricing.



•  Bundling of products and services normally used with results and performance-based contracts.



•  Promotional pricing used to encourage first-time use.



•  Geographical pricing guidelines for establishing prices in the different regions.



436

•  Type of tender and tender analysis



•  Market factors

•  Negotiation strategy



Chapter 10 “Competitive Bidding” discussed how to select the most appropriate sell-

ing approach based on the type of relationship the customer wants with their suppli-

ers for each opportunity. This module discusses how the other factors are taken into

account in the basic and advanced pricing models. Market shares and competitive

offerings are included in the market factors analysis. The customer’s pricing sensitiv-

ity is taken into account in the customer pricing sensitivity analysis. The costs, rev-

enues and profit factors are analyzed in the pricing strategy. The contracted price

typically comes after negotiations, which is discussed in the next chapter.



The bid team has two pricing models for how they can apply the published price

catalog  to  arrive  at  their  offer  price.  These  are  the  basic  and  advanced  pricing



models, discussed next.



Pricing Models

The two pricing models—basic and advanced—can be used to set the offer price. The

basic pricing model is more appropriate for standard contracts as shown in the first two

levels  of  Fig.  11.2.2  This  model  is  the  most  commonly  used  in  B2B  bidding  for  Type  2

Consultative  and  Type  3  Transactional  Tenders,  where  the  customer  pays  per  unit  of

product or service delivered.

The Moment of Truth



437



Fig. 11.2

 Basic and advanced pricing models.





The advanced pricing model is used in higher-level contract models on the value

pyramid and includes additional incentive schemes to calculate your company’s

remuneration, not only for the amount of products and services delivered but also

additional  sums  or  discounts  based  on  other  factors,  such  as  your  company’s

performance, the customer’s perception of results, or joint value enhancement.

The  use  of  higher-level  contract  models  decreases  as  you  move  up  the  value

pyramid from performance-based to results-based to value-based contract mod-

els.  The  advanced  pricing  model  is  used  for  Type  1  Enterprise  Tenders.  The

advanced pricing model should never be used for Type 2 Consultative or Type 3

Transactional Tenders.



Basic Pricing Model3



The basic pricing model uses three analyses to arrive at an offer price: market analy-

sis,  pricing  sensitivity,  and  pricing  strategy.  A  pricing  workbook  created  in  Excel  is

used to demonstrate the basic pricing model and is available for download from the

textbook website. The pricing workbook workflow is shown in Fig. 11.3, and each step

is described and illustrated with pricing examples given in the following sections.



Market Analysis



In  the  market  analysis,  you  determine  which  benchmark  you  will  use  to  decide

your  target  TTV,  as  discussed  in  Chapter  10  “Competitive  Bidding.”  The  bench-

mark is what you believe will be the TTV of your most serious competitor from the

customer’s perspective. This rate is based on what you believe your competitors

will  offer  based  on  historical  data  and  market  intelligence.  The  sales  staff  is

responsible for completing a market analysis for each pricing proposal, and the

market  rate  analysis  should  include  a  survey  of  their  specific  region  and  other

applicable  regions  in  which  the  customer  and  your  competitors  operate.  The

market rate analysis consists of the following:



Fig. 11.3

Pricing workbook workflow. Each dotted

box represents a worksheet in the pricing

workbook. In box 1, Market Rate Analysis

includes establishing the scope of work

and your company’s book price, and gath-

ering market intelligence to establish the

market rate. In box 2, the Pricing

Sensitivity Analysis uses the market rate

and customer pricing sensitivity analysis,

which includes a customer value analysis

to arrive at the market-based price. In

box 3, the Pricing Strategy Worksheet is

used to compare your market-based price

against your internal financial and risk

analyses to select one of three pricing

strategies: long-term, short-term excess

capacity, and short-term limited capacity.

When the short-term limited capacity

strategy applies, you first complete an

Opportunity Costs analysis. The last

worksheet of the pricing workbook is the

Negotiating Strategy Worksheet. In this

worksheet, you identify potential trades

that maintain the value of the deal within

an acceptable range should the customer

wish to negotiate the final offer. After

completing steps 2, 3, and 4, your team

takes a specific pricing action to support

the pricing and negotiating strategies.



438



CHAPTER 11  Pricing for Bidding



•  Establishing the scope of work and calculating your company’s book price



•  Reviewing market information



•  Establishing the market rate



Scope of Work and Your Company’s Book Price



In the first step of the market analysis, you must have a clear understanding of

the scope of work after completing the tender assessment, as discussed in Chapter

10 “Competitive Bidding.” Only then can you calculate your company’s book price

using the published price catalog.



Market Review



The best place to begin the market review is by searching your company’s CRM

opportunity database. From the CRM database, a global search should be done to

select the most appropriate opportunities to include in the market rate analysis:

historical, active, and future opportunities with the associated revenues and other

information for each opportunity. The best  way to search for information regard-

ing the market rate analysis is to look for opportunities with similar scope of work

and time frame, making it easy to compare opportunities on a revenue-per-month

basis. The data should also be sorted by account and country and then exported

into an Excel worksheet or printed.



The  market  rate  information  and  any  other  market  intelligence  collected  from

other  sources,  such  as  other  sales  staff  and  HQ  marketing  staff,  your  company

CRM competitor database, or customers, are analyzed.



The next step of the market review is to convert the pricing for similar projects

to a comparable pricing metric, such as revenue per month, per job, or per seat.

The comparable pricing metric should include only similar products and services,

and adjustments may be needed to make relevant comparisons. The market rate

information is then entered into a Market Analysis Worksheet.



Establishing the Market Rate–Competitor TTV Benchmark



Once the market survey is completed, the information is then analyzed. The first-

level  analysis  looks  for  trends  in  the  pricing  behavior  of  your  competitors  and
