The Moment of Truth



439





u You’re So Sensitive!



Buyers are sensitive people, too. In one of the more popular

books on pricing, The Strategy and Tactics of Pricing, 4 authors

Hagel and Holden state that estimating economic value to the

customer is fundamental to marketing in general and to pricing

in particular, but it is just one facet of the role of price in custom-

ers’ decision making. When dealing with knowledgeable and

high ly sophisticated purchas ers, economic value analysis alone

can describe and predict buyer behavior quite adequately. How-

ever, most customers do not make purchase decisions exactly

as economic value analysis would predict. Although getting

a good value is often one important purchase consideration, cus-

tomers will not always choose the very best value for the price

they pay. The pricing sensitivities the authors suggest that

should be considered along with economic value are as follows:



•  Perceived substitutes effect



•  Unique value effect



•  Switching cost effect



•  Difficult comparison effect



•  Price-quality effect



•  Expenditure effect



•  End-benefit effect



•  Shared-cost effect



•  Fairness effect



•  Inventory effect



These and other factors affect the extent to which actual pur-

chase decisions correspond to the model of economic value.

Before attempting to determine the price a customer could

be expected to pay for a product or service, sales personnel

need to understand these factors. They are necessary if the

salesperson wants to influence the way customers make

their decisions and to anticipate responses to your pricing

proposals.



estimates, given competitors’ market share both locally

and globally, and their asset utilization level. For exam-

ple,  are  their  prices  going  up  or  down?  Do  they  have

assets available in country? Based on these answers, the

sales  team  must  determine  whether  your  competitors

will use a long-term or short-term pricing strategy (pric-

ing strategies are discussed in more detail later in this

section).



Trends  in  customer  behavior  are  also  analyzed.  For

example, is there a split in the work system used by the

customer?  Does  the  customer  award  certain  types  of

projects to specific suppliers?



Finally, the sales team must compare the scope of work

for this project against other projects and then factor in

any additional costs or discounts that may apply.



Based  on  this  analysis,  the  sales  team  estimates  the

market rate the customer will use to compare your offer

against other offers. Figure 11.4 shows the market rate

analysis for a real B2B tender for oilfield products and

services in Indonesia, which will be referred to as Nir-

wana. The same tender will be used to illustrate how the

pricing was established using the techniques described

in this chapter. After analyzing all the market informa-

tion,  the  sales  team  determined  that  Competitor  A

would  tender  the  project  at  $50,000  per  month,  an

increase  of  $20,000  per  month  due  to  additional  costs

incurred to mobilize people and equipment for the Nir-

wana  project.  With  the  market  rate  established,  the

sales team completed the pricing sensitivity analysis.



Pricing Sensitivity Analysis



Chapter  1  “Understanding  the  Role  of  Marketing  and

Sales” discussed the segmentation of buying groups in an effort to identify what

was most important to a buyer (price, value, relationship, or convenience) in order

to best manage the sales conversation.



This  section  takes  the  concept  one  step  further  by  trying  to  understand  your

customers’ reactions to price. Many factors impact the customer’s final pricing

decision,  as  described  in  the  “You’re  So  Sensitive!”  sidebar.  This  section  dis-

cusses  the  most  common  factors  pertaining  to  pricing  sensitivity  in  the  B2B

competitive bidding and illustrates how to capture this information on the Pric-

ing Sensitivity Worksheet, which is used to arrive at a market-based priced.



The goal of pricing sensitivity analysis is twofold:



•  To clearly identify what the customer will view as a fair and  equitable price



for products and services.



•  To devise a winning strategy and tactics to justify your company’s price.



The ongoing process of a pricing sensitivity analysis is the respon sibility of the sales

staff.  The  salesperson,  working  with  the  customer  and  your  company’s  service

delivery team, identifies the key buying  center members and the most significant

factors affecting their decisions. With this information, the salesperson devises a

strategy and tactics to minimize negative pricing factors and leverage positive pric-

ing factors to put your company in the most favorable position to secure the project.



440



CHAPTER 11  Pricing for Bidding



u What Value?



Value, like so many terms,

gets used often but can have

many meanings, so it is best

to define which value you are

referring to in your pricing dis-

cussion. Listed below are

some common value defini-

tions used by scholars. Which

do you think is appropriate for

the pricing decision?



•  User value = value to cus-



tomer (Total $ Benefits) ver-

sus doing without



•  Economic value = value to

customer versus a competi-

tive alternative



•  Perceived value = value



actually recognized by cus-

tomer



•  Expressed value = “price



willing to pay” communicat-

ed as part of price negotia-

tion demand



When you refer to value in the

sales environment, you are

referring to the economic

value, as the customer has to

decide if the premium or dis-

count from the competitive

alternative is justified.



Perceived Substitutes Effect



The perceived substitutes effect states that the higher the product’s price relative

to prices of the buyers’ perceived substitutes, the more price sensitive the buyers.

The key word here is “perceived.” The perception of available substitutes differs

widely among customers and across purchase situations. In your industry, sophis-

ticated tendering procedures guarantee that your customers have access to alter-

native suppliers. The challenge for you as the salesperson is to consider how to

best  present  your  company’s  offer  given  the  customer’s  price  sensitivity.  The

perceived substitutes effect is the most significant negative pricing sensitivity a

salesperson  encounters.  The  most  effective  method  of  offsetting  the  perceived

substitutes effect is discussed next.



Unique Value Effect



As the customer’s perception of substitutes is such an important determinant of

price sensitivity, much of the marketing effort must be directed toward reducing

the  effect  of  substitutes  (the  reference  value)  on  the  total  economic  value  as

described in the “What Value?” sidebar. The goal is to offer something unique—a

“differentiation”—that buyers will pay for despite the existence of lower-priced

alternatives. Buyers are less sensitive to a product’s price the more they value any

attributes  that  differentiate  the  offering  from  competing  products.  This  unique

value  effect  is  the  rationale  behind  companies’  ongoing  efforts  to  differentiate

their  products  and  services  from  those  of  their  competitors.  Some  companies

devotes enormous effort to creating unique attributes that customers value. Their

motivation to excel in R&D is based on the understanding that the more custom-

ers value the unique attributes of a product and service, the less importance they

will place on price when deciding whether to buy. Differentiation alone, however,

does not produce this effect. Customers must first recognize a differentiation and

then be convinced of its value. The most critical component of sensitivity analysis

is getting the customer’s agreement on the unique value your company provides

and addressing any negative pricing sensitivities. Steps for completing a pricing

sensitivity analysis are covered later in this section.



Switching Cost Effect



The greater the monetary and non-monetary costs of switching suppliers, the less

sensitive the buyer is to price. This effect is also commonly referred to as “creat-

ing barriers to entry.” Many products require the buyer to make supplier-specific

investments in order to make use of them. If that investment does not have to be

made on a repeat buy from a current supplier but would have to made with a new

supplier,  that  difference  is  a  switching  cost  that  limits  price  sensitivity.  For

example, a customer may be unwilling to switch from the current supplier for an

offshore exploration project because of the additional cost and risk of disrupting

operations  incurred  with  a  new,  cheaper  supplier.  Another  example  is  once  a

company  deploys  a  software  or  management  system  across  the  enterprise,  it  is

very  difficult  to  switch  to  another  system  because  of  the  switching  cost  of  re-

training the staff and installing the new software.



Personal relationships also represent a significant investment that can limit the

attractiveness  of  competing  suppliers.  For  example,  if  your  company  provides

customer support staff, such as engineers or support center personnel, the cus-

tomer has probably made an additional investment in training staff in their pro-

cesses  and  procedures.  Developing  such  working  relationships  takes  time  and

considerable effort. Once these investments are made, customers may be reluc-

tant to repeat them simply because otherwise equally qualified suppliers offer a

lower price.



The Moment of Truth



441





Difficult Comparison Effect



The  concept  of  economic  value  assumes  that  customers  can  actually  compare

what alternative suppliers have to offer. In fact, it is often difficult to determine

the true attributes of a product or service prior to purchase. Buyers are less sen-

sitive  to  the  price  of  a  known  or  reputable  supplier  when  they  have  difficulty

comparing alternatives. The difficult comparison effect is particularly important

in business markets. Corporate buyers are commonly thought to seek many sup-

pliers,  whom  they  play  against  one  another  for  lower  prices.  Corporate  buyers

usually follow such a policy only for those products whose quality and reliability

they can easily evaluate at the time of purchase. For purchases that are particu-

larly risky and difficult to evaluate (such as services for high-cost, high-risk proj-

ects),  corporate  buyers  will  often  develop  relationships  with  a  list  of  approved

suppliers  with  whom  they  have  had  previous  satisfactory  experience.5  They  will

not even consider purchasing from an unknown supplier, even though that sup-

plier claims to offer the same quality at a lower price.



Fairness Effect



The  concept  of  a  fair  price  has  bedeviled  marketers  for  centuries.6  Buyers  are

more sensitive to a product’s price when it is outside the range that they perceive

as  fair  or  reasonable  given  the  purchase  context.  But  what  is  fair?  Sales  staff

should  note  that  the  concept  of  fairness  has  little  to  do  with  profitability.  Oil

companies are frequently accused of gouging, although their profits are below the

average  of  most  other  global  industries.7  In  the  B2B  sales  environment,  several

factors appear to determine a buyer’s perception of fairness in pricing:



•  How  the  current  price  compares  to  prices  previously  encountered  for  the

product or service. A common example is the price the customer paid for

the product or service in the last tender.



•  How large the price increases or decreases are relative to standard indices.

Customers often compare price movements to changes in commodities and

rates of inflation.



•  Prices paid for similar products or services in similar purchase situations.

Your customers have sophisticated purchasing systems that track the most

recent  prices  paid  for  similar  services  on  a  global  basis.  For  example,  a

customer who discovers that one of their subsidiaries operating in another

country received a higher discount for the same services will expect the

same level of pricing.



•  Geographic adjustment. Customers expect to pay more or less depending on

where  they  are  operating  and  how  developed  and  large  the  industry  is  in

the specific country. For example, most customers expect to pay less in the

Americas than they do in other parts of the world.



Your ability to anticipate and address the customer’s pricing sensitivity as affected

by the fairness effect can greatly reduce the customer’s negative reactions to your

pricing  proposal  when  your  company’s  offer  is  the  most  expensive.  Two  areas

where  the  fairness  effect  can  impact  the  customer’s  decision-making  process  is

when you introduce new products and services and when you try to raise prices.

In such cases, the salesperson must carefully plan the approach to the customer

in order understand the customer’s perception of fair. The “You Can’t Be Serious!”

sidebar offers several suggestions for avoiding a customer’s “you can’t be serious”

reaction to a salesperson’s proposal.



Your understanding of key buyer pricing sensitivities in the B2B sales environment

is the foundation for completing the Pricing Sensitivity Analysis Worksheet. Sales



442



CHAPTER 11  Pricing for Bidding



staff  deal  with  well-informed  buying  centers  that  use  a

well-defined project management process, as described in

Chapter  4  “Opportunities  Management.”  The  customer’s

goal is to make a highly informed purchase decision. As

most    companies  require  a  competitive  tender  for  all

major procurements, you as the salesperson must be well

prepared to justify your pricing offer. The Pricing Sensitiv-

ity Analysis Worksheet is a key part of that preparation.



Pricing Sensitivity Analysis Worksheet



Several  questions  must  be  answered  to  complete  the

Pricing Sensitivity Analysis Worksheet:



•  What is the market rate of the perceived substitute(s)?



•  What are the positive pricing factors?



•  What are the negative pricing factors?



•  What is a fair and equitable price?



The  sales  team  uses  the  completed  Pricing  Sensitivity

Analysis  to  support  your  company’s  price  and  gain  the

customer’s agreement. An example of a completed Pric-

ing Sensitivity Analysis Worksheet for the Nirwana tender

is shown in Fig. 11.5.



Market Rate of the Perceived Substitute



u You Can’t be Serious!



You have all heard this reaction in response to an offer.

Such a reaction might be a negotiating tactic, but it also

may be one of genuine surprise from the customer. The

reason customers have these reactions is their expecta-

tions have been influenced by several of the pricing sen-

sitivity factors. How do you avoid such defensive and

negative reactions? Listed below are several strategies

that can be used to avoid or reduce this type of reaction.



•  Anticipation. By anticipating customers’ reactions and

putting ourselves in their position, you can prepare to

address their negative pricing sensitivities to your pric-

ing proposals. In many cases, you must do some up-

front research to determine the pricing benchmarks the

customer uses to compare your offer and then  present

your offer in a more favorable fashion by considering

the customer’s frame of reference.



•  Justification. Always justify your pricing to  yourself

first and then to the customer. A well-rehearsed jus-

tification of your pricing is more convincing and easier

for customers to repeat when challenged by their own

organization. Justification can come from many per-

spectives, the easiest being differential value. The bot-

tom line is know why you have the price you do.



•  Reverse Justification. In special situations, ask the

customer to justify why they want the product or ser-

vice. If you have recently completed a field test for new

products or services, take the new products or servic-

es out of the service area. Let the customer approach

you to request the products or services, and ask the

customer to explain why they want the new products

or services. This is a much better starting point for the

pricing discussion. However, even if you save the cus-

tomer hundreds of thousands of dollars, don’t be sur-

prised if they still don’t like your price (see additional

discussion in Chapter 12 “Collaborative Negotiations”).



The  market  rate  is  the  TTV  of  the  perceived  substitute,

which in most cases will be that of a competitive alterna-

tive.  Part  of  the  market  analysis  is  to  determine  which

competitive alternatives are being seriously considered as

a substitute to your company’s solution or who is the pre-

ferred supplier. Often, the bidder’s list may include many

competing companies, only several of which have support-

ers  in  the  buying  center  that  can  actually  win  the  con-

tract. Your team must determine who is on the list during the evaluation of options

stage of the opportunities management process. When completing the Market Rate

Analysis  Worksheet,  the  estimated  market  rate  is  copied  automatically  into  the

market price cell of the Pricing Sensitivity Analysis Worksheet.



Positive Pricing Factors



To determine the positive pricing factors, identify the customer’s value drivers for

the project. The identification of value drivers was discussed in detail in Chapter

2 “Value Drivers.” Most value drivers are direct, such as increased production and

cost  reduction.   Indirect  value  drivers  can  also  be  key  factors  in  the  customer’s

decision-making process. Two types of positive pricing factors must be con sidered:



•  Unique value factors



•  Intangible factors



Once the positive pricing factors have been identified, you must  quantify the differ-

ential impact of each factor on the customer’s value drivers to make a comparison to

the differential impact offered by  competitors. The unique value effect is described

in terms of the economic value of your solution.



The Moment of Truth



443





Market Price – Competitor TTV



Market-Based Price



Reduced operating costs and fewer risks for customer’s operations.



Drilling Manager said 10%; however, believes that they would actuallly

pay a $10k premium per month to not have to switch out suppliers.



No value determined for excellent QHS&E; however, customer’s QHS&E manager

said he would give us a premium for our QHS&E record, assumed to be 10%.



Sum of above differentials.



Cost of insurance premium for downhole risks Company A will not

accept. Competitor A has accepted.



Procurement Manager,

Evaluator



Fig. 11.5

Customer Pricing Sensitivity Analysis Worksheet for Nirwana tender. In this example, Company A’s solution has positive differential value for rig time reduction by

using the advanced solution and for the benefit of continued operations as the incumbent on the existing Nirwana operations  (positive switching costs).

Additional intangible benefits were added for Company A’s QHS&E performance, estimated to be worth $5,000 per month. A negative differential was included

for an insurance premium the customer would have to pay as a result of Company A not accepting downhole risk, which the competitor did. The total economic

value to the customer is $75,000 per month, and Company A’s total differential value is $25,000 per month ($35,000 positive less $10,000 negative). Company A’s

market-based price, which is based on a 50/50 sharing of Company A’s differential value, is $62,500 per month. In this worksheet, the white cell values are

entered directly into the worksheet, yellow cell values are computed from other cell entries, and the other shaded cells are copied from the Market Rate Worksheet.



Unique value factors. Some of the more common unique value factors are as

follows:



•  Total cost reduction



•  Increased sales of the customer’s product and services to their customers



•  Improved performance



•  Reduced risks



•  Improved operating efficiency



The most convincing way to describe a unique value factor is in financial terms

that  make  the  connection  between  what  your  company  provides  and  the  eco-

nomic value to the customer. The unique value factor must be explicit and eas-

ily understood by all buying center participants, and you do this by communicat-

ing  within  the  receiver’s  field  of  experience  as  discussed  in  Chapter  5

“Communication Skills.” The surest way to communicate in most people’s field

of  experience  is  to  relate  the  unique  value  in  terms  of  dollar  benefits.  Each



444



CHAPTER 11  Pricing for Bidding



unique value factor is identified and entered into the Customer Pricing Sensitiv-

ity  Worksheet  as  a  dollar  amount.  The  benefits  should  be  stated  for  the  same

reference period as the market rate; for example, total project costs should be

compared  to  total  project  benefits,  and  monthly  market  rates  should  be  com-

pared to monthly benefits.



During  the  existing  contract  in  the  Nirwana  example,  Company  A’s  team  intro-

duced the advanced solution and reduced the customer’s operating costs and the

risks associated with the operations. The customer saved approximately $20,000

of rig time per well. In addition, the drilling manager indicated that his company

would prefer to continue the project with your company and would be willing to

give your company a premium if the prices quoted by the competitors and your

company were close in the upcoming tender. The salesperson estimated this to be

worth $10,000 of differential value.



Intangibles. Intangibles are positive pricing factors that are not unique to your

company, but the customer agrees your company is better than the competition

in a specific area. Intangible benefits are commonly referred to as your company’s

strengths, as discussed in Chapter 6 “Sales Activities” and Chapter 7 “Sales Call

Skills.”  Every  effort  should  be  made  to  quantify  intangibles  for  each  customer

proposal. This can be done as part of your service review meetings during project

implementation. Quantified intangibles make it easier for evaluators to compare

tenders and become very important when the evaluation between your company

and  competitors  is  close.  Your  company’s  intangibles  can  even  be  the  deciding

factor in winning the bid. Examples of intangible positive pricing factors include

the following:



•  In-country support facilities.



•  More experience in difficult environments.



•  In-country technical experts.



•  Back-up capabilities.



•  Quality, Health, Safety, and Environment (QHS&E) performance.



•  Existing relationships.



The “Intangibles” heading in the Customer Pricing Sensitivity Worksheet contains

a  dollar  amount  that  represents  the  premium  a  customer  would  pay  that  is  not

included in the specific unique value factors. Under the “Intangibles” category in

Fig. 11.5, the salesperson has estimated the value of no safety  as $5,000 per month

to the customer. The customer’s QHS&E manager said this had real value, despite

the inability to quantify it. However, this could be quantified by doing the research,

as demonstrated by the Lake Maracaibo example described in Chapter Highlight

4.5: Performance That Can’t Be Beat in Chapter 4 “Opportunities Management.”



Negative Pricing Factors



Once  each  of  the  positive  pricing  factors  is  entered,  the  negative  differential

values  are  entered.  Negative  differential  is  when  your  company’s  solution  for

certain  aspects  of  the  project  is  not  optimal  and  the  customer  will  incur  extra

costs as a result of using your company rather than a competitor. All the positive

pricing factors for your company can, under different circumstances, be negative

differentials. Other examples of negative differential value are as follows:



•  Switching costs, as described in the pricing sensitivity section



•  Any competitor’s unique value



•  A strong relationship with a competitor, and supporters who would give the



competitor a premium over your company



The Moment of Truth



445





u Fair To You!



“Fair and equitable” is a relative measurement. Sales personnel com-

monly ask what a fair and equitable share of the economic value you

provide your customers really is. Here are some guidelines for making

that determination:



•  The more confident you are the customer is convinced they will



receive the benefit, the larger the share of the value you should take.



•  The sooner the benefit is realized by the customer the larger the



share you should take.



•  The more risk you are willing to take, the larger the share of the



value you should take (i.e., you are paid based on delivered perfor-

mance or customer results).



The next question is what is the maximum share of the economic

value you should take. When using the basic pricing model, book price

is the maximum. In one case where Company A was providing $2 mil-

lion worth of reduced rig time for the operator, Company A took 50%

of the savings, which represented a premium of over a 100%, com-

pared to the competitor. See Case 4: Alaska example discussed

Chapter Highlight 8.2: We Would Like to Use You, BUT in Chapter 8

“Handling Customer Objections.”



When the book price for the product or service does not provide

what you consider to be a fair and equitable share, be sure that you

are not taking too narrow a view of book price. In many regions, crew

rentals, mobilization, transportation costs, stand by charges, etc., are

rarely charged. These are part of the book price and can be used to

capture a larger share of the differential value you provide the custom-

er. Of course, pricing actions would need to be taken to justify these

charges to the customer prior to opening the tender.



If using the advanced pricing model, your share can be more than

what would be charged for running the services as per the price book.

The bonus is agreed to as part of the incentive mechanism. The down-

side to this in the advanced pricing model is that you assume more

risk and, if you do not perform to an agreed benchmark, your rates can

be discounted below the market rate. This is discussed in more detail

in the advanced pricing model section.



•  Enemies in the buying center who would



discount your company’s value



•  Innovation  risks  that  are  inherent  when



introducing new products or services



Once  the  negative  values  are  entered,  the

worksheet  will  calculate  the  total  economic

value  of  your  company’s  solution  compared  to

the alternative.



In the Nirwana example, a negative differential

value of $10,000 per month was entered for an

insurance  premium  the  customer  would  pay  if

Company A did not accept the customer’s down-

hole risk—a risk the competitors had accepted.

Therefore,  the  total  differential  value  of  Com-

pany  A’s  solution  was  $25,000  (total  positive

factors minus total negative factors).



Fair and Equitable Price Determination



With the market rate and positive and negative

sensitivity  factors  entered  into  the  worksheet,

the  total  economic  value  is  calculated.  In  the

example, the value is $75,000 (total differential

value + market rate). The last step in the cus-

tomer pricing sensitivity analysis is to enter the

share  of  the  differential  value  the  sales  team

believes would be viewed as fair and equitable

by members of the buying center. As discussed

in  the  fairness  effect  of  the  pricing  sensitivity

section,  the  sales  team  must  determine  which

factors—such as other similar contracts or pre-

vious prices paid—impact buying center mem-

bers’ perceptions of fairness. For ideas on how



to determine What is Fair?, see the sidebar “Fair to You!”



In the Nirwana cementing example, the sales team decided that a fair and equitable

sharing of Company A’s differential value was 50% as the benefits were fairly certain

and the customer would recognize the benefits in the short term. This value was

entered into the “% Your Company’s Share of Differential Value” field, resulting in

a  calculated  market-based  price  of  $62,500.  This  number  was  then  used  for  com-

parison against financial and risk metrics used in the pricing strategies worksheets;

these metrics will be discussed in the next section. The last calculated field on the

worksheet is the “Pricing Premium from Customer’s Perspective,” which is calcu-

lated by dividing the difference between the market price and Company A’s Market

Based  Price  by  the  market  price.  This  calculation  is  done  to  ensure  that  you  see

your offer price from the customer’s perspective.



Pricing Actions



The Pricing Sensitivity Analysis Worksheet is a powerful tool that should be used

in the initial planning stages of a project, later in the selling phase, and finally as

a  follow-up  to  evaluate  performance  during  the  implementation  stage.  It  helps

ensure that you are delivering on your promises during the pursuit stages of the

opportunities management process. It is used during customer review as a value

scorecard that tracks the dollar benefits of your company’s unique and intangible



446



CHAPTER 11  Pricing for Bidding



Table 11.1

Critical bidding information. Sections discussed

in this chapter are highlighted in yellow.



factors. This worksheet is an integral part of the ongoing value-based communica-

tion,  both  with  the  customer  and  internally,  so  your  team  is  aligned  with  the

expectations the sales team established with the customer.



The worksheet can also be used to hold discussions with mentors in the buying

center to gain agreement on the final total differential value. Each value compo-

nent  should  be  presented  to  buying  center  members  to  build  support  for  your

solution during the evaluation of options and procurement stages of the opportu-

nity management process.



Each unique value should have a sales support document to demonstrate the dif-

ferential value, and for negative differentials a drawback strategy should be pre-

pared,  as  described  in  Chapter  8,  “Handling  Customer  Objections.”  This  same

support document can also be used as part of the drawback strategy for overcom-

ing  the  negative  differential  of  the  added  insurance  premium  costs  to  cover

downhole risks. The justification would be that the additional costs for the insur-

ance premium are much less than the benefits the customer will receive from the

advanced solution when compared to the competitor. Pricing actions for the Nir-

wana opportunity would have to include contacts with the buying center members,

as identified in the supporters and non-supporters columns.



Pricing Examples



In terms of timing and the opportunity management process, the pricing steps of

market rate analysis and pricing sensitivity analysis should are completed before

the receipt of the tender. Ideally the market rate analysis is an ongoing activity as

part of the competitor and market rate analysis. The pricing sensitivity should be

completed  after  the  sales  team  has  completed  their  sales  calls  to  execute  their



Fig. 11.6

Type 3 Tender TTV and EV.



The Moment of Truth



447





competitive sales strategy using the tac-

tics  of  “Prove  Value,”  “Emphasize

Strengths,” and “Minimize Weaknesses.”

Table  11.1  lists  the  critical  pricing

information  for  each  type  of  tender.

Highlighted in yellow in the table is the

information  discussed  in  this  chapter.

By  completing  this  analysis  before

receiving the tender, the core bid team

can  focus  on  the  tender  analysis  and

build a bid strategy to beat the competi-

tion  and  achieve  their  financial  targets

during  the  implementation  stage.  After

the bid team has completed the tender

analysis, the bid team has to make sev-

eral  strategic  decisions  depending  on

the type of tender.



u Just Enough



Honestly, in a Type 2 or 3 Tender, anyone can win the tender by having a very

low price. Of course, the sales team that does so might not generate

enough revenue to make their targeted profit, if any profit at all. However,

this is not the only bad outcome of bidding too low. The other equally bad

outcomes are:



•  In some companies and when government companies are bidding, if a bid is

below a minimum TTV as set by the bid committee, the bid will be rejected.



•  If the customer uses the TTV to create their budget, they will most likely



run out of funds before the end of the project. A lack of funds will make it

very difficult for the seller to exploit the issues in the tender to sell more

than the customer’s budget.



•  The lower the price, the larger the differential between standard services

included in the bid and added-value products and services the sales team

is planning on selling to make additional revenue. The large differential can

make it very difficult for the customer to justify the additional premium.



The bid team wants their price to be just enough to be slightly lower than the

award criteria used by the customer’s bid team to make the award decision.

Just enough depends on the following:



•  The customer’s bid criteria



•  How many supporters you have in the buying center



•  Who the incumbent is



In a real example involving a government agency, a new tender was issued,

meaning, no company gained any advantage from being the incumbent. The

buying center was neutral to the final three companies they selected to send

the RFP.  The customer’s buying center went through a very detailed and pro-

longed bidder qualification to identify the best companies for their project. It

was a Type 3 Tender. All government agencies in this country had to follow a

very strict tendering procedure. The buying center was required to set an engi-

neering estimate; any bidder more than 15% above or below the engineering

estimate would be disqualified. All bids were opened at a public opening, and

the bid price announced and displayed. The winner was announced after the

L1 bid was checked for compliance. The TTV’s for the three bids were:



Type 3: From the benchmarking exercise

in the market rate analysis, the TTV is set

slightly lower than the market rate. How

much  is  slight?  See  the  sidebar  “Just

Enough”  for  the  answer.  Shown  in  Fig.

11.6  is  how  the  price  for  this  strategy

would  appear  on  the  pricing  sensitivity

graph  shown  in  Chapter  9  “Strategic

Sales  Plan.”  For  the  Nirwana  example

discussed earlier, assuming this is a Type

3 Tender, the bid team would have bid a

TTV of $49,500. This price is 1% less than

the  market  rate  as  shown  on  the  “Pre-

mium from Customer Perspective” gauge

on the right of the figure. Also, the cus-

tomer  will  benefit  from  receiving  the

“Strengths”  items  circled  in  green  that,

in  this  case,  are  included  as  part  of  the

TTV price. The bid team will then include

a discussion of these strengths as compo-

nents in their written proposal. The team

will not include the advanced solution in

the  offer  and  up-sell  the  solution  once

advanced  solution  is  included  in  the  EV

of $70,000. In the Nirwana example, the

tender  scope  of  work  was  accurate,  and

there were not product or service speci-

fication  issues.  The  only  tender  analysis

issue  was  the  customer  including  the

conditions,  which  the  bid  team  added  a

pricing note not accepting the downhole

risks. The bid team considered the com-

petitor’s willingness to accept the risks as a negative differential. However, in reality,

it is unlikely the customer would have added the costs for an insurance premium to

the team’s offer price. If there were additional tender issues, then the best way to

present the bid strategy would be to use the Excel pricing worksheets and include



1.  $1,856,000



2.  $1,500,000



3.  $1,489,000



These figures show just how slight a 1% differene is among bidders. Bidder 3

was 1% lower than Bidder 2 and awarded the work after the engineering esti-

mate was disclosed as this was also the customer’s budget for the project.

The engineering estimate was $1,700,000.  Bidder 3 was able to sell-up addi-

tional services to sell approximately $1,750,000 and easily meet their profit

objectives and developed relationships during the project implementation.

Bidder 3 in this situation received an A grade for an excellent bid.  They beat

the competition; they were within the customer’s engineering estimate, and

they were able to meet their profit objectives and strengthen the relationship

between them and the customer. This is called the “3H World.” In the 3H

World, the:



•  Customer is happy because they received good value for the money spent;



•  Salesperson is happy because they won the tender;



•  Operations are happy because they met their profit objectives.



When you bid too low, you may find yourself in this situation after the bid

award. The good news is you won. The bad news is you won. Bid just enough

and you will be happier!

CHAPTER 11  Pricing for Bidding



Fig. 11.7

Type 2  Tender  TTV and EV.



the other items in the analysis. If there were extraneous items, the bid team would

have to decide if the competition would include this in their offer. The best way to

be confident what the competition will do is reviewing their pricing in recent tenders

captured as part of the market rate benchmarking exercise.



Type 2: TTV is chosen using the customer’s premium over market rate and pricing

sensitivity analysis. In the case where your company has net positive differentials,

you can have a premium over the competitor up to the maximum allowed. If the

competitor has more net positive differentials, then your TTV will be set to force

the competitor’s expected pricing to fall outside of the pricing premium window.

Figure  11.7 shows how the price for this bidding strategy would appear on the

pricing  sensitivity  graph.  For  the  Nirwana  example,  assuming  this  is  a  Type  2

Tender, the bid team would have a TTV of $55,000. This price is 10% more than

the market rate as shown on the “Premium from Customer Perspective” gauge on

the right of the figure. For this premium, the customer will benefit from receiving

the “Strengths” items circled in green that, in this case, are included as part of

the TTV price. The bid team will then include these as components in their writ-

ten  proposal.  This  is  important  because  it  is  the  unique  business  value  of  the

continued  operations  and  better  QHSE  performance  that  justifies  the  customer

choosing your bid team rather than the competitor.  The team will not include the

advanced  solution  in  the  offer  and  up-sell  the  solution  once  awarded  the  work.

The revenue for the advanced solution is included in the EV of $70,000.



Type  1: TTV price is set using the market-based price and a perceived fair and

equitable  sharing  of  the  value  your  solution  provides.  Figure  11.8  on  the  next

page shows how the price for this bidding strategy would appear on the pricing

sensitivity graph. For the Nirwana example, assuming this is Type 1 Tender, the

bid  team  would  have  set  the  TTV  at  $65,000.  This  price  is  25%  more  than  the

market rate as shown on the “Premium from Customer Perspective” gauge on the

right of the figure.  For this premium, the customer will benefit from receiving the

“Strengths” items circled in green and value from the advanced solution. In this

case, TTV and EV prices are the same. The bid team will then include these as

components in their written proposal.



As  shown  in  Table  11.1  earlier,  the  Ideal  Competitive  Sales  Strategy  is  frontal  or

flanking.  These  are  strong  position  sales  strategies  that  allow  you  to  leverage  a

strong  evaluation  from  the  customer  using  their  formal  decision  criteria.  If  you

Fig. 11.8

Type 1 Tender  TTV and EV.



discover you are in a weak position before you receive the tender, then the bid team

would choose a weak position competitive sales strategy. The bid team would con-

sider  which  of  the  weak  position  strategies  would  give  them  the  best  chances  of

winning defend or fragment as discussed in Chapter 9 “Strategic Sales Plan.”



At this point of the pricing analysis, the main focus has been on identifying the

winning TTV and future EV revenues. The next factor to consider is the pricing

strategy to confirm the profitability objectives will be achieved using the TTV and

EV prices.



Pricing Strategy



Once the market-based price has been established, the next step is to check the

market-based price against the costs and targeted field margin for the location,

which  is  responsible  for  delivering  the  products  and  services.  This  step  is  com-

pleted using the Pricing Strategy Worksheet for one of the three standard pricing

strategies, which are discussed later in this section. The factors used on each of

the Pricing Strategy Worksheets are as follows:



•  Incremental costs



•  Fixed costs



•  Risk financing



•  Field margin



Incremental Costs



Incremental costs are the direct out-of-pocket costs incurred when a product or

service is provided. These are costs that would have been avoided if the product

or service were not provided. The incremental costs are subtracted from the sell-

ing price to arrive at the contribution margin, also referred to as gross profit. The

contribution  margin  is  the  amount  of  money  that  is  made  for  each  product  or

service  sold.  The  contribution  margin  is  the  amount  available  to  pay  for  fixed

costs,  profits,  and  taxes.  When  comparing  two  or  more  projects  or  pricing  sce-

narios,  the  contribution  margin  for  each  project  or  pricing  scenario  should  be

computed  and  compared.  All  other  factors  being  equal,  the  project  or  pricing

scenario that produces the highest contribution margin should be selected.



450



CHAPTER 11  Pricing for Bidding



Fixed Costs



Covering  only  incremental  costs  on  a  long-term  basis  does  not  produce  excess

funds necessary to replace assets or cover the costs of the other indirect support.

Fixed costs are the long-term portion of the cost structure that must be recovered

in order to continue to invest and grow the business. Fixed costs are made up of

two parts: overhead and shared.



Overhead costs have two components: direct overhead costs, such as salaries of

personnel, and depreciation of the assets dedicated to the project. These costs are

absorbed  completely  by  the  project.  The  other  portion  of  overhead  is  prorated

according  to  the  amount  of  revenue  generated  by  the  project.  Examples  of  pro-

rated overhead costs are a portion of location management, shop expenses, etc.

Shared costs are all other costs used by more than one location, such as region,

or your company’s corporate headquarters, and research and engineering.



Field Margin



Like all companies, your company is in business to generate a profit and increase

the wealth of shareholders. Profit is used to fund future growth and pay dividends

to shareholders. Each location has a target field margin (revenue – incremental

costs  –  fixed  costs)  /  revenue),  which  represents  the  location’s  commitment  to

generating profit.



Risk Financing



Risk  financing  is  the  additional  cost  associated  with  securing  additional  insur-

ance  or  hedging  instruments  to  protect  your  company  against  the  risks  not

accounted for in the basic pricing structure. Basic pricing model prices are based



u What Risks?



The relationship among liability (especially for negligence), the various forms of indemnity commonly given, and risk alloca-

tion is often a cause of confusion. For most, risk is generally regarded as an adverse probability. The measure of a risk is the

best estimate of the probability that one or more detrimental events will occur. The definitions below should help clear up

some of confusion.



Indemnity



Agreement to compensate or the act of compensation for a loss or injury sustained by another. Sometimes known as a

“hold harmless” clause, the essential element of an indemnity is a promise to underwrite the losses of another indepen-

dently of any default by the indemnitor (the party giving the indemnity). A common example is the indem nity against

legal liability incurred in the performance of duties given to a corporate officer by the company he serves.



Liability



Legal liability consists of an obligation to compensate an injured party and is the objective culpability borne by a person who

is held to have caused (or contributed to causation of) the detrimental events that led to the injury or loss. Liability is normally

determined by a court of law, or by reference to a hypothetical finding by a court. In other words, those making the assess-

ment of liability form a view as to what a court would conclude about the matter under review.



Contractual liability is a form of legal liability. A party to a contract who breaks any of its terms acquires a contractual liability

for the breach. Contractual liability arises out of a wrongful act, which is in the nature of a broken promise. Liability for negli-

gence arises from breach of a duty of care independent of the existence of a contract (see below).



Negligence



A civil wrong that arises independently of any  contract when the following three questions are answered affirmatively:



•  Was there a duty of care?



•  Was there a breach of that duty of care?



•  Was there an injured party who suffered loss?



If the three elements are present, the tort of negligence is established and the wrongdoer (the negligent party) is liable to

compensate the injured party.



The Moment of Truth



451





on  the  usual  liabilities  your  company  agrees  to  assume  under  client  contracts.

For some risks, your company “self-insures.” In each contract, your objective is

to limit its liability as much as possible. Your liability policy, on which prices and

operations are based, is based on the following principles: Each party should be

liable for its own personnel and property and for its other contractors’ and sub-

contractors’ personnel and property.



As an exception, the client would be liable for your company’s equipment under

the following circumstances:



•  While being transported by or on behalf of the client



•  While located at the worksite and outside your company’s custody and control



Your company should accept liability for third parties only to the extent of your

company’s fault or negligence, and if the client does not extend its indemnity

to  its  other  contractors,  your  company  should  have  a  fixed  financial  limit  on

third-party liability with the client indemnifying your company above the limit.

Your  company  must  be  totally  indemnified  by  the  client  against  catastrophic

risks. A mutual hold harmless clause should be put in place for incidental and

consequential  damages.  For  more  clarification  of  such  terms,  see  the  “What

Risks?” sidebar.



Risk financing is a critical part of the risk management process, which is part of

the tendering process. Companies must make a calculated and economic assump-

tion  of  risk  when  deciding  whether  to  make  contractual  commitments.  Risk

financing comes at the end of the process, as follows:



•  Identify and assess the risks to which your company is exposed



•  Select  and  implement  cost-effective  risk  control  measures  to  avoid  or



reduce exposures to loss



•  Arrange appropriate risk financing (including but not limited to insurance)

to offset the effects of any losses that may occur, so that the lowest sustain-

able cost of risk is obtained over the long term



u Insurance—Not So Fast



Before moving too quickly to accept additional risks and expecting to cover the

risks with insurance, consider the points below.



Insurance: A Risk Management Tool



Insurance is only one mechanism of risk financing and comes at the end of the

risk management process. It should not be used as a criterion for accepting

risk, as insurance does not diminish the risk; it transfers the financial exposure.

Arranging insurance will not stop an event from happening, but it might cover

some of the loss incurred. How ever, insurance does not offer a guarantee

of certainty. Insurers may dispute large claims, policies have exclusions, or they

may be rendered invalid or weakened in their effect (“vitiated”) by actions of

the insured. Many risks are, in fact, uninsurable. Premium charges for insurance

are cyclical and follow the insurance market cycle. Thus, using insurance as a

risk-funding mechanism should be an option compared to other options.



Insurance is not a rem edy for a poor risk allocation accepted by your company.

Insurance might not exist, or be available to your company (for example, conse-

quential damages). Even if insurance exists, it might be so costly that it defeats

the purpose of signing the contract with the client. Most liability insurances are

self-insured up to a certain amount. However, the deductible can be a signifi-

cant portion of the primary liability policy and is typically self-funded at the cor-

porate level, so any risk assumption within this level can affect the bottom line,

hence the importance of maintaining contractual indemnity protections.



Always have your contract checked by your contracts department before sub-

mitting your offer.



•  Monitor  and  continuously  improve

the  execution  of  the  risk  manage-

ment process



Risks must be quantified to the extent

possible,  and  the  magnitude  of  the

exposure  analyzed  in  the  context  of

factors such as the potentially hazard-

ous nature of the work to be performed,

the size of the contract, the anticipated

profitability,  and  the  general  business

relationship.  The  risk  of  loss  must  be

limited through realistic and practical

operating  procedures,  contractual

indemnification  and,  ultimately,  risk-

financing mechanisms.



There are three basic forms of defense

against identified risks:



•  Transfer  of  the  risk,  as  happens

when liability is excluded, restrict-

ed, or avoided (for example, by con-

tract), thus necessarily shifting it to

another party.



•  Hedging  the  risk,  as  happens  in



452



CHAPTER 11  Pricing for Bidding



currency  exchange  situations  when  options  are  purchased  to  balance  an

exposure to fluctuation.



•  Insuring  against  the  risk  (see  the  “Insurance—Not  So  Fast”  sidebar  for  a



cautionary note regarding insurance as a risk management tool).



The liability provisions in your contracts represent the reallocation of the liability

or  potential  liability  of  the  parties.  Liabilities  and  indemnities  are  a  complex

issue. Often, changing a word or moving a phrase can shift the entire liability. It

is,  therefore,  a  matter  for  experts,  and  the  contracts  department  should  be

involved  in  all  liability  and  indemnity  negotiations  and  risk-financing  deci-

sions. In commercial contracts, each party’s ideal position is to put the liability

for as much risk as possible on the other party, seeking all the benefits from the

operations and no exposure.



Pricing Strategy Worksheet



The  Pricing  Strategy  Worksheet  organizes  the  factors  required  to  arrive  at  a  the

offer price for the bid in one worksheet, as illustrated on the next page. The Pricing

Factors Worksheet is part of the pricing workbook, which links the previous work-

sheets with the Pricing Strategy Worksheet and finally to the Negotiation Worksheet,

which  is  discussed  in  the  next  chapter.  Together,  the  salesperson  and  operations

manager responsible for operations and field margin complete the Pricing Factors

Worksheet. Each factor of the Pricing Strategy Worksheet is explained here.



Estimated Market Price. The estimated market price and comments are copied

from  the  Pricing  Sensitivity  Worksheet.  This  represents  the  reference  price  for

which the customer will compare your offer, as discussed in the pricing sensitivity

section of this chapter.



Total Differential Value. This is the estimated market price and is copied from

the Pricing Sensitivity Worksheet.



Customer Price Ceiling and your Company’s Price Floor. A useful way to think about

pricing is to first create a pricing window. The ceiling for the pricing window is the

combination of market price plus the full value of the differential value. Clearly, the

customer should not pay more than the total economic value of the services delivered.

The window also has a pricing floor, which is valued at the incremental cost to do the

project plus a mark-up. In the Nirwana example, the incremental cost mark-up is equal

to  14%.  This  value  should  be  discussed  and  agreed  upon  by  the  location  manager

responsible for the field margin of the location. In most cases, the pricing window as

calculated above should dictate the highest to lowest prices charged unless there are

other overriding factors, such as limited equipment availability. The price floor value

is calculated after the cost components have been entered.



Offer Price. The initial offer price is copied from the market-based price cell of the

Customer Pricing Sensitivity Analysis Worksheet. This value should be reviewed and

adjusted if the entered value is too high or too low when the financial components

are considered. In the example in Fig. 11.9, the sales team decided to tender a price

of $70,000, which is the market price plus 80% of the differential value. The default

offer price calculated in the Customer Pricing Sensitivity Analysis worksheet is equal

to the market price plus 50% of the differential value, which for this example gave

an offer price of $62,500 and an estimated field margin of 28%. With an offer price

of $70,000, the estimated field margin is 36% compared to the location target field

margin  of  25%.  After  considering  the  current  market  conditions,  the  sales  team

decided to present an offer price of $70,000 because of the proven track record of

the advanced solution, supporters in the buying center who wanted to use the sup-

plier’s solution, and the intangible benefit of the excellent QHS&E record. Also, the

sales team was successful in addressing non-supporters by addressing their concerns

and by demonstrating the economic value of Company A’s solution.



The Moment of Truth



453





Expendables, supplies, maintenance, overtime, bonuses, transportation

of supplies and crews.



Customer contract conditions require payment in local currency. Cost per

month for hedging instrument, as provided by contracts manager.



Includes salaries of crew, depreciation of equipment ($13k/month), location

overhead and shared costs ($7k/month).



Incremental  Costs.  The  incremental  costs  for  the  project  are  entered  into  this

cell using the guidelines discussed above.



Risk  Financing.  Risk-financing  costs  for  the  project  are  entered  into  this  cell

using  the  guidelines  discussed  above  and  in  Chapter  10  “Competitive  Bidding.”

These  costs  could  be  included  in  the  incremental  costs  as  they  adhere  to  the

definition of incremental costs. In order to highlight the additional costs, however,

these  costs  are  entered  separately  in  preparation  for  the  negotiation  strategy.

They can be used to help the customer recognize the additional costs of increasing

your liabilities and risks and thus convince the customer to agree to a price reduc-

tion and take back certain risks.



Fixed Costs. The fixed costs for the project are entered into this cell using the

guidelines discussed above.



Project Field Margin. The project field margin is calculated by subtracting the

incremental, risk financing and fixed costs from the offer price. In the example

in Fig. 11.9, the computed monthly project field margin is $20,000.



Contribution  Margin.  The contribution margin is calculated by subtracting the

incremental and risk-financing costs from the offer price. In the example in Fig.

11.69, the monthly contribution margin for this project is $40,000. This is one of

the  factors  to  use  to  compare  projects  whenever  there  is  limited  availability  of

resources and choices have to be made on which projects to pursue. The contribu-

tion margin is also used to compute the value of the proposed offer when entering

negotiations.  The  goal  of  a  negotiation  is  to  create  a  win–win  deal  for  both  the

customer and your company where the contribution margin of the proposal stays

within an acceptable range. For the example project, the total contribution mar-

gin over the 12-month period is $480,000. Therefore, in a negotiation, trades are

Pricing Strategy Worksheet. This work-

sheet is used to develop a long-term pric-

ing strategy. As with the other pricing

worksheets, the white cells represent

data input, and the yellow cells are cal-

culated based on earlier inputs. The other

shaded cells should be reviewed and the

default values, which are calculated

using general guidelines, should be over-

written if necessary. In this worksheet,

the bar charts and labels are plotted

automatically as the values are entered.



454



CHAPTER 11  Pricing for Bidding



made  based  on  maintaining  an  equal  or  higher  contribution  margin  over  the

12-month period. Reducing incremental costs or increasing the scope of the proj-

ect over the 12-month period would achieve this. This aspect is discussed further

in Chapter 12 “Collaborative Negotiations.”



Discounting  Offer  Price  to  Match  Market  Price.  The  discount  percentage  is

calculated  by  subtracting  the  estimated  market  price  from  the  offer  price  and

then dividing by the offer price.



Premium Over Market Price—Customer Perspective. This premium is calculated

by subtracting the estimated market price from the offer price and dividing by the

market price. This percentage is used to evaluate the way the customer is expected

to view the difference between the offer price and the market price.



Your Company’s Share of Differential Value. The share of differential value is

calculated  by  subtracting  the  customer’s  price  ceiling  from  the  offer  price  and

dividing by the offer price.



Discount of Reference Price Catalog. The discount off the reference price cata-

log is entered in this cell. This param eter is useful for gauging the success of the

pricing  strategies  and  the  degree  of  competitiveness  among  the  markets  in  the

various regions.



Mark-up  on  Incremental  Costs.  The  mark-up  for  incremental  costs,  which  is  also

applied to the risk-financing costs, is entered into this cell.



Risk  Financing  as  %  of  Revenue.  This value is calculated by dividing the risk-

financing costs by the offer price. This percentage value is useful in negotiations.



Location Target Field Margin. The location target field margin is entered into

this field. This value is used to compute the Target Field Margin, which is plotted

on the pricing strategy chart.



Estimated Project Margin %. This percentage is calculated by dividing the proj-

ect  field  margin  by  the  offer  price  and  can  be  compared  against  the  location

target field margin.



Your Company’s Contribution Margin %. This percentage is calculated by divid-

ing the contribution margin by the offer price.



Estimated Contribution Margin at Market Price. This margin is calculated by

subtracting the incremental costs from the market price. It is used to determine

whether the competitor would be generating positive cash flow if the market price

seems too low.



Pricing Strategies



Three  basic  model-pricing  strategies  can  be  followed  for  any  given  opportunity

depending on a number of factors:



•  Long-term price



•  Short-term price excess capacity



•  Short-term price with limited capacity



Long-Term  Price  Strategy.  The  long-term  price  strategy  is  the  most  common

pricing strategy used for preparing tenders. The long-term price strategy prices

the product and services at the upper portion of the pricing window. The market

price and a share of the differential value drive the long-term price strategy. The

steps are:



•  Establish market price of competitors.



•  Determine and validate differential value with key buying center members.



•  Add a share of differential value to market price.



The Moment of Truth



455





Price set to secure project which is available using a short-term excess

equipment pricing strategy.



Expendables, supplies, maintenance, overtime, bonuses, transportation

of supplies and crews.



Customer contract conditions require payment in local currency. Cost per

month for hedging instrument, as provided by contracts manager.



Includes salaries of crew, depreciation of equipment ($13k/month), location

overhead and shared costs ($7k/month).



Fig. 11.10

Arriving at the offer price using a short-

term pricing strategy with excess equip-

ment. Even at these prices, the project

contributes positive cash flow. If the sit-

uation were to persist, future investment

decisions would have to be reviewed as,

at these prices, the targeted field

margins cannot be achieved and future

investments or the cost structure would

have to be reduced.



•  Check final price to ensure that the targeted field margin is achieved and,

if not, the prices are increased to meet the profit objective to result in your

company taking a larger share of the value.



•  Address buying center members’ negative pricing sensitivities by justifying

prices  with  added  value  of  solution  and  emphasizing  your  company’s

strengths.



In  cases  where  the  differential  value  is  not  sufficient  to  justify  the  higher

prices,  perhaps  due  to  depressed  market  prices  (competitors  taking  a  short-

term pricing strategy or having significantly lower fixed costs), extra effort must

be  made  to  address  buying  center  members’  negative  pricing  sensitivities  to

justify  the  offer  price.  In  such  cases,  it  is  critical  that  the  salesperson  begin

early in the opportunity management process to discover additional value driv-

ers  and  key  buying  center  members’  objectives  that  would  benefit  from  the

solution. The example in Fig. 11.9 illustrates arriving at the offer price using a

long-term pricing strategy.



If  the  opportunity  is  determined  to  be  strategic  and  the  pricing  differential

between your company and the competitor cannot be overcome by demonstrating

the value of the solution, a short-term pricing strategy should be considered.



Short-Term  Pricing  Strategy  Excess  Capacity.  The short-term pricing strategy

with excess capacity is used for very specific situations:



•  When there is excess capacity for the amount of activity worldwide where



long-term pricing strategies are used.



456



CHAPTER 11  Pricing for Bidding



•  The strategy can be defended by a rationale that does not send a signal to

the  competition  that  your  company  is  starting  a  price  war,  which  would

deteriorate the long-term pricing strategy.



•  The customer understands the restrictions of the pricing offer.



•  Sufficient differential value does not exist to achieve the long-term pricing



strategy.



•  Management has accepted that the project is not expected to achieve the



targeted field margin.



•  There  are  strategic  reasons  as  agreed  upon  by  upper  management  not  to

warrant the risk of losing the opportunity using long-term pricing strategies.



The short-term pricing strategy sets the price in the lower portion of the pricing

window. The offer price is set somewhere between the targeted field margin and

price floor. By definition, short-term pricing strategies are better suited and more

defendable for short-term, one-time projects. A possible situation where using a

short-term  pricing  strategy  is  appropriate  is  when  resources  are  idle  between

committed projects and the project does not need advanced services. In this case,

the price could be set at the market rate or slightly higher, with the stipulation

that the price applies only until the resources are needed for an upcoming project.

Figure 11.10 illustrates a situation where a short-term pricing strategy might be

warranted.



In this situation, it is believed that Competitor A will not raise prices to cover the

mobilization  of  additional  resources  and  will  offer  to  do  the  project  for  $30,000



Fig. 11.11

Opportunity Costs Worksheet for short-

term pricing strat egy with limited capacity.



The Moment of Truth



457





per month as excess equipment exists worldwide. In this scenario, the offer price

is $55,000 per month, which results in a 9% field margin—much below the tar-

geted  field  margin  of  25%.  At  this  price,  however,  the  project  would  generate  a

contribution margin of $25,000 per month.



Management and the sales team must answer the following questions in order to

choose the best course of action:



•  Do you still have time in the opportunities management process to try to

find more differential value to justify a higher price to achieve the target

margin?



•  Should you reduce the price as calculated on the short-term Pricing Strategy

Worksheet and miss the targeted field margin but still generate the contribu-

tion margin during a period of reduced activity, or should you risk generating

no revenue with the assets as they sit idle?



•  Will the short-term pricing strategy negatively impact the long-term pricing



strategy for future long-term contracts?



The last question is difficult to answer, but can be evaluated by considering the

timing and competitive threat for the upcoming long-term tenders.



Short-term  Pricing  Strategy  Limited  Capacity.  When  the  market  condition  is

limited in capacity, there are more tenders than you have resources to handle. In

this  situation  you  want  to  ensure  that  the  tenders  you  win  achieve  the  highest

contribution  margins  possible  with  the  current  resources.  In  the  situations  of

limited capacity, the pricing floor is increased as a result of opportunity costs.



Opportunity Costs. Opportunity costs are the difference between the contribution

margin of the best alternative and the contribution margin of the current oppor-

tunity you are trying to secure. Figure 11.11 illustrates the process used to arrive

equipment to cover the work. In this case,

opportunity costs is entered into the price

floor cell, and this price would be used as

the new offer price, which is $80,000.

CHAPTER 11  Pricing for Bidding



at the opportunity costs adjustment. In the Nirwana example, the offer price using

the long-term pricing strategy as described above was set to $70,000. The contribu-

tion  margin  at  that  price  was  $40,000  per  month.  In  another  opportunity  in

another region that is short on resources, however, there is another comparable

project, which was discovered during the market rate analysis step in Fig. 11.4.

The offer price for the best alternative was $85,000 and has a contribution margin

of $50,000. The opportunity cost for the Nirwana project is $10,000 per month. This

opportunity cost is then used to raise the Nirwana Company A offer price floor to

$80,000 (long-term pricing strategy Company A offer price + opportunity costs),

up from the previous price floor of $34,200 per month.



Therefore, for the Nirwana opportunity, the offer price now includes 120% of the

differential value, which will make the sale more difficult. However, if you cannot

win the Nirwana project at this price, you should pursue the other opportunity—

all  other  factors  being  equal.  The  pricing  workbook  for  the  market  condition  is

shown in Fig. 11.12.



As  shown  in  the  above  examples,  there  are  three  different  prices  ranging  from

$55,000 to $80,000, depending on the different market conditions. Managing the

customer’s reactions to each pricing scenario is the responsibility of the salesper-

son, who must ensure that, by the time the prices are presented as part of a formal

offer, the customer perceives the prices as fair and equitable and chooses to use

Company A for the project. The best chance you have to ensure that the customer

accepts your offer is to begin formulating the pricing strategy early in the oppor-

tunities management process and to keep in touch with the market pricing.



Advanced Pricing Model



The  pricing  discussion  to  this  point  has  focused  on  the  basic  pricing  model,

whereby the offer price is a function of the type of tender, market price, custom-

er’s  pricing  sensitivity,  and  a  chosen  pricing  strategy.  In  the  advanced  pricing

model, the additional factor of incentives is included to arrive at the offer. How-

ever, greater importance is placed on the level of relationship you have with the

customer and is typically used only for “Type 1 Enterprise Tenders.” The advanced

pricing  model  is  a  complex  and  involved  process,  and  as  the  size  of  the  project

and length of commitment grow, so does the necessity to involve all members of

the project team—from the sales staff to operations, contracts, finance, tax, and

management  review—before  submitting  an  advanced  pricing  model  (commonly

referred to as an incentive contract).



The goal of this section is to create an awareness of the considerations that must

be  taken  into  account  when  using  the  advanced  pricing  model  to  make  a  pro-

posal. This is becoming increasingly important within the B2B large sales envi-

ronment  when  companies  offer  some  form  of  performance,  or  results-based,

proposal. The essential nature of an advanced pricing model is that the revenue

received  may  vary  in  relation  to  factors  of  time,  quality,  volume  of  production,

safety, cost control, engineering accomplishment, and management performance

in any combination.



Advanced pricing models carry a greater risk to profit than a model based on unit

rates (basic pricing model). Whereas a unit rate contract essentially means that

the contractor is paid for a volume of usage, the incentive contract is based on

performance of a specific service for a share of the revenue saved or generated by

the  supplier.  If  the  risk  is  not  properly  calculated  and  defined,  the  contractor

could ultimately incur losses on the contract.



When incorporating any type of incentive into a tender, it is critical that all aspects of

the  model  be  clearly  worded  in  the  contract.  It  is  often  helpful  to  include  specific



The Moment of Truth



459





Fig. 11.13

Advanced pricing model components.

The main difference between the basic

pricing model and the advanced pricing

model is that, in addition to the price

schedule from which the basic pricing

model offer is based, the advanced pric-

ing model also includes an incentive

model as part of the remuneration formu-

la. In the advanced pricing model, much

more effort is given to fully understand-

ing the risk/reward implication of the

incentives. You must accurately capture

the intent of the risk/reward as part of

the contractual obligations and fully

agree on the scope of work and your con-

trol over the scope of work with the cus-

tomer. As the figure suggests a relation-

ship is crucial in deciding when to apply

an advanced pricing model.



examples. Before beginning the discussion on the incentive model selection process,

it  is  worth  repeating  that  the  customer  relationship  is  a  significant  factor  when

choosing the advanced pricing model. Before investing significant effort into devel-

oping an advanced pricing model, the sales team must  ensure the tender is  a Type

1 Enterprise. As mentioned earlier to confirm a Type 1 Tender, the  executives from

the  customer  and  your  company  should  be  discussing  the  customer’s  project  and

agreeing that an enterprise relationship is the best option for both companies. Only

this type of tenders using an advanced pricing model has a good chance of success.



If you are going to pursue an advanced pricing model for type 2 or type 3 Tenders,

then  you  must  adopt  the  defend  a  competitive  sales  strategy  and  know  which

executives will defend awarding your company the work when you are not evalu-

ated  the  strongest  against  their  formal  decision  criteria.  For  this  strategy,  you

must have proven value and show a direct impact of the business priorities the

executive has for the project.



Incentive Model Selection Process



Experience has shown that careful selection of a suitable commercial framework

is  central  to  realistic  and  effective  alignment  at  the  project  level  when  using

advanced pricing models. The objective is for your company to benefit from the

gains  to  be  made  through  effective  participation  in  a  project.  Customers  some-

times prefer the advanced pricing model, as they view it as a method of motivating

service providers to avoid the financial pain that flows from poor performance or

poor service attitude.



An advanced pricing model (Fig. 11.13) represents a means by which the success

or  non-success  of  a  project  can  be  translated  into  financial  terms.  Along  with  a

pricing schedule, the incentive model is applied to an agreed contractual scope of

work  under  a  legally  binding  agreement.  You  have  to  build  a  relevant  model  in

order  to  achieve  financial  success.  It  is  illogical  to  propose  a  quality  model  to

clients whose primary goal is to limit financial risk or a lump sum model to clients

whose highest priority is maximizing their operations. Both examples either mis-

understand or ignore the main objectives of the project. A vital competitive advan-

tage may be lost at the tendering stage, and even if the tender is awarded to your

company,  the  potential  value  of  the  commercial  schedule  can  be  significantly

reduced. A structured approach to selecting a suitable commercial framework and

incentive model can identify many of the key factors of a project’s success.

CHAPTER 11  Pricing for Bidding
