Fig. 11.14

Incentive Model Process, including

the seven phases and each

step in those  phases.



Seven key steps are taken in the process of developing incentive schemes (Fig. 11.14):



•  Identify the key objectives and hence simplify the process of setting rele-



vant performance benchmarks.



•  Define the scope of work and hence the roles and responsibilities of par-



ticipants in the project.



•  Clarify  what  is  expected  of  the  supplier  and  clearly  word  this  in  the



contract.



•  Ensure  that  the  incentive  model  for  the  project  is  achievable  within  the



scope of work you are contracted to undertake.



•  Align your commercial terms with your client’s objectives within the area



of your control.



Identification Phase 7



The identification phase consists of three steps, as follows:



•  Identify project objectives



•  Define scope of work



•  Identify the project’s position in the hydrocarbon cycle



In this phase, you identify the top-level business drivers that will shape the way

the selection process is conducted. All participants must have a clear understand-

ing  of  the  priorities  for  project  delivery,  not  only  for  the  commercial  model  but

also for the overall success of a project. Information gathered in the identification

phase determines the route of the rest of the steps.



Identify Project Objectives



This stage seeks to recognize the client’s objectives. Broadly speaking, these objec-

tives can be grouped into the elements described below. Although this list is by

no means complete, it provides a path by which for conducting a review.



Desire to Minimize Participation. Does the client want to commit as few of their

own people to the project as possible? For example, the client might be seeking to

do business in a new country where they have no infrastructure and no resources,



The Moment of Truth



461





yet the client does not wish to create one. Alternatively, the project economics may

be such that the client cannot justify applying internal resources to the project, or

the client might not have in-house expertise or access to technology.



Reduce Physical Risk. Is the client’s main concern to limit overall risk? Is the

client working on a fixed budget? For example, a client’s main objective could be

to stay within a spending budget given uncertainties about the area, field opera-

tions, etc. Typically, such a client would look for lump sum or turnkey arrange-

ments. This objective could also be associated with operational uncertainty due

to a lack of local experience.



Minimize  Performance  Risk.  In  the  same  way  that  a  client  may  want  to  limit

accidental or other risk, the same can apply to operational costs, either generally

or in the particular area of the project.



Maximize  Efficiency.  This  client  wants  to  encourage  operational  efficiency  by

reducing  time  and  costs  through  the  project.  Approval  for  expenditure  (AFE)

concerns  encourage  a  risk/reward  approach  to  ensure  alignment  and  therefore

increases  that  client’s  confidence  that  your  company  will  supply  efficient,  cost-

controlled  services.  For  an  example  of  an  efficiency-based  incentive  model,  see

Chapter Highlight 11.2: Advanced Pricing Model Example, Case 2.



Value  Enhancement  Initiative.  Adding value to a project can take many forms.

Examples of this in the energy industry are:



•  Production  enhancement  projects  and  delayed  abandonment  costs  by



extending the production output of mature oilfields.



•  In well construction tenders, the objective could be to maximize horizontal

drain exposure of the reservoir, thereby enhancing the relative value of the

wellbore in production terms.



If value enhancement is recognized as a main objective, it should be further ana-

lyzed to understand the overriding reasons behind the value driver:



•  Avoid additional cost



•  Improve economics



•  Improve performance against objectives



Understanding the reasons behind the desire to add value enables you to establish

priorities and address the real issues.



Final Quality of the Delivered Product. In some situations, quality or environ-

mental considerations take precedence over budgetary limitations, or are at least

considered essential to overall project success. A program in an environmentally

sensitive  area  cannot  be  considered  a  success  if  the  price  to  pay  for  meeting

financial targets is environmental damage.



Alternatively, in an exploration program where data acquisition is of significant

importance,  the  operational  budget  may  well  be  insignificant  compared  to  the

benefit  of  identifying  greater  reserves  through  an  extended  logging  or  testing

program.



Funding  Partnership.  A  growing  number  of  clients  look  to  suppliers  as  a  non-

equity partner to partially fund their projects, with payback in the form of a por-

tion of their future revenues. Often, their main reason is to optimize cash flow,

and if so, this must become a major component in the model.



Such  proposals  must  be  treated  differently  and  include  considerations  such  as

non-equity issues, repayment risks, risk of accident, and financial loss probability.

When financing is part of the tender, risk exposure in excess of your product and

services revenue must be carefully analyzed. This dictates the form of the agree-

ment and is significantly different from the basic pricing model. The basic pricing



462



CHAPTER 11  Pricing for Bidding



model  has  a  maximum  liability  that  is  the  cost  of  your  products  and  services,

provided  you  have  limited  the  contractual  risks  as  recommended  in  Chapter  10

“Competitive Bidding.”



Technical  Application.  If  the  project  depends  heavily  upon  the  development

and  application  of  new  technology,  these  drivers  should  be  reflected  in  the

incentive model in a similar way as the final quality considerations. The identi-

fication of the customer’s value drivers is more easily done early in the opportu-

nity management process, in the evaluation of options stage, and as part of the

ongoing account management process.



By identifying broad groups of drivers in this way, the choice of relevant incentive

model(s) becomes simpler. Above all, at this stage, it becomes easier to identify

which type of incentive is unsuitable and should therefore be eliminated.



Scope of Work Definition



The aim of this process is to define and agree on contractual responsibility. This

could be in the form of a list of as many predictable situations as possible, with

clear  agreement  as  to  which  responsibilities  are  within  your  scope  of  work.  The

scope of work definition is critical when considering any form of lump-sum contract

and would include not only performance items, but also liability risks. The identi-

fication of potential liability exposure could lead to the need for risk financing or

calculated provisions in your service charge to cover the eventuality of such occur-

rences. These considerations in turn influence the subsequent steps of the process.



Many  commercial  risks  can  be  offset  by  a  carefully  worded  scope  of  work  and

through the avoidance of ill-defined terminology. The price schedule should give

a clear declaration of what is included within the proposed prices, such as mobi-

lizations and maintenance. It might be necessary to add an assumptions adden-

dum to the pricing schedule.



Assessment Phase



Once the objectives and scope of work have been identified and agreed upon the

appropriate incentive scheme can be selected. This assessment assists in incen-

tive model selection and, more importantly, determines your business approach.

At  this  level  you  must  assess  which  business  strategy  is  likely  to  be  the  most

effective  in  addressing  the  overriding  objectives  of  your  client.  The  aim  of  this

process is more to eliminate options than to select them.



The incentive model would be one or more of the following:



•  Performance oriented



•  Engineering opportunities



•  Financed



Performance Models



Performance  models  revolve  around  the  cost  of  the  operation.  They  can  range

from modified turnkey or lump sum projects where the operator’s risk is lowest

and your risk exposure highest, to a more conventional pricing schedule linked to

a risk/reward scheme based upon cost performance against AFE. In performance

incentive models, it must be realized that it will be difficult to demonstrate added

value in non-cost efficiency terms. The ideal performance incentive model creates

a win–win situation where bonus payments are neither so high that the operator

feels abused, nor so low that the contractor is demotivated rather than incentiv-

ized. Customer risk limitation dominates this approach when subsea uncertainty

is high or financial resources are limited.



The Moment of Truth



463





Engineering Opportunity Models



Engineering  opportunity  models  focus  on  quality  and  efficiency  improvements.

Engineering  opportunity  models  recognize  some  value  to  your  services  beyond

market  prices.  Although  they  may  still  need  to  be  supported  by  a  risk  element,

clients  are  more  likely  to  accept  the  upside  as  a  gainshare  split  on  improved

performance.



Continuous improvement would play a significant role in this type of model, and

such arrangements promote the ability to build long-term technical and commer-

cial  relationships.  Technology  and  its  effective  application  would  hold  a  high

priority in reaching stretch targets in projects with considerable technical diffi-

culty, such as deepwater projects, as well as efficiency-driven projects in mature

fields, where a step change improvement could drive down overall costs. Although

this might be the main driver, you should not forget the cost implications that any

project must carry.



Financed Models



The financed models can occur in the form of risked service charges, upfront non-

equity  funding,  or  even  acceptance  of  deferred  payments,  and  as  such  must  be

treated  differently  from  other  proposals.  The  commercial  considerations  go

beyond those of the basic pricing model provisions.



Selection Phase



The main criteria influencing the choice of a model are more or less the same for

all three incentive models described above. However, the relative importance of

each criterion discussed varies enormously. For example, in a quality-driven proj-

ect where liabilities are totally accepted by the client, the relative importance of

insurance constraints or physical risk liabilities is much lower than, say, a risk-

dominated  model  where  the  client  must  remain  within  budget  limits  and  has

passed many risk issues to us, which you take some form of risk financing to cover.

Equally, it will be more difficult to demonstrate added value to a client who is risk

dominated than to a client looking for quality and efficiency improvements.



Risk Review



Steps  taken  in  the  risk  review  and  mitigation  stage  are  the  same  as  those  dis-

cussed in the basic pricing model. Except in advanced pricing models, the added

factor  of  incentives  puts  additional  emphasis  on  closely  examining  the  perfor-

mance  and  commercial  risks  associated  with  the  project  and  financial  model

being developed.



The key aspect to consider when reviewing the risk and risk mitigation is the need

to produce a risk evaluation matrix. To convert potential risk into actual financial

exposure, you require historic probability of occurrence, multiplied by the maxi-

mum cost impact. This analysis is often in the form of a worksheet, from which

all  realistic  eventualities  can  be  assessed.  Once  the  evaluation  has  been  made,

decisions on how to reduce the risks can be made. These can be in the form of

insurances, exclusions from the scope of work, or scaled increases in rates if you

decide to carry the risks.



When considering an advanced pricing model, the inclusion of incentives requires

additional  emphasis  on  evaluating  performance  and  the  resulting  commercial

risks.  This  is  the  form  of  risk  most  commonly  referred  to  in  commercial  terms.

However, this risk is often applied under a common misinterpretation. The pur-

pose of accepting risk with a client is primarily to demonstrate a willingness to

align with your client’s objectives through financial pain in the event of failure.



464



CHAPTER 11  Pricing for Bidding



The level of financial penalties applied to your services, which is necessary to encour-

age alignment, bears no direct relationship to the overall financial cost to your client.



For  example,  a  10%  penalty  on  your  services  may  represent  $50,000,  which  is

critical to your own profitability but has limited impact on a client’s AFE budget

of $5,000,000 (i.e., only 1%). Conversely, a positive result achieved due to good

performance  is  commonly  awarded  to  your  client  at  a  higher  rate  than  to  you

(i.e., a 10% upside on your services may bring $50,000, but to your client a 10%

 savings on $5,000,000 will yields $500,000). This simple calculation shows that a

±10% risk/reward scheme does not automatically represent corresponding ben-

efits  and  exposures  for  your  company  and  client.  Essentially,  this  type  of  risk

should be largely within your sphere of  control.



Performance risk, or more precisely the risk of poor performance, can result from

the  risk  of  accepting  technical  tasks  beyond  your  capability.  This  might  stem

from  an  overly  aggressive  commercial  proposal  for  the  scope  of  work  or  as  a

consequence of overly ambitious technical targets. Effectively, the end result is

reflected in commercial losses or penalties, limited contractually to a percentage

of your service revenue.



Large-scale advanced pricing model projects demand much more careful risk iden-

tification  and  assessment  than  traditional  basic  pricing  model  well  service  con-

tracts.  Such  projects  may  require  a  full  project  evaluation  using  input  from  all

functions—operations, QHSE, legal, finance, contract management, and risk man-

agement.



Main Drivers



Identifying  the  main  drivers  for  a  project  has  been  recognized  as  the  essential

first  step  in  selecting  a  business  framework.  That  same  information  should  be

used as a constraint in selecting the incentive model. For your clients to pay a

bonus,  success  must  be  clearly  defined.  If  the  incentive  model  is  not  clearly

aligned  to  this  success,  it  will  be  more  difficult  to  secure  full  and  undisputed

bonus payments.



Control



Although  the  incentive  model  should  reflect  the  main  drivers  for  a  project,  it  is

essential that it also be feasible within the agreed-upon scope of work. It is unwise

to commit to penalties based on achieving targets over which you have limited or

sometimes  no  realistic  control,  such  as  when  the  nature  of  the  project  requires

your  company  to  work  closely  with  another  contractor  in  whom  you  have  little

confidence or has no incentive to minimize costs. It would also be imprudent to

commit to an incentive scheme if the project is very complex and you do not have

sufficient  experience  in  these  types  of  operations.  These  situations  should  be

avoided by a clearly defined scope of work beyond which the incentive is not valid.



Added Value



The  purpose  of  an  incentive-based  model  is  to  provide  a  mechanism  by  which

performance  can  be  awarded  or  penalized  in  financial  terms.  As  such,  it  is  an

integral part of your pricing schedule and must therefore be compatible with this

schedule. It is also a means by which you can assess the added value you bring

to  your  clients  through  innovation  or  more  effective  planning,  which  is  not

directly related to your service or product charges.



As  mentioned  in  the  “Fair  to  You!”  sidebar,  the  advanced  pricing  model  is  used

when the applicable price schedule does not provide a pricing range sufficient to



The Moment of Truth



465





capture  a  fair  value  for  the  product  and  services  your  company  provides  the  cus-

tomer given the level of risk the customer asks you to take. Thus, additional finan-

cial terms must be clearly written and added as part of the financial terms of the

offer so that you have the opportunity to earn more revenue than if the products

and services were invoiced using the price book. This could be in the form of added

value incentives built around cash flow, net present value, or other value driver.



Lessons Learned



A successful incentive model must be flexible to adapt to the evolution of a proj-

ect. It may work well initially, but if the model is static and does not include an

expected  and  natural  performance  improvement,  it  could  be  open  to  various

interpretations of your entitlement.



As processes are improved, the benchmarks that your performances are measured

against  are  often  changed  to  reflect  the  new  standard,  and  opportunity  to  gain

payments decrease with time. If, however, a mechanism is incorporated into the

model  to  accommodate  lessons  learned,  there  will  be  less  justification  for  step

revisions. Such mechanisms result in an effective motivating model, as the con-

tinual improvement anticipated through the duration of the project is reflected in

the bonus system.



Verification Phase



The verification phase is the first step in an iterative process to check that the

incentive model proposed reflects the objectives and key drivers for the project.

You  must  check  the  model  to  ensure  that  it  promotes  the  intended  behaviors

needed  from  the  customer,  your  company,  and  your  partners  on  the  project  to

achieve the goals and reap the reward if success is achieved.



Refine Phase



Similarly, the refining step scrutinizes the scope of work within your control. This

process should satisfactorily answer the question, “Can your company achieve the

incentive payments without exceeding your agreed-upon scope of work?”



Application Phase



The  ultimate  goal  of  following  the  approach  described  in  these  guidelines  is  to

produce a coherent and relevant commercial framework that is competitive in a

tendering environment and suitably reflects the project goals. An undisputed pay-

ment in the event of success is the tangible result.



Once a framework and suitable incentive model have been chosen, the strategic

decision lies in determining to merge it into the pricing schedule, if at all. This can

only be achieved in the process described in this section. The pricing approach and

schedule take their form from a suitable incentive model. It is impossible to pro-

duce a coherent package if the components are assembled in the wrong order.



If, for example, you are confident that there are sources of an unidentified value

to be captured through your incentive model, you can propose a highly competitive

basic  pricing  model  to  achieve  success  in  a  competitive  situation  and  add  an

incentive model to capture a share of the added value delivered in a combination

of  basic  pricing  and  advanced  pricing.  In  many  cases,  a  combined  basic  and

advanced pricing model can result in a successful and relevant approach. If, how-

ever,  the  process  shows  that  there  is  little  realistic  incentive  bonus,  it  is  not

considered  unsuccessful;  rather,  it  has  identified  that  for  this  opportunity  the

basic pricing model is the most appropriate.



466



CHAPTER 11  Pricing for Bidding



Amendment Phase



The  final  step  of  the  process  is  that  of  periodic  reality  checks.  Rarely  does  one

model remain unchanged throughout the life of any but the shortest of projects.

Indeed, it is best to anticipate changing objectives within the model by making

the initial version valid for only a defined phase of operation or time. In this way,

the model coincides with the progression from the initial project phase, toward

full operations and management priorities. The timeframe on such advanced pric-

ing models is rarely less than one year and more commonly lasts five years or more

on larger projects.



The  purpose  of  using  the  advanced  pricing  model  is  to  motivate  better  perfor-

mance  and  sustain  it  throughout  the  project.  Also,  performance  risk  is  often

included  as  an  alignment  mechanism  to  discourage  poor  service  or  inadequate

cooperation through financial penalties. If the existing contractual model fails to

achieve these goals over time, due to either the evolution of the project or chang-

ing circumstances, there must be provision for its modification. Ideally, this should

be built into the contract. An agreed-upon mechanism can be of great benefit later

in the project when circumstances or personnel may change.



Incentive Contract Examples



There  is  no  standard  advanced  incentive  model:  The  guiding  principle  is  that  the

efforts of your company to improve the benefit to the client are rewarded by reference

to  a  predetermined  mechanism.  Usually,  the  structure  of  the  mechanism  is  bonus

based, but sometimes it involves a “malus” or penalty for overruns in time or costs.



The different types of incentive contracts can be categorized as follows:



•  Lump sum payment (LSP) models



•  Day-rate plus performance bonus models



•  Gainshare, risk/reward models



•  Reservoir development models



•  Field development and production models



•  Project management models



•  Performance benchmarking models



Chapter  Highlights  11.1  and  1.2:  Advanced  Pricing  Model  Examples,  two

examples  of  advanced  pricing  models.  The  first  is  a  basic  application  of  the

advanced pricing models, “Double or Nothing,” and the second is the “Beat the

Budget” incentive scheme used by a North Sea operator.



Calculating the correct bid price to win the work is not easy. There are levels of

uncertainty and risk with the projected TTV and EV revenues. The measure of cor-

rectness is not purely whether you won or lost the work. The correct price achieves

your long-term profit objectives while at the same time improves your competitive-

ness within your market and contributes to the customer’s success. Calculating the

correct bid price is a complex interaction of many factors. These factors include

market  strategies,  profitability,  the  customer’s  perception  of  your  value,  market

share, competitors and, perhaps as important as any factor, your relationship with

the  customer.  It  is  possible  to  price  correctly  by  considering  these  factors  and

splitting the pricing decision into processes that carefully analyze each of the fac-

tors.  This  work  is  part  of  the  professional  salesperson’s  and  bid  team’s  due  dili-

gence in bidding. Completing your due diligence will give your management confi-

dence in your bid strategy and TTV and your customer the confidence that what

you submit is your best price for their scope of work and terms and conditions.



The Moment of Truth



467





Chapter Highlight 11.1: Advanced Pricing Model Example, Case 1



Cook Inlet Advanced Pricing Perforating Project



Double or

Nothing!



In  the  Cook  Inlet  of

Alaska,  Operator  A  was

operating  several  off-

shore  production  plat-

forms. On several of the

platforms  the  producing

wells had a scaling prob-

lem. As the oil was pro-

duced,  heavy  precipi-

tates  were  also  pro-

time,  a

duced;  over

period time the production levels would drop off to less than the economic production rate, as the precipitates

would plug the perforation tunnels. When this happened, the Operator A production engineer would send out

a competitive tender. He would send this to Company A, Competitor 1, and Competitor 2 for through-tubing

reperforating for a package of four wells in order to bring the wells back up to acceptable production levels.

This procedure was done on a regular basis, and typically the work was split among the three service com-

panies over a period of a year.



At this time Company A introduced an advanced perforating gun; the sales engineer for Company A ran a perforat-

ing analysis. She was convinced that the advanced guns would more than double the production Operator A was

achieving with the standard perforating guns provided by Company A and the other service companies. It was

decided to offer the advanced guns using an advanced pricing model that included a double-or-nothing incentive

clause. The offer was for a commitment to perforate four wells with the advanced guns at no charge if the reper-

forating with the advanced guns did not double production. If they doubled the average production of 65 bbl/well,

Operator A would pay Company A $100,000 instead of the average revenue of $25,000 per well using standard

guns. The results of the four-well program are listed above.



In the end, Operator A almost tripled the average production from the four wells. The payout for the extra

perforating expense was 14 days. After the four-well project, Operator A asked for a bid for a one-year

contract for all the reperforating work. Operator A asked for a standard price per well and agreed to accept

the risks for normal operating failures. The agreed price was $50,000 per well. In this case, the willingness

of Company A and the engineering work of Company A’s sales engineer resulted in a win–win agreement.

Company A secured 100% of the re-perforating work at double the price, and Operator A realized triple

the production.



In  this  example,  the  project  began  as  a  competitive  bid  using  a  basic  pricing  model,  then  moved  to  an

advanced pricing model using a results-based incentive contract. Once the technology was proven, the project

finished with a basic pricing model as Operator A was willing to assume the risks for a lower price. However,

once the value had been proven, Operator A was satisfied with the new pricing, despite the 100% increase

over what it had been paying—little surprise when the added value of extra production was so explicit. The

secret  formula?  Company  A’s  value,  people  dedicated  to  trying  something  better,  and  a  solid  Level  2

Consultative relationship with the client.



However, there are times when the customer will come back to you and claim that

the offer is not good enough. Even after all the hard work and time spent getting

the numbers correct and knowing you submitted a winning offer, the customer will

ask  for  more.  Now  it  is  time  to  negotiate  in  a  collaborative  approach.  The  next

chapter, “Collaborative Negotiations,” will explain how.



468



CHAPTER 11  Pricing for Bidding



Chapter Highlight 11.2: Advanced Pricing Model Example, Case 2



Beat the Budget



Beat  the  budget  is  an  advanced

pricing  model  based  on  perfor-

mance  and  results. The  incentive

scheme  is  based  on  beating  the

targeted cost for a project as bud-

geted. The actual well construction

cost versus budget is used to split

the  savings  between  the  North

Sea  operator  and  the  contractors

on a sliding scale of additional dis-

counts,  which  is  capped  if  the

wells are over budget.



The diagram on the right graphically

illustrates  how  the  incentive  model

works. In addition, another incentive

quality multiplier for the contractor’s

profit can be included.



In  the  case  of  two  projects  for  the

North Sea operator, a percentage of

the wellbore multiplier was used.



The benefits of this model are that it:



•  Can be applied to single or



multiple wells;



•  Is simple to administer;



•  Promotes alignment and

reinforces teamwork.



Capped Risk,

e.g. 10%



The model works best for integrated services contracts with multiple company business line= participation.

It also requires active participation of all the key contractors.



The weaknesses of the model are that it:



•  Is heavily dependent on drilling efficiency;



•  Has effectively no scope of work limitation;



•  Does not take into account the operator’s upsides;



•  Has limited upside potential.



The Moment of Truth



469





Summary of Chapter Objectives



1.  Describe the basic pricing model.



The basic pricing model is used for the majority of bids. This model uses three analyses to identify the offer price: mar-

ket rate analysis, price sensitivity analysis, and pricing strategy analysis. Each analysis provides a critical price deter-

mination  for  the  preceding  analysis.  The  market  rate  establishes  the  competitor’s  TTV  for  the  tender,  which  is  the

benchmark you will use to establish your TTV. The market rate is used in the price sensitivity analysis to establish the

market-based  price  for  your  added-value  product  and  services.  The  pricing  sensitivity  analysis  groups  the  customer’s

price sensitivities into positive and negative factors and quantifies each in order to establish your company’s differential

value compared to the market or reference price. The sum of your company’s differential value and the market rate is

the total economic value of the solution. The final step of the pricing sensitivity analysis is to decide what is a fair and

equitable sharing of the differential value to identify the market-based price. The market-based price is compared to

the  financial  aspects  of  delivering  the  products  and  services.  The  financial  aspects  are  the  incremental,  fixed,  risk-

financing costs, and field margin targets. The final output is the offer price. The type of tender will determine exactly

what you enter in your bid for the customer to evaluate. Type 3 Transactional Tender you will make your TTV slightly

less than market rate. Type 2 Consultative Tender, depending on how your offer is positioned against your competition,

may be at or below market rate. If you are considered better than your competition, you are allowed to have a premium

price. If you are not better than your competition, then the competition is allowed to have a premium price and your

bid must be priced low enough compared to your competitors so their price is outside the allowed premium and you win.

For Type 1 Enterprise Tenders, you will present your market-based price adjusted for market conditions and your pricing

strategy. The final offer price is then presented to the customer supported by the published price book as part of your

bid submission. Many times, however, the customer’s acceptance is conditional upon acceptance of the offer price after

negotiations.  In  the  basic  pricing  model,  the  analysis  is  organized  into  a  pricing  workbook  to  simplify  the  process.

Included in the workbook is the Negotiation Worksheet that takes input from all three of the basic model analyses to

prepare a negotiation strategy.



2.  Understand when to use the different pricing strategies.



Part of the market rate analysis involves assisting your team in selecting a pricing strategy for arriving at your offer price.

There are three pricing strategies. The first and most common pricing strategy is the long-term pricing strategy, which

is driven by the differential value and field margin objectives. The market-based price is compared to the target field

margin of the location servicing the project. If the market-based price does not meet the targeted field margin, it is

adjusted by taking a larger share of the differential value. The second strategy is the short-term excess capacity pricing

strategy.  Anyone  with  more  than  a  few  years  of  B2B  sales  experience  will  have  gone  through  a  downturn  in  activity

resulting in excess capacity. In such market situations, pricing pressures are intense among industry competitors. The

short-term excess capacity strategy focuses more on the contribution margin calculated by subtracting the incremental

costs from the offer price. It is possible to have a positive contribution margin and a negative field margin. The question

in a period of excess is “Does it make better sense to secure the project at a lower price than to lose the project and

risk not only a negative field margin but also a negative cash flow?” The third pricing strategy is the short-term limited

capacity pricing strategy. This pricing strategy adds opportunity costs for other projects that your company could secure

at a better contribution margin than the project being pursued. The opportunity costs act as a hurdle. If the current

project cannot be secured at the prices with the adjustment for opportunity costs, your company is better off to do the

alternative project.



3.  Describe the advanced pricing model.



The advanced pricing model includes all the parts of the basic pricing model plus incentives. Incentives are included as

part  of  the  financial  framework.  The  advanced  pricing  model  is  to  be  used  with  Type  1  Enterprise  Tenders  only.  The

advanced pricing model is typically used to motivate partners in a project to higher levels of performance by compensating

the higher levels of performance above a target with incentives; if the target is not achieved, the partners suffer penalties.



470



CHAPTER 11  Pricing for Bidding





The advanced pricing model offers greater financial reward than the basic pricing model; however, the greater financial

reward is typically attached to greater risk, which is why the advanced pricing model requires the full participation of the

project team and management’s commitment and review. The first step in deciding whether an advanced pricing model is

appropriate is to decide if the relationship between your company and the customer is strong enough to ensure a win–win

outcome. As the advanced pricing model requires much more effort, it is only appropriate for Type 1 Enterprise Tenders.



4.  Understand the process for selecting incentive models for the advanced pricing model.



The selection process of the incentive model in the advanced pricing model consists of seven phases. The identify phase

consists of two steps: First, identify the customer’s project objectives and have a clear understanding of the scope of

work. The better and more complete this phase, the easier the following phases. Assess the most suitable commercial

framework and incentive scheme given the project’s objectives and scope of work. There are three main types of incen-

tive  schemes,  based  on  one  or  a  combination  of  performance,  efficiency,  or  financing.  After  assessing  each  potential

incentive scheme, one or a combination of schemes are selected in the select phase. In the select phase, the potential

scheme is put through a rigorous check for risks, drivers, degree of control, and value. In the verify phase, the incentive

model is verified as part of an iterative approach. The main question to be answered in this phase is “whether the model

reflects the project objectives. After the verify phase, the model is fine-tuned for the scope of work in the redefine phase.

The  incentive  model  is  then  added  to  the  commercial  framework  along  with  a  pricing  schedule  to  describe  how  the

remuneration procedure will be implemented in the Apply phase. The last phase is carried out over the life of the proj-

ect. The amend phase reassesses the model as each new phase during the project is entered. As a project progresses

over the years, the original objectives, risks, profitability, etc., change, and the initial advanced pricing model financial

framework or even incentive model may have to be amended.



Applying the Concepts



1.



In your location today, how does your pricing process compare to the basic pricing model processes described in the

chapter?



2.  How much time do you spend today on the pricing decision, and who has the ultimate responsibility for setting the



offer price?



3.  What percentage of your opportunities are priced using the basic pricing model?



4.  What percentage could be possible candidates for an advanced pricing model?



5.  On average, what portion of the differential value do you share?



6.  What is the typical amount of differential value the customer acknowledges that your company has over the com-



petitor?



7.    For an upcoming opportunity, complete the basic pricing model workbook worksheets 1–3. Consider how the price

changes  as  you  identify  the  market  rate,  the  market-based  price,  and  the  offer  price.  For  the  same  opportunity,

identify an incentive model that could be applied. Which of the three models or combination of models makes the

most sense: performance, efficiency, or financing? Does the relationship as it exists today support an advanced pric-

ing model?

Co. (1985); and “Build Customer Relationships that Last,” Harvard Business Review (Nov.-Dec. 1985), 120–128.



In the Dark Ages, merchants were put to death for exceeding public norms regarding the “just price.” In the more recent dark

history of communism, those who “profiteered” by charging more than the official prices—at which the state was unable to meet

demand—were regarded as criminals. Even in modern market economies, “price gougers” are often criticized in the press, has-

sled by regulators, and boycotted by the public. Consequently, it is well worth your time to understand this phenomenon.

472



CHAPTER 11  Pricing for Bidding



12



Chapter Objectives



After reading this chapter, you

will be able to:



n  Describe the process of col-

laborative nego tiations.



n  Understand the planning



stages and know how to use

the negotiation planning

tools.



n  Be able to deliver and nego-

tiate an agreement in a

negotiation meeting.



n  Know how to react to com-



petitive negotiators and their

tactics.



Collaborative

Negotiations



Successful Negotiations



“The  only  place  success  comes  before  work  is  in  the  dictionary.”1  Successful  negotia-

tions,  as  measured  by  a  win-–win  agreement,  require  that  your  team  invest  time  and

effort in managing your opportunity from start to finish. It is a lot of work, but as men-

tioned many times already, it is worth the effort needed to win in competitive environ-

ments. This chapter references many concepts, processes, and tools discussed earlier in

the  textbook  particularly  in  the  section  “Selling  in  Competitive  Environments.”  The

earlier content forms the foundation and preparation for a collaborative negotiation.



Negotiations  are  a  normal  part  of  an  ongoing  business  relationship  with  your

customers.  The  goal  of  this  chapter  is  to  provide  you  with  the  skills  needed  to

conduct  a  successful  negotiation  while  strengthening  the  relationship  between

you and the customer. It is not unusual, even after preparing a detailed scope of

work, that the customer wishes to change your offer. Your customers should never

feel your submitted offer is final, so in a spirit of collaborative negotiation, it is

your  responsibility  to  work  with  the  customer  to  better  satisfy  the  customer’s

needs and still maintain the value of your initial offer. This is achieved by taking

a flexible approach to finding mutually beneficial trades to modify the offer. This

chapter describes the processes you can follow to prepare and skillfully manage

the negotiation meeting to arrive at a win–win agreement.



Finally,  you  know  that  not  all  negotiators  will  come  to  a  negotiation  with  the

principled  approach  described  above.  Customers  may  use  pressuring  tactics  to

obtain a concession from you in an effort to secure a better deal for their company.

The last part of this chapter discusses how to effectively handle competitive nego-

tiators in an effort to get them back on track to finding a win–win agreement.



Types of Negotiations

Negotiators generally adopt one of two types of negotiating strategies:



•  Competitive or zero-sum negotiation, in which the parties compete over the



distribution of the benefits of an agreement.



Collaborative Negotiations



475





•  Collaborative  or  win–win  negotiation,  in  which  the  parties  cooperate  to

achieve maximum benefits by integrating their interests into an agreement.



Most, if not all, negotiations combine elements of each type. There will be some

direct  competition  between  the  parties’  interests  and  goals  as  well  as  some

opportunities to integrate the parties’ interests and preferences. The negotiator’s

dilemma  is  to  determine  whether  the  other  party  is  acting  collaboratively  or

competitively. See “The Negotiator’s Dilemma” side-

bar for a more complete description.2



u The Negotiator’s Dilemma



Most business negotiations are neither pure zero-sum or pure

win–win situations; rather, competitive and cooperative ele-

ments are intertwined. The resulting tension, known as “the

negotiator’s dilemma,” requires difficult strategic choices. It

means balancing competitive strategies that make it hard to

cooperate and create value effectively, with cooperative strate-

gies that make it hard to compete and claim value effectively.

Knowing whether to compete where interests conflict—claim-

ing more instead of less—or to create value by exchanging

information that leads to mutually advantageous options is at

the core of the negotiator’s art.



One should also know the possible outcomes of a negotiation

before sitting down at the negotiation table. There are four pos-

sible outcomes to a negotiation:



•  Lose-–lose: Both parties lose with this result. It  happens



when you and your customer are indifferent to the outcome

of the negotiation.



•  Lose–win: This is a concession for the customer. It happens

when you are not prepared to negotiate, when you are trying

to avoid conflicts, or when you are not aware of the value

creation your proposal brings to the table. This is a zero-sum

negotiation.



•  Win–lose: This is a positional result for you. It happens when

you are not considering the long-term  relationship with your

customer. This is a short-sighted and short-lived type of deal,

a zero-sum negotiation.



•  Win–win: This is a principled result for all. Both  parties coop-

erate to achieve maximum benefits by integrating their inter-

ests into an agreement. It  happens when you are prepared

and when you know the value that you bring to the table.



As you are in a business environment and need to maintain

a customer relationship over time, the win–win result should be

your goal.



The  best  business  relationship-building  negotiating

strategy  is  one  of  collaboration.  Collaborative  nego-

tiations improve the value to both parties and create

stronger  business  relationships.  When  confronted

with competitive negotiators, your strategy is to per-

suade  the  other  negotiator  to  adopt  a  collaborative

approach  and  share  in  the  value  of  a  better  negoti-

ated agreement.



Negotiation and Value

Successful negotiations are a process that adds value.

This  is  the  context  in  which  negotiations  are  dis-

cussed in this chapter. Negotiation is defined as the

process by which:



•  Two  sides  review  and  explore  the  trades  for  the

technical, service, and financial aspects of an offer.

A  trade  is  the  process  of  submitting  offsetting

counterproposals in response to customer issues.3



•  A key element of a negotiation is that neither side

is  forced  to  make  concessions.  A  concession  is  a

unilateral  giving  up  of  a  proposal  component

(price reduction, performance guarantee, scope of

work increase, etc.) without an offsetting benefit

from  the  other  side.  Instead  of  making  conces-

sions,  the  negotiating  process  focuses  on  setting

up and gaining agreement on trades.



•  The  goal  is  to  develop  a  mutually  satisfactory

agreement that addresses the needs of both sides.



The trades are based on an analysis of negotiable components and how much can be

traded in return for something of equal value from the customer. A good trade pro-

tects the overall value of the proposal to you while meeting the needs of the customer.



A successful negotiation maintains or improves the value of a sale going into the

negotiation  while  at  the  same  time  strengthening  the  relationship  between  the

customer  and  you.  An  efficient  negotiation  wastes  little  time  with  negotiating

tactics  and  self-interests;  rather,  it  moves  to  implement  an  agreement  with  a

minimum amount of effort and maintenance.



Negotiations happen for many reasons:



•  As a formal part of the opportunities management process, some companies

before the final award must, as defined by their procurement process, com-

plete a negotiation meeting with the short-listed suppliers.



•  In  other  cases,  customers  wish  to  modify  one  or  more  components  of



your offer.



476



CHAPTER 12  Collaborative Negotiations



•  Competitive negotiators believe they must always negotiate to get a better deal.



•  Negotiations can take place internally as part of the determination of what

is a fair and equitable sharing of the economic value provided by your solu-

tion. It is a compliment to your team when, after reviewing your offer, the

customer decides there is no need to negotiate.



•  You initiate a negotiation in an effort to increase the value of your solution

after the offer has been submitted. Often, you must submit an offer accord-

ing to the customer’s request for proposal format. Often, too, you can make

a better offer that gives the customer and you more value, and this would

be made in the form of a negotiation.



Negotiating can be a simple or complex procedure, depending upon the situation, and

can involve short-term or long-term objectives. Whatever the specifics of the situation,

the process and the goal should remain the same. Figure 12.1 illustrates the ideal

negotiation  as  described  by  “pareto  optimality.” 4  In  an  ideal  situation,  negotiators

can maximize the total value derived from an initial offer by presenting trades that

move the value of the agreement closer to the efficient limit. An agreement is said to

be pareto optimal when none of its terms can be changed to the benefit of one side

without diminishing its value to the other. In your environment, it is your goal to aim

for the efficient agreement by improving your initial offer through trades that make

the agreement more valuable to both you and your customer. The remaining sections

of this chapter describe the processes you use to reach an efficient agreement.



Negotiation Process

To assist your team in preparing for the negotiation meeting, follow a five-stage

negotiation process:



•  Situation analysis: A comprehensive method for reviewing the key factors



involved in a negotiation.



•  Proposal analysis: Building a proposal with specific components  for dis-

cussion in the negotiation meeting and preparing a trades and negotiation

strategy.



•  Negotiation meeting: Specific techniques and skills for presenting a nego-



tiation proposal.

Successful Negotiations



477



Fig. 12.1

The ideal negotiation. The diagram represents

the ideal negotiation. In the ideal negotiation,

each party makes trades that improve the

value to the other party in an effort to reach

the most efficient limit of the agreement. The

pareto optimal agreement point is where

there is an equal sharing of the value of the

agreement between the two parties. In the

B2B sales environment, you submit an initial

offer, which is the starting point for negotia-

tions, and then attempts to improve upon the

initial offer by trading components of the

offer that are valuable to you but do not cost

the customer a lot as well as components

that are valuable to the customer but do not

cost you a lot. These are called high-quality

trades and are discussed later in this module.

An example of a high-quality trade is where

the customer agrees to use their air charter to

move people and equipment (this does not cost

the customer a lot as they pay for the charter

anyway), and in return you will not charge

crew standby charges. This is illustrated with

the first trade point. You would then continue

to trade until you reach the efficient limit for

the agreement or you run out of trades.











Fig. 12.2

Preparation is the key to negotiation suc-

cess. Negotiations are part of the normal

business relationship and procurement

process stages 3b through 3f. You use

the opportunities management process

to be better prepared for negotiations. In

stage 3—the evaluation of options—you

identify the optimal solution based on

the customer’s requirements and amount

of differential value your solution pro-

vides. You then decide on a fair and equi-

table sharing of the value to arrive at an

offer.  The sharing of the value is affect-

ed by the “Type of Tender.” Throughout

the opportunity management process,

you try to build buying center support for

your company and your solution. Once

you arrive at the negotiation meeting,

your focus turns to maintaining or

improving the value of your offer for both

the customer and your company.

Immediately after the nego tiation meet-

ing, you follow up with key buying center

members to ensure that the contract is

signed and to prepare for any last-minute

negotiations resulting from competitors’

maneuvers or competitive negotiators’

late-stage tactics. Once you begin the

implementation (4) and performance

review (5) stages, you follow up to

ensure that the expected value levels of

the agreement were provided as prom-

ised for both the customer and you.



•  Competitive  negotiators:  A  process  for  handling  competitive  negotiators



and dealing with their tactics



Each component is discussed in more detail below. It is important to realize that

preparing  for  negotiations  is  a  constant  process  that  benefits  from  following

through on each stage of the opportunity management process. The negotiation

process described in this chapter is only as effective as your management of the

opportunity. Figure 12.2 represents how the opportunities management process

facilitates preparing for a negotiation. It also demonstrates that, after the negoti-

ated agreement is signed, you must follow up with the service delivery team during

the implementation (4) and performance review (5) stages to track your perfor-

mance. This ensures that the value of the win–win agreement is realized and that

the customer has followed through on their end of the agreement.



Situational Analysis



A  situational  analysis  is  a  review  of  what  you  have  learned  and  accomplished

throughout the opportunities management process up to the point in time of the

negotiation meeting. As shown in Fig. 12.3, the situational analysis reviews the

key inputs used to develop your initial offer. By completing a thorough review of

all  the  factors  affecting  the  outcome  of  a  negotiation,  you  place  your  team  in  a

position of power in the negotiation meeting. See the “Negotiating Power” sidebar

for more ideas on how to increase your negotiating power.



The situational analysis, is done by completing a Level 1 assessment review prior

to the negotiation meeting to establish your relative strength in the four strategic

questions:



•  Is there an opportunity?



•  Can you compete?



•  Can you win?



•  Is it worth winning?



If a strategic sales plan was created for the opportunity, it is a simple process to

update the most recent Level 1 assessment to reflect any changes in the assess-

ment since the last assessment date. For large and strategically important nego-

tiations, it is a good practice to complete a Level 2 peer review to fully assess the

situation  prior  to  negotiation.  If  a  strategic  sales  plan  was  not  created  for  the

opportunity, an assessment can be completed as described in Chapter 9 “Strategic

Sales Plan.”



478



CHAPTER 12  Collaborative Negotiations



u Negotiating Power



Negotiating power is not to be confused with negotiating authority. Power has several aspects:



•  The ability to persuade.



•  The ability to motivate a competitive negotiator to negotiate in a collaborative fashion.



•  Credibility equal to that of the other negotiating party, which gives the other party confidence that you can carry through



on your trades.



Power can come from different sources. Any area in which you can improve your power may be the difference between

being able to negotiate a win–win agreement and not being able to negotiate at all. Consider the following power sources

before your next negotiation.



•  Knowledge of the other negotiator(s). Power that comes from learning as much as you can about the other negotiator

before the negotiation, including information such as types of negotiation style and tactics used and business and person-

al agendas. You can find this information from others who have negotiated with the person and in the CRM database.



•  Situational. Power inherent in the situation. Examples are deadlines, risks, and the customer’s best alternatives. This



information can be gained from mentors and supporters in the customer’s organization, previous experiences, and your

knowledge about competitors’ capabilities and capacity.



•  Personal. Your own reputation within the customer’s organization of delivering on your commitments; your ability to nego-

tiate effectively and to build trust and relationships. You can improve your own ability to nego tiate effectively by gaining

experience, doing role-plays prior to the negotiation, and having very good sales communication skills. Improvements in

personal power can be achieved by attending negotiation seminars. Building your reputation and relationships comes with

a proven track record.



•  Information. Knowledge about the negotiation issues and customer position as well as  circumstances such as possible

barriers to accepting trades. The best source for information is the work invested in the opportunities management pro-

cess, discussion with contacts in the customer’s organization, and your team.



•  Preparation. Having a well-developed  negotiation strategy with high-quality trades. Time invested in preparation produces the

greatest returns. If you go into a negotiation with a blank sheet of paper, you will probably leave the negotiation unsuccessful.



Strategic Questions



Of the four strategic questions, it is assumed that “Is there an opportunity?” has

been answered as part of the pursuit decision discussed in Chapter 4, “Opportuni-

ties  Management.”  For  your  situational  analysis,  focus  on  the  other  three  ques-

tions in more depth.



Can You Compete?



To answer, “Can you compete?,” as described in Chapter 9 “Strategic Sales Plan,”

you must have completed the vulnerability analysis and customer pricing sensitiv-

ity analysis. At this point, it is critical that you establish your validated differential

value and have clear strategies for handling any drawbacks your solution may have

compared to competitors’ solutions.



If you are dealing with a competitive negotiator, you must be prepared to deflect

the negotiator’s attempts to leverage your drawbacks to gain price concessions.

The  best  strategy  is  to  outweigh  the  drawback  with  your  differential  value  and



Fig. 12.3

A situational analysis reviews the

key factors considered when decid-

ing on your initial offer and the

reaction to the offer from the buy-

ing center and, in particular, mem-

bers of the negotiating team. The

more thorough the analysis of the

key factors, the stronger your posi-

tion in the negotiating meeting.



intangible benefits.



If you do not have sufficient

value to overcome any weak-

nesses in your offer, then you

must  have  a  longer  list  of

trades  to  make  the  offer

more  attractive.



The bottom line to knowing

whether you can compete is

determining how much dif-

ferential  value  your  solu-

tion  brings  to  the  negotia-



Successful Negotiations



479





u You Can Offer 5%, But You Do Not



A major oil company was soon to enter the

next phase of a costly exploration program

and had made a decision to purchase a more

sophisti cated surface seismic interpretation

workstation. Company A and a competitor

were short  listed as the preferred  systems.

The exploration manager was personally

involved in the negotiation to select the

 winner. In the negotiation meeting with

Company A, the exploration manager stated

that they preferred to use Company A’s

 system but he could not justify the 35%

higher prices between Company A’s and

Competitor A’s systems. He asked if anything

could be done to reduce the gap.



The salesperson responded that the best he

could offer was another 5%, but Company A’s

team decided not to do that. What they would

offer is their top geophysicist and data loader

for the first three months of the agreement if

the exploration manager would agree to a

three-license agreement instead of the two

requested.



The exploration man ager said he could not

commit at that moment, but would let

Company A team know in the next few days.

He also said he was hoping for more.



The next day, the exploration manager called

to award the work to Company A.



What Company A’s salesperson knew was

that the exploration manager and his team had

significant  personal incentives based upon

completing the seismic interpretation to

decide on the next drilling locations by the

end of the year (the negotiation took place in

September).



Company A’s geophysicist, who had been

committed to the project, was well respect-

ed in the industry as an excellent interpreter

and was well known to the exploration man-

ager’s team.



The competition was shocked as they thought

they had made the exploration manager an

offer he could not refuse.



tion  table.  Without  knowing  this,  you  are  in  a  weak  negotiating

 position.



Can You Win?



To answer “Can you win?” you must have completed the buying center

analysis  described  in  Chapter  9  “Strategic  Sales  Plan.”  At  this  late

stage in the opportunity, you must determine the following:



•  Who will be on the customer’s negotiation team? It is critical

to know this before entering the negotiation meeting. Every

contact you have in the buying center is one less variable in

the negotiating meeting.



•   Have  your  non-supporters  and  enemies  been  neutralized?  If

not, will they try to block or prevent you from negotiating an

acceptable agreement?



•  Who are the mentors and supporters you can leverage during

the  negotiation  process?  Can  they  assist  in  getting  trades

accepted if resisted by the non-supporters and enemies? If so,

what type of support and documentation will they need from

you?  You  may  also  need  assistance  from  your  mentors  and

supporters  if  the  negotiations  span  several  days,  weeks,  or

months.  If  your  supporters  are  not  directly  involved  in  the

negotiations,  you  may  need  to  contact  them  between  meet-

ings to update them on the progress of the negotiations or to

ask for their support. As part of your negotiation strategy, you

must include a contacting strategy to pass information to or

request information from your supporters.



•  For  the  influential  buying  center  members,  are  there  any

businesses and personal priority items for which you can build

trades? By understanding what is important to the influential

members, you can focus your attention on developing trades

that address their needs. Knowing what they consider to be

personal wins, as discussed in the buying center profile sec-

tion  of  Chapter  9,  “Strategic  Sales  Plan,”  you  can  fine-tune

your trades to have the maximum impact. See the “You Can

Offer 5%, But You Do Not” sidebar for an example of how trad-

ing on a personal objective secured a contract.



If it is determined that you cannot win, you want to adopt a disengage

strategy,  as  described  in  the  competitive  sales  strategy  section  of

Chapter  4  “Opportunities  Management,”  and  use  the  negotiation

meeting to defend your offer.



Is it Worth Winning?



To answer “Is it worth winning?” you must have completed the pricing strategy, as

described in Chapter 11 “Pricing for Bidding,” to ensure that you have identified

the financial objective of the negotiation and the account development plans to

identify the strategic objective of the negotiation. Any negative areas in the assess-

ment can be possible areas to focus on in the negotiation. If during the negotiation

the weak areas can be improved, suitable trades can be prepared. For example,

you may want to increase the scope to improve short- or long-term revenue poten-

tial. If there are areas that represent additional risks, your team should explore

trades that address these areas.



480



CHAPTER 12  Collaborative Negotiations



For any important opportunity, you complete a strategic sales plan as part of the

opportunities management process in preparation for the negotiation. By complet-

ing this task, the Level 1 assessment can be easily updated and, as a good practice,

you must always conduct a Level 2 peer review as part of the situational analysis

for the upcoming negotiation.



Ideally, you should begin strategic planning as early as possible in the opportunity

management process. By starting early, time becomes your team’s advantage, and

any negative areas of the assessment can be addressed prior to the negotiations.



If the completed situational analysis turns up numerous negative areas, your offer

is likely in a weak position. The project is probably at risk of being turned over to

a competitor or, at the very least, the offer is vulnerable to competitive negotiators.

When  dealing  with  competitive  negotiators,  the  upfront  work  gives  negotiators

confidence and places them in a position of power from which to negotiate.



With the completed situational analysis, you are ready to complete the proposal

analysis.



Proposal Analysis



In a situational analysis, you determine how strong or weak your position is likely

to be in the negotiation meeting. Using this information, you can build a negotia-

tion proposal, identify trades, and create a negotiation strategy that leverages your

strengths and minimizes your weaknesses. The goal is to build a win–win proposal

that improves your initial offer and secures an agreement beneficial for you and

the customer. The following steps go into completing a proposal analysis:



•  Set objectives



•  Determine negotiable and non-negotiable points



•  Build trades for both you and the customer



•  Build a negotiation strategy



•  Review the negotiation proposal



Negotiation Objective



To determine what you have to offer, you must know what you want to accomplish.

The  first  step  in  determining  the  negotiation  range  is  to  set  an  objective.  The

objective consists of two parts:



•  Strategic objective



•  Financial objective



Strategic Objective



As described in the strategic sales plan, each opportunity should describe how the

opportunity is going to be used to develop your position in the account, such as to

improve or expand your current relationship or to establish a relationship. Other

strategic objectives might include marketing issues such as establishing a pres-

ence in an area, introducing a new product, or gaining experience in a particular

environment. With the strategic objectives identified, your team then decides the

value associated with each strategic objective to establish appropriate trades as

part of the negotiation strategy. For example, your team may agree that the cus-

tomer can release data for a case study in support of marketing a new technology

included in the scope of work in exchange for eliminating rental charges for the

specific technology.



Successful Negotiations



481





Financial Objective



The  financial  objective  is  the  targeted  contribution  margin  as  identified  in  the

pricing  strategy.  The  targeted  contribution  margin  becomes  the  benchmark  for

building financial trades. For example, if the customer has indicated they need

another 15% discount, you may agree as long as they increase the scope of work

to include two rigs instead of one, giving you a 50% larger contribution margin over

the  same  period  due  to  increased  activity  and  efficiencies  gained  from  two  rigs

sharing equipment and personnel.



Determine Negotiable and Non-Negotiable Points



With the financial objectives set, you then classify the expected or stated customer

issues and demands as negotiable and non-negotiable.



Negotiable



Negotiable  means  that  business  movement  from  your  viewpoint  is  possible.  For

instance, a price adjustment of a few percentage points may be negotiable in a

specific situation, as described in the financial objective example. For each nego-

tiable item, you prepare trades.



Non-Negotiable



Not  everything  is  negotiable.  The  safety  of  your  employees,  your  business  and  moral

ethics, and certain liabilities (formation damage or reservoir damage) are examples of

non-negotiable aspects of an agreement. Non-negotiable issues are items that are not

traded in the negotiation. These non-negotiable items are also known as “deal breakers.”



You must probe and confirm to build as completely as possible your understanding

of a customer’s request before labeling it as non-negotiable. You must understand

why and what benefits the customer may derive from a non-negotiable item. You

must not assume that the issue is non-negotiable on the grounds that the customer

might not be willing to pay for all the costs of implementing a request. You must

always  present  all  aspects  of  an  issue  so  the  customer  can  fully  understand  the

implications of a request and the additional costs required to meet that request.



For example, the customer might request that equipment and crew be on stand-

by 24/7. You cannot label this item as non-negotiable just because you assume

the customer would not want to pay for a dedicated crew. Instead, the salesper-

son must inform the customer of the cost of a dedicated crew so the customer

can decide whether a dedicated crew is that important.



In a different scenario, however, the same request made by a customer in an area

where you have only enough crews and equipment to cover committed work is a

non-negotiable item. Handling a non-negotiable issue is covered in the presenta-

tion plan section of this chapter.



With the negotiable and non-negotiable items identified, you then build trades for

negotiable items.



Build Trades



Your ability to build trades or your lack of trades can mean the difference between

a  successful  or  failed  negotiation—a  failure  not  only  in  the  case  of  losing  the

work, but also a failure when the value of the negotiated agreement is much less

than the value of the offer initially given to the customer.



Increasing  the  number  of  trades  is  important  as  the  more  options  you  have  to  offer,  the

greater your options and the better your chances of closing the deal. In any negotiation, a

key objective is to avoid take-it-or-leave-it situations by having a well-developed list of trades.

Fig. 12.4 Break-even analysis. In this worksheet, the negotiating team has built a financial model to calculate the break-even point for changes in volume,

price, incremental costs, and fixed costs for the specific tender. The base case for the calculations is shown on the right. Each change in one of the factors is

input into the appropriate factors in the appropriate break-even calculation boxes. Beneath the cell is the break-even change in number of jobs or volume

required to maintain the total contribution margin for the project at the same value as computed for the base case. For this example, the targeted project contri-

bution margin is $700,000. Therefore, the goal of any trades should be to maintain or improve the contribution margin at or above $700,000. In this example,

the customer has requested an additional 10% discount; this would be acceptable provided the customer commits to increasing the volume by 16.67%, or 33

jobs. In the actual case, the customer agreed to increase the scope of work by 50 jobs, which were pulled from a competitor’s project.



The benchmark you use to measure success is the targeted contribution margin

for the project as decided by the team during the pricing strategy session described

in Chapter 11 “Pricing for Bidding.” Trades must maintain the targeted contribu-

tion margin.5 Without using the targeted contribution margin as the benchmark,

there can be no negotiating strategy. For example, you can agree to a lower price

(customer’s interest) if you have a larger scope of work (your interest) because

the value (your initial offer contribution margin) is the same or better. The key to

developing trades is a break-even analysis.



Break-Even Analysis6



A break-even analysis is calculated to determine what changes would have to be

made in an offer to maintain or improve the contribution margin as a result of an

anticipated customer negotiation issue or demand. A break-even analysis can be

calculated from the same spreadsheet used for the financial analysis for a specific

offer. The financial analysis (profit and loss calculations) should be done by the

operations manager responsible for the field margin of the location. A break-even

analysis is used to determine the changes in the contribution margin (increase or

decrease) from the base case (initial offer). A break-even should be done to sup-

port the possible trades, which would involve changes in the following:

Changes  can  be  made  for  one  variable  at  a  time  or  a  combination  of  variables,

depending on the complexity of the trades. In Fig. 12.4, a break-even analysis was

used for a perforating tender in which the customer made a negotiation demand

for  an  additional  10%  discount  from  your  initial  offer.  The  break-even  analysis

shows that, to maintain the contribution margin of $700,000, a possible trade would

be to increase the scope of work by 33 jobs. In this case, the customer bid out two

separate  packages  each  for  200  perforating  jobs,  as  input  in  the  base  case,  and

requested separate bids. The customer originally intended to award one package

each to two companies. In this case, the salesperson agreed to the additional dis-

count if the customer increased the package by 50 jobs—a win–win trade.



Another  trade  that  could  have  been  offered  would  have  been  to  decrease  the

incremental  costs  per  job  as  shown  in  Fig.  12.5.  In  this  tender,  the  base  case

contribution  margin  was  calculated  using  a  specific  perforating  charge.  This

perforating charge used in the base case (initial offer) could have been substi-

tuted for a lesser charge that cost less. The break-even analysis shows that, if the

lesser charge reduces the costs by $500 or more per job, this would also be an

acceptable trade.



Figure 12.6 illustrates yet another possible trade, where the customer agrees to

pay for transportation costs into the country and to allow you to do the project

with one truck instead of two, as requested in the original scope of work, thereby

reducing the incremental costs by $100/job. By using one truck instead of two, the

fixed costs are reduced by $80,000. The combination of the reduced transportation

charges  and  reduced  fixed  costs  are  equivalent  to  the  10%  decrease  in  price,

which would be an acceptable trade. However, this trade is less desirable than the

other trades as the field margin is maintained but not improved.7



This example illustrates what could have been interpreted as a take-it-or-leave-it

demand for a 10% price decrease from the customer that was negotiated by using

a trade that improved the project contribution margin. The break-even analysis

for several trade scenarios enabled your team to identify the best and most fea-

sible trade.



If the customer did not like the trade offer or was working under a restriction that

prevented them from increasing the scope of work, other trades could have been

offered. Of course, not all trades are as easy to evaluate as the examples above.

Your team may have to assume a value for some trades. For example, in such a

situation, the marketing manager could recommend giving the 10% discount pro-

vided the customer agrees to release the production results as part of a case study

and to act as a reference for other customers to validate the new charge perfor-

mance. Without any additional trade components, however, this trade would cost

Company A $100,000 in the contribution margin if no other trade components or

conditions are added. The marketing manager may want to re-evaluate the value

of the case study. In the actual negotiation, the value of this trade was reevaluated

to be worth $50,000 to Company A, but it was never offered. It was a last resort

trade to be used only if the other trades were not accepted.



Successful Negotiations



485















































































































Quality of Trades



The  previous  examples  illustrate  that  not  all  trades  are  of  the  same  quality  or

value.  Higher  quality  trades  cost  one  of  the  negotiating  parties  little,  yet  have

significant value for the other negotiating party. The higher quality the trade, the

greater the chance of concluding a successful negotiation. It is a good practice to

reassess the trades and make adjustments to make the trade higher quality, as in

the case of the previously described field study trade.



Your negotiating team should prepare a list of trades for both your company and

the customer to identify the costs and value of each trade and then prioritize the

trades in terms of preferred trading order. The trade order is typically decided by

the quality of the trades so that the trade with the highest value is offered first.

Figure 12.7 shows the trade section from the Negotiations Preparation Worksheet.

The worksheet helps your team develop trades and then rank the trades based on

value to identify the trading order. It is a good practice to have more trades than

you expect to use in case the customer rejects the first trade offered.



The more you learn about your customer’s circumstances and requirements, the

better your ability to develop high quality trades that bring value to the customer

and your company in the spirit of a collaborative negotiation. Chapter Highlight
