to maintain or improve the targeted

contribution margin.



used in successful negotiations. This list can be used as part of a brainstorming

session to develop trades in preparation for a negotiation.



With a list of possible trades for which you can negotiate a win–win agreement,

you are ready to prepare a negotiation strategy. The negotiation strategy uses the

earlier analysis of the negotiation processes and serves as the roadmap for pre-

senting the negotiation to both the customer and management.



Negotiation Strategy



A well-prepared negotiation strategy improves your chances of obtaining a win–

win  agreement  for  you  and  the  customer.  The  negotiating  strategy  creates

a trading plan by which you introduce trades in response to customer’s negotiation

issues. The goal of the negotiation strategy is to maintain the value of the initial



486



CHAPTER 12  Collaborative Negotiations







offer submitted to the customer as identified in the pricing strategy and to achieve

any strategic objectives set by the negotiating team, as described in the objectives

section in this chapter. To achieve this, the negotiating strategy defines the nego-

tiating  range  for  you  and  the  customer.  To  assist  in  constructing  a  negotiating

strategy, use the Negotiating Worksheet.



Negotiating Worksheet



The Negotiating Worksheet is part of the pricing workbook discussed in Chapter

11 “Pricing for Bidding.” The Negotiating Worksheet guides the negotiating team

through the six steps of completing a negotiation strategy. Parts of the Negotiating

Worksheet are from other pricing worksheets or computed automatically. Copied

or computed cells are shaded yellow. Values that should be reviewed and overwrit-

ten, if necessary, are shaded a different color. White cells have values entered as

described below. The Nirwana example analysis proposal developed in Chapter 11

“Pricing for  Bidding” is used to illustrate the use of the Negotiating Worksheet.

The six areas of the Negotiating Worksheet are as follows:



•  Objectives



•  Market factors



•  Initial positions



•  Trades



•  Negotiating ranges and zones of possible agreement



•  Negotiating factors chart



Objectives



The objectives fields list the financial and strategic objectives as  identified by your

negotiating team.



Financial Objective: This value is copied from the Pricing Strategy Worksheet’s

project contribution cell. The goal of the negotiation  strategy is to maintain the

value of the offer as measured by the contribution margin.



Strategic Objective: Enter the strategic objective or objectives the team has identi-

fied for the negotiation as described in the objectives section of this chapter.



For the Nirwana negotiation, the team established a target contribution margin of

$40,000 per month and a strategic objective of securing a two-year contract period

instead of the one-year contract period offered in the scope of work by the customer.



Market Analysis



All  values  are  copied  from  the  Market  Analysis  Worksheet  and  are  explained  in

the market analysis section of Chapter 11, “Pricing for Bidding.”



Initial Positions



The  initial  positions  area  of  the  Negotiating  Worksheet  lists  your  and  the  cus-

tomer’s expected starting points for negotiation.



Market Price: The market price value is copied from the specific Pricing Strategy

Worksheet is selected by your team. In the Nirwana example, using the long-term

pricing strategy, the estimated market price was $50,000 per month.



Supplier  Premium  from  Customer:  This  is  the  premium  you  estimate  the  cus-

tomer  will  pay  for  your  company  over  a  competitor  or  the  discount  that  would

apply if your company is not the preferred supplier.



Successful Negotiations



487





Chapter Highlight 12.1: That Sounds Reasonable



Inexpensive Valuable Trades

The  trades  listed  below  are  of  high  quality  as  measured

by the cost given by one party of the negotiation and the

value received by the other party. The specific value varies

for each negotiation. The test for inexpensiveness is the

incremental  cost  the  customer  or  your  company  must

incur as a result of making the trade, and the value is the

amount that affects the profitability or other value driver

of the project or the individual making the decision. This is

a good starting point when drafting possible trades for a

negotiation.



Increased Scope of Work: This is probably the most fre-

quently  used  trade.  By  expanding  the  scope  of  work  to

include additional volume over the same period, your contribution margin dollars increase proportionately. This

typically costs the customer little but makes a significant impact for your company, especially if there are efficien-

cies to be realized as the volume of work increases, such as one set of equipment for one rig project versus one

set shared between two rigs or two sets between three rigs. Increasing the scope of work is not the same as

extending the contract period, which from a contribution margin perspective does not increase if the contract

period  is  extended. Therefore,  the  trade  for  an  extended  contract  period  should  be  much  smaller  than  an

increased scope of work unless strategic considerations dictate otherwise.



Exclusivity:  Most  suppliers  work  on  a  call-out  basis  with  many  customers,  and  many  customers  value  having

exclusive  use  of  your  equipment  and  personnel.  In  many  cases,  you  assign  crews  and  equipment  to  specific

projects, and in times of decreased activity this trade costs you little but has value in terms of the added insurance

of getting the customer’s work done as planned. Exclusivity has been traded for a decrease in discounts or agree-

ments that the customer pays for transportation costs. When considering exclusivity for limited availability equip-

ment, you must be sure to factor in the opportunity costs for potential lost revenue if jobs will be missed as a

result of assigning the equipment to one customer.



Payment Terms—Days Sales Outstanding (DSO): This is a standard trade in most parts of the world. In this situ-

ation the supplier trades one or two points of additional discount for early payment. The costs of paying early are

minimal compared to getting an additional discount. The value to the supplier is a better management of cash flow.



At Your Convenience: When activity is high, this trade can be very valuable to a supplier. When the appropriate

situation  exists,  having  the  added  convenience  of  scheduling  jobs  can  result  in  the  more  efficient  use  of  your

resources  and  fewer  missed  jobs. The  costs  to  a  customer  are  minimal  if  the  work  only  has  to  be  advanced  or

delayed by a day or two. For example, customers will typically agree to installing products or providing services at

suppliers’ convenience in trade for an additional discount.



Technical Support: Your company has many recognized experts in your organization. Dedicated  technical support

can be invaluable to a customer given the right set of conditions, such as start-up operations in a new area or in an

operating  environment. Technical  support  can  be  traded  for  lesser  discounts,  increased  scope  of  work,  etc. The

incremental cost to your company is minimal as you keep your technical experts as part of your organizational struc-

ture. If the technical support does generate revenue, however, you must ensure that by committing your expert to

a project, you do not risk losing other revenue. In such cases, the trade must be associated with opportunity costs

(as discussed in Chapter 11 “Pricing for Bidding”).



Released  Data:  How  many  times  would  a  released  case  study  have  helped  convince  an  indifferent  or  skeptical

customer to choose your company? What would it be worth for a customer to act as a reference for other custom-

ers to get a second opinion of how well a product or service worked? This trade costs the customer little or nothing.

For your company, it may be a trade worth an extra percentage point discount or waiver of some indirect charges.

The most effective way to present the released data trade is to use the data in a technical paper and to get the

customer’s commitment to coauthor the paper.



Logistics Costs: Often, contracts stipulate that the contractor must pay their own logistical support costs, such as

charters,  broker’s  fees  to  clear  customs,  and  work  permits. These  can  add  signifi cantly  to  incremental  costs.

However, the customer often has ongoing operations in place to support your operations, so the cost to include your

personnel within their logistical support structure is minimal. Once these costs are established, you can trade them

for improved contract conditions for the  customer.



488



CHAPTER 12  Collaborative Negotiations



In  the  Nirwana  example,  the  customer  was  expected  to  give  Company  A  a  10%

premium for not having to disrupt operations to switch to a new supplier, and in

fact, this is what the customer stated during the negotiations.8



Customer’s  Initial  Offer:  The  customer’s  offer  is  their  response  to  your  initial

offer and is commonly known as the opening or counter-proposal. Typically, the

customer compares your offer to the best alternative (competitive offer) received.

The  best  alternative  offer  may  not  be  the  cheapest;  it  might  be  considered  the

best for other reasons, such as technical or operational factors.



Sometimes,  the  customer  demands  a  discount  off  your  offer  to  match  or  come

within an acceptable range of a cheaper competitor offer. Or the customer may

request that you match specific components of the competitor’s offer that were

not included in your initial offer. For example, a competitor has chosen to waive

mobilization charges, and the customer has asked you to do the same.



Commonly, a competitive negotiator, no matter how good your offer, always makes

a  counter-offer  or  asks  for  an  additional  discount,  because  it  doesn’t  cost  the

customer anything to ask. The competitive negotiator may even describe your offer

as ridiculous. Handling these counter-offers is discussed later in the competitive

negotiators section of this chapter.



In the Nirwana example, it was estimated that the market rate or best alternative

for the customer was approximately $50,000 per month—a gap of $20,000, which

was  justified  by  Company  A’s  differential  value  and  described  as  part  of  their

initial offer. The customer was expected to ask for a price reduction, which Com-

pany A was prepared to do with the high quality trades the team had identified.

The  customer’s  initial  offer  was  predicted  to  be  at  or  near  $55,000  per  month,

which was based on the 10% premium discussed above.



Supplier  Share  of  the  Differential  Value:  This  is  the  percentage  share  of  the

differential value that your team has decided is a fair and equitable sharing of the

differential value, as discussed in Chapter 11 “Pricing for Bidding.” This percent-

age is copied from the appropriate Pricing Strategy Worksheet. It is included in

the  initial  offers  section  to  remind  your  negotiators  of  how  much  your  offer  is

priced above the market rate.



Supplier  Initial  Offer:  Your  initial  offer,  the  starting  point  of  your  negotiation

range, is based on one of the three basic pricing strategies: long-term, short-term

excess capacity, and short-term limited capacity, as discussed in Chapter 11 “Pric-

ing for Bidding.” Your offer is the formal offer made to the customer in response

to their defined scope of work. In the Nirwana example below, Company A’s offer

was $70,000 per month. The initial offer is copied from the supplier offer cell in

the Long-Term Pricing Strategy Worksheet.



Trades



The  trades  area  is  where  your  team  enters  the  prioritized  trades  that  will  be

introduced  during  the  negotiation  meeting.  Each  trade  appears  in  the  order  in

which it will be used along with a brief description of the trade, cost to the cus-

tomer, and the price adjustment to your offer. The negotiated offer is automatically

calculated by subtracting the price adjustment from your initial offer in the initial

positions area.



First Trade: The first trade is the offer made in response to the customer’s initial

request.  This  trade  is  based  on  the  highest  quality  trade  developed  during  the

trading analysis. The highest quality trade should address the interests of the most

influential parties involved in the negotiation meeting. The first trade should be

the  most  significant  movement  as  measured  in  percentage  terms  off  the  initial

offer that you plan to make during the negotiation.



Successful Negotiations



489





In preparation for the Nirwana negotiation, Company A’s team decided to make their

first trade a $7,000-per-month price reduction provided the customer paid for trans-

porting  the  cement,  which  added  $15,000  per  month  in  incremental  costs  to  the

project.  Company  A’s  team  believed  the  customer  could  add  the  cement  to  their

regular shipments of mud, tubing, etc., with additional costs of no more than $5,000

per month.



Second Trade: Second trades are dependent on the scope of work and ability of

the negotiating team to develop additional trades. If there is more than one trade,

it is important to make clear to the customer whether the second trade is condi-

tional upon accepting the terms of the first trade, or if the second trade is inde-

pendent of the first trade. In most cases trades are cumulative, and the second

trade should satisfy the negotiation issues under discussion. Generally, the second

trade should be of lower value than the first trade, as this strategy communicates

that the first trade was the best you could offer for the negotiation point under

discussion.



In the example in Fig. 12.8, Company A’s second trade was in anticipation that

the customer would agree to an extension of the contract period for one year and

would offer an additional price reduction of $3,000 per month.



Final Offer: The final offer is typically the third trade.9 The final offer in terms

of percentage movement should be the smallest as it will also signal to the cus-

tomer that you have no further trades, given that each trade decreases in value

as you move from the first trade to the final offer.



In the Nirwana example, the final offer was an early payment discount worth 1% or

$1,000  per  month  with  no  associated  cost  to  the  customer  other  than  the  cost  of

capital, which was negligible. In larger projects, however, this cost could be significant.



Negotiating Ranges and Zone of Possible Agreement10



All the fields in this area are computed from the other values copied or entered into

the worksheet. These fields represent the negotiation range for the supplier and the

customer. These values are plotted in the negotiating factors chart to illustrate the

zone of possible agreement that may or may not exist depending on the estimated

values your team made for the customer’s initial offer, as explained below.



Supplier Negotiating Range: Your negotiating range values are copied from the

above trade area cells for the initial offer, first trade, second trade, and final offer,

as described above.



Customer Negotiating Range: The customer negotiating range values are calcu-

lated  based  on  values  input  from  other  cells  on  the  Negotiating  Worksheet,  as

explained on the next page.



Customer Initial Offer: This value is copied from the customer initial offer cell

in the initial position area. The cell value is computed based on the above descrip-

tion. The customer’s initial offer is heavily influenced by what they consider to be

their best alternative.



In the Nirwana negotiation, the customer stated that the price difference between

Company A and the competitor was too great for them to accept Supplier A’s offer.

If they wanted to continue the project,  they would have to move closer to $55,000

per month compared to Company A’s initial offer of $70,000.



Customer First Trade: In most negotiations, your customers do not invest time

and energy into developing a negotiation strategy as described in this chapter.

Usually,  the  customer’s  negotiation  strategy  is  based  on  what  you  offer  for

trades, and as such, the condition you ask them to accept becomes their first

trade.  This  fact  highlights  the  importance  of  preparing  trades  that  clearly

Negotiating Worksheet for the Nirwana

opportunity with a long-term pricing

strategy.



describe what you expect in return from the customer, because you are, in fact,

preparing their negotiation range. As long as your trades are of high quality, the

customer should agree unless there are reasons they cannot accept the terms

or they are using a competitive negotiation tactic to extract the best price. If

the customer does not accept your first trade, you should try to find alternative

trades they can agree to. If the customer has prepared trades, you must evalu-

ate  their  impact  on  your  contribution  margin  before  accepting,  making  a

counter-offer, or rejecting the trade.



The default value in the customer’s first trade cell is computed by adding to the cus-

tomer’s initial offer the value of your first trade less any additional costs the customer

would incur for agreeing to the trade. In the Nirwana example, the customer’s first

trade is computed as $59,000, which represents the value of Company A’s first trade

($9,000) less the $5,000 cost to the customer of providing transportation of the sup-

plier’s materials. The default value must be reassessed based on how well you know

the customer’s negotiation team and adjusted up or down to meet your expectations.



If the negotiation meeting proceeds as predicted from the default trade values,

you will know after the first trades are made that you probably cannot reach an

agreement as your first trade point is above the customer’s first trade point. In

this case, discussion on the second trade should be initiated by you.



In the Nirwana example, the customer accepted in principle the trade of trans-

porting materials for the work from the supplier’s storage facilities to the job site.

However, the customer asked for a $9,000-per-month reduction in price and would

not commit to awarding Company A the contract as their price was still too high.

The customer then asked for a $10,000-per-month reduction in order to award the

contract, but they were unable to accept that price.



Successful Negotiations



491





Customer  Second  Trade:  If the customer does not accept the first trade (most

strategic competitive negotiators as a part of their negotiating strategy will never

accept the first trade), the customer’s second trade point is usually a modification

of your first trade—either a reduction in their commitment or an addition to your

commitments as part of the trade. This is what happened in the Nirwana example.



The default value in the customer’s second trade cell is computed by adding to

the customer’s first trade the value of your second trade less any additional costs

the customer would incur for agreeing to the trade.



In the Nirwana example, the customer’s second trade is computed as $62,000. If the

negotiation meeting proceeded as predicted from the default trade values, after the

second  trades  were  made,  you  could  possibly  reach  an  agreement  as  Company  A’s

second trade point is below the customer’s second trade point.



In the Nirwana negotiation, the salesperson asked the customer for suggestions

on how to improve the scope of work. The customer offered a two-year contract

instead of one if Company A would reduce the monthly charges by another $5,000

per month. This became the customer’s second trade point, which brought Com-

pany A’s offer closer to their best alternative offer. Company A then accepted in

principle the value of a two-year contract but modified the trade to better reflect

the true value of a contract extension at a $3,000-per-month reduction, as shown

in Fig. 12.8.



Customer  Final  Offer:  If at all possible, your negotiators should encourage the

customer  to  state  their  final  offer  first,  as  they  should  respond  to  your  second

trade  before  making  any  other  statements  or  trades.  An  important  negotiating

skill is to be silent after a trade has been offered. Once the customer responds to

your  second  trade  as  part  of  their  final  offer,  you  can  then  accept  or  offer  your

final trade in an effort to reach agreement.



The default value in the customer’s final offer is computed by adding to the cus-

tomer’s  second  trade  the  value  of  your  final  offer  less  any  additional  costs  the

customer  would  incur  by  agreeing  to  the  trade.  In  the  Nirwana  example,  the

customer’s default final offer is equal to $63,000, which is sufficiently above Com-

pany A’s final offer. If the second trade did not close the deal, then in this case

the final offer should. If the customer does not accept it at least in principle, it is

a good indication they are still negotiating from a competitive position. In such

situations, your team must leave the negotiation meeting without a commitment

and depend on mentors or supporters in the buying center to influence the cus-

tomer’s negotiating team to accept your final offer.



In the Nirwana negotiation, the customer accepted the final offer and Company

A was awarded the contract for two years.



The last fields on the Negotiating Workshop are zone of possible agreement flags

and the premium paid by the customer.



Zone of Possible Agreement: These fields provide a “No” or “Yes” flag for each

trade, indicating when your adjusted offer price after each trade is less than the

customer’s adjusted offer price after each trade. The sooner you reach the zone

of possible agreement, the better your chances of reaching an agreement. Ideally,

you should reach the zone of possible agreement after the first or second trade,

reserving  the  third  trade  as  a  cushion  for  unexpected  constraints  the  customer

may have that reduces the value of your trades.



Premium  Paid  by  Customer:  This field calculates the percentage premium the

customer would pay based on your final offer compared to the market rate. The

purpose is to ensure that you look at the final outcome from the customer’s per-

spective. In this case, the customer paid a 14% premium to continue with Com-



492



CHAPTER 12  Collaborative Negotiations



pany A compared to what they would have paid had they chosen the competitor

at $50,000 per month. The 14% premium is 4% more than they anticipated to pay.

This view is different than the view taken by most sales staff, who would look at

the negotiation as having to offer an additional 19% discount to keep the project.



Negotiating Factors Chart



The negotiating factors chart graphically presents the data contained in the Nego-

tiating  Worksheet.  The  plot  values  are  beneath  the  main  worksheet  area.  The

market price is plotted along the left axis. As it changes, the market price indica-

tor  moves  to  the  appropriate  value.  The  supplier’s  negotiation  range  is  plotted

using the Excel stock price chart function. The initial offer is the high value, the

supplier’s  first  trade  is  the  close  value,  the  supplier’s  second  trade  is  the  open

value, and the final offer is the low value.



The  next  bar  represents  the  zone  of  possible  agreement.  If  the  bar  is  white,  it

spans the zone of possible agreement, and if there is no zone of possible agree-

ment,  the  bar  is  blue,  spanning  the  gap  between  your  final  offer  and  the  cus-

tomer’s final offer.



The  next  bar  is  the  customer  negotiation  range,  again  plotted  using  the  Excel

stock price chart function. In this negotiation range, the customer’s final offer is

the high value, the customer’s second trade is the close value, the customer’s first

trade is the open value, and the customer’s initial offer is the low value.



The  next  bar  is  your  differential  value  as  computed  in  the  pricing  sensitivity

analysis described in Chapter 11 “Pricing for Bidding.” Your differential value is

plotted starting at the market price. In this presentation, the top of your differ-

ential value bar represents the customer’s theoretical price ceiling. It also visually

shows how much differential value your team believes the customer is giving you

when you compare the position of the customer’s initial offer to the starting point

of your differential value bar.



The  next  four  points  plotted  on  the  chart  are  the  estimated  prices  for  the  best

supplier  alternative  opportunity  and  other  existing  contract  prices  for  similar

projects for other customers, suppliers, and the competitor. The last point on the

graph is an existing contract price the customer may use as a reference point, as

discussed  in  the  market  analysis  section  of  Chapter  11,  “Pricing  for  Bidding.”

These values are listed in the market analysis area.



The negotiating factors chart provides an effective format for presenting a negotia-

tion strategy for review by other members of the negotiating team or management.



Analysis of the Nirwana Negotiation



The  Nirwana  deal  was  a  successful  negotiation.  Company  A  was  awarded  the

project for two years (strategic objective achieved), and the contribution margin

was  improved  by  $3,000  per  month,  or  7%  (materials  transportation  costs  of

$15,000 per month were taken by the customer for a $9,000 price reduction, and

a two-year contract was awarded for a $3,000 price reduction).



One area that should have been factored into the negotiation strategy was Com-

pany  A’s  differential  value  of  $25,000.  As  shown  in  Fig.  12.8,  the  customer’s

starting position was to give Company A a 10% premium over the competitor due

to the switching costs incurred to replace Company A. However, there was still

$20,000 of differential value for QHS&E performance and reduced costs by using

the  advanced  product—points  that  were  never  discussed  in  the  negotiation

meeting. Had the negotiating team been able to get the customer’s negotiation

team  to  agree  on  the  additional  values,  the  zone  of  possible  agreement  would



Successful Negotiations

increasing the premium 30%. In this sce-

nario, the zone of possible agreement is

reached after the first trade.



have been reached with first trade, as shown in the Nirwana Negotiating Work-

sheet in Fig. 12.9, which has been updated for a customer premium of 30%. This

is  calculated  by  assuming  the  customer  agrees  to  $15,000  of  differential  value

compared to the market price of $50,000 per month.



The benefit of including a discussion of your differential value as part of the negoti-

ating meeting is that it justifies the initial offer, which is based on the market price

plus  your  differential  value.  Your  costs  are  higher  due  to  investments,  training,

maintenance, etc., when providing the differential value. Often, these justifications

can enlighten the customer’s negotiating team of the added value from using Com-

pany A over a competitor. See the “Do You Know?” sidebar describing the a B2B sales



u Do You Know?



In the exchange described below, the

salesperson uses a standard probe

that is very effective in dealing with a

competitive negotiator. This exchange

is taken from the negotiation meeting

for the exploration project (described in

Chapter Highlight: 12.2: “Let’s

Review”). The lead customer negotia-

tor began the meeting by asking, “Do

you know you are 100% more expen-

sive than your nearest  competitor?”



It was obvious from the question that

the negotiator was taking a competi-

tive approach to the negotiation. After

asking for clarification of what the cus-

tomer meant by 100% more expen-

sive, the salesperson then replied:



Salesperson: “Yes. This is where I

expected them to be based on our last



bid. I understand that your costs to

complete this project are significant,

and if you don’t mind I would like to

ask a few questions to ensure I under-

stand the situation correctly.”



Customer: “Go ahead, but you are

going to have to reduce your prices if

you want this project.”



Salesperson: “Thank you. First, just to

make sure we are on the same page,

please tell me how much value you

give our solution over the competitor in

terms of reducing your rig time and

reducing risks.”



Customer: “None. You and your com-

petitor are both acceptable suppliers.”



Salesperson: “Well then, I can see

why you are surprised by our prices.

The added costs of our proposal are

due to the added services we provide



that result in your being able to cut $2

million worth of rig time and reduce

the chances of a bad hole.”



Customer: “Is that right? Do our tech-

nical people agree with this?”



Salesperson: “Yes sir. In fact I have

documented a demonstration job that

we ran for you in another one of your

wells to prove the system. Your techni-

cal people requested this be run on

this project. So for the extra $800,000

you are paying for us, you are in fact

reducing your total costs by $1.2 mil-

lion compared to the cost of using the

competitor:”



Customer: “I will have to validate this

with our people.”



With this issue resolved, the custom-

er’s negotiator moved on to other less

critical issues.

Updated Nirwana Negotiating

Worksheet for a new market rate of

$40,000 and Company A’s pre mium from

the customer of 30%. In this scenario,

the zone of possible agreement is

reached after the  second trade. Had the

market rate in the first scenario been

$40,000 and included only a 10%

negotiation meeting in the competitive negotiators section at the end of this chapter

for an example of a negotiation that begins with a review of differential value.



In the Nirwana example, having the first trade in the zone of possible agreement

provides a safety margin for Supplier A’s team. If there were factors the team did

not identify or accurately estimate, and had the competitor submitted a price of

$40,000 per month instead of $50,000 per month, the negotiating team would still

have additional trades to reach the zone of possible agreement after the second

trade. and win the deal. Fig. 12.10 shows the updated market price and impact

it has on the Negotiating Worksheet calculations. Had this been the case with only

a 10% differential, Company A’s negotiating team would not have had any trades

in the zone of possible agreement and most likely would have lost the contract or

made a concession to secure the project.



What this analysis highlights is the earlier the pricing and supporting negotiation

strategy can be drafted, the more obvious it becomes where the team should focus

to improve the chances of securing the project. In this case, Company A’s team had

$20,000 worth of differential value that did not impact the negotiations even though

the team had factored it into their pricing decision. It would have helped to solicit

the support of the buying center members who could validate the financial benefits

of using this advanced solution to leverage the differential value in the negotiations.



There  are  two  key  points  to  take  away  from  this  section.  Using  the  negotiation

worksheet to prepare your negotiation strategy allows you to see if you are in the

zone of possible agreement. Chapter Highlight 12.2: “If You Want Them To Say

Yes, Don’t Make Them Say No,” explains how to read the negotiation worksheet

in more detail.  This allows the negotiation team to work on a negotiation strategy

and know they have a winning deal. From a management point of view, this also

makes the coaching process much more productive because the coach now knows

exactly  what  assumptions  the  negotiation  team  has  made  when  building  their

strategy. For example, what is the market rate the team used?



Successful Negotiations



495





The second very important takeaway is the team can test their negotiation strat-

egy for different scenarios and determine if they would still win when using their

strategy. The team can ask themselves “what if” questions to determine the limits

for each of the factors and still win the deal. For example:



•  What is the lowest market rate (competitor offer) at which Supplier A would



win after using all three of their trades?



•  What is the lowest pricing premium we could offer to the teams and still win



with all the trades?



•  How much differential value could the competitor have at the current market

rate  (this  effectively  lowers  the  net  differential  value  of  Supplier  A’s  offer)

and still win the deal after the second trade?



Each of the above scenarios are answered by changing the appropriate value in

the negotiation worksheet until cells in the zone of possible agreement change to

yes. For example, to answer the first scenario using the base case as shown in Fig.

12.8, the lowest market rate is $45,000 and the team still wins after using all three

of their trades. Figure 12.11 shows the final offer value is “Yes” while the other

cells are “No.” If the team is confident that the competitor will not go as low as

$45,000, there is no need to change the strategy. If they believe there is a possibil-

ity of the competitor bidding $44,000 or lower then they must change their nego-

tiation strategy, perhaps adjusting their initial offer of increasing the amount they

will give for one or more of their trades. They need to be prepared to answer these

types  of  questions.  In  the  negotiation  proposal  review,  these  types  of  questions

will be asked by the managers reviewing the negotiation.



With the negotiation strategy completed and all the most likely what if scenarios

answered, the negotiation strategy is now ready to be reviewed. The next section

discusses the negotiation proposal review.



Fig. 12.11

Negotiating ranges and zone of possible agreement. Scenario: Market rate $45,000.



Negotiation Proposal Review



Once  the  negotiation  proposal  has  been  completed,  it  is  reviewed  following  the

same  procedure  as  a  level  2  strategic  sales  plan  review.  The  negotiating  team

(selected from the bid team) presents to the reviewers the following:



•  Negotiation situational analysis



•  Negotiation strategy



•  Negotiation proposal components



After the negotiating team presents their proposal, the reviewers ask clarification

questions. For examples of clarification questions, see the sidebar “I Have a Ques-

tion.”  After all the clarification questions have been answered, the reviewers will

then prepare their feedback:



496



CHAPTER 12  Collaborative Negotiations



Chapter Highlight 12.2: If You Want Them To Say Yes, Don’t Make Them Say No



I hope this advice was not the result of an expensive marketing study, but when it comes to negotiations, some

salespeople could benefit from this advice. A lot of the problems started in the late 1970s and early 1980s, when

negotiating occurred more frequently. In B2B sales at that time buyers became more sophisticated and competitors

became less differentiated; with this came every buyer’s dream of getting “their best price.” To combat this increase

in negotiating prices, sales consultants developed an easy solution (or what seemed easy): Bid high and when the

customer wanted a greater discount, look devastated, give them 5%, and shake hands. Buyers quickly learned that,

because  the  5%  came  easy,  they  should  ask  for  more. The  consultant’s  recommendation  was  to  give  a  smaller

discount—in this case 2%—and shake hands, but if the customer asked for more, give them 1% and shake or walk

away. This  sounds  like  a  game;  indeed,  it  was.  In  this  textbook,  it  is  not  recommended  to  play  games  with  your

customer when it comes to business.  When you submit a bid, the customer should know they are getting your best

price for their scope of work, terms, and conditions at the day’s market conditions. They should know you have done

your due diligence and have offered your best price the first time you submit your bid. If they want a better price,

then you negotiate. The measure of a successful negotiation is if it:



•  Maintains or improves the value of the deal for both the customer and you.



•  Is a better deal than the customer’s BATNA.



•  Strengthens the relationship between the customer and you.



•  Wastes little time with negotiating tactics for self-interest.



•  Results in an efficient agreement with a minimum amount of effort and maintenance .



Successful  negotiations  are  the  hallmark  of  a  professional  salesperson’s  approach  to  bidding  and  negotiation  in  a

competitive marketplace. Later in this chapter, it is acknowledged that not everyone will come to the negotiation

table in this spirit of win–win. In managing competitive negotiators, the first step is to ensure that your first trade is

a good faith trade that you believe puts your offer into the zone of possible of agreement. The negotiation worksheet

lets you see when this is the case. The example below from the Nirwana case illustrates this point. This example

was discussed earlier in this chapter, and the negotiation worksheet can be seen full size in Fig. 12.8.  The negotiation

worksheet is for the situation where the market rate is $50,000 and the customer premium is 10%.



Successful Negotiations



497





Chapter Highlight 12.2: If You Want Them To Say Yes (cont.)



In the negotiating ranges and zone of possible agreement of the negotiating worksheet (Ref. 1), it can be seen

that on the zone of possible agreement line the result is “No” for the initial offer, “No” for the first trade, “Yes”

for the second trade, and “Yes” for the final offer. Earlier in this chapter, the ideal negotiation was presented in

Fig. 12.1 (pareto chart). The pareto chart is shown in the Ref. 2 area of the figure with the negotiation values

from  this  example.  On  the  pareto  chart,  these  points  show  the  initial  offer  and  first  Trade  are  in  the

“Unacceptable to Customer” area of the pareto chart. The second trade is in the green zone of the zone of

possible agreement. It should be obvious that, if you want your customer to say “Yes” to the deal, you need

to  make  them  an  offer  that  is  better  than  their  best  alternative  (BATNA). This  being  the  situation,  then  the

salesperson’s trading strategy would combine trades 1 and 2. The salesperson would ask the customer to use

their trucks to move the materials to the job site and commit to a two-year contract. If the customer agrees to

that, then the salesperson would discount their original offer $12,000 per month, making their negotiated offer

$58,000 per month.



Plotting the pareto chart in Excel is difficult. However, it is possible to show a similar graph using the open-

high-low-close stock price chart in Excel; see Ref. 3 on the negotiation worksheet. To make it easier to see,

the Ref. 3 area has been enlarged and shown in Ref. 4. Whenever your price, as plotted, is higher than the

customer’s price, there is no deal.  You are in the “Unacceptable to Customer” area of the pareto chart. From

Ref. 4 it can be seen the initial offer of the supplier is $70,000 and customer’s initial offer is $55,000 this no

deal. After the first trade, the supplier drops their price to $61,000 in exchange for the customer transporting

the materials to the job site. However, because it costs the customer $5,000 to transport the materials, their

net gain is $4,000 ($9,000 price drop less the $5,000 to transport the materials). After the second trade, their

price moves to $59,000, still lower than the $61,000 of the supplier, therefore, still no deal. The second trade

states that in exchange for a two-year contract, the supplier will drop their price to $58,000. The customer

gains another $3,000, and their price goes to $61,000. Now their offer is higher than the supplier’s, and yes

there is a deal.  The offer is now in the green area of the pareto chart’s zone of possible agreement.



At this point, some salespeople will question what the customer’s motivation is for negotiating with you.  Why

not go to the competitors and ask them what discount will they give if the customer transports the materials

and gives a two-year contract? This is an excellent question, and the answer is unique business value—the

blue bar shown in Ref. 4 which is the result of the customer value analysis. The customer wants this value,

which is a value they do not get if they give the work to the competitor. As a sales manager, one way to explain

the job of the salesperson is that it is your job to make the customer want us. When the customer recognizes

that your products or services have value (something worth money to them or other benefits), then they will

want to negotiate with you when the competitor is offering a cheaper price.



Ref. 5 shows the “Distribution of Value”—namely, the $25,000 worth of net benefits the customer receives from

the supplier’s solution.  This was discussed in the “Pricing Sensitivity Analysis Worksheet” section in Chapter

11 “Pricing for Bidding.” In the initial offer, the supplier’s price was $70,000 and the market rate was $50,000,

indicating that $20,000 of the $25,000 of reduced costs was given back to the supplier while the customer kept

$5,000  for  themselves  in  the  way  of  reduced  costs.   When  the  supplier  dropped  their  price  to  $61,000,  the

customer still had the $25,000 in cost reduction, of which now only $11,000 of the savings was used to pay the

supplier’s price. $5,000 has to be spent to transport the supplier’s materials to the job site. The customer has a

net benefit of $9,000. For the second trade, because there were no costs associated with the customer agree-

ing to the two-year contract, the additional $3,000 benefit now makes their total net benefit $12,000. At this

point, the customer has two options: Select the competitor and pay $50,000 plus an additional $25,000 of costs

for a net cost of $75,000, or select the supplier and pay $58,000 and additional costs of $5,000 to transport the

materials for a total of $63,000. The customer is now $12,000 better off when awarding to the supplier. The last

trade was for an early payment where the supplier dropped their price to $57,000 and the customer received

another $1,000 benefit. Now the customer is paying $62,000 versus net $75,000 to the competitor.



The reason the supplier was willing to offer the trades was because transporting the materials would cost the

supplier $12,000 per month. The value of having a two-year contract was worth $5,000 to the supplier. The

early payment cost them $1,000 per month, but the management considered this worth the expense of hav-

ing more efficient receivables collections and better cash flow. Therefore, with their initial offer, the supplier

was earning a $40,000 contribution margin or gross profit. Dropping their price to $57,000 reduced the con-

tribution  margin  by  $13,000  to  $27,000.  However,  their  costs  were  reduced  by  $12,000  plus  $5,000  less

$1,000;  resulting  in  $16,000,  reduction.  Now  the  contribution  margin  is  $27,000,  plus  $16,000  or  $43,000.

$3,000 better than when they started. This is a win–win negotiation.



498



CHAPTER 12  Collaborative Negotiations



u I Have a Question



At the negotiation strategy review meeting, managers will ask

clarification questions. Below is a short list of the most com-

mon questions:



•  Are you in the zone of possible agreement with your initial



offer?



•  What is the lowest market rate that you can have and still



negotiate an agreement with all your current trades?



•  How much more premium would you need to make your

second trade good enough to close the deal at the lowest

market rate?



•  How much differential value and premium from the customer

would you need to make your initial offer acceptable to the

customer at the lowest market rate?



•  Can you think of more trades?



•  What they like about the proposal.



•  Challenges  and  recommendations  to  improve



the proposal.



As the reviewers present their feedback, the nego-

tiating  team  clarifies  and  confirms  the  challenge

and recommendations. The objective of the review

is  approval  for  the  negotiation  strategy.  This

includes which trades are to be used and any condi-

tions  with  the  trades,  which  trades  presented  by

the negotiating team are not to be used, and which

issues are non-negotiable and how to handle them.



This  review  is  supported  with  materials  used  in

earlier reviews, as follows:



•  Level 1 strategic sales plan review



•  How confident are you that each trade will be accepted?



•  Have you ever used these trades before?



•  Bid strategy review



•  Proposal review



•  Could anyone in the customer’s organization validate your



trades for you?



•  Have you asked anyone in the customer’s organization what



types of trades might be appealing to them?



By  leveraging  the  earlier  materials,  the  team  the

can  focus  on  developing  the  negotiation  strategy

and proposal components.



•  Are there any business or personal priorities of the decision

maker, approver, or buying center members with high influ-

ence for whom you could build trades to motivate them to

help close the deal?



•  Are they negotiating with anyone else?



Negotiation Situational Analysis



The  purpose  of  presenting  the  negotiation  situa-

tional analysis is to provide the background infor-

mation  for  the  review  team  not  familiar  with  the

opportunity.  The  best  tool  for  this  is  the  Negotia-

tion Summary Worksheet, which is created by adding information for negotiation

factors to the bid review worksheet (discussed in Chapter 10 “Competitive Bid-

ding”).  The  negotiation  factors  are  listed  in  Table  12.1.  Each  factor  has  a

description and how to interpret the influence each factor has on a negotiation.

Generated from this information is the negotiation summary handout shown in

Fig.  12.12.  The  red  box  highlighted  in  the  left-hand  corner  is  the  additional

information regarding the negotiation factors, and the red circle on the radar plot

shows where the negotiation factors are plotted. The other factors are the same

as presented at the bid strategy review meeting. At this meeting, however, if there

has been any additional information obtained since after submitting the bid, the

bid factors are updated.



As  required,  the  team  can  present  parts  of  the  level  2  strategic  sales  plan,  bid

strategy review, and proposal review depending on how familiar the reviewers are

with the opportunity.



Negotiating Strategy



The negotiating team presents the negotiating worksheet discussed earlier in this

chapter. The strategy will describe for each negotiating issue the trades and the

rationale for each, the details of the trade, any conditions of the trade, and stan-

dards to be used when supporting the trade with the customer. A key decision is

the order in which the negotiation issues will be discussed and if any of the trades

are to be bundled. A sound negotiation strategy is to always have your first trade

or  combination  of  trades  put  the  deal  into  the  zone  of  possible  agreement,  as

discussed  in  Chapter  Highlight  12.2:  “If  You  Want  Them  to  Say  Yes,  Don’t  Make

Them Say No.”



Successful Negotiations



499





Factor



Description



Lead

Negotiator



Who from the customer’s organization is the lead

negotiator?



Lead

Negotiator

Alignment



What is the alignment of the lead negotiator

towards your company?



Positive



Approver

If the lead negotiator is the

approver, then this is the best

case as you are dealing with

the person who can consider

all trades immediately at the

negotiation.



Influence



Negative



User/Evaluator

If the lead negotiator is a user/

evaluator, then any negotiated

deal has to be approved by two or

more levels and this is not a good

situation. You will need to leverage

other factors surrounding the nego-

tiation to mitigate this factor.



Supporter/Mentor

Negotiating with the lead nego-

tiator who is a supporter/mentor

has a very positive impace on

the negotiator. It should create

a negotiating environment that

facilitates creating value and

a win–win outcome for both

parties.



Non-supporter

If the lead negotiator is a non-

supporter or enemy, then this

will have a negative effect on the

negotiation. If the lead negotiator

is neutral, a non-supporter, or an

enemy, this may require one of your

mentors assisting you with having

your negotiation proposal accepted.



Lead

Negotiator

Alignment



This factor relates to the lead negotiator’s influ-

ence as described in Chapter 9 “Strategic Sales

Plan.” This factor refers to the person’s influence

within the customer organization, and their ability

to influence the decision beyond the consider-

ation of the formal criteria used by the evaluator

to identify the preferred offer.



BATNA

Customer



What are the customer’s best alternatives to a

negotiated agreement with your company?



BATNA Your

Company



What is your best alternative to a negotiated

agreement with the customer?



Negotiation

Price Issues



Overall, what is your ability to reach an accept-

able solution for the price issues?



Negotiation

Other Issues



Overall, what is your ability to reach an accept-

able solution for the non-price issues?



High

High influence is the best

situation as it means the lead

negotiator has the discretion to

consider all trades that deliver

value to the organization and

all stakeholders’ business and

personal agendas.



Worst

If the customer does not accept

your proposal, they will be worse

off. This should never happen;

however, there are times due to

factors beyond the control of the

customer’s negotiating team that

could crease a situation where

the customer would accept the

worst deal.



Better

In limited capacity markets, this

can be the case that is good for

the supplier. In situations like

these when you are negotiating,

you must know what the op-

portunity costs are.



Strong

A strong negotiation position

indicates that you have one of

the more desirable trades for

the price issues. You have a

rationale and relevant standard

to help convince the customer to

accept your trade.



Strong

A strong negotiation position

indicates that you have one of

the more desirable trades for

the non-price issues. You have

a rational and relevant standard

to help convince the customer

to accept your trade. When you

have price non-negotiable or

weak price position, you will

need to use these non-strong

positions and have the customer

consider the overall deal.



Table 12.1

Negotiation factors and guidelines on how each can influence a negotiation.



500



CHAPTER 12  Collaborative Negotiations



Neutral



Approver

If the lead negotiator is the

decision maker, this is considered

neutral to the negotiation.



Neutral

Neutral is someone who has a pref-

erence for two or more companies

to award the work. Since they are

not yet convinced, the negotiation

can be used to convince them to

award the work to your company.

However, this also applies to the

other competitor. In this situation,

the alignment factor will have a

neutral effect on the negotiation.



Medium

Lead negotiators who have me-

dium influence will also consider

trades outside the formal decision

criteria, but cannot change the

decision criteria like the high

influence lead negotiators.



Same

The customer’s alternative is the

same as your offer. This will have

 a neutral effect on the negotiation.



Low

The worst situation is when the lead

negotiator has low influence, which

indicates the lead negotiator must

follow the formal decision criteria.



Better

The customer has a better

alternative to your offer. This is not

good and you will need to look for

other trades that have value for the

customer that the alternative does

not have to offer.



Worst

Need to ensure you close this deal.

Ensure that you make the trades the

best you can offer.



Same

This has a neutral effect on the

negotiation.



Non-negotiable

A price non-negotiable negotiation

position will need to be addressed

in the negotiation meeting as per

the guidelines discussed in Chapter

12 “Collaborative Negotiations”

and does not put you in a negative

position regarding the negotiation.

Unresolved non-negotiable price

issues will cause you to lose the

work. Non-negotiable price issues

are rarely true non-negotiables. This

is more likely a negotiation tactic

from the customer to have a better

deal. You should consult your men-

tors in the customer organization

for guidance.



Non-negotiable

A price non-negotiable negotiation

position will need to be addressed

in the negotiation meeting as per

the guidelines discussed in Chapter

12 “Collaborative Negotiations”

and does not put you in a negative

position regarding the negotiation.

You should consult your mentors

in the customer organization for

guidance.



Weak

A weak price position indicates

your trading strategy for this issue

is most likely not sufficient to

satisfy or find a totally acceptable

solution. It is possible you can make

some improvements regarding this

negotiation price issue; however,

it is most likely you will need to

minimize your weak position on this

issue with other strong position

issues. You will need to handle an

unresolved weak price position as

a drawback. Overall, the effect on

the negotiation will be neutral to

your position and certainly not as

negative as non-negotiable.



Weak

A weak non-price issue position in-

dicates that your trading strategy for

this issue is most likely not sufficient

to satisfy or find a totally acceptable

solution. It is possible you can make

some improvements regarding

this negotiation issue; however,

it is most likely you will need to

minimize your weak position on this

issue with other strong position

issues. You will need to handle an

unresolved weak non-price position

as a drawback. Overall, the effect

on the negotiation will be neutral to

your position, and certainly not as

negative as non-negotiable.

Fig. 12.12

Negotiation summary handout.



Successful Negotiations



501































































































































































































































































































































































































































































































































































































Negotiation Components



To review the negotiation proposal components, the negotiation team reviews the

negotiation meeting planner, which will be discussed in the next section.



Negotiation Meeting



Before discussing specifics of the negotiation meeting, you must review the steps

taken  to  this  point.  First,  you  used  the  opportunities  management  process  to

answer the four strategic questions:



•  Is there an opportunity?



•  Can you compete?



•  Can you win?



•  Is it worth winning?



From the effort invested in answering these questions, you construct a solution

and offer that differentiate your company in your capability to:



•  Assist  the  customer  in  meeting  the  project  and  team  members’  individual



objectives in the most efficient and cost-effective manner.



•  Provide the margin your company needs to ensure the level of commitment

to  excellence  to  deliver  a  value-differentiated  solution  at  a  high  level  of

operational efficiency.



The time taken for you to get to this point in the opportunities management pro-

cess may have taken weeks, months, or even years. The negotiation meeting is one

of the last few hurdles to determining whether all your efforts will result in win-

ning the project.



The  commitment  to  the  customer’s  and  your  company’s  objectives  must  be  a

critical  part  of  the  proposal  presentation,  and  your  negotiation  strategy  is  your

insurance that the customer’s concerns with any aspect of your proposal do not

create  an  obstacle  that  prevents  you  from  winning  the  project.  The  more  well

developed your negotiation strategy, the better your chances for success. All that

remains to be done is to present the negotiation proposal.



With the negotiation situation and proposal analysis completed, the next step of

the negotiation process is to prepare for the negotiation meeting. The goal of the

meeting is to:



•  Build a perception of the proposal’s value by explaining the rationale of the



initial offer



•  Introduce trades and ask for commitments back from the customer



•  Address  any  of  the  customer’s  negative  reactions  to  your  proposal  ele-

ments. Every differentiating element of the initial offer and each trade

must be explained in terms of how they offer a benefit that satisfies the

customer’s need that drives the specific negotiation issue.



•  Handle any non-negotiable issues. You can handle them as a drawback or

difference of opinion depending on the nature of the non-negotiable item.



To facilitate planning for the negotiation meeting, you use the negotiating meeting

model shown in Fig. 12.13. Specifically, the negotiation meeting stages are as follows:



•  Opening



•  Review of the initial offer



•  Introduction of negotiating issues



502



CHAPTER 12  Collaborative Negotiations



Fig. 12.13

The negotiation meeting.



•  Address of non-negotiables



•  Close



Each stage and the appropriate planning issues are discussed below.



Opening



The opening of the negotiation meeting follows the same process as a sales call:



•  State the agenda



•  Present the value proposition



•  Check for acceptance



•  Determine if the customer has any other issues to be discussed



State the Agenda



The agenda is an overview of the order in which you wish to introduce the pro-

posal elements. This can be presented in the form of a flip chart, a handout, or

verbally (if the number of items to be discussed is limited). The agenda is the

presentation outline.



When preparing an agenda, you should know who from each side will attend the

meeting. For important negotiations, it is recommended that at least three team

members participate:



•  One person in charge of the meeting and does most of the presenting.



•  One person takes notes.



•  One person has the negotiations support documents and can perform any

calculations if the customer wishes to modify a trade or offer trades. The

impact of each modification or new trade on the contribution margin should

be considered before you accept.



The level of authority for each participant should be established. If the customer

has  a  negotiator  in  the  meeting  who  has  authority  to  commit  to  an  agreement,

your company should also have someone present with same level of authority. See

the “Okay, Who Signs?” sidebar for more about authority issues. It is also practical

to have someone with expertise in each of the negotiation issues. For example, if

operational  issues  will  be  discussed,  include  someone  from  operations  and  if

contractual issues are on the agenda, someone from the contracts department.



Successful Negotiations



503





u Okay, Who Signs?



Authority—Theirs and Yours



Conventional wisdom says you should

insist that the negotiator on the other

side of the table have full  authority.

Otherwise, you fall victim to the esca-

lating authority tactic described in the

tactics section of this module. There

are advantages to negotiating with the

person who has the power to approve

the agreement.



•  You know that all your reasoning is



heard directly by the approver, and if

you have a good relationship with

them, you are more likely to have a

collaborative negotiation.



•  You are less likely to have disputes



or confusion about the interpretation

of a particular provision.



As a practical matter, you may not

always be able to negotiate with the



individual (or committee) who retains

final authority. This can sometimes be

an advantage, as people without for-

mal authority may be freer to discuss

their company’s interests and invent

creative options. If you are dealing with

someone who does not have full

authority:



•  Suggest using the opportunity to

discuss your respective interests

and to come up with creative

options and packages.



•  When negotiating dollar issues,



only go as far as your second trade

in case the final negotiator  pushes

harder in a second round.



•  If you do make your final offer,



strongly convey the message that

this is your final offer unless they

can offer something else.



Instead of insisting on full authority, it’s

more important that you determine the



authority level of the person with

whom you will negotiate so that you

can plan accordingly.



If you learn that the negotiator has

very little formal authority and is not

respected or listened to by the

 decision-makers, try to get another

representative to participate in the

negotiations as well. One tactful way

to do this is to suggest that you will be

bringing a colleague (either with more

formal authority or because your joint

recommendation will carry more

weight) and request that the other side

do the same.



As for you, always know exactly how

much authority you have in the negoti-

ation. If you plan to be the one authori-

ty in the negotiation, then do not bring

your manager or his or her superior to

the negotiation, unless that is part of

your strategy.



Value Proposition



The value proposition must be strong enough to stimulate the customer’s interest

and  motivate  the  customer  to  participate  in  the  meeting.  Characteristics  of  a

strong value proposition are that it is believable and achievable, raises the cus-

tomer’s listening level, and encourages participation in the meeting.



With the value proposition, you explain how all the issues can be addressed by one

overall approach that meets the specific and interrelated need expressed by the

customer. You accomplish this with a statement that reviews the general benefit

addressed during preparation of the presentation analysis and strategy. Your value

proposition should:



•  Specify  the  main  areas  of  need.  For  example,  “Today,  I’d  like  to  review

where you stand in resolving the issues raised at the last meeting. In terms

of finances, you identified a need for cost savings. In terms of services, you

identified a need for reduced risks in completing sour gas zones.”



•  Explain that each item covered contributes to the overall satisfaction. For

example, “We will be reviewing a number of points, each of which addresses

one aspect of these needs and combines with all the other points to build

an overall solution to the issues we have identified.” This statement empha-

sizes the fact that the proposal should be considered as a package and not

as separate offers.



•  Explain how the proposal addresses these needs. For example, “To meet these

needs, we’ve come up with a comprehensive proposal designed to provide an

integrated solution to your needs as well as additional costs savings.”



•  State your commitment. For example, “We believe you will find our proposal

satisfactory and focused on your needs. However, we are still committed to

including  your  suggestions  on  how  to  improve  the  offer  and  better  meet

your company’s objectives.” This encourages the customer to participate in

the meeting.



504



CHAPTER 12  Collaborative Negotiations



Check for Acceptance



Two things happen when you check for acceptance:



•  It recognizes that there are two negotiating parties in the meeting and that



you want to ensure that the agenda and value proposition are complete.



•  You  can  gauge  the  customer’s  enthusiasm  for  beginning.  If  your  value

proposition is motivating, you should hear a reply such as, “Now that sounds

good.  Let’s  get  started.”  If  you  do  not  get  such  a  response,  your  value

proposition probably was not very effective.



Determine Whether the Customer Has Other Issues To Discuss



Unless  the  customer  adds  points  during  your  check  for  acceptance,  you  should

specifically  ask  to  ensure  that  all  issues  can  be  addressed  as  part  of  the  entire

package.  It  is  much  better  to  discover  any  additional  issues  at  the  start  of  the

meeting  so  you  can  demonstrate  how  the  current  proposal  addresses  the  new

issue. If a new issue is not addressed by the proposal components you have devel-

oped, you will need to agree on a time to discuss the issue.



Review the Initial Offer



The  initial  offer  is  the  benchmark  from  which  all  negotiating  trades  are  refer-

enced, and it is dangerous to assume that each person in the room recognizes the

benefits of your initial offer. In the initial offer review, you present a summary of

the highlights and clarify how the initial offer’s components address the negotia-

tion issues about to be discussed. An effective way to present the review is in the

form of bullet points, that clearly state the end benefit to the customer in finan-

cial  terms  as  much  as  possible.  Chapter  Highlight  12.3:  “Let’s  Review”  is  the

initial offer review used for the Shell Chukchi Sea negotiation discussed in Chap-

ter 4 “Opportunities Management.” The review made up part of the negotiation

document used in the negotiation meeting.



Preparation of Negotiating Issues



After reviewing your initial offer, the first priority is to transition into a discussion

of  the  first  trades  developed  by  your  team.  The  following  steps  are  taken  in

preparation of the negotiation issues:



•  Develop a negotiation rationale



•  Determine the sequence



•  Develop a general benefit statement



Develop a Negotiation Rationale



Just because your team has decided that a particular negotiation strategy is a

collaborative  win–win  proposal  does  not  mean  the  customer  shares  in  that

perception. The customer may be completely focused on their demands and not

the needs that drive that demand, as the demand from their perspective is a

quick fix for their issues. Your team must translate your trades into benefits the

customer  can  recognize  and  accept.  You  must  build  a  perception  of  the  pro-

posal’s value by explaining the rationale in terms of benefits. Every element of

each first trade must be explained in terms of how it offers a benefit that satis-

fies  the  customer’s  need  that  drives  the  demand.  Each  negotiation  rationale

should include:



Successful Negotiations



505





Chapter Highlight 12.3: Let’s Review



The Starting Point



The starting point of all negotiations is your

offer.  Beginning  a  negotiation  meeting

without  reviewing  the  initial  offer  is  a  bad

practice.  It  assumes  that  all  parties  in  the

negotiation are  familiar with the differential

value your solution provides. Presenting a

review provides an opportunity for the cus-

tomer’s  negotiating  team  to  include  the

differential  value  in  the  premium  they

assess  to  your  proposal.  The  additional

premium makes reaching the zone of pos-

sible agreement more  likely.



At  right  is  Company A’s  initial  offer  review

that  was  presented  to  the  customer’s

negotiating team. This summary was used

to  overcome  an  $1,300,000  (165%)  price

differential  between  Company  A  and

Competitor 1.



The review was included at the start of the

negotiation  presentation  document.  The

negotiation  trading  strategy  was  included

in  the  “Enhanced  Proposal”  section. The

enhanced  proposal  listed  Company  A’s

first trade for each of the main issues iden-

tified by the key buying center members,

who were also present in the negotiation meeting.



Company Logo



Company will provide the winning lease sale strategy and reduce

your rig costs and risks by using supplier’s advanced technology.



The following proposal has three parts:



                  Tab #



Part 1:  Why the company should choose supplier over

a competitor.



Commitment to safety

Experienced and Alaska-based personnel

Comprehensive Kenai support base

Unique risk reduction equipment

Special “Emergency Services Package”



Part 2:  Added value of advanced technology.



$1,500,000 reduced rig costs



Large borehole evaluation equipment

•

Combinable primary data tool strings

•

$300,000 reduced rig costs

Advanced formation testing operations

$50,000 reduced rig costs

•



Part 3: Competitive pricing proposal.



Main offer

Enhanced proposal

Reference price catalog



Supplier Logo



1

1

1

2

2



3



3



3



4

5

6



This example was discussed in Chapter Highlight 8.2: “We Would Like to Use You, But.”  This is case four

from Alaska, and the same example was also discussed in Chapter 9 “Strategic Sales Plan” Highlight 9.1:

“But it is Only One Piece of Paper.”



•  A general benefit showing the value of the proposal as a whole.



•  An explanation of how the technical benefits translate into business/finan-



cial benefits.



•  A demonstration of the added value in dollars.



Each step allows you to justify your trades and position them in terms of:



•  How they address the customer’s concerns, which are driving the demands.



•  How each trade and the proposal, as a whole, offer the customer valuable



and relevant benefits.



Once the negotiation rationale has been developed, the next step is to deter-

mine the sequence in which the negotiation issues will be presented.



Determine Sequence



In  determining  the  negotiation  sequence,  you  must  decide  which  demands  and

customer negotiating team members have the highest priority. The most important

demand (expressed by the most important member) should be the first agenda

item.  This  is  critical  because  addressing  the  most  important  negotiating  issue

immediately shows you have made their priorities the key issue. Also, if you satis-



506



CHAPTER 12  Collaborative Negotiations





















































factorily resolve the most important demand, you gain a measure of buy-in to the

lower  priority  demands  addressed  later.  If  there  are  any  non-negotiables,  these

should be discussed after the negotiation issues as shown in Fig. 12.11. Agreement

on negotiable issues can make the non-negotiables less important  and easier to

discuss and find solutions.



When  beginning  the  discussion,  you  must  be  sure  to  identify  to  whom  the  trade  is

directed. If there are other issues that are addressed with a specific trade, you must

include them as part of the discussion. This approach allows you to build an integrated

case  for  each  major  area  of  need.  It  uses  all  the  first  trades  as  developed  in  your

negotiating strategy and relates them to the overall logic and benefits of the proposal.



Develop a General Benefit



A well-developed negotiation proposal has value as an integrated whole, not just

in terms of specific trades. The proposal reflects your overall negotiation strategy.

In  addition,  each  trade  is  weighted  and  balanced  with  the  others  to  create  an

integrated  whole.  Since  the  proposal  is  an  integrated  whole,  you  should  avoid

allowing  the  negotiation  to  break  down  into  a  series  of  give-and-take  exchanges

that address only isolated elements. The more you keep the entire proposal in front

of the customer as you discuss individual points, the better your chances of suc-

cess, and because each trade in your proposal is related to all others, you use the

various elements to support one another during the negotiation process.



When developing a general benefit, the first step is to determine which major

areas of concern are reflected in the demands. This takes the focus away from

the demands for concessions and shifts it to the benefits you plan to introduce.



Next, position the entire proposal in terms of how it satisfies all the customer’s

needs.  This  positioning  provides  an  integrated  approach  that  addresses  all  the

implications  of  the  needs  and  is  not  a  random  collection  of  individual  and  nar-

rowly focused points.



Finally, explain that you wish to address each issue of concern (not each demand)

individually. Then, show how each issue interacts with the others to provide over-

all benefits. For example:



Salesperson:



“In  reviewing  the  discussion  we  had  at  your  last  meeting,  it

became clear that the major area of concern is keeping costs

down  while  maintaining  the  high  level  of  quality  and  service

you’ve come to expect. Our objective in developing today’s pro-

posal is to respond to these needs and provide a way for you to

realize the maximum possible benefits. Each of the points that

went  into  the  proposal  is  designed  to  help  build  that  overall

approach to the needs you’ve expressed since we last met.”



Negotiating Issues



This  is  now  the  main  part  of  the  negotiation  meeting.  In  the  discussion  of  the

negotiation issues, you will:



•  Describe the negotiation issue



•  Explain the trade rationale



•  Explain the trade



•



Support the trade



•  Check for acceptance



•  Make transition statements to the next negotiation issue or non-negotiable



Successful Negotiations



507





Describe the Negotiation Issue



The general benefit statement acts as the introduction to the entire negotiation

proposal for each negotiation issue. When introducing each negotiation issue, you

only  need  to  refer  back  to  your  general  benefit  statement.  However,  for  each

negotiation  issue,  state  the  specific  benefit  for  the  negotiation  issue  being  dis-

cussed. Translate each demand into the underlying need. If you are presenting a

trade  in  which  you  offer  a  technical  benefit  in  response  to  a  financial  demand,

you must translate that technical benefit into a financial one. It is poor negotiat-

ing  to  offer  a  benefit  that  is  not  introduced  in  terms  of  an  underlying  need  or

demand.  Otherwise,  the  customer  may  not  see  the  relevance  of  the  benefit,  as

discussed in Chapter 7 “Sales Call Skills.”



For example, the demand for a 45% price reduction is translated into a need for

cost containment. This takes the focus off the demand and puts it onto the need,

which can then be addressed.



Explain the Trade Rationale



After you have introduced the negotiation issue, explain the trade rationale. The

trade rationale introduces the trade.  Introducing trades is very similar to offering

supports in a sales call. You have a customer need, which is the negotiation issue,

and  you  are  using  a  feature  and  benefit  (your  trade)  to  address  this  need.  You

should introduce your first trade as a way to address this need. You could say: “As

we have discussed earlier, cost containment is a key concern. We have reviewed

the options that we can offer to provide the high level of quality you expect, while

still ensuring that you minimize overall costs of the project.



Explain the Trade



Explain the details of the how the trade will work. For example:



Salesperson:



“We’ve identified a way to achieve that objective by providing a

23% price adjustment in return for your agreement to give us

your business on the next 15 wells.”



Support the Trade



Just as you provide support for the customer’s needs in a sales call, you should

offer supports for the trade to illustrate how the trade will be implemented or has

been used in other situations and, if possible, customer references. This addresses

the  customer’s  concerns  and  helps  the  customer  accept  the  trade.  For  trades

including discounts, it is best practice to show an example of how the discounts

are applied to ensure that there is no misunderstanding. It is not uncommon for

trades to be complex and difficult to understand for the customer who is seeing

it  for  the  first  time.  The  example  will  allow  you  to  make  a  direct  connection  of

how the trade will apply to their project. You might say,



Salesperson:



“As you can see, in this calculation this option gives you very

significant  dollar  savings  and  locks  up  those  savings  for  the

next  15  wells,  and  my  company  benefits  from  the  additional

volume that allows us to maintain the required margin to give

you  the  same  high  level  of  service.  We  have  used  this  same

discounting mechanism for your operations in Thailand.”



Whenever possible, introduce a standard that can be used in support of your posi-

tion. For example, the salesperson could have said that for the level of additional

discount the customer was requesting your company only considers commitments

based on a dollar volume described in your price schedule. The salesperson could



508



CHAPTER 12  Collaborative Negotiations



u That’s Standard



What do you do when you are trying to protect your posi-

tion or persuade the customer to accept your trade as a

reasonable offer, but your rationale is not convincing the

customer? Try introducing a standard or searching for an

independent standard that is agreeable to both parties and

could be used to assess the offer. It is best if you prepare

a list of standards to have as backup prior to the negotiation

meeting.



Some useful standards in a negotiation are:



Example Calculation

Show an example calculation of the impact the trade will

have for the customer.



Market Value or Going Rate

What is the average contract price for similar  contracts?

This is more convincing if the data are included in a report

and even better if the report is part of a  market analysis by

an independent analyst.



Revenue Per Month

If significant resources are required for servicing a customer,

in most industries there is a minimum revenue per month

charge. This is a standard for small jobs. Explaining this stan-

dard might persuade the customer to accept your offer even
