though the price may seem high on a per-job basis.



Precedent

You can refer to what was done in the last contract or other

previous contracts. There may even be a master agreement

for the account you could refer to as the standard.



Industry Practice

Many industries have a list of industry practices to ensure

continuous improvement and help newcomers to their

industry. If there is an appropriate industry practice, this

can be useful. When negotiating clauses in  contracts for

liabilities, insurance, etc.



Competing Contracts

What did recent agreements consist of?



Equal Treatment

Are there established volume discounts based on revenue

per month, or market share commitments printed or includ-

ed in the published price catalog? If printed, these appear

to be more “standard.”



then  show  the  customer  the  volume  rebate  clause  in

the  price  schedule.  This  could  convince  the  customer

that the demand for a concession is unreasonable. For

more  discussion  on  using  standards,  see  the  “That’s

Standard” sidebar.11



Check for Acceptance



Confirm  the  customer’s  acceptance  of  the  trade.  For

example,  “Is  this  acceptable  to  you?”  If  the  customer

accepts the trade, introduce the next topic.



Below  are  some  tactics  you  can  use  if  the  customer

rejects the first trade.



Make Transition Statements to the Next

Negotiation Issue



When moving from one negotiation issue to the next, you

should relate the next negotiation issue back to the over-

all proposal benefit. You do this to build and reinforce the

perception of the proposal as an overall solution, as well

as the manner in which each trade satisfies the need. The

transition statement is the verbal tool that allows you to

do this. When making a transition statement:



• Review how the trade just discussed satisfies the



overall need.



•  Introduce the next trade.



• Preview how it satisfies a specific need or needs. For



example:



Salesperson:



“You’ve  just  seen  how  the  price  adjust-

ment  addresses  the  need  for  the  cost

savings. Now, I would like to review the

next  item  in  the  proposal  that  also

addresses  the  need  for  reducing  risks

when completing sour gas zones.”



When doing so, refer to the agenda flip chart or handout to

refocus the client’s attention on the next item for negotiation.



Handling the Customer’s Rejection of a First

Trade



If the customer’s rejects the first trade, follow the steps used to handle customer

negative reactions as discussed in Chapter 8 “Handling Customers’ Objections.”



•  Probe to understand the rejection. In this step you want to ensure that the

rejection  is  not  due  to  indifference,  misunderstanding,  skepticism,  or  a

restriction  the  customer  may  have.  For  example,  if  you  offer  a  10%  price

reduction in exchange for the customer paying for transportation of materi-

als needed for the work (as was the case in the Nirwana negotiation), the

customer might reject the trade if the company policy prohibits accepting

additional responsibilities for transporting suppliers’ materials to and from

the job site.



•  Confirm your understanding of the rejection.



Successful Negotiations



509





•  Handle the rejection according to the type of objection, whether it is indif-

ference toward the benefits of the trade, misunderstanding of the trade, or

skepticism that the trade can be implemented.



•  Check  for  acceptance.  After  resolving  any  of  the  negatives,  the  customer

may  now  accept  the  trade.  If  the  customer  states  that  the  first  trade  is

insufficient for what you are asking them in return, you use the negotiating

sequence described below.



Negotiation Sequence After Rejection of First Trade



If the customer has a restriction that prevents them from accepting the first trade

or they are not satisfied with the trade given the commitments they must make in

order to receive the benefits, adhere to the following negotiating sequence.



State  the  Concern:  The  more  clearly  you  can  state  the  concern,  the  more  pre-

cisely you can introduce benefits/options to resolve the concern. When stating the

concern:



•  Phrase it in terms of needs rather than demands.



•  Identify the obstacle to accepting the original trade.



•  Express your willingness to consider alternatives. For example:



Salesperson:



“Just to put the issue into perspective, your position is

that  you  need  as  great  an  up-front  savings  as  possible

and  feel  that  our  proposal  is  not  good  enough.  We  are

more than willing to explore the situation further as we

want to walk away from this session knowing we did all

we can to arrive at a mutually acceptable agreement. I’m

certain we can do that by working together.”



•  Confirm the customer’s objectives and restrictions. By asking and confirm-

ing objectives and restrictions, you encourage the customer to clearly and

directly  express  the  negotiating  range.  By  exploring  the  reasons  behind

these objectives and restrictions, you can better understand which aspects

the customer is not satisfied with. For example:



Salesperson:



Customer:



Salesperson:



Customer:



Salesperson:



Customer:



“The best way to resolve an apparent impasse is to see

where  you  stand  and  why.  Could  you  tell  me  what  you

want to accomplish on this point?”



“That’s  obvious.  I  want  to  cut  an  agreement  that  will

offer  me  the  cost  savings  already  offered  by  your  com-

petitor.”



“I see . . . now, in addition to the money, what level of

service and quality are you willing to give up in return?”

(confirming  restrictions  in  an  area  in  which  the  sales-

person knows his or her company enjoys a competitive

advantage)



“Well . . . none. And I don’t see that I would be giving up

anything.  After  all,  everyone’s  basic  services  are  the

same.”



“And  if  you  had  to  sacrifice  some  of  the  quality  you’ve

come to expect, would that be acceptable?”



“Not really. But I’m not convinced that I will not be los-

ing anything.”



During this interchange, the salesperson asked for and confirmed objectives and

restrictions. The restriction being implied is a large price concession that could



510



CHAPTER 12  Collaborative Negotiations



mean  a  reduction  in  quality.  However,  the  salesperson  did  not  respond  to  the

customer’s  unwillingness  to  accept  this  restriction  as  being  pertinent,  which  is

developed next when the salesperson states the objectives and restrictions.



State Your Objectives and Restrictions: When stating your objectives and restric-

tions, it is critical they be tied to added value and benefits. For example:



Stating objectives:



Salesperson:



“The  bottom  line  is  we  want  your  business,  and  we  want  to

continue to provide you with the level of service we’ve provided

in the past. And we want you to end up with a package that’s

satisfactory.  We’re  willing  to  make  as  much  of  a  price  adjust-

ment as we can to achieve that goal of mutual satisfaction.”



Stating restrictions:



Salesperson:



“As  you  realize,  though,  even  with  basic  services,  we  provide

additional  support  and  resources  to  ensure  that  the  level  of

service is maintained. For example, we offer highly trained field

engineers  and  state-of-the-art  technology.  All  these  additional

resources, which have a long-term dollar value to you and could

cost you money in the future if you couldn’t get them, cost my

company money to maintain. So we can’t adjust the price to the

level  you  ask,  and  any  price  adjustment  has  to  be  tied  into  a

commitment for services on additional wells that allows us to

maintain our targeted margin to cover our costs.”



At this point, the customer may respond by exploring the relevance of the addi-

tional resources offered, even on a basic service level. When this occurs, you use

it as an opportunity to introduce additional benefits.



If possible, support the relevance of these added-value benefits by presenting a

number of “what if” scenarios that could apply in the situation. “What if you had

a technical question that couldn’t be answered promptly and you had to make a

decision? What influence could that have on costs?”



Ask for Alternatives: Rather than proceed with your second trade, ask the cus-

tomer for alternatives that fall within the stated objectives and restrictions. This

makes the negotiation an interactive process. It also lets you gauge how effectively

your case has been made. Finally, it motivates the customer to think of solutions

within specific and reasonable parameters. The result may be one that is satisfac-

tory or even more beneficial to you than your own second trade. At this point, the

customer may even agree to accept the original trade.



Offer Second Trade: If the customer cannot offer an acceptable alternative, you

introduce your second trade and state it in terms of added value and benefits. For

example:



Salesperson:



“I have an alternative that addresses all the issues involved. My

company is willing to adjust the price even further, to 29%, if you

agree to give us all your business this year and pay your invoices

within 30 days. This means you’ll get the up-front cost savings you

want. Plus, that adjustment will be locked in for the entire year.

And we will be able to maintain that high level of quality service

and  additional  support  you  have  now.  From  our  point  of  view,

gaining  agreement  on  the  whole  year’s  program  justifies  this

volume pricing, and the 30-day payment ensures our cash flow.”



Determine and Confirm Client Acceptance: Once you have offered your second

trade, ask if it is acceptable. If it is, move to the next proposal topic. If it is the

last topic to be discussed, conclude the session. If the alternative is not accept-



Successful Negotiations



511





u One More Thing



What do you do when a customer demands a concession

you haven’t anticipated or planned for? Following the steps

below will put you in a better position to negotiate.



Define the demand by probing to determine:



•  What the customer specifically wants.



•  Needs driving the demand.



•  Level of importance attached to the demand.



Determine the benefit the customer derives if the

demand is met.



For example:



Salesperson:



Customer:



“When you say you want you to commit to

having a crew on your rig at your discre-

tion, can you tell me what that means?”



“It means I want your people at the rig the

minute I’m ready for them, and I want that

guarantee as part of the proposal. If

they’re not there, I want price reductions

on the service. I had an experience with

your people before. The truck was late, and

you had already pulled pipe. I lost a half

day of production.”



Salesperson:



“So, loss of production is the issue?”



Customer:



“Yeah, that and the money it costs me to

pay for an idle rig.”



able,  you  must  find  out  why  and  respond  accordingly.

Remember, whenever the customer rejects a trade or an

element in a trade, your subsequent action cannot lead

to a concession or offering more than the final offer you

established.



If you have a third trade, you would then repeat the nego-

tiation sequence as described for introducing the second

trade. Once you have discussed all the negotiable issues,

you can then focus on the non-negotiable issues. The non-

negotiable issues are handled in one of two ways, depend-

ing on the type of non-negotiable, as discussed next.



Address Non-Negotiable Issues



Non-negotiable  issues  are  treated  as  drawbacks,  as

described in Chapter 8, “Handling Customer Objections.”

If after searching for a solution or outweighing the draw-

back with previously accepted benefits the customer still

does not accept your explanation, handle the non-nego-

tiable as a difference of opinion, as described in Chapter

5 “Communication Skills.”



If you have to treat the non-negotiable as a difference of

opinion,  you  must  be  sure  that  when  you  conclude  the

discussion  on  that  issue  you  reiterate  your  company’s

willingness to discuss any alternative solutions the cus-

tomer may come up with later.



Salesperson:



“Then, you’re looking at the price reduction as

a way of compensating for lost production?”



Closing



Customer:



“Right, I’m not going to have my produc-

tion schedule messed up because of a late

truck. So if I do, you’re going to pay for it.”



In this exchange, the salesperson:



•  Uncovered the specifics.



•  Identified the need (maintaining productivity).



•  Determined the level of importance (it was the productivi-



ty, not the price reduction, that was the real issue).



Based on this, the salesperson can begin to develop a strat-

egy that addresses the real issues involved.



Ask for Time.



When you ask for time, you:



•  Acknowledge the concern (don’t agree with it).



•  State that you will develop a solution to the problem.



Once all the negotiation and non-negotiable issues have

been discussed, the lead negotiator closes the meeting.

Although this may seem obvious, you want to be sure to

close the meeting in a positive fashion, especially when

non-negotiable  items  were  treated  as  a  difference  of

opinion. Follow the same steps used in the sales call to

close the meeting. In closing the meeting:



•  Ask for any outstanding issues.



•  Summarize the agreements.



•  Review next steps (if any).



•  Ask for customer commitment that will secure the

project  (if  necessary,  this  commitment  may  be

conditional).



Ask for Any Outstanding Issues



•  Indicate when you will be back with a specific response.



You must always ask about any outstanding issues the

customer has. This ensures you do not miss any issues

the customer might have thought of during the meeting. A competitive nego-

tiator may seize this opportunity to introduce additional demands. When this

happens, follow the steps outlined in the “One More Thing” sidebar. As disap-

pointed as you may be to uncover new issues, it is better to have the issues

raised with those who are responsible for reaching an agreement so the dis-

cussion of next steps can continue.



512



CHAPTER 12  Collaborative Negotiations



Summarize the Agreements



To summarize, review the accepted trades in terms of the following:



•  Areas of concern the trades address



•  Benefits provided



For example:



Salesperson:



“We’ve covered a lot today. To briefly review, you’ve seen that, to

provide you with maximum short-term and long-term cost sav-

ings, we’ve agreed to a 23% price adjustment locked in for the

next 15 wells. We will continue to supply you with all the addi-

tional resources needed for completing your wells.”



Review Next Steps



If there are next steps, you briefly review what they are, who will be involved, and

when the steps will be taken. For example, assume that the customer demands a crew

be on the rig 24 hours before needed, which is not a demand you previously planned

on  addressing.  You  might  review  this  way:  “And  as  far  as  the  crew  request  is  con-

cerned, I’ll get back to you by Friday to discuss what I find out regarding this issue.”



Ask for Customer Commitment



The purpose of a negotiation is to reach a win–win agreement. The meeting is not

over until you have the commitment for some action that will advance the sale or

secure an agreement. During the meeting, your team must be alert to recognize

the  customer’s  comments  or  gestures  that  indicate  they  are  satisfied  with  the

meeting’s progress and are ready to conclude the negotiation and make an agree-

ment. This may occur before all issues have been discussed. It may also occur even

if  a  trading  range  has  been  exhausted  and  no  agreement  was  reached  on  some

issues. As stated before, you must continually remind the customer of the benefits

and  value  of  the  entire  package—even  if  there  is  no  commitment  on  some

issues—so the customer views the overall package as reasonable.



For example, you might ask for commitment in this way:



Salesperson:



“Since we’re all in agreement and we’ve developed a proposal

that addresses all the concerns on the table, let’s finalize the

proposal  by  drafting  the  contract  with  the  updated  clauses

reflecting our discussion today. I can send you my summary of

our discussion by tomorrow morning. How does that sound?”



If the customer has raised concerns or made demands that you cannot resolve

during the session and that require additional steps, you should still ask for a

commitment to advance the sale. You should consider this a conditional close,

as described in Chapter 7 “Sales Call Skills.”



Conditional  Close:  A  conditional  close  requests  customer  commitment  to  the

sales  objective  when  the  additional  concerns  are  eventually  resolved.  During  a

conditional close, you say, “Then, once you resolve the question of the crew, you’ll

be in complete agreement on all the issues you’re addressing. Based on that reso-

lution, you can go forward with the contract (date of service, etc.), agreed?”



A conditional close is important because it:



•  Builds a positive environment that results in closure.



•  Reinforces in the customer’s mind the important perception that eventual



closure will be reached.



•  Conditions the sale on the resolution of a specific point.



Successful Negotiations



513





If the customer rejects either your request for commitment or conditional close,

you should explore reasons for concern and respond accordingly. However, a nego-

tiation  does  not  always  result  in  an  either/or  outcome.  The  customer  does  not

always accept or reject the proposal. Frequently, there is a measure of acceptance

but not total acceptance. When the customer shows some buy-in to the trade but

does not accept the trade as it is, you may have to introduce your final offer fol-

lowing  the  same  steps  as  introducing  the  second  trade  as  previously  discussed.

But you do not make a concession. There is always the possibility that the cus-

tomer  is  being  competitive—trying  to  extract  an  even  better  deal  than  they

already seemingly agreed to.



To help you prepare for the negotiation meeting, create a meeting plan using the

“Negotiation Meeting Planner” found in Appendix 1 of this chapter.



Negotiation Meeting Planner



The negotiation meeting planner is used when preparing for the negotiation meet-

ing with the other team members. The completed negotiation meeting planner is

used to discuss the negotiation proposal components in the negotiation proposal

review meeting. The planner helps you prepare for each part of the negotiation

meeting  as  described  in  this  section.  Each  person  who  will  be  in  the  meeting

should have a copy for them to follow during the meeting. The planner can be used

to review the meeting plan with non-team members and mentors in the customers’

organization  when  seeking  their  feedback.  The  person  who  is  assigned  the

observer role uses the planner to keep their meeting notes. The negotiation plan-

ner is completed in addition to the sales call preparation worksheet discussed in

Chapter 7 “Sales Call Skills.”



Competitive Negotiators



The recommended negotiating style to take with your customers is collaborative

win–win approach where neither party is required to make a concession. As men-

tioned before, not all customers take a collaborative win-win approach to a nego-

tiation. This section discusses how to recognize and deal with competitive nego-

tiators.  It  is  also  addresses  ensuring  that  your  team  members  do  not  turn

competitive and use tactics to force an agreement. There are two types of com-

petitive negotiators:



•  Reactive



•  Strategic



Reactive Competitive Negotiators



Reactive competitive negotiators adopt a competitive negotiating style in reaction

to your offer because the offer has not met their expectations, either because a

comparable  competitive  offer  was  better  than  your  offer;  or  your  offer  was  not

acceptable given one of the customer’s key pricing sensitivities (such as the fair-

ness effect). When it comes time to negotiate, a reactive competitive negotiator

will do one of the following:



•  Make  a  demand,  which  is  a  concession  sought  by  the  other  negotiating

party. In most cases the concession is a single negotiable, such as a dis-

count, contract condition. For example, the  customer may state a demand,

as follows:



 Customer:



“If  you  want  the  project,  you  are  going  to  have  to  give  an

additional 20% discount. Do that and the project is yours.”



514



CHAPTER 12  Collaborative Negotiations



Demands

Positions



Analytical



Driver



High concern

for the result



High concern

for the result



Moderate concern for

the relationship



Low concern for

the relationship



Amiable



Expressive



Moderate concern

for the result



High concern

for the result



High concern for

the relationship



High concern for

the relationship



Fig. 12.14

Anticipating competitive negotiating

styles based on social profile.



•  Take  a  position.  A  position  is  a  statement  expressing  disappointment



over an aspect of the offer. For example,



  Customer:



“We are not happy with the call-out package.”



Social profiling, a communication skill discussed in Chapter 5 “Communication

Skills,” may help you anticipate a negotiator’s competitive style. Figure 12.14

shows that each social style tends to have different levels of concern for the

task  and  relationship.  The  combination  of  a  high  concern  for  results  and  a

moderate to low concern for relationship suggests that in competitive negotia-

tions Drivers and Analyticals make more demands while Amiables and Expres-

sives take positions. To help you prepare a focused negotiation strategy with

competitive negotiators, review the negotiating party’s social style as recorded

in your CRM contact database. Another tool that has been developed by labor

relations professionals is a negotiation style self-assessment. The assessment

has  been  modified  and  created  in  Excel  so  the  marking  and  analysis  of  the

answers are automatic. To learn more about your negotiating style, see Chapter

Highlight 12.4: “I Knew You Had Style.”



When confronted with a competitive negotiator, your goal is to move the other

negotiator from being competitive to being collaborative. To do this, you:



•  Focus  on  the  interest  or  needs  behind  the  other  negotiating  party’s

demands or positions and do not let their demands or positions force you

to make a concession. The key, as discussed in Chapter 8 “Handling Cus-

tomer Objections,” is to probe to fully understand the reason the customer

is being competitive and the benefit the customer expects from the con-

cession. For example, the customer demanding an additional 9% discount

is  concerned  about  costs,  so  you  want  to  focus  on  controlling  the  costs

rather than on deciding whether you can give an additional 9% discount.



•  Make trades for demands. To encourage the competitive negotiator mak-

ing a demand to adopt a more collaborative style, offer trades. For exam-

ple,  the  customer  demands  an  additional  5%  discount.  After  probing  to

understand the need behind the demand, you may offer a first trade of an

additional 3% discount for a commitment for all their work, based on the

fact that this trade would maintain your margin at the same level over the

same  period  and  allow  you  to  maintain  the  same  level  of  service.  After

making  this  offer,  you  test  the  customer’s  willingness  to  give  you  some-

thing in return to offset the additional discount. In most situations, this



Successful Negotiations



515





Chapter Highlight 12.4: I Knew You Had Style



Negotiating Style Self-Assessment Background

The negotiating style self-assessment is based on a book titled  The Labor Relations Process in which the authors

identify two distinct and very different approaches to bargaining.12



•  Distributive bargaining approaches negotiation as a win–lose exercise, where the gains of one party must come

at the expense of the other party.  The sole focus of the negotiator is to maximize his/her own outcomes. In the

negotiation literature, this orientation is referred to as assertiveness.



•  Mutual  gain  bargaining  approaches  negotiation  as  a  win–win  exercise. The  negotiators  focus  on  fulfilling  the

mutual interests of both parties. In the negotiation literature, this orientation is referred to as cooperativeness.



Negotiators  that  exhibit  assertive  tendencies  are  more  likely  to  engage  in  distributive  bargaining  behavior  while

negotiators  that  are  high  in  cooperativeness  are  more  likely  to  use  a  mutual  gain  bargaining  approach. Thus,  this

self-assessment will help you examine your levels of assertiveness versus cooperativeness.



According to the negotiation literature, the measurement of assertiveness and cooperativeness requires the consid-

eration of five distinct negotiation styles:



•



•



•



•



•



Positional – Negotiators that exhibit this style are very concerned with “winning the deal”. They are results-ori-

ented, self-confident, and assertive; are focused primarily on the bottom line; have a tendency to impose their

views upon the other party; and in the extreme can become aggressive and domineering. This style is high in

assertiveness and low in cooperativeness.



Indifferent – Negotiators that exhibit this style are passive, prefer to avoid conflict, make attempts to withdraw

from  the  situation  or  pass  responsibility  on  to  another  party,  and  fail  to  show  adequate  concern  or  make  an

honest attempt to get to a solution. This style is low in both assertiveness and cooperativeness.



Collaborative – Negotiators that exhibit this style use open and honest communication, focus on finding creative

solutions that mutually satisfy both parties, are open to exploring new and novel solutions, and suggest many

alternatives for consideration. This style is balanced in assertiveness and cooperativeness. This style will try to

negotiate an agreement but not if better alternatives exist for either party.



 Accommodating – Negotiators that exhibit this style is very concerned about maintaining relationships with the

other party, smooth over conflicts, downplay differences, and ensuring the needs of the other party are satis-

fied. This style is low in assertiveness but high in cooperativeness.



Compromising – Negotiators that exhibit this style are very concerned that an agreement be reached, often split

the difference between positions, frequently engage in give-and-take tradeoffs, and want satisfaction of both

parties’ needs.  This style is high in assertiveness and high in cooperativeness.



The first figure below displays the relationship among these five negotiating styles and the dimensions of assertive-

ness versus cooperativeness.



The second figure places five negotiating styles based on the social styles grid. A salesperson can determine the

other person’s social behavior through their everyday interactions such as fallback behavior prior to a negotiation.

Then social style can be used as a predictor of negotiating style.13



516



CHAPTER 12  Collaborative Negotiations



Chapter Highlight 12.4: I Knew You Had Style (cont.)



Below is the input for the Excel-based negotiating style analysis worksheet. The worksheet is designed to under-

stand your preferred behavior regarding the five elements that affect negotiations. You read five sentences that

describe a person’s behavior for each of the five elements and rank the sentences as most to least “like you.”  The

output is how much of each negotiation style you have based on your answers. The graphical analysis is the radar,

bubble, and donut shown below. There are three reports; each one has the description of each negotiating style

and one of the negotiating style graphs on the top of the report. Instructions for completing the worksheet are

included in the file.



NEGOTIATING STYLE QUESTIONNAIRE

Read the five sentences in “Element 1: Decisions” below. After you have read the 5 sentences,



consider each as a possible description of yourself.



RATE ONLY the first 4 sentences. The 5th sentence will be assigned a rating based on your first



4 answers. There can be no ties.



Put a “5” beside the sentence you think is most like yourself.

Put a “4” beside the sentence you think is the next most like yourself.

Continue ranking the other sentences with “3” for the third, “2” for the fourth, and “1” for least



like yourself.



Follow the same procedure for Elements 2 thru 6.



Element 1: Decisions



1.a)



I place high value on getting sound, creating decision that result in understanding



and agreement.



I accept the decision of customers and others.



I place high value on maintaining good relations.



I search for workable solutions, even though not perfect, agreed upon by customers



1.b)



1.c)



1.d)



and others, including myself.



1.e)



I place high value on getting a decision that sticks.



Element 2: Convictions



2.a)



I prefer to accept opinions, attitudes, and ideas of customers and others rather than



push my own.



2.b)



I stand up for my ideas, opinions and attitudes, even though it sometimes results in



stepping on people’s toes.



2.c)



I listen for, and seek out, ideas, opinions and attitudes different from my own. I have



clear connections, but respond to sound ideas by changing my mind.



2.d)  When ideas, opinions, or attitudes differ from my own, I initiate middleground positions.



2.e)



I go along with opinions, attitudes, and ideas of customers and others to avoid taking sides.



Element 3: Energetic Enthusiasm



3.a)



I am enthusiastic enough to get by.



3.b)



3.c)



3.d)



3.e)



I offer positive suggestions to keep things moving along.



I know what I am after and pressure others into accepting what I want.



I direct my full energies into what I am doing and others respond enthusiastically.



I support, encourage, and complement others on what they want to do.



Element 4: Conflict



4.a)   When conflict arises, I try to be fair but firm and get an equitable solution.



4.b)  When conflict arises, I try to identify reasons for it and to resolve underlying causes.



4.c)  When conflict arises, I try to remain neutral or stay out of it.



4.d)  When conflict arises, I try to stop it or to win my position.



4.e)



I try to avoid generating conflict, but when it does appear, I try to soothe feelings and to



keep people together.



Element 5: Temper

5.a)   When things are not going right, I defend, resist, or come back with counter-arguments.



5.b)  Because of the disturbance that tensions can produce, I react in a warm and friendly way.



5.c)  When aroused, I contain myself, although my impatience is visible.



5.d)  Under tension, I feel unsure which way to turn or shift to avoid further pressure.



5.e)  By remaining neutral, I rarely get upset.



Element 6: Humor

6.a)   My humor aims at maintaining friendly relations; when strains do arise, it shifts atten-



tion away from the serious side.



6.b)  My humor is intended to be persuassive in gaining acceptance for myself or my views.



6.c)  My humor is seen by others as rather pointless.



6.d)  My humor is hard-hitting.



6.e)  My humor fits the situation and gives perspective. I retain a sense of humor even under pressure.



Finished. Now you can review your profile.



Which Style Is Preferred?



People  often  ask,  ”which  is  the  best  negotiation  style?” As  with  much  management  theory,  there  is  no  single

”best” or ”right” approach. All five profiles of dealing with negotiation are useful in different situations. Although

people are capable of using all five, most of us tend to have one or two preferred negotiation conflict styles that

we use unconsciously in most conflict situations. Why? Either because our preferred styles have worked for us in

the past, or because of our temperament (nature) or upbringing (nurture)14



In B2B negotiations with customers with whom you want to build repeat business, it is suggested that a consis-

tent application of the collaborative style is best. The collaborative style offers the greatest probability of producing

negotiating results of the highest quality and most enduring satisfaction for the customer and you. If mutual need

satisfaction is of paramount concern to the parties and problem-solving strategies are harnessed to satisfy these

needs, then both the outcome of the negotiation and the relationship of the parties will benefit.



Successful Negotiations



517





Fig. 12.15

In competitive negotiations, your

customers may be focused only on their

demands or positions, and your company

may be focused only on your initial offer.

You may forget the bigger picture where,

like icebergs, the critical mass lies beneath

the surface. Moving a competitive negotia-

tor to a more collaborative style requires

probing to move beyond the stated demands

and positions to discover the common value

drivers both negotiating parties share.

These demands and positions—usually

defined by the negotiator’s personal inter-

ests, project requirements and corporate

strategies, objectives, and goal—can be

impacted by the other party. The common

impact area is where options and trades can

be made to build a win–win agreement



leads  to  the  second  trade  developed  in  your  negotiation  strategy.  At  this

point, you are negotiating rather than making concessions, and the customer

is collaborating.



•  Prepare options for positions. To work with the competitive negotiator who is

taking a position, create options. For example, the customer states that your

offer is not good enough and that they will not award you the contract given

the  current  offer.  The  key  here  is  to  move  the  negotiator  from  the  current

position by uncovering the negotiator’s interest and then exploring for options

to make the offer more attractive to them. Once the negotiator takes a more

collaborative approach, you can offer trades.



The key point for both types of competitive reactions (demands or positions) is to

find  common  areas  between  the  customer  and  you  that  can  be  impacted.  The

common impact area is where you can find options and trades to build a win–win

environment. The strategy for moving the competitive negotiator to a collaborative

position is to probe to discover the objectives and needs your company and the

customer have in common, then use those to develop trades and options (see the

“Tip of the Iceberg” analogy in Fig. 12.15).



Negotiating with reactive competitive negotiators is very possible as long as nego-

tiators maintain an open, collaborative style during negotiations and leverage the

up-front investment made to discover the value drivers of the project and individual

interests of the negotiating parties. Armed with a negotiating strategy with trades

that impact the forces driving the demands or positions of the competitive nego-

tiator, your can probe to bring the customer’s interests and needs to the surface.



Much like preparing for an indifferent customer in a sales call, your negotiating

team should use the Sales Call Preparation Worksheet to prepare the salesperson

to  lead  probing  strategies  that  confirm  the  customer’s  needs  so  that  you  can

demonstrate the impact of each of your trades, as discussed in Chapter 7 “Sales

Call Skills.” See the “Do You Know” sidebar (pg. 340) for a standard probe to use

at the beginning of any negotiation meeting with a competitive negotiator.



Strategic Competitive Negotiators



Unlike reactive competitive negotiators, strategic competitive negotiators not only

use demands and positions to extract concessions from your negotiating team, they

also consciously use negotiating tactics to get what they want. Strategic competi-

tive negotiators believe the use of tactics is the best way to achieve their objectives.



518



CHAPTER 12  Collaborative Negotiations



u I’ve Got a Secret



How can someone tell if you are dealing with a reactive

competitive negotiator or a strategic competitive negotia-

tor? This is a very good question, and the answer is very

important. After you discover, someone is strategic rather

than reactive, you know they are not going to agree to a

deal during your meeting in most situations. They will reject

most or all of your trades. Their strategy is to discover all

the trades you can offer and then force you to give them

the trade, but they give back nothing in return. They know

that if you say you have nothing left and walk away, they

have seen your best offer and you can’t offer anything

more. In negotiation training in the 1970s and 80s, this was

called the walk-away price. The consultant’s advice was to

establish a walk-away price at which, if the customer would

not pay that price, you would walk away.



The secret is, when you offer your first trade ensure your

trade brings your offer into the zone of possible agreement.

This may require you to bundle one or more trades to make

your offer their best alternative.  After this good faith trade,

you should expect a positive reaction from the customer.

They may not accept the deal, but there should be a positive

reaction. If this is the case, then more than likely you are

dealing with a reactive negotiator and can come to an agree-

ment by offering some additional trades.



If after your first trade the customer exhibits one of the tac-

tics described in Chapter Highlight 12.5, then most likely

they are strategic. If after you try to resolve their objections

they continue to not accept your trade and use tactics, then

treat their refusal to negotiate as a difference of opinion. In

this situation you agree to disagree. Thank them for their

time. Tell them you will leave your offer open for a certain

period, after which your offer reverts to the original offer.

Shake hands and tell them you are looking forward to work-

ing with them, then leave the meeting. By handling the situ-

ation like this, you leave the door open; do not be surprised

when the customer calls you back the next day.



The good news is once a customer recognizes you know

they are strategic, they will be less inclined to try this in the

future as they know it is counter-productive.



A tactic is defined as a pressuring technique to secure

a concession from the other negotiating party.



The strategy for your negotiating team is to recognize the

tactic and respond by letting the other negotiator know

that you are not going to be pressured into a concession.

Once  this  has  been  communicated,  you  use  the  same

techniques described earlier to move the other negotia-

tor  to  a  more  collaborative  negotiation  style.  Chapter

Highlight 12.5: “My Most Favorite Tactics” lists some

of the common negotiation tactics used by strategic com-

petitive negotiators. Many strategic competitive negotia-

tors consider tactics their secret weapons. The good news

is the tactics are not really a secret, and techniques for

reacting to them are well documented.



When  a  strategic  competitive  negotiator  refuses  to

change  to  a  collaborative  style,  you  make  your  first

trade offers on the condition that the customer agrees

to their part of the trade. Your negotiating team should

conclude the meeting by stating their disappointment

that an agreement could not be reached but that the

trades and conditions for the trades as offered remain

in  effect  for  a  specified  time  period.  You  should  also

make it known that you would welcome the opportu-

nity to discuss any aspects of the offer. If at all possi-

ble,  you  should  get  the  customer  to  agree  to  meet

again. After the meeting your team should determine

whether  mentors  or  supporters  in  the  buying  center

could offer advice on next steps.



As  discussed  in  the  situational  analysis  section  of  this

chapter,  your  team,  in  preparation  for  the  negotiation

meeting, should review the outcome analysis from previ-

ous  opportunities  for  the   customer,  particularly  the

notes about previous negotiations. Strategic competitive

negotiators  generally  use  similar  tactics  in  all  their

 negotiations.  The  secret  to  discovering  if  someone  is



being reactive or strategic is explained in the sidebar “I’ve Got a Secret.”



The next section is a brief discussion regarding pricing law as there is one law

in  particular  that  can  be  a  very  good  defense  against  strategic  competitive

negotiators.



Pricing Law



Getting a driver’s license in most countries requires the person to study, and

take tests; then their performance is tracked closely. Too many mistakes can

lead you to lose your license and possibly go to jail. Drive well and you can get

lower insurance rates. In Chapter 6 “Sales Activities,” a sidebar described how

to stay out of sales jail to dramatize best practice for sales calls captured in

the three laws of sales calls. This section has no humor. It is a serious business,

and unfortunately too many professional salespeople are unaware of the laws

of the country they are selling in and where their parent company is incorpo-

rated.  As  a  professional  salesperson,  you  need  to  be  aware  of  the  relevant

pricing laws as they pertain to sales and know what you can and cannot do as

governed by the law. This section is not meant to be an exhaustive review of

the pricing law. The objective is to make you aware that it is your responsibil-



Successful Negotiations



519





Chapter Highlight 12.5: My Most Favorite Tactics



Tactics—Secret Weapon of Strategic Competitive Negotiators



Strategic competitive negotiators use their favorite tactics during negotiations. Some tactics

are even used before the negotiation meetings, while most are saved for the right moment

during the negotiation meeting. The challenge for your negotiating team is to recognize

the tactic and react to tit by letting the other negotiator know that you recognize their

tactics  and  will  not  make  a  concession  in  response.  You  then  use  the  negotiation

sequence described earlier to continue to try to move the other negotiator to a more

collaborative  style  toward  a  win–win  agreement.  Listed  below  are  some  commonly  used  tactics,



grouped into categories, and suggested countermeasures.



Budget Based and Scope of Work Tactics

Budget Constraints or Company Policy: The customer says, “We want to work with you, but our budget for this

work is only US$100,000” or “we want to work with you, but our company policy does not allow us to accept these

charges.” Countermeasure: You should change your offer by eliminating or changing components to reduce costs

to fit their budget. You take the stand that it should not be up to you to address the customer’s budget restrictions.

You suggest that the stated budget restrictions can probably be dealt with if the customer thinks your position is

reasonable, and the issue should be explained to the customer’s boss or partners. Finally, you point out that neither

budgets nor standard policies are cast in stone.



Budget Estimate: The customer gets you to make an estimate for budgeting purposes. They use this as the start-

ing point for future negotiations if you treat it as bid. Countermeasure: Your response should be full price book for

that work. Once customers have their budget approved internally, they will ask for a bid from all potential suppliers.



Exaggerated Scope of Work: You get interested, hooked, and mentally committed by being offered an attractive

deal, which later becomes more and more unattractive. Examples: Negotiations are started on the basis of an

attractive initial offer, which in the course of the negotiations is changed for the worse and referred to as a mis-

understanding. The program calls for 20 wells, complete drilling, and completion programs. You give your best

deal but end up with only 2 wells. Countermeasure: The best countermeasure to this tactic is to assume that

the scope of work will not materialize and take steps to financially protect your proposal for that scenario. For

example, you propose escalating discount tables. In the 20-well example, you might propose the following: 1 to

5 wells = 0 discount, 6 to 10 wells = 3% discount, 11 to 15 wells = 6% discount, 16 to 20 wells = 11% discount.

Then, calculate the overall discount and emphasize that this is the discount the customer is getting if they com-

plete the stated work scope.



Additions to Scope of Work: This is the reverse of the exaggerated scope of work. The customer tells you, “This

is an easy job with plenty of competition. I just want an across-the-board discount.” You accept and give the dis-

count. Later, the client requests “add-ons:” “In this part of the field, you need your latest technology and state-of-

the-art interpretation capability, etc.” Meanwhile, you are thinking, “How can I possibly give him that at the earlier

agreed-upon  low  prices?”  Countermeasure:  Before  offering  anything,  you  should  precisely  define  the  scope  of

work and state that any products or services requested outside the scope of work will be quoted separately at that

time or will be charged as per the attached price catalog.



Pressuring Tactics

Competitive Negotiator/Compromise Negotiator: One negotiator is very competitive, rigid, unreasonable, and

unpleasant.  But  an  accompanying  colleague  is  compromising,  reasonable,  and  almost  seems  to  side  with  you

against the “competitive negotiator.” After a while, you find it difficult to disagree with the “compromising nego-

tiator.”  Feelings  of  gratitude  and  a  desire  to  reciprocate  start  to  undermine  your  bargaining  effectiveness. The

same person can use the two opposing negotiation styles: The customer has been very unpleasant  and com-

petitive  but  suddenly  grows  rather  pleasant  and  reasonable.  Often,  the  effect  is  to  feel  inclined  to  reward  the

person for the sudden, unexpected reasonableness, or to capitalize on the customer’s good mood—by making

concessions. Countermeasure: You must constantly keep in mind that both negotiators are on the same team

and are actually conspiring against you.



Take It or Leave It: If the customer tells you it is a take-it-or-leave-it offer, you are typically inclined to accept a con-

tract clause or reduce the price. Countermeasure: You should always test a take-it-or-leave-it position as most posi-

tions are still flexible. You should meet the negotiator’s hurdle, but adjust your offer so your contribution margin is

the same. For example, you can modify the deal toward larger or smaller quantities, different service levels, delivery

periods or payments terms, etc. You can also take the position that you do not wish to negotiate a take-it-or-leave-it

situation or create a deadlock. If you have to make your last trade and still do not have an agreement, you want to



520



CHAPTER 12  Collaborative Negotiations



end the discussion in such a way that you always leave the other party a way out of their tactic so they can resume

negotiations without having any angry feelings.



Ridiculous Offer: The purpose of this tactic is to destabilize you in order to bring your expectations down. It is often a

very low opening price given by the customer, such as, “I expect a 45% discount on this well.” Countermeasure: You

treat this tactic as a take it or leave it and keep on negotiating. The more confident you are in your market analysis, the

more certain you can be that this is a negotiating tactic. When a customer describes your offer as ridiculous, you should

ask to see the competitor’s offer, as the competitor may have overlooked some charges. Some salespeople think it is

unethical for the customer to show them the competitor’s prices. In fact, it is just the opposite. In many countries, anti-

trust legislation prevents suppliers from reducing their prices unless it is to meet a competitor’s price.



Short Notice: The customer wants you to negotiate at the last minute by asking for your commitment or best offer

within a day or less. The customer hopes to force you to your final offer immediately so they can begin negotiations

at this point. Countermeasure: The main purpose of the opportunities management process is to make time your

advantage and avoid such situations. Even if the customer does not reveal that a tender is coming out, you can expect

a tender and prepare in advance if you have managed the opportunity early. Part of completing a reply to a request

for tender is to also complete the Negotiating Worksheet as part of the pricing strategy. This helps you be prepared

in the event of short notice. However, if the customer tries to use a competitive negotiating tactic by leveraging time,

you ignore the time deadline, offer your first trade, and make no commitments to the customer’s trade offer until you

have fully evaluated the impact of the offer.



Silence:  Silence  is  the  easiest  tactic  to  prepare  for. The  customer  knows  most  people  are  uncomfortable  with

silence, so they will be silent when you make an offer in the hopes that you break the silence first. This tactic is

especially effective against an inexperienced negotiator, as the inexperienced negotiator is inclined to offer another

trade before the customer comments on the trade already under consideration. Countermeasure: The countermea-

sure for a negotiator who is silent after you make an offer is silence. You do not say anything until the other nego-

tiator comments on your offer. If the silence becomes unbearable, you take a break and tell the negotiator you are

prepared to give him or her more time to consider your trade.



You Have to Do Better than That: Strategic competitive negotiators are never satisfied. They keep saying that your

proposal is not enough. Typically, they use phrases such as “you have to do significantly better than that,” “you have

to do better than that,” “you are getting there,” and/or “you are finally getting closer.” In reality, you may have exceed-

ed their expectations, although their strategy is to continue asking until you say no. Countermeasure: The customer

has basically accepted your proposal, so you acknowledge their statement by saying that what you have offered is very

good considering the scope of work. You can ask the customer for any ideas on what else could be done—for example,

increase the scope of work or lower your costs. If not, you close the meeting by summarizing the benefits of your

solution and express your willingness to discuss further proposals. You also remind them that your offer is valid for a

limited time.



Emotional  Outbursts  or  Irrationality:  Some  negotiators  use  emotional  outbursts  or  irrationality  as  a  pre-

meditated tactic; others are really upset. It does not matter whether the emotion is genuine or tactical. What

matters is how you react. How do you deal with a customer’s rage and manage your own emotions at the same

time? Countermeasure: Against controlled/simulated emotional outburst: You are not intimidated, and you

make it clear that you are not impressed. Customers sometimes use emotion—usually anger—to rattle sales-

people into making concessions they would not otherwise make. Salespeople use three different techniques

for handling a customer who uses anger—wittingly or unwittingly—as a manipulative tactic: You withdraw. You

ask for a recess, consult with the boss, or reschedule the meeting. A change in time and place can change the

entire landscape of a negotiation. You listen silently while the customer acts emotionally or irrationally. You

do  not  nod  or  agree. You  maintain  eye  contact  and  a  neutral  expression  but  do  not  reinforce  the  customer’s

behavior. When the outburst is over, you suggest a constructive agenda. You react openly to the customer’s

outbursts. You say that you find it unproductive and suggest focusing on a specific, non-emotional issue. There

are two keys to this technique. The first is timing. You don’t rush the process or risk backing the customer into

a corner from which there is no graceful escape. The second is to insist that the use of manipulative tactics is

unacceptable and then suggest a constructive agenda. You can’t be timid. The only way to pull this off is to be

strong  and  assertive.  For  example:  Imagine  this  response  to  a  customer  who  just  finished  screaming: “This

attack is not constructive. [Strong eye contact, assertive tone.] You’ve spent three hours working the issues and

trying to arrive at a fair and reasonable solution. I suggest you go back to the question of payment terms and

see if you can finalize those.” The essence of negotiating effectively with aggressive customers is to sidestep

their  attacks  and  convince  them  that  a  common  effort  at  problem  solving  is  more  profitable  and  productive.

Countermeasures: Against genuine emotional statement and irrationality: You first try to determine what

is making the other negotiator angry by listening very carefully. Then you apologize if you did something to cause

the anger. Finally, reschedule if the tension is too high to continue and if you cannot negotiate in a collaborative

manner.  If  necessary,  you  can  also  change  one  or  more  members  of  the  negotiating  team  to  complete  the

negotiation.



Successful Negotiations



521





Research-Based Tactics

What If?: The customer presents various scenarios to encourage you to move from your position and calibrate how

much of a concession they can push for. For example, “How much of a discount do you get if you go for a three-year

contract?”  or  “How  much  of  a  discount  if  you  also  give  your  company  the  wireline  and  well  testing  work?”

Countermeasure: You do not make any offer until you have time to evaluate the full impact of their offer on your

contribution margin. You also probe for the underlying trade. For example, “Has the scope of work changed?”



Escalating  Authority:  When  you  think  you  have  completed  the  negotiation,  a  higher  authority  person  who

should have been there before appears in the room, or the party you’ve been dealing with tells you, “You will

now take it to their manager for approval.” You have to restart the negotiation with the higher authority from the

point already reached. The new authority considers this a fresh starting point; as a result, you may be pressured

to  make  a  concession.  Countermeasure:  When  discussions  with  a  customer  first  begin,  you  should  clarify

whether there is a person in authority at the negotiation table before engaging in the negotiation. If the person

in authority is not present, you have two options: Postpone the negotiations until the higher authority is available,

or offer only your first trade. Hold back on your offer so you have room to negotiate when the higher authority

comes to negotitate.



Devious Tactics

Accidental Information: Seemingly by mistake, the negotiator leaves notes or documents in plain view on the

table. The notes give you information on the competitor’s offer or what seems to be the negotiator’s alternative.

This tactic was probably planned. Or during the negotiations, the customer makes notes in a way that you can

read them. The customer might leave the room to take a phone call, asking you to wait briefly to resume the

discussion. The left-behind notes may actually be a competitor’s proposal. In both staged scenarios, the “informa-

tion”  may  be  real  or  staged.  Obviously,  the  information  is  meant  to  encourage  you  to  lower  your  offer.

Countermeasure: When you obtain information in this dubious fashion, you must be extremely cautious. The

planted notes should be viewed merely as an assumption. The information may or may not be true, but in any

case it cannot be taken for fact. If the notes are valid, you should not be threatened by the competitor’s lower

prices. If you are confident about the value you bring to the customer’s business with your proposal, you can

treat  the  customer’s  reaction  as  a  financial  drawback  and  overcome  the  price  difference  with  your  differential

value. If you think the competitors used a short-term pricing strategy and have made a much lower offer than

your market analysis predicted, ask to see the competitor’s offer to be sure they did not leave anything out. If

your  prices  are  based  on  a  long-term  pricing  strategy,  you  may  want  to  reduce  your  prices  as  long  as  target

margins can still be met; in an extreme case, take a short-term pricing strategy. However, such decisions are not

made in front of the customer; include your location management in the discussion and contact the segment

headquarters marketing group to determine whether this pricing strategy has been used elsewhere by the com-

petitor.



Last-Minute Changes: Customers may use this almost unethical tactic during the final stages of negotiation. The

customer brings an updated, “signed” contract and asks you to sign the contract as soon as possible. They may also

tell you that to save time they have updated a few of the standard clauses. Countermeasure:  Never sign any con-

tract that has not been fully reviewed or agree to any modified clauses that have not been discussed. Despite any

urgencies, never succumb to this tactic.



One More Thing: After the deal is done, one party tries to change the deal to get a more favorable edge. It’s gener-

ally just a little—enough to make it more beneficial for that party but not enough to make the other side cancel the

offer. For example, customers might request that a certain item of your services (back-up equipment, data process-

ing, support, etc.) be included at no cost to them. Sometimes, they may even ask for big concessions such as no

“lost-in-hole” charges. Countermeasure: Do not accept any items added to the agreement. Politely calculate the

cost of the additional items and add this to the cost of the previous offer.



Online Reverse Auction

A customer conducts an on line reverse auction where all bidders see the other bidders’ prices and are allowed to

change their prices based on the last bid. The objective is to get the suppliers to come down to their absolute low-

est price. Countermeasure: There are several strategies that can be followed. One is submitting your offer price as

determined by the information you gathered to complete your pricing strategy and not changing it. In most online

reverse auctions the customer is not committed to taking the lowest bidder. If you believe that the customer will

most likely take the lowest bidder and you cannot compete, use this opportunity to execute a disengage strategy

and submit a very high price. This will establish a higher market rate for the next tender. Another option is not par-

ticipating  in  the  online  reverse  auction.  Explain  to  the  customer  properly  preparing  a  value-added  solution  and

financial offer requires you to invest time and effort, which costs your company money.  This information is contained

in your offer price, and you do not want to give this to the competition. You will, however, submit a sealed offer for

them to consider.



522



CHAPTER 12  Collaborative Negotiations



This concludes this discussion on tactics. Below is a summary the author prepared for a common ques-

tion from students: “When do competitive strategic negotiators like to negotiate?” The answer is below



the summary.



Always! Strategic negotiators always like to negotiate and use all the tactics to get the best price.  The figure

above shows the opportunities management stage in which you can expect to see a tactic from a competi-

tive  negotiator  to  secure  new  work. The  above  graphic  does  not  have  any  tactics  in  the  implementation

stage, which is not to suggest that strategic negotiators do not use tactics in the implementation stage. The

reality is they can use all the tactics in the implementation stage, but your countermeasure is still the same

as described in this highlight.



Review this summary and see if you recognize a tactic perhaps one of your customers has used with you.

If so, you can expect them to use it again in a future negotiation and, if they do, you can be prepared by

using the countermeasure to move the negotiation back to a win–win collaborative negotiation.



ity to know the applicable laws for your company and where you sell and how this

applies  to  negotiations.  The  laws  that  govern  pricing  and  negotiations  are

referred to as antitrust law.



Antitrust Law



Antitrust law is the law of competition.  The laws seek to promote fair competition

on the merits and to protect consumers and wronged competitor businesses from

anti-competitive  business  practices  undertaken  in  an  effort  to  undermine  com-

petitive commercial behavior in a given market or line of commerce. These laws

were  originally  established  to  check  the  abuses  threatened  or  imposed  by  the

immense “trusts” that emerged in the late nineteenth century. These trusts con-

trolled or threatened to control entire nationwide markets for rail transport, steel,

petroleum,  banking,  and  related  lines  of  commerce.  The  antitrust  laws  were

established to ensure that these trusts did not permanently undermine competi-

tion in these or other markets.



The antitrust laws, therefore, forbid the wrongful acquisition or preservation of a

monopoly of power, the abuse of a monopoly of power in order to establish a new

monopoly, and concerted restraints of trade (i.e., business practices undertaken

by two or more firms that improperly stifle or suppress “competition on the mer-



Successful Negotiations



523





its” in a given market). They also govern proposed mergers and acquisitions that

are sufficiently large to constitute a threat to competition, and they address com-

mercial practices that pose an arguable danger to competition on the merits in a

properly defined antitrust market.16  Virtually all of the world’s antitrust regimes,

including the U.S., Europe, the U.K., Japan, Brazil, and Australia, have laws that

forbid the following:



•  Bid rigging



•  Market allocation



•  Price fixing



•  Price discrimination



•  Bribery



The penalties for violation of the antitrust and bribery laws can be serious, both

for the company and for individuals involved, and individual liability can extend

to  those  involved  in  making  and  carrying  out  the  terms  of  an  anti-competitive

agreement as well as to officers and directors of the company. Under U.S. federal

law, for example, criminal penalties for individuals can include fines up to $1 mil-

lion and ten years in prison for each offense. Criminal penalties for companies can

reach $100 million per offense. In Europe, penalties for violation of E.C. competi-

tion law can be up to 10% of a company’s global turnover in the prior business year.

In the U.K., corporate penalties are also up to 10% of worldwide turnover, and the

use  of  criminal  charges  against  individuals  is  increasing.  The  U.K.  saw  its  first

criminal convictions of individuals for antitrust violations in 2008.17 As a profes-

sional salesperson, you must discuss with the legal department in your company

what are the laws you must be aware of. In general, you cannot have any discus-

sions  with  your  competition  at  any  time  about  bidding,  negotiations,  pricing,  or

market share. If you do, you are in violation of most antitrust laws. The laws from

the U.S. are often referred to, and the most relevant ones are listed below.18



Sherman Act. This statute is the premier article of federal law. It is the original,

principal,  and  foremost  antitrust  statute  in  the  United  States,  setting  forth  the

broad statutory proscriptions that act as a “charter of the marketplace” and “con-

stitution of competition law” in American jurisprudence. The Sherman Act codifies

the principal antitrust offenses—conspiracies to restrain trade, monopolization,

attempted monopolization, and conspiracies to monopolize. The Sherman Act is

worded in broad, open-ended language, so that clever competitors cannot elude

its provisions. The Sherman Act sets forth the fundamental standards, and it is for

the  courts  to  decide  in  each  case  whether  the  challenged  conduct  constitutes

monopolization of a line of commerce or the concerted imposition of an improper

restraint of a line of commerce.



Clayton  Act.  This  law  is  another  federal  statute  that  imposes  restrictions  on

proposed mergers and acquisitions. It also supplements the Sherman Act, prohib-

iting certain kinds of commercial practices that excessively stifle competition on

the merits. It sets forth various civil remedies. Indeed, it was the Clayton Act that

established a private right of redress for civil relief under the Sherman Act, award-

ing  treble  damages  and  attorneys’  fees  to  a  private  plaintiff  who  prevails  on  a

claim made under the antitrust laws of the United States. In addition, the Clayton

Act usefully allows the courts to enjoin anti-competitive conduct before it actually

causes harm.



Robinson–Patman Act. This statute forbids sellers from making sales of the same

or similar products at the same time to commercial customers at different prices,

if the practices causes harm to competitive processes in the seller’s market, the

buyers’ markets, or further downstream markets.



524



CHAPTER 12  Collaborative Negotiations



Federal Trade Commission Act. This is another federal statute that established

the Federal Trade Commission (“FTC”), which has regulatory authority to enforce

the Sherman Act, the Clayton Act, and the Robinson–Patman Act. Significantly,

Section 5 of the FTC Act confers additional authority on the FTC, allowing it to

test the limits of antitrust policy. An aggrieved firm that concludes that it has no

civil  remedy  under  the  Sherman  Act  or  Clayton  Act  might  decide  that  its  best

recourse is to complain to the FTC, asking that it invoke its authority under Sec-

tion 5 of the FTC Act in order to investigate the matter and initiate administrative

proceedings in order to enjoin the challenged conduct.18



The Bribery Act 2010.19 This law is an Act of the Parliament of the United King-

dom that covers the criminal law relating to bribery. It is illegal to offer, promise,

give, request, agree, receive, or accept bribes. Broadly, the act defines bribery as

giving or receiving a financial or other advantage in connection with the “improper

performance” of a position of trust, or a function that is expected to be performed

impartially  or  in  good  faith.  Bribery  does  not  have  to  involve  cash  or  an  actual

payment exchanging hands and can take many forms, such as a gift, lavish treat-

ment during a business trip, or tickets to an event. Some simple examples of the

types of bribery considered illegal follow:



•  Secure or keep a contract.



•  Secure an order.



•  Gain any advantage over a competitor.



•  Bribea local, national or foreign official to secure a contract.



•  Bribe  an  official  to  ignore  a  health  safety  issue  or  poor  performance  or



substitution of materials or false labor charges.



•  Falsify an inspection report or obtain a certificate.



For  negotiations,  the  law  that  can  actually  be  a  shield  when  dealing  with  com-

petitive  negotiators  is  the  law  against  price  discrimination  covered  by  the  Rob-

son–Patman Act.



Price Discrimination



A seller is entitled to set prices as he or she chooses when dealing with different

customers. Most sellers would resent any effort to restrict their discretion to set

and  vary  their  prices  for  different  sales.  In  market  economies,  firms  decide  for

themselves  what  to  charge  for  their  products  and  to  whom  they  wish  to  make

sales. It is contrary to the fundamental principles of market economics to regulate

what sellers charge for their products or impose a rule that they cannot vary their

prices  for  the  same  goods  when  selling  them  to  different  customers.  Thus,  the

general rule is that a seller can practice price discrimination, except when doing

so poses a substantial risk of injury to competition. A seller can therefore charge

one  customer  $5  for  a  widget  and  another  customer  $10,  unless  this  price  dis-

crimination is likely to limit competition in any market affected by the sales of

the same or similar products to commercial customers at different prices. There

are levels of harm at which competition can be affected.



•  Primary-line harm, which occurs when the seller uses low prices to under-

mine its own direct competitors by underselling them until they are ruined.



•  Secondary-line  harm,  which  occurs  when  the  seller’s  low  prices  to  the

favored customer allow this customer to undersell and thereby ruin its own

direct competitors.



•  Tertiary-line harm, which occurs in downstream markets when the seller’s

low prices to the favored customer allow the favored customer or its own

customers to undersell and thereby ruin the disfavored customers.



Successful Negotiations



525





In the event that each of the elements of a Robinson–Patman violation is proved,

there are two important defenses which, if established, permit the defendant to

prevail  despite  the  injury  to  competition  that  may  result:  cost  justification  and

meeting competition.



Cost justification. The statute expressly permits discriminations that “make only

due allowance for differences in the cost of manufacture, sale or delivery resulting

from the differing methods or quantities” sold or delivered to the purchasers. Cost

justification is extraordinarily complex. Suffice it to say that a successful defense

involves intricate accounting techniques hobbled by stringent rules.



Meeting competition. If the lower price was made in good faith to meet the equally

low price of a competitor, the seller has an absolute defense to a Robinson–Pat-

man charge even if every other element of a violation is proved. The key to the

defense is the seller’s good faith. The seller must obtain sufficient facts to justify

a reasonable belief that the lower price is equal to—not lower than—that of the

competitor.  In  other  words,  it  is  permissible  to  meet,  but  not  to  beat,  the  com-

petitor’s price. It is thus advisable to make a record of every available fact dem-

onstrating  the  identity  of  the  competitor  and  its  price.  To  avoid  Sherman  Act

problems, these facts should be sought from the customer, not the competitor.20



The specific clause from the Robinson–Patman act is shown below.



Clayton Act Section 2(b) [If a seller is found to have discriminated in his

pricing,  the  seller  can  rebut  the  charge  by]  “showing  that  his  lower  price

was made in good faith to meet an equally low price of a competitor. . . .”



What this means for you in a negotiation is, if a customer asks you to reduce your

price to match a competitor, then according to the law you must review the com-

petitor’s offer. The Robinson–Patman Act can be used to protect your offer from

customers who are being competitive. For a real example of how this was done,

see Chapter Highlight 12.6: “I’m Surprised to Hear You Say That.”



After the Negotiation



After  the  negotiation  is  completed,  you  are  in  the  second-to-last  stage  of  the

opportunities  management  process  of  monitoring.  Follow  the  recommended

actions  as  described  in  Chapter  4  “Opportunities  Management”  for  the  monitor

stage. The most important factor at this time is to be available for the customer

and, in particular, your mentors inside the customer’s buying center and organiza-

tion. It is at this time that you need to be “insulated from the competition.” The

final  decision  will  be  made  in  most  cases  when  you  are  not  in  the  customer’s

office. You will need your supporters and mentors to defend the decision to choose

your company for the tendered work.



As such, it is very important during this time that you:



•  Record what the final negotiated offer? Ensure that any changes are added

to the contract and communicated during the contract handover meeting

between the bid team and contract owner for the implementation stage.



•  Follow-up with your supporters and mentors to review with them the nego-



tiated offer.



•  Document who was on the customer’s negotiation team.



•  Identify their negotiation style.



•  Identify the negotiation issues.



•  Determine the tactics they used and how you responded?



•  Update the pricing workbook.



526



CHAPTER 12  Collaborative Negotiations



•  Identify the specifics of the competition’s offer and negotiation strategy.



•  Detail any other lessons learned.



Share all these notes with the bid and sales team and attach them to the oppor-

tunity in your company’s CRM system.



In this chapter, there has been a total of eight processes and tools referred to that

can help you become a better negotiator. The tools and processes are:



1.  Opportunity Management Process



2.  Strategic Sales Plan



3.  Pricing Workbook



4.  Tender and Negotiation Summary



5.  Negotiation Meeting Model



6.  Sales Call Skills and Handling Customer Negative Reactions



7.  Negotiation Meeting Planner



8.  Collaborative Negotiation Process



To help you apply these tools, use the “Collaborative Negotiations” summary card.

You  can  refer  to  the  card  when  you  are  preparing  or  waiting  in  the  customer’s

reception before a negotiation. The card summarizes the sections discussed in this

chapter onto two pages and can be printed double sided and added to your orga-

nizer  discussed  in  Chapter  4  “Opportunities  Management”  in  Chapter  Highlight

4.4: “Get Organized.” The card is in Appendix 2 of this chapter.



By  applying  and  using  these  tools,  you  will  improve  your  confidence  and  power

that  you  will  have  when  preparing  and  negotiating  with  your  customers.  Table

12.2 is a summary of where the sources for building your confidence and negotiat-

ing power are from. Each of these were described at the start of this chapter in

the sidebar “Negotiating Power.” Of the different categories of power listed in the

table, the most significant is personal power, which is your ability to build trusting

relationships. It is much easier and more efficient to negotiate with someone you

respect and trust than someone you do not know. See the sidebar “A Deal in 20

Minutes” for an example.



The other component of personal power is negotiating effectively by experience

and  role-playing  prior  to  a  negotiation  meeting.  Specifically,  role-playing  is  like

practicing.  All  true  professionals  practice  their  trade  before  they  need  to  apply

  as much as you can about the other

  negotiator before the negotiation

  sales plan
