Successful Negotiations



527













































































Chapter Highlight 12.6: I’m Surprised to Hear You Say That



Know the Law

Unfortunately,  a  lot  of  salespeople  think  it  is  against  the  law  or  unethical  for  the  customer  to  show  them  the

competitor’s offer. In fact, as discussed earlier, if the customer asks you to drop your price to meet a competitor's

offer you cannot match the offer unless you see the competitor’s offer first.



The purpose of the review is to carry out your good faith effort to confirm that the competitor is offering like-for-

like products and services with the same terms and conditions. The following examples are actual conversations

between a customer and salesperson.



The  first  case  is  from  North  America. The  customer  was  being  strategic  and  used  a  competitive  negotiation

tactic to try and secure a better price from the salesperson.



Customer:



“You are 40% more expensive than your competition!”



Salesperson:



“Wow, I’m surprised to hear you say that. Can you explain where they are cheaper?”



Customer:



“Their prices for the same product and services are 40% less than yours.”



Salesperson:



“I’m surprised to hear you say that. I know the prices for what I’m winning and losing

work.  What I submitted to you should have been a winning proposal. I suggest

you let me review their offer to ensure they understood your requirements, and

you get what you are expecting.”



Customer:



“Nice try, but I cannot do that.”



Salesperson:



“Why is that?”



Customer:



“It is against company policy.”



Salesperson:



“I see. Well, then I cannot unilaterally reduce my price unless I confirm the

competitor’s offer is like-for-like products and services and terms and conditions.”



Customer:



“What, don't you trust me?”



Salesperson:



“Yes I do trust you, but I cannot assign my due diligence to you. I must make a good

faith effort to confirm the competitor’s offer is the same as my offer. This is the

law. If I don’t, I could be guilty of price discrimination and fined, and I don’t want that.”



Customer:



“Sorry, I never heard of this. I cannot show you.”



Salesperson:



“I understand and I hope you understand that I cannot move my price. But what I can

do is if you agree to buy 200 parts rather than 100 parts in one order, then I can give

you an additional 5%. How does that sound?”



The above negotiation concluded the following week. The customer committed to ordering 300 parts over a six-

month period for a 7% additional discount—a win–win agreement.



What makes it easier for salespeople to be confident when a strategic negotiator is trying to get a better price

is your due diligence. By doing your upfront work to know the market rate, your differential value, and the com-

petition  you  and  your  team  can  prepare  an  offer  that  you  know  should  be  in  the  customer’s  zone  of  possible

agreement. Should they suggest they are getting a better offer and they want you to match that price—don't. As

in the conversation above, tell the customer you have done your homework and ask to see the competitor’s offer

to confirm like-for-like.



The example comes from a very senior manager coaching a new salesperson and explaining that, “If the cus-

tomer wants you to drop your price, this is a good thing. It means they want you.”



This second case is from Asia. The salesperson received a telephone call from the evaluator, who was also a

supporter of the salesperson’s company as they had always delivered on their promises during the execution of

the customer’s projects. For the current project, the salesperson’s company was the incumbent. However, due

to government regulations, the project had to be re-tendered every two years. The bid for this tender was submit-

ted two weeks earlier. There were two other companies competing for the contract. During the telephone con-

versation, the customer told the salesperson that he was disappointed that the competition was being evaluated

as much cheaper, and it was looking as if the salesperson would lose the work.



528



CHAPTER 12  Collaborative Negotiations



























Chapter Highlight 12.6: I’m Surprised to Hear You Say That (cont.)



The salesperson asked what the customer meant by cheaper, and he said approximately 30% less than

the salesperson’s bid. The salesperson said he was very surprised because his company was the incum-

bent; his offer was the same as what he submitted, plus an adjustment of 11% for the increase in costs

over the last two years since winning the work. The salesperson asked if he could come to the custom-

er’s office to discuss the situation in person; the customer agreed.



Customer:



“I guess your competition really wants this work.”



Salesperson:



“I am very surprised. You know what their prices were two years ago and our

costs like everything else has increased, not gone down. So I’m surprised to hear

you say that. What I suggest is you let me look at their bid to make sure they

have understood your requirements and you get what you expect.”



Customer:



“I can’t let you take their bid but you can look at it. Do you have time now?”



Salesperson:



“If you buy me a coffee (smile). Can I sit over here at the table?”



Customer:



“Go ahead.”



After 30 minutes, the salesperson asked the customer if he could have the competitor’s last bid from two

years ago. It took some time for the customer to find it, but he did and he gave it to the salesperson.

After a couple of minutes, the salesperson was shaking his head in disbelief and said:



Salesperson:



“I think I know what is happening.”



Customer:



“What?”



Salesperson:



“You see here is this year’s bid, theirs and our bid. The main services are the same, and

they have offered a 30% discount from these prices. But per their pricing note, all out-

of--scope  work  services  priced  per  the  attached  price  catalog.  If  you  compare  their

price catalog prices to our price catalog for the services that were bid, these are almost

the same. Now look at the previous bids: Their price book and ours are almost the same

for the main services and for the out-of-scope work services. But if you look at their

price book for this bid as I said for the main services, we are the same now.”



The salesperson stopped talking and let the customer study the out-of-scope price book prices from the

last bid price book and then again at this year’s bid price book. As he studied it longer, it was obvious he

was becoming very, very upset. What happened, and what is not unusual for this part of the world, was

that suppliers would print individual price books for each contract. In this situation the competitor had

doubled their prices for out-of-scope services. It was obvious they were trying to trick the customer.



The customer then announced to the salesperson that there had been a change to the scope of work and

he sat down at his computer and added in out-of-scope services to his cost worksheets, using the sales-

person’s reference price book and the competitor’s until the competitor became more expensive.  With

that done, the customer said:



Customer:



“Well congratulations, you have won the work!”



Salesperson:



“Thank you. I appreciate your business.”



 Customer:



“Now let me thank you for being a true professional. I look forward to another

two years of working you. I will never consider the competition again.”



The benefits of this approach are numerous:



•  The customer will know they are dealing with a sales professional and the original offer was your best offer.



•



If they are being genuine, they will let you look at the competitor’s offer afterwards so you can decide what

to do depending on what you find. If you find the competitor is cheaper, then you can offer trades or  bundled

trades to make an offer that is in the customer’s zone of possible agreement. If you find there is a misunder-

standing, then you can handle the objection (see the sidebar “Did You Know” in this chapter for an example

of how to handle a misunderstanding).



•



If the customer is being strategic and trying to have you make a concession, they know it won‘t work; if they

want a better deal, they will have to negotiate in good faith.



Successful Negotiations



529



















Chapter Highlight 12.6: I’m Surprised to Hear You Say That (cont.)



The other take-away from these two real cases is that, as a sales professional, one of your most valuable

assets is your reputation. If you have a reputation of taking your customers‘ business seriously and being a

professional who submits your best offer given their evaluation model, scope of work, and terms and condi-

tions, you will be respected.



In the second example, the author was also good friends with the customer, who retired about five years

after the incident described here. He never did business with the competitor after that day, and he made it

a point to tell his peers to be very careful when dealing with that competitor. At the same time, he was a

reference many times for the salesperson. The competitor created an enemy, and the salesperson earned a

mentor.



Your due diligence to build a value-added solution that provides value for money spent will give you the con-

fidence to defend your offer as being your best offer given the customer’s scope and terms and conditions.



Below is a summary for two of the common price negotiation scenarios.



Type 1 or 2 Tender.  You have submitted a price you believe is within the customer’s allowed premium over

the lowest price “L1” for a Type 2 Tender or price you believe is economically justified for a Type 1 Tender.



Customer:



 “You are double the price of the competition!”



You should:



•  Probe to understand the difference.



•  Acknowledge the difference.



•  Ask how much added value the customer gives your solution.



•  Handle as a misunderstanding.



•



•



•



If customer is not satisfied, ask to see the competitor’s offer to ensure like-for-like products and services

and terms and conditions of offer.



If the price difference is real, then begin the collaborative negotiation process.



If  the  customer  will  not  commit  to  the  negotiated  offer  and  wants  you  to  lower  your  price,  review  the

situation with your team to make a final determination if you will lower your price to match the competi-

tive offer.



•  For a real example, see the sidebar in this chapter “Do You Know.”



Type 3 Tender. You have submitted a bid at market rate.



Customer:



“You are 40% more than your competition!”



•  Probe to understand the difference.



•  Acknowledge the difference.



•  State that you know what price you are winning, and losing contracts at, and what you submitted should



have been a winning proposal.



•  Ask to see the competitor‘s offer to ensure like-for-like products and services and terms and conditions



of offer.



•



•



If the price difference is real, then begin the collaborative negotiation process.



If customer will not commit to negotiated offer and wants you to lower your price, review the situation

with your team to make a final determination if you will lower your price to match the competitive offer.



•  For real examples, see the examples in this chapter highlight.



To  emphasize,  in  B2B  bidding  and  negotiations,  if  the  customer  wants  you  to  lower  your  price,  your  due

diligence  is  to  review  the  entire  offer  from  the  competitor. You  cannot  accept  only  seeing  cover  pages  or

summaries or what the customer says is the difference. You must review all their terms and conditions, pric-

ing notes, and exceptions to any customer requirements or contract clauses.



Thank you Mr. Robinson and Mr. Patman.



530



CHAPTER 12  Collaborative Negotiations



u A Deal in 20 Minutes



A supplier submitted a large two year contract for a new busi-

ness with their major customer. The marketing manager had

recently transferred to this location and decided he would go

with his account manager to review their bid with customer’s

vice president, who was the approver in the customer's buying

center.



The marketing manager was very nervous about the thought of

losing this important piece of business; this would not be a good

way to start a new assignment. The account manager reassured

him not to worry. The sales team and the account manager had

submitted a very good offer and they had some trades just in

case. The account manager said this was his insurance policy.



After the preliminaries and introductions at the meeting, the

account manager asked the vice president for feedback on the

team's bid. The vice president said that they were satisfied and

pleased, but disappointed with the costs. The marketing manager at

this point started to worry again. The account manager asked the

customer to explain why he was disappointed. The customer said:



Customer:



We like the new ideas. My team believes a

new approach is what we need to make our

objectives. But in my annual review at head-

quarters, I committed to reducing my costs

by 7%, and your prices are a 15% increase

from the current contract.



The account manager acknowledged the vice president’s concern

and explained the higher prices would result in overall benefits

that would reduce his overall costs. The vice president agreed but

still was not sure, given the current industry conditions, if the

partners would approve increasing the budgets for the supplier’s

products and services. The account manager then explained that

these were new products and services, and this was the first

time these were being offered in this market. As a result, all the

upfront costs of bringing support and maintenance equipment

and the staff were to be recovered over the life of the two-year

contract. He asked if the vice president could commit to a four-

year contract as the supplier’s team had already done the calcula-

tions and could offer the products and services with a 6% cost

increase. The vice president called in one of his accountants to

discuss the offer and agreed. All four people in the meeting

shook hands, and the vice president joined the account manager

and marketing manager for lunch.



Over lunch, it became obvious the account manager and vice

president had known each other for a long time—25 years in fact.

They had started in the industry working for the same company

25 years ago. They were friends, and their families would enjoy

doing many activities together.  The bottom line was they trusted

each other. Each knew in the short 20-minute meeting they were

both there to make sure they had a win–win agreement.



their skills in a real situation. This can be sports,

lawyers,  making  presentations,  etc.  The  same  is

true  for  negotiations.  Before  you  go  into  a  nego-

tiation,   role play with your team. If you do the

role  play  and  the  negotiation  goes  as  you  pre-

dicted,  this  is  perfect,  and  it  demonstrates  you

know  your  customer  and  the  situation  very  well.

If  the  role  play  and  the  real  negotiation  are  sig-

nificantly  different,  then  this  is  also  good  in  the

long  term  because  it  demonstrates  your  under-

standing of either the client or situation (or both)

is  not  very  good.  The  reason  this  is  good  in  the

long term is now you know and you can correct it

before future negotiations.



The  author  also  provides  consulting  services  for

high  stake  sales  calls  and  negotiations.  For  one

assignment with a negotiation team, they wanted

to negotiate a contract extension with a national

oil company with whom they had an existing con-

tract. The existing contract stated explicitly that

the  pricing  was  firm  for  the  contract  term  that

which at the time still had six months remaining.

After completing the preparation as described in

this  chapter,  the  team  had  a  sound  negotiation

strategy and negotiation meeting plan. It was now

time  for  role-playing.  One  of  the  managers  who

knew  the  customer  well  but  was  not  part  of  the

negotiation team played the customer when role-

playing. At the end of the first role play, there was

no deal and, in fact, the person playing the cus-

tomer  said  he  was  almost  ready  to  throw  the

negotiation team out of his make- believe office.

He  said  he  was  frustrated,  and  the  negotiation

team was not listening.



The negotiation team was by the end disorganized

and lost. Each member had an assignment, such

as  lead  negotiator  or  observer.  However,  by  the

end, everyone was talking and no one was listen-

ing or observing. The role play was recorded, and

the  consultant  reviewed  the  recording  with  the

team,  highlighting  strong  points  and  areas  for

improvement. This one session lasted three hours.

The team agreed to return the next day to do the

role play one more time.



The second time was much better, although there

were still some areas for improvement. After the review, the team agreed to do

the role play one more time on the Sunday before the meeting with the customer

on Monday. The role play, recording, and feedback were repeated one more time

and, at the end of this session, there was a handshake and a deal struck. Now it

was time to do it for real.



The real negotiation meeting was perfect. The team was confident in offering the

customer a win–win proposal to extend the contract for another two years with a

significant double-digit price increase. There was a lot of discussion throughout



Successful Negotiations



531





the  meeting,  but  always  the  momentum  was  going  forward,  addressing  the  cus-

tomer’s questions and concerns. The meeting ended after two hours with a hand-

shake and commitment for the next two years of work. The author was present at

the negotiation meeting as an observer for the sales team. Without any doubt in

the author’s opinion, there would not have been an agreement reached without

the three role-playing experiences, which fixed many problems and gave the team

the confidence to win the deal. In fact, the relationship between the supplier and

the customer would have been strained as a result of the failed negotiation.



If you win the role play before going to see the customer, you will do it better the

second time.



In conclusion, there are four important factors that create a successful collabora-

tive negotiation:



•  Preparation in knowing the customer who will be involved in the negotia-

tion and which are their business and personal priorities are relevant to the

negotiation.



•  Preparation in knowing your value versus the customer and the customer’s



BATNA.



•  Preparation in creating trades that are desirable and feasible.



•  Preparation in creating a winning negotiation strategy that delivers a win–



win result for the customer and your company.



This is the end of the chapter and the “Selling in Competitive Markets” section.

The last section of the textbook, “Putting it All Together,” is next.



532



CHAPTER 12  Collaborative Negotiations



             Summary of Chapter Objectives



Once you reach the negotiation meeting, you are nearing the decision point that determines whether you are awarded

the  project  you  have  worked  to  secure.  You  should  be  in  a  strong  position  to  win  the  work  if  you  have  followed  the

opportunities management process. All the upfront investment in the earlier stages of the opportunities management

pays off in the negotiation process. First, your preparation is thorough and complete, putting you in a position of power

to  reach  a  win–win  agreement  with  the  customer.  Second,  the  intelligence  gathered  from  your  earlier  efforts  in  the

opportunities  management  process  helps  you  build  a  solid  negotiation  strategy  consisting  of  high-quality  trades  that

make up the final agreement.



1.  Describe the process of collaborative negotiations.



Collaborative negotiations seek to produce a win–win result for both you and the customer. In a collaborative negotiation,

neither party is forced to make a concession to reach an agreement. The collaborative negotiation is conducted by start-

ing with the initial offer and exchanging trades directed at concerns the customer has and that increase the value of

the  agreement  for  both  you  and  the  customer.  A  collaborative  agreement  is  efficient  as  both  parties  negotiate  in  a

cooperative spirit rather than wasting time with negotiating tactics. Collaboration improves the value of the agreement

for both parties because it is win–win and executable with a minimum amount of effort.



2.  Understand the planning stages and know how to use the negotiation planning tools.



Your negotiation planning process is broken into four stages to facilitate preparation for the negotiation meeting. The

first stage is the situation analysis, which is a comprehensive method for reviewing the key factors involved in nego-

tiation. The key tool used to complete the situation analysis opportunity assessment as a level 1 review, discussed in

Chapter 9 “Strategic Sales Plan.” The next process is the proposal analysis. This stage sets the negotiation objectives—

both strategic as determined in the strategic sales plan process and financial as determined in the pricing strategy using

the Pricing Strategy Worksheet. Next, you determine negotiable and non-negotiable points and then build trades using

a break-even analysis and the Negotiating Worksheet to help you brainstorm possible trades and rank their quality. The

next step in the proposal analysis is building the negotiation strategy using the Negotiating Worksheet, which is part of

the pricing workbook and leverages the work done during the pricing decision processes. After the proposal analysis is

complete, the negotiation strategy is reviewed using a test-and-improve process similar to the level 2 strategic sales plan

review. The next stage is the preparation for the negotiation meeting, where you plan how to present your negotiation

strategy in a negotiation meeting with the goal of reaching a mutually beneficial agreement. The negotiation meeting is

very similar to a sales call, and to help you prepare for the negotiation meeting you can use the negotiation meeting

planner. The last stage in your negotiation planning process is preparing to negotiate with competitive negotiators. Not

all negotiators take a principled approach to negotiations, so you must be prepared to recognize the negotiating tactics

of competitive negotiators in order to deal with them effectively and reach a win–win agreement.



3.  Be able to negotiate an agreement in a negotiation meeting.



Negotiating effectively in a negotiation meeting requires the negotiator to be prepared and armed with a strong negotia-

tion strategy. The negotiation strategy is built with the goal of improving the value of the agreement for both parties. Your

negotiator must be able to deliver the proposal in a compelling and effective manner, which motivates the customer’s

negotiating team to participate in the collaborative negotiation process of reaching a win–win agreement. Key parts of

the negotiation meeting are very similar to those of a sales call. The meeting consists of the opening, value proposition,

review of the initial offer, introduction of the negotiation issues, handling of the non-negotiable issues, and closing.

A skillful negotiator involves the customer’s negotiating team in the collaborative negotiation process so as to build own-

ership of the final agreement. The skillful negotiator can demonstrate how the initial offer addresses many of the cus-

tomer’s key concerns and points out the differential value of the initial offer. When the customer rejects a trade proposal,

the negotiator can gain an understanding of why the customer did not accept and can persuade the customer to offer

alternative trades to reach a satisfactory result on a particular negotiation issue. The skillful negotiator also keeps the

customer’s negotiating team focused on the entire proposal as an offer that addresses all the customer’s issues with more

than one component. Even if there are issues for which the teams cannot find acceptable trades, the value of the entire

proposal is still sufficient to reach an agreement and win the contract for your company. The skillful negotiator is also be

able to present the non-negotiable issues in such a way as to demonstrate how they are important but not critical enough

to prevent reaching an agreement. Once the customer indicates a readiness to close the negotiation and sign an agree-

ment, the negotiation meeting is summarized in the closing and next steps are agreed to.



Successful Negotiations



533





4.  Know how to react to competitive negotiators and their tactics.

Reacting to competitive negotiators and their tactics can be difficult for inexperienced negotiators. Preparation is the

key to dealing with competitive negotiators. You must be able to recognize a competitive negotiator and react accord-

ingly. Competitive negotiators come in two flavors: reactive and strategic. Both prefer to make demands or take positions

regarding  your  initial  offer  in  order  to  gain  concessions  from  you  to  improve  the  offer.  When  competitive  negotiators

make  demands,  you  must  move  them  to  a  more  collaborative  negotiation  style  so  that  you  can  make  trades.  For  the

competitive negotiator who takes a position, you invent options. Of the two types of competitive negotiators (reactive or

strategic), the reactive competitive negotiator typically reacts more collaboratively once you make an effort to under-

stand their demands or positions and then show them how you can work together in a collaborative approach where

both parties win. The strategic competitive negotiator may intentionally use negotiation tactics to force an agreement

by requiring you to make concessions. To deal with strategic competitive negotiators, you must recognize their negotia-

tion tactics, react in such a way that demonstrates you recognize their tactics, and then move to take a more collabora-

tive  approach.  There  are  some  strategic  competitive  negotiators  who  never  negotiate  in  a  collaborative  spirit.  When

dealing with this type of negotiator, you offer only your first trades and make clear the conditions associated with the

trade. In such situations, you usually conclude the meeting without an agreement but may still be awarded the contract,

although strategic competitive negotiators never agree easily to an agreement. You may have to solicit the assistance of

your mentors and supporters to help persuade the customer’s negotiating team to accept your offer.



Applying the Concepts



1.   Make a list of high-quality trades that are specific to your customers. Complete a Negotiating Worksheet for an upcom-

ing opportunity that will be tendered. Establish how large the zone of possible agreement is for you and the customer

to reach an agreement. How much premium does the customer give you and how much differential value do you have?

Is there anything you can do prior to submitting the tender that could enlarge the zone of possible agreement?



2.  In  your  location  today,  how  do  your  negotiating  processes  compare  to  the  negotiation  processes  described  in  the



chapter?



3.  How much time do you spend today preparing for negotiations? Who has the responsibility for negotiating with the



customer?



3.  What percentage of your opportunities are  negotiated?



4.  What percentage of your negotiations are done in a collaborative spirit?



5.  How many negotiation tactics described in the chapter have you seen?



6.  Of the negotiation processes described in this chapter, which ones will bring the biggest improvement to your nego-



tiation results?

Successful Negotiations

SECTION 4

PUTTING IT ALL TOGETHER



The  preface  and  on  the  cover  of  the  textbook  compared  the  many  parts  of  the

professional  sales  approach  to  a  puzzle;  when  all  the  pieces  come  together,  it

makes for a beautiful picture. This section is about bringing all the pieces together

and creating a well-thought-out and strategic plan.



In the last section selling in competitive markets, the focus was winning individual

opportunities by selecting the best strategic sales, pricing, bid and negotiations

strategies to win in a competitive B2B marketplace. In this section, the focus is

on long-term planning for multiple opportunities over several years either in one

account or a territory. This is the final section of the textbook because it builds

on all the concepts presented in all earlier chapters. There are three chapters in

this section:



•  Pipeline Management



•  Relationship Profiling



•  Planning



The “Pipeline Management” chapter discusses how to analyze the salesperson’s

pipeline. The “Relationship Profiling” chapter shows how to determine what level

a relationship is with specific customer groups. The “Planning” chapter shows how

to  create  a  multi-year  account  and  territory  plan  to  position  you  better  to  win

more business. The account and territory plan is also used for briefings and deci-

sions related to long-term sales strategies.



The pipeline management and profiling tools described in the chapters are avail-

able to you from the www.b2bprofessionalsales.com website.



Putting It All Together



541





Pipeline

Management



The Sale’s Balance Sheet



Pipeline management was introduced earlier in Chapter 6 “Sales Activities.” This

chapter will build on those earlier concepts to demonstrate how to use the value

of pipeline management. Pipeline management is a visualization of the salesper-

son’s business. The salesperson’s pipeline consists of the opportunities the sales-

person handles effectively to generate revenue.



A pipeline is a powerful tool that can be used by:



•  The salesperson on a regular basis to prioritize and plan their specific sales



activities.



•  Operations people for resource planning.



•  Sales management for coaching, planning, and reporting.



•  Executives for reporting to company stakeholders and strategy setting.



When each salesperson’s pipelines are added together, the consolidated pipelines

form the company’s pipeline. The company pipeline is very similar to a balance

sheet in that it:



•  Is a snapshot of the business at a specific time.



•  Provides a picture of the finan-

cial  health  of  the  business

in  terms  of  its  ability  to

generate revenue.



•  Shows  if  the  business  is



increasing  or  decreasing  by

comparing  an  earlier  pipeline

with the current pipeline.



•  Shows how effective the marketing and sales strategies are by providing



market share performance.



13



Chapter Objectives



After reading this chapter,

you will:



n  Understand the composi-

tion and how to create a

sales pipeline.



n  Know how to manage



effectively a pipeline and

use it to prioritize your

daily sales activities.



n  Use the sales pipeline to

communicate with others

important dates and activi-

ties to support the man-

agement of your pipeline.



n  Know which reports can



be created from a pipeline

for input to management

for reporting and planning.



Fig. 13.1

The sales pipeline. When the

salesperson manages their assigned

opportunities effectively, then a com-

pany asset revenue is generated.



Pipeline Management



543





The  quality  of  the  company  pipeline  will  have  a  significant  impact  on  the  per-

ceived  current  and  future  value  of  a  company.  The  secret  to  having  a  quality

company pipeline is that each salesperson manages their pipeline effectively.



Pipeline Creation

The sources of pipelines are the opportunities in the company’s CRM database.

Most sales organizations today have a CRM system. What is needed is to take the

CRM  opportunities  and  ensure  that  each  opportunity  can  be  mapped  to  a  sales

stage,  activity,  and  expected  revenue  the  opportunity  will  generate.  It  is  very

important the sales process discussed in Chapter 4 “Opportunities Management”

and Chapter 6 “Sales Activities” is reflected in the pipeline. The salesperson man-

ages  the  opportunities  in  the  CRM.  The  pipeline  creation  does  not  require  any

other  input  from  the  salesperson.  The  pipeline  creation  needs  to  be  automatic

and  available  on  demand.  There  are  two  main  views:  the  pipeline  management

prioritization view and the pipeline revenue forecast summary, as shown in Fig.

13.2. Each view is discussed in detail below. Other valuable performance reports

are also possible and discussed later in this chapter.



CRM Opportunity Database



Capture



Your  company’s  opportunity  database  is  a

subset of your CRM system. In the opportu-

nity  database,  each  opportunity  is  associ-

ated  with  a  customer  account  and  any

activities made in support of the opportu-

nity.  At  a  minimum,  for  pipeline  manage-

ment purposes, the opportunity record must



include:



•  Opportunity creation date. The date at which the



opportunity is entered into the CRM.



•  Sales stage. What is the current sales stage of the opportunity? The sales

stage will change over time as the opportunity progresses through its oppor-

tunity lifecycle as shown in Chapter Highlight 13.1: Opportunity Lifecycle.



•  Award  date.  The  expected  date  the  customer  will  make  the  final  buying



decision.



•  Job start and end dates. These dates are the start and end of the revenues

earned over the life of the opportunity.  In large B2B sales, it is not unusual

for the revenue stream from an opportunity to span from a single invoice to

revenue generated over months to years. When this is the scenario, the start

and  end  dates  of  the  revenue  streams  must  be  known,  and  the  revenues

distributed  over  the  contract  period.  The  distribution  of  revenues  is  an

automatic calculation made when the opportunity’s details are entered into

the CRM. This technique of distributing revenues will make forecasts more

accurate than any other revenue forecast methods.



Once the opportunity has been entered into the CRM, the salesperson responsi-

ble  for  the  opportunity  updates  the  opportunity  sales  stage  as  the  opportunity

progresses from one sales stage to the next sales stage as well as any changes in

revenue projections. There is no single way of presenting this information; how-

ever,  what  will  be  discussed  is  a  proven  successful  method  used  to  manage  a

global salesforce for large B2B opportunities with an average sales cycle of 6 to

12 months.



Fig. 13.2

Pipeline management information is cre-

ated from the company’s CRM opportunity

database. Two of the key reports are the

prioritization view shown on top and the

summary forecast summary report. Other

performance reports can also be generat-

ed from the same data.



544



CHAPTER 13   Pipeline Management



Chapter Highlight 13.1: Opportunity Lifecycle



Each  opportunity  has  a  lifecycle  lasting

from  the  time  it  is  born  when  it  was

discovered while prospecting and until it

closes  after  it  was  lost  to  the  competi-

tion, withdrawn by the customer, or won

and  the  goods  and  services  delivered,

and a review completed. The opportuni-

ty lifecycle is shown in the figure on the

right.  The  golden  triangles  represent

each of the sales activities, and the col-

ored  boxes  represent  one  of  the  sales

stages  introduced  in  Chapter  6  “Sales

Activities.” A pipeline consists of oppor-

tunities  in  one  of  the  sales  stages

described below:



•  1-Leads.  Potential  opportunities  that

may  or  may  not  happen.  Normally

these  are  added  to  the  pipeline  as

the  result  of  successful  prospecting

activities.  For  example,  a  customer

expresses  interest  in  a  product  or

service  at  a  trade  show  and  would

like  to  discuss  an  application  they

have for your product.



•  2-Confirmed  lead. The  customer  will

buy  the  products  or  services  your

company and your competitors sell.



•  3-Sales  opportunity.  An  opportunity

the  salesperson  believes  they  can

win and will submit a proposal.



•  3-Disengage.  An  opportunity  the

salesperson  will  not  pursue  and  the

competition will win.



•  4-Bid/Proposal.  The  bid  or  proposal

has been submitted and the proposal

will  pass  through  each  of  the  cus-

tomer’s procurement processes.



•  5-Won.  Your  bid  proposal  was

accepted,  and  you  have  signed  the

contract.



•  5-Lost. Your competition has signed a contract.



•  5-Withdrawn. The customer has decided not to make a purchase. The customer can make this decision at any



stage of the opportunity lifecycle.



The time required for an opportunity to progress from sales stage 1- Lead to sales stage 5-Won, Lost, or Withdrawn

is described as the opportunity lifecycle or sales cycle. The sales cycle can last from days to years, depending on

the customer’s buying cycle. It is suggested that in today’s B2B marketplace the sales cycle is more effective when

aligned to the customer’s buying cycle.1  The sales organization should tailor their opportunity management process

and sales activities to align with their customer’s buying cycle. Chapter 4 described the opportunity management

process designed to align with the Chevron project management process. This sales process is widely used in the

oilfield  services  industry,  where  the  majority  of  B2B  purchases  are  made  following  a  well-defined  procurement

process. The reason for having an opportunity management process tailored to fit the industry and the customer’s

buying cycles is that an average or model cycle time can be established. For example, in the oilfield services indus-

try, the average sales cycle was:



Sale’s Balance Sheet



545





Chapter Highlight 13.1: Opportunity Lifecycle (cont.)



•  1-Lead to 2-Confirmed Lead. One month.



•  2-Confirmed Lead to 3-Sales Opportunity or 3-Disengage. One month and a minimum of six months before bid



submission.



•  3-Sales Opportunity to 4-Bid Proposal. Set by customer’s bid due date.



•  4-Bid  Submission  to  5-Won,  5-Lost,  or  5-Withdrawn.  Set  by  customer’s  award  date.  Average  time  taken  by



customer to make the award decision is one month.



•  5-Won, 5-Lost, 5-Withdrawn to follow-up with customer. Two weeks.



•  Average contract period. One year.



With the average times established, direct comparisons can be made for a single opportunity, the salesperson’s

average sales cycle, or the sales manager’s consolidated sales team’s pipeline to identify if the opportunity sales

cycle is:



•  Accelerated. Shorter sales cycle than the average, which will generate revenue quicker.



•  Lagging. Longer sales cycle than average-generating revenue slower.



•  Stalled. Opportunities that have not changed sales stage for what is considered an excessive amount of time



in the pipeline. Not generating revenue.



Depending on the flexibility of the customer’s buying cycle, the salesperson may be able to accelerate or shorten

the sales cycle. If this is the case, opportunities will move faster from lead to closed won, resulting in more revenue

from the salesperson’s pipeline. If the salesperson cannot accelerate the sales cycle because the customer’s buy-

ing cycle will not allow that, then another way to improve the pipeline efficiency is making more effective contacts

throughout the sales cycle. If a salesperson can move an opportunity from lead to closed won by making fewer

customer contacts, the available time can be used to manage additional opportunities and generate more revenue

from the salesperson’s pipeline.



The follow-up activity after sales stage 5 is very important and too often not done. As shown below, no matter

what sales stage 5 is (won, lost, or withdrawn) following up after the customer’s buying decision can result in new

opportunities.



5-Won:  New  business

awarded  to  you  under

the same contract.



3-Sales  Opportunity:

New  business    you

have to compete for to

win.



2-Confirmed  Lead:

New business you will

need to make addition-

al  customer  contacts

to determine if you will

compete or disengage.



1-Lead Potential: New

business for which you

need to qualify.



If there are no opportu-

nities  resulting  from



this opportunity, then the salesperson will need to replace this opportunity by prospecting for more leads.



From the opportunity lifecycle it is possible to calculate how many opportunities of each sales stage an ideal pipe-

line must have to achieve a specific revenue objective.2  The ideal pipeline will be based on the averages of deal

size, days per sales stage, and conversion rates from each stage to the next stage. Examples of an ideal pipeline

will be discussed in the coaching section of this chapter.



546



CHAPTER 13   Pipeline Management



Fig. 13.3

Pipeline management

prioritization view.



Fig. 13.4

Pipeline management

prioritization view of single

opportunity components.



Pipeline Management Prioritization View (PMPV)



The purpose of the PMPV is to assist the salesperson in managing their pipeline.

Each opportunity in the salesperson’s pipeline is presented as shown in Fig. 13.3.

The  example  shown  is  a  real  pipeline  for  a  salesperson  assigned  to  one  large

account in one territory that, in this example, is a single country. The pipeline has

opportunities shown from the previous twelve months and for the next two years.

The day at which the PMPV is generated is highlighted by the dashed lined labeled

“Today.”  Each  component  of  the  opportunity  as  presented  is  explained  in  Fig.

13.4. Before reading further, take the time to familiarize yourself with the com-

ponents. The next section will refer to the various components described in Fig.

13.3 and how to use this view to prioritize your sales activities.



The next section references the opportunities in Fig. 13.4. The opportunity refer-

 on the PMPV. For the purpose of this

ence number is shown in the blue bubble



Sale’s Balance Sheet



547





discussion,  the  scenario  is  the  salesperson  wants  to

prioritize his or her activities from “Today” onward.



Priority 1. Opportunity 11 sales stage 4-Bid Proposal.

The customer is expected to make the award decision

within the next week. It is important to be available for

your buying center supporters and mentors in case they

need your assistance to answer questions or clarify your

offer. This opportunity can be in any one of the following

opportunities management procurement sub-stages:



•  Resolution of concerns



•  Negotiations



•  Monitor



It depends on what contacts you have made up to this

date  in  the  opportunities  management  process.  For

recommended actions during these stages, review Chap-

ter  Highlight  4.9:  “Opportunities  Management  Stages:

Description,  Objectives,  and  Issues.”  There  is  a  list  of

recommended  actions  for  each  one  of  the  opportunity

management  stage  as  in  Chapter  9  “Strategic  Sales

Plan” Appendix A. A benefit of the prioritization view is

that,  visually,  opportunity  11  stands  out  because  the

term of the contract is twice that (two years) of all the

other  opportunities.  A  contract  in  most  cases  will  be

very competitive and will take your best sales effort to

secure.



u You’re So Sensitive!



The setting is an industrial complex in a large Southeast Asian

city. Most of the suppliers had operational bases in the com-

plex, but rarely did they see or speak to each other they were

very competitive and did not want to break any antitrust laws.

Immediately behind the complex was a golf driving range

where it was not unusual to see the managers from the com-

panies practicing after work. One evening, one of the manag-

ers from Competitor A saw the Competitor B manager and

asked if he had received the major tender from the customer.

The other manager answered, “Not yet. Hope to see it soon.”

These infrequent meetings happened once and sometimes

twice a week over the next two months. Competitor A’s man-

ager always had the same question for Competitor B’s man-

ager, who always had the same answer, “Not yet. Hope to

see it soon.”



It was now in the third month, and again Competitor A’s man-

ager saw Competitor B’s at the driving range. The same ques-

tion, but this time as Competitor B’s manager was putting

away his clubs and walking away, he answered. “I heard they

did a direct award. There will not be a tender this year.” After

hearing this, Competitor A’s manager said to himself, “Oh no,

this kills any chance of us making our budget.”



What Competitor A’s manager did not know was that, for the

last six months (three months before the tender was to be

sent and the following three months), Competitor B’s sales

team had been working continuously with their supporters

and mentors inside the customer’s buying center. The sales

team was helping their customer build the justification and

presentation to the customer’s executive, recommending a

direct award to Company B. Their efforts were successful,

and the direct award was given.



The difference between losing and winning, in this case, was

not better pricing or advanced technologies. The difference

was one company choosing to engage with the customer

while the other was waiting to engage after receiving the ten-

der—which never happened.



Priority 2. Opportunities 8 and 9 sales stages 5-Won

and 5-Lost. The customer has recently made the award

decision.  The  peculiar  thing  about  opportunity  9  is  it

does not have a thin line. This is because the salesper-

son  did  not  realize  the  customer  was  going  to  make  a

split award. The salesperson only realized this after the

customer  announced  the  award.  When  the  customer

splits the award and the salesperson was not expecting

this outcome, the original opportunity in the CRM database should be copied. The

sales stage set to 5-Lost with the appropriate revenue amount assigned to the lost

opportunity and the 5-Won opportunity revenue reduced. Because the opportunity

create date and award date are the same, there is no line.



The outcome analysis needs to be done as soon as possible to understand why a

split award was made. This is even more urgent if the pricing offered was for a

100% award. As the customer did not make an award, 100% of the new pricing will

need to be clarified with the customer. If this is not done, there could be difficul-

ties when it comes time for the customer to pay the invoices.



Priority 3. Opportunity 4 sales stage 4-Bid Proposal. The priority is similar to

Priority 1, discussed earlier, except this opportunity is still in the early (resolution

of concerns) to middle (negotiations) procurement sub-stages. This is a good time

for an executive-level visit as discussed in Chapter Highlight 9.1: “But It Is Only

One Piece of Paper.”



Priority  4.  Opportunity  2  sales  stage  5-Lost  and  Opportunity  3  sales  stage

3-Confirmed  Lead.  This  is  not  an  uncommon  situation.  B2B  contracts  are  fre-

quently awarded for a specific period—typically for one year when the customer’s

project will continue for multiple years.  In this case, the first phase of the project



548



CHAPTER 13   Pipeline Management



(opportunity 2) was lost to the competition, and the salesperson confirmed there

will be a re-tender. The RFP is expected in approximately a month from today. The

issues for the re-tender (opportunity 3) are that the RFP is expected soon and the

sales team at this time does not know if this is an opportunity they can win. If the

decision had been made to compete, then the sales stage would have been set to

sales stage 3-Sales Opportunity. This decision needs to be made soon, and visits

with the people in the customer’s buying center must be made to ensure that the

customer is going to re-tender.



Often customer’s in B2B procurements will have the option to extend a current

contract. The best way to ensure that the contract is not extended is to visit the

customer and express your desire to win their business. One of the worst strate-

gies in a B2B competitive marketplace is for the salesperson to wait for the RFP

to be sent. Too often when one competitor is waiting for the tender to arrive, the

other  competitor  is  busy  putting  their  company  in  a  strong  position  to  win  the

work.  See  the  sidebar  “Have  You  Seen  the  Tender  Yet?”  The  salesperson  may

decide their company cannot compete for this opportunity. This can be due to high

switching costs for the customer to replace the competitor or because the com-

petitor has provided very good products and services and has significant support

from within the customer’s buying center.  If this is the situation, the competitor’s

supporters will try to extend the contract so as not to have to change their current

supplier. In this situation, the sales stage should be changed to 3-Disengage until

the  after  the  award  has  been  made  to  the  competitor,  at  which  point  the  sales

stage is changed to 5-Lost.



Priority 5. Opportunity 5 and 6 sales stage 5-Won and 5-Won. For this opportu-

nity, the sales team took a flanking sales strategy. They bid a low price for generic

products and services and planned to sell up once the contract was awarded. As it

is  now  the  middle  of  the  project,  there  are  two  priorities  for  this  opportunity  at

this  time.  First,  compare  what  has  happened  to  the  original  plan.  Has  the  sales

team been able to sell up as expected? If so, ensure that the successes are docu-

mented  and  references  requested  as  appropriate.  Handling  successes  was  dis-

cussed in Chapter 6 “Sales Activities” in the follow-up section. If the sell up has

not occurred, then now is the time to create a plan to achieve the sell ups.  The

second priority for this opportunity is to hold a middle-of-the-project review with

the customer as discussed in Chapter Highlight 4.9:  “This is as Good as it Gets?”



This example also illustrates how to enter the opportunity in the CRM when the

sales team has a flanking sales strategy, such as bid low and sell up. When this is

the situation, one opportunity is entered into the CRM database for the value of

the opportunity without the sell up. Once the opportunity is won, the opportunity

is copied. The copied opportunity name has the suffix SU (sell up) added to iden-

tify this opportunity as the sell up opportunity, and the initial revenue value for

the opportunity is set to zero. As the sell ups occur, the value of the sell up oppor-

tunity can be edited to reflect the value of the total sell up achieved over the life

of the opportunity. The benefit of this technique is that, with time and results from

other  opportunities,  the  sales  team  will  have  a  very  good  understanding  of  how

much they can sell up. This information can be used to make more accurate rev-

enue forecasts and is discussed later in this chapter.



Priority 6. Of the remaining opportunities, two are in sales stage 3-Sales Oppor-

tunity (opportunities 10 and 13), one is sales stage 2-Confirmed Lead (opportunity

7), and two are sales stage 1-Lead (opportunities 12 and 14). From these oppor-

tunities,  opportunity  10  is  the  next  priority  as  it  has  the  least  amount  of  time

before the bid is submitted, and it has been decided to try to win this opportunity.

The salesperson must ensure they are making progress on executing their com-

petitive sales strategy for this opportunity.



Sale’s Balance Sheet



549





Priority 7. Opportunity 7 sales stage 2-Confirmed Lead. As discussed in Chapter

4 “Opportunities Management” and Chapter 9 “Strategic Sales Plan,” the salesper-

son or team will need to be ready to complete a level 2 review of their strategic

sales  plan.  The  review  takes  place  after  the  sales  team  has  visited  with  all  the

people  in  the  buying  center  and  selected  their  competitive  sales  strategy.    As

discussed earlier for opportunity 3, the sales team may decide to take a disengage

sales strategy if they believe they cannot win this opportunity.



Priority 8. Opportunity 13 sales stage 3-Sales Opportunity. As per priority 6, the

salesperson  must  ensure  that  they  are  making  progress  on  executing  their  com-

petitive sales strategy for this opportunity and have started to prepare their bid.



Priorities 9 and 10. The two leads could be good candidates for some marketing

or business development activities, such as lunch and learn, to make the custom-

ers aware of some of the options they can consider for possible projects. Opportu-

nity 12 sales stage 1-Lead has only one year before a possible tender compared to

18  months  before  opportunity  14  is  bid.  Therefore,  opportunity  12  should  be  a

higher  priority  to  qualify  to  a  confirmed  lead  or  take  it  out  of  the  pipeline  by

changing the sales stage to 5-Withdrawn. The same action should then be taken

for opportunity 14.



Opportunity Selection



In general, sales teams do not challenge themselves enough to consider seriously

selecting a disengage sales opportunity over an attack sales strategy. Too often, a

salesperson will try to win all their opportunities, which is, of course, impossible

and consumes time and other valuable resources in such a strategic question, the

PMPV is an excellent tool. With the PMPV shown in Fig. 13.4, the salesperson can

identify the best opportunities to win given the value of the opportunity, timing,

and competitive position. Even though the opportunity assessment is a very good

tool  for  qualifying  an  opportunity,  it  does  not  take  into  account  the  timing  and

other factors, such as whether the customer manages the market share of their

preferred suppliers. If the customer controls how they award or split their work,

you must identify which opportunities you want to win and which ones you will



Fig. 13.5

Pipeline management prioritization

view, with opportunities color

coded by competitor assignment.

Green: Company A,

black: Competitor B, and

orange: Competitor C.



550



CHAPTER 13   Pipeline Management



disengage.  But  when  you  disengage,  you  must  do  so  in  a  way  that  gains  a  com-

petitive advantage on the opportunities you want to win, as discussed in Chapter

4 “Opportunities Management.” Additional insights for making this decision can

be provided from the PMPV. This is done by color coding the opportunities in the

PMPV by a competitor who won an opportunity. With this view, a clear strategy

starts to become obvious, as shown in Fig.  13.5, and the sales team can better

identify  which  opportunities  in  the  future  are  the  best  focus  and  use  their

resources to win.



With this view, it now becomes more apparent why opportunity 8 was split. When

considering  which  companies  had  active  contracts,  both  Company  A  and  Com-

petitor  C  had  contracts  while  Competitor  B  had  no  work.  The  customer,  in  this

case,  split  the  contract  for  Company  A  as  Company  A  already  had  one  active

project.  If  in  this  example  the  salesperson  is  convinced  for  whatever  reasons

(historical data, customer’s procurement policy, etc.) that the customer will keep

three companies for their business, then the salesperson for Company A targeted

the green colored opportunities and uses attack sales strategies (defend, frontal,

flanking, fragment) to win the work. The other opportunities the salesperson will

disengage (orange and black colored opportunities). However, they will disengage

in a way to gain a competitive advantage for their targeted win opportunities. The

 identify for which competitor opportunities

three grey colored circles with D’s

the salesperson was planning to take a disengage sales strategy. Specifically, the

salesperson  for  Company  A  executed  a  disengage  strategy  as  described  in  the

sidebar “What! I Thought They Were Going to Bid Low—Damn that Hurts.”



u What! I Thought They Were Going to Bid Low—Damn that Hurts!



A disengage strategy leverages the concepts of game

theory and a phenomenon called the winner's curse. Like

the childhood game Rock Paper Scissors, sometimes

in B2B bidding, salespeople have to guess what the

competition will do. If Company A wants to win

opportunity 13, what they should do to gain a



The next two opportunities after opportunity 13 were identified as two more oppor-

tunities for which to take a disengage strategy. This decision is taken because the

salesperson  anticipates  that  the  customer  will  want  to  award  opportunity  7  to

Competitor  B  and  opportunity  14  to  Company  A.  The  timing  fits  perfectly  as  the

pricing  Company  A  will  use  to  win

Opportunity  13  will  influence  the

competitor’s  pricing  strategies  for

opportunities  7  and  14.  If  the  com-

petitors lose opportunity 13, they will

drop their prices for opportunities 7

and  14  and  Company  A  raisies  their

prices  as  part  of  their  disengage

strategy.  If  the  strategy  works  as

planned, then for the future opportu-

nities  after  opportunities  7  and  14,

Company  A  should  be  in  a  better

competitive  position.  This  is  a  very

good illustration of how professional

salespeople manage the competition.



competitive  advantage  is use opportunties 10 and 11 to condition their com-

petitors. Company A wants the competitors to make the wrong market rate

estimate when the companies bid for opportunity 13. By carefully studying their

options, Company A concluded the customer that will use all three companies

and will manage the suppliers’ contracts. As shown in Fig. 13.5, Company A is

convinced that if they win the two-year opportunity 11, then most likely the cus-

tomer will not award them opportunity 10. Furthermore, since Project 9 will be

ending just before opportunity 10, Competitor B will most likely be the winner.

Competitor C, if awarded opportunity 3, will most likely want to win opportunity

12 due to the better timing.



In this situation, Company A should execute a disengage strategy on opportun-

ities 10 and 12. Company A would make it appear as if they will try to win each

of these opportunities, perhaps by holding several well-advertised lunch and

learns or making high-level presentations. When Competitors B and C learn of

Company A’s sales efforts, game theory suggests Company A will bid low on

these opportunities to win. However, Company A will bid higher than B and C

to ensure they lose and have Company B or C feel the pain of the winner’s

curse, making them believe they bid too low. They will not want to make the

same mistake when bidding for opportunity 13. As a result, the competitors will

raise their prices, but this time Company A will bid aggressively, possibly using

several of the bidding techniques discussed in Chapter 10 “Competitive

Bidding” to secure the project.



This  type  of  strategic  opportunity

selection  is  best  supported  with  the

PMPV.  This  approach  is  superior  to

trying  to  manage  a  pipeline  with

tables  listing  lots  of  data  about  the

opportunities. The table view is what

most  CRM  databases  use  for  data

input  and  list  creation.  It  is  not  the

best  choice  as  a  strategic  manage-

ment tool.  The PMPV visualization is

not a new technique. Project manag-

ers since the 1950’s have been using



Sale’s Balance Sheet



551





u  Gantt and PERT: What is

That?



•  Where activities overlap with



other activities, and by how much



A Gantt chart, commonly used in proj-

ect management, is one of the most

popular and useful ways of showing

activities (tasks or events) displayed

against time. On the left of the chart is

a list of the activities and along the top

is a suitable time scale. Each activity is

represented by a bar; the position and

length of the bar reflects the start

date, duration, and end date of the

activity. This allows you to see the fol-

lowing at a glance:



•  What the various activities are



•  When each activity begins and



ends



•  How long each activity is sched-



uled to last



•  The start and end date of the



whole project



The first Gantt chart was devised in the

mid 1890s by Karol Adamiecki, a Polish

engineer who ran a steelworks in south-

ern Poland and had become interested in

management ideas and techniques.

Some 15 years later, Henry Gantt, an

American engineer and management con-

sultant, devised his own version of the

chart and it was this that became widely

known and popular in western countries.

Consequently, it was Henry Gantt whose

name was to become associated with

charts of this type.3



PERT, or Program Evaluation Review

Technique, was developed by the

United States Navy for the Polaris sub-



marine missile program in the 1950s.

A PERT chart is a graph that represents

all of the tasks necessary to a project’s

completion, and the order in which

they must be completed, along with

the corresponding time requirements.

A PERT chart is a project management

tool that provides a graphical represen-

tation of a project’s timeline.



Certain tasks are dependent on serial

tasks, which must be completed in a cer-

tain sequence. Tasks that are not depen-

dent on the completion of other tasks

are called parallel or concurrent tasks and

can generally be worked on simultane-

ously. PERT charts are preferable to

Gantt charts because they more clearly

identify task dependencies; however, the

PERT chart is often more challenging to

interpret. As such, project managers fre-

quently employ both methodologies.4



Gantt and PERT charts to manage projects. See the sidebar “Gantt and PERT: What

is  That?”  The  PMPV  is  a  sales  Gantt  chart  and  the  color-coded  competitor  award,

and the assignment view is a sales PERT chart. A salesperson who has mastered the

sales skills described in this textbook armed with the PMPV, has made the step from

a salesperson to professional sales. Professional salespeople use the PMPV to stra-

tegically select which opportunities to take an attack sales strategy on and when to

use a disengage sales strategy. By executing the disengage sales strategy correctly,

the salesperson gains a competitive advantage on the opportunities they want to win.



Another benefit of the PMPV is it can be used to create a pipeline plan. The plan

includes specific actions such as relationship management, marketing, and busi-

ness development activities to support the salesperson’s efforts to effectively man-

age  their  pipeline.  The  creation  of  a  pipeline  plan  is  very  similar  to  creating  an

account plan or territory plan, as discussed in Chapter 9 “Strategic Sales Plan.”



Pipeline Revenue Forecast Summary (PRFS)



As  mentioned  earlier,  opportunities  are  like  assets  on  the  balance  sheet;  when

managed effectively, the company generates revenues from the pipeline. Using the

pipeline  revenue  forecast  to  estimate  future  revenues  in  addition  to  using  his-

torical data gives management a much better estimate of what revenues will be

in the future. Better pipeline management leads to better forecasts, and research

by  Aberdeen  Group  shows  a  clear  link  between  forecasting  best  practices  and

better sales performance.5



How to forecast pipeline revenue effectively is a topic of many discussions in the

professional  sales  community.  Most  people  agree  that  using  a  weighted  pipeline

(opportunity revenue X probability to win) to forecast revenue is not a very accurate

technique. One of the reasons why this technique is not very accurate is the sales-

force provides the probabilities to win. The salesforce in most companies will pro-

vide the probability to win based on their intuition only. One study that tested B2B

sales forecasting techniques showed this technique to be the second worst-perform-

ing technique of the eight techniques tested.6 The best technique was to use his-

torical data to weight each of the sales stages. The author, when in a sales opera-

tions  position,  used  historical  data  and  the  weighted  sales  stage  technique  to

forecast accurately future revenues and make better business decisions.



552



CHAPTER 13   Pipeline Management



Historical Data



The historical data needed to accurately forecast from a sales pipeline includes

the following:



•  Market share



•  Conversion of leads to confirmed leads



•  Conversion of confirmed leads to sales opportunity



•  Conversion of sales opportunity to bid proposal



•  Conversion of bid proposal to closed won



•  Percentage of sell up



With this data, forecasted revenue is accurately estimated from the sales pipeline.



Market  share.  Percentage  of  the  total  revenue  in  the  salesperson’s  pipeline  for

opportunities  in  the  sales  stage  5-Won.  Total  revenue  is  sales  stage  5-Lost  plus

5-Won.  The  average  is  the  last  twelve  months’  rolling  average.  The  length  of  the

rolling  average  can  be  varied  to  give  the  most  accurate  results.  Most  B2B  sales

organizations use the last twelve months; however, some also use a quarterly rolling

average while others use six months. The abbreviation for “market share” is MS%.



Conversion  of  leads  to  confirmed  leads.  Earlier  in  this  chapter,  it  was  recom-

mended  to  track  how  many  opportunities  are  determined  at  some  point  in  the

opportunity lifecycle not to be real opportunities and then change the sales stage

to 5-Withdrawn. These are used to calculate the ratio of sales stage 5-Withdrawn

to  sales  stage  2-Confirmed  Lead  for  the  same  period  as  the  market  share  ratio

(twelve months, six monthgs, or quarterly rolling average). This ratio is used to

determine what percentage of the revenue from future leads will be converted to

revenue in confirmed leads. The abbreviation for “conversion of leads to confirmed

leads” is L2CL%.  In many B2B sales organizations, confirmed leads are passed to

the sales organization from the marketing or business development groups. There-

fore, the sales pipeline only has opportunities starting from the sales stage 2-Con-

firmed Lead and later. However, if it is the responsibility of the sales organization

to prospect and generate leads, then the sales pipeline will include the sales stage

1-Lead, and L2CL% ratio will be used as described above.



Conversion  of  confirmed  leads  to  sales  opportunity.  The  ratio  at  which  con-

firmed  leads  are  converted  to  sales  opportunities  for  the  same  period  as  the

market share ratio. The abbreviation for “conversion of confirmed leads to sales

opportunity” is CL2SO%.



Conversion of sales opportunity to bid proposal. The ratio in which sales oppor-

tunities result in bids or proposals being submitted to the customer for the same

period as the market share ratio. The abbreviation for  “conversion of sales oppor-

tunity to bid proposal” is SO2BP%.



Conversion of sales stage bid proposal to closed won. Also referred to as the

win rate and should not be confused with market share as discussed earlier. The

win  rate  is  the  ratio  of  proposals  or  bids  won  sales  stage  5-Won  divided  by  the

number of proposals or bids submitted sales stage 4-Bid Proposal. The abbrevia-

tion for “conversion of bid proposal to closed won” is BP2CW%.



Percentage  of  sell-up.  The  additional  revenue  as  measured  by  the  sales  not

included in the revenue estimate for individual opportunities. The sell up can be

from selling:



•  More than the customer has included in their scope of work.



•  Products or services not included in the scope of work. This revenue can be

from omitted, implied, under-specified and unique product and services.



Sale’s Balance Sheet



553





Fig. 13.6 (top)

Starting point for the Q2 revenue fore-

cast. This is the pipeline showing the job

start and job end over which the revenues

will occur.



Fig. 13.7 (above)

Distribution of revenues. For the reve-

nue forecast, the revenues are dis-

tributed evenly between the job start

and job end dates on a monthly basis.



Fig. 13.8

Q3 forecast revenue for sales

stage 5-Won.



•  Complimentary product or services.



•  Bundling of other product and services your company provides, but not in



the scope of work.



The abbreviation for “sell up” is SU%.



Revenue Forecast Calculation



Using the ratios from above, calculations can then be made to forecast sales revenue.



Step 1: Total the revenue from each sales stage for a specific period (most com-

monly, a month, or three months).



Step 2: Multiply each sales stage with the conversion factor to calculate equiva-

lent 4-Bid Proposal revenue.



Step  3:  Add  together  the  revenues  from  Step  2  with  opportunities  currently  in

sales stages 4-Bid Proposal.



Step 4: Multiply the total revenues from step 3 by the win rate or BP2CW%.



Step  5:  Multiply  the  total  revenue  from  step  4  by  (1  +  SU  %)  to  arrive  at  the

forecasted revenue for the period.



554



CHAPTER 13   Pipeline Management



Fig. 13.9

Q3 forecast revenue for sales stage

5-Lost and market share %.



The figures on this page and the next provide an example of forecast calculation

for Q2 revenues. The calculation is presented in the next four figures. Fig. 13.6

is a pipeline showing the job start and end dates for each opportunity.  The num-

ber  on  each  bar  is  the  total  revenue.  The  color  of  the  bar  represents  the  sales

stage of the opportunity at the time the forecast is being prepared. Fig. 13.7 are

the forecasted revenues.



Fig. 13.8, Column 2 shows the conversion factors to the next sales stage needed

to make the forecasted revenue. For example, 20% of all leads entered into the

pipeline  are  converted  to  confirmed  leads,  and  60%  of  all  confirmed  leads  are

converted  into  sales  opportunities.  The  confirmed  leads  not  converted  to  sales

opportunities are changed to 5-Withdrawn or 3-Disengage. Figure 13.9 shows the

conversion factors used to convert the summed revenues for Q3 to an equivalent

5-Close Won revenue shown in Column 5. The forecasted total revenue is the sum

of the revenues from Column 5. In this example, the forecasted total revenue is

$143,738. Added to the forecast are the sell up revenues, making this salesperson’s

total forecast with sell up $158,100 for Q3.



For the same example, the market share for this salesperson is 31.0%. To calculate

the market share, all that needs to be done is forecast the 5-Lost revenues. Market

share is then calculated:



Market Share =                                  Forecast Revenue 5-Won



                                    Forecast Revenue 5-Won + Forecast Revenue 5-Lost



One of the key requirements of pipeline management is that the sales team should

only have to enter their opportunities into the CRM. After the opportunities are

in  the  CRM,  it  is  the  salesperson’s  responsibility  to  keep  the  sales  stages  and

revenue estimates up to date. With that requirement met, the reports shown in

this chapter are straightforward calculations and should be available on demand

in real-time from the CRM system. Today there are several software providers of

pipeline management tools, such as Pipedrive, SalesClic, and PipelineDeals, all of

which integrate easily into the CRM systems from SalesForce, Microsoft Dynamics,

SAP CRM, and Oracle CRM.



As mentioned earlier, the revenue forecast can be done for any period—monthly,

quarterly, or annually. For annual budgeting and strategy setting the most often

used  forecast  is  one  year  to  three  years.  Figure  13.10  shows  an  example  of  an

annual PRFS. In this example, the opportunities in the sales stage 1-Lead have

not been included in the chart but are in the pipeline and can be seen in the CRM

database and the PMPV discussed earlier. For the purpose of the forecast, some

B2B  sales  teams  only  receive  confirmed  leads  from  their  marketing  team,  who

handles  prospecting  and  capturing  the  leads.  Confirmed  leads  not  converted  to

sales  stage  3-Sales  Opportunities  are  changed  to  sales  stage  3-Sales  Disengage;

once the opportunity has been awarded, the sales stage is then set to 5-Lost.



Sale’s Balance Sheet



555





Fig. 13.10

Annual PRFS.



Sales Stage 5 – Lost



Sales Stage 4 – Bid Proposal



Sales Stage 3 – Sales Opportunity



Sales Stage 2 – Confirmed Lead



Sales Stage 5 – Won



Forecast with Sell Up



In Fig. 13.10, the revenue pipeline forecasts show an increase in revenue from the

current year for Plan Years 1 and 2. There is also a significant portion of the fore-

casted revenue that is already won. The green colored bars indicate revenue com-

ing from the secured opportunities sales stage 5-Won. The chart also shows a sig-

nificant  portion  of  opportunities  already  lost  that  will  generate  revenues  in  the

next two years. The opportunities for which the company is competing are the red

opportunities sales stage 2-Confirmed Lead, blue sales stage 3-Sales Opportunity,

and yellow sales stage 4-Bid Proposal. The yellow bid proposal opportunities show

that the sales team has already submitted bid and proposals to the customer and

the revenue from those bids will occur in Plan Year 1 and 2. The total forecasted

revenue  for  Plan  Years  1  and  2  is  the  green  dashed  line.  All  this  information  is

generated from conversion ratios calculated from historical data. The salespeople

only need to keep their revenues and sales stages for their opportunities up to date

to have an accurate sales forecast. This lets the sales team focus on strategy and

sales activities with their customers. Chapter Highlight 13.2: “That is My Pipe-

line!” is about a student in a sales management seminar.



The  pipeline  revenue  forecast  creation  should  be  flexible  enough  to  allow  the

chart to be available on demand and with sufficient filters to provide the forecast

view as required. For example, it should be available for a single salesperson to

allow  them  to  compare  their  personal  revenue  forecast  against  their  quota.  A

consolidated  view  for  an  entire  sales  team  covers  single  locations  or  an  entire

business unit. Once the initial set-up is completed, the reports should be access-

able  to  the  managers  who  need  this  information  via  corporate  management

dashboards. The information should be available to managers without having to

go into the CRM system.



Sales Management



The  pipeline  is  also  a  very  powerful  tool  for  the  sales  management.  From  the

pipeline, sales management can:



•  Prioritize and make strategic decisions regarding sales strategies.



•  Use the pipeline to communicate with other company departments, such



556



CHAPTER 13   Pipeline Management



Chapter Highlight 13.2: That is My Pipeline!



The author was delivering a five-day sales management seminar to a group of twelve sales managers from a single

company in Paris, France. The topic was pipeline management principles for sales managers, and the sales trainer

showed the three PRFS below. Each PRFS shows for the current year and the next two years. The revenue objec-

tives are identified by the diamonds on each year’s bar chart. In each scenario the sales stage 5-Won is the same

and the objectives are the same.  What is different is the health of the pipeline. The health of the pipeline refers to

the confidence management has that the sales pipeline will achieve the revenue objectives.



The instructor described the first pipe-

line on the left as a healthy pipeline: A

large portion of the revenue is already

secured with sales stage 5-Won con-

tracts. There are also sufficient oppor-

tunities in the other sales stages, and

historical  data  can  be  used  to  deter-

mine  conversion  rates  from  leads  to

sales opportunities, market share, and

average  sell  up  revenue,  indicating

that  the  revenue  forecast  from  the

sales pipeline is healthy.



In contrast, the middle pipeline is not

as  healthy.  The  forecast  shows  for

Plan Years 1 and 2 will not achieve the

revenue objectives. This pipeline does

not  have  the  same  level  of  secured

contracts  sales  stage  5-Won  as  the

first  pipeline. That  is  the  bad  news.

The good news is the sales manager

has time to work with their teams to

take actions to improve the pipeline’s

health.  This  can  be  done  by  more

prospecting  efforts  to  discover  leads

to add to the pipeline. It may be pos-

sible  to  develop  new  business  to

deliver additional revenue. Depending

on the nature of the business and the

future  opportunities,  the  sales  man-

ager  might  try  to  capture  a  larger

share  of  the  future  sales  opportuni-

ties. This is not a great situation to be

in, but it is still possible for the sales

manager to meet the revenue objec-

tives.



Sales Stage 5 – Lost



Sales Stage 4 – Bid Proposal



Sales Stage 3 – Sales Opportunity



Sales Stage 2 – Confirmed Lead



Sales Stage 5 – Won



Forecast with Sell Up

Sales Stage 4 – Bid Proposal

Sales Stage 5 – Lost



Sales Stage 4 – Bid Proposal



Sales Stage 3 – Sales Opportunity
