Forecast with Sell Up



The  instructor  described  the  last

pipeline as sick—at least that is how

the sales manager would feel if they

owned that pipeline. In this case, the

sales organization does not manage

their  pipeline.  They  only  capture

what  they  win.  They  do  not  track

what they lose or what is coming in

the  future.  This  pipeline  does  not

have the same level of secured contracts sales stage 5-Won as the first two pipelines. The instructor said the strategy

for the sales manager who owned this pipeline would be hope—namely, hope that they would be transferred before

the end of next year. With that statement the class laughed, but then one sales manager said, “I hate to admit it, but

that is my pipeline.” The instructor asked him to explain. The sales manager said, “That is what my pipeline looks like.

And what you said about it is true: My team is not doing pipeline management, but you know I’m going to change that

when I return. You have shown me the value of pipeline management, and we are going to do it.”



The  sales  instructor  was  pleased  to  hear  such  a  commitment  from  his  student  and  asked  him  to  send  in  their

pipeline when he got it healthy again. The sales manager did send the instructor two pipeline forecasts: one at the

start of his initiative and another three months after the seminar. Both are shown on the next page.



Sale’s Balance Sheet



557





Chapter Highlight 13.2: That is My Pipeline! (cont.)



The  sales  manager  was  correct:  His  cur-

rent  pipeline  (top  right)  was  not  healthy.

His  team  was  capturing  what  they  were

winning,  losing,  and    bidding.  His  team

was using their CRM system as an admin-

istrative  tool    to  record  what  they  were

bidding on and the results of the bids. This

is not a professional sales process.



Once  he  returned  to  his  location,  the

sales manager got his team together to

explain what pipeline management was

and  the  value  of  having  a  healthy  and

accurate  pipeline.  His  team  also  got

excited  and,  after  three  months,  their

pipeline

looked  much  healthier,  as

shown  in  the  lower  right  figure. There

was  still  much  work  to  be  done  if  the

team was to achieve the revenue objec-

tives,  but  at  least  now  the  team  could

implement a strategy consisting of mar-

keting, business development, key sales

opportunities strategy reviews, and new

product introductions. The good news is

the team did make their objectives and

the  sales  manager  was  promoted  to

take on more sales responsibilities.

Sales Stage 4 – Bid Proposal

as  marketing,  product  development,  operations,  finance,  and  executive

management.



•  Make coaching of the salespeople focused and relevant.



Each of the above points will be discussed in more detail and referenced in Fig. 13.11,

the  sales  management  pyramid  model.  The  sales  management  pyramid  model  is  a

representation  of  the  sales  manager’s  duties  and  responsibilities.  At  the  top  of  the

pyramid are business results that all company employees work together to achieve.

Specifically, the contribution of the sales manager is to create a sales strategy that will

result in generating the revenues necessary to achieve the business results.



Fig. 13.11

Sales management pyramid model.



558



CHAPTER 13   Pipeline Management



To  ensure  that  the  sales  strategy  and  business  results  are  achieved,  the  sales

manager will establish sales objectives shown as one of the cornerstones in the

pyramid.  These objectives are for his team and will include key metrics such as

new product introduction, new business, revenue targets, and market share. The

sales team must execute the required sales activities (the last cornerstone of the

pyramid), as discussed in Chapter 6 “Sales Activities.”



In addition to creating and managing the sales strategy, the sales manager must

also complete his or her sales manager administration responsibilitiess such as

monthly  and  quarterly  reporting,  forecasting  revenues,  and  reviews.  They  must

also participate in sales opportunities assuming a sales role typically for negotia-

tions and executive-level contacting in support of their team’s opportunities. As a

sales manager, they should also be the main resource for sales domain expertise.

Other functions such as finance also have domain experts. For example, when staff

have finance-related questions, they will go to the controller for advice. Much of

what is considered sales domain expertise is discussed in this textbook; however,

the individual sales managers will add to this theory their company-specific sales

standards and local market knowledge.



The  other  significant  role  of  a  sales  manager  is  to  coach  their  sales  team.  The

sales manager is the main coach, but they also rely on others’ participation. The

sales manager identifies a team of qualified coaches for specific sales skills, such

as  technical  staff,  account  managers,  operations  managers,  and  when  required

sales training.



On the outside of the sales management pyramid model are the key components

for each one of the pyramid cornerstones:



•  Pipeline. The company pipeline should be the key input when establishing



business results.



•  Rewards. To motivate the sales team to be committed to achieving the sales

objectives, they receive rewards based on achievements of successful com-

pletion of specific sales activities. The rewards can be bonuses, promotions,

and recognition.



•  Sales Skills. Salespeople will be able to complete all their required sales activi-

ties when they have mastered the needed sales skills for each sales activity.



Sales  managers  bring  all  the  pillars  together  like  a  strong  structure  with  sales

management. When salespeople receive sales management and coaching focused

on  their  pipeline’s  relevant  sales  activities  that  help  them  achieve  their  sales

objectives and rewards, they will fully engage with their managers and teams. For

a much more in-depth discussion of the sales management concepts, read Crack-

ing the Sales Management Code by Jason Jordan and Michelle Vazzana.7



Prioritizing and Strategic Decisions



As already discussed, the PMPV is an excellent tool for salespeople to prioritize

and decide the best sales strategy for the opportunities in their pipeline. For the

sales manager, this is also a tool to ensure that key opportunities are clearly com-

municated, for which there will be a:



•  Qualification assessment



•  Level 2 strategic sales plan review completed



•  Bid strategy review



•  Middle project review



•  End-of-project review

Table 13.1

Approval matrix.



Minimum day days before negotiation meeting. Prepared by lead negotiator and salesperson.



One week after RFP reception. Prepared by bid team. Presented by bid manager.



Six months before bid submission date. Prepared by salesperson.

One week after bid submission or other date, recommended by salesperson. Contact to be made with one of the people who is either

the decision-maker, approver or higher-level executive. If contract is awarded, follow-up contract to be made with the same person in

the middle of the project and at the end of the project.



One week after sales stage changed from confirmed lead to sales proposal. Contact to be made with the person responsible for

evaluation to determine formal decision criteria. All buying center members to be contacted before Level 2 SSP.



For each activity there should be clear guidelines established and communicated

to the sales team to ensure that they are fully aware of the criteria established to

trigger a review with management. Such criteria can include:



•  Sales stage change. When opportunities are moved from one sales stage to

the next, this is what must be done. See the sidebar “All In A Day’s Work.”



•  Revenue. Opportunities with revenues greater than a threshold value are to



be reviewed.



•  Strategic Opportunities. There are several factors that can cause an oppor-

tunity to be considered strategic. As discussed in Chapter 6 “Sales Activi-



560



CHAPTER 13   Pipeline Management











ties,” strategic opportunities need to be identified by the company execu-

tive,  who  identifies  the  strategic  opportunities  as  a  must  win.  As  such,

must- win opportunities are given similar attention as high revenue oppor-

tunities. Some of the more common strategic opportunities are listed below:



•  Key accounts. Opportunities for key or major accounts will be reviewed

regardless of the revenue. The key accounts could be major accounts for

which  the  company  does  not  want  to  lose  market  share  or  possibly  key

accounts for which the company has targeted to establish a relationship.



•  Strategic  markets.  Opportunities  from  specific  markets  in  which  the



company wants to establish or increase their participation.



•  Technology. Opportunities that require specific technology or are can-



didates for introducing new products or services.



•  Competitive response. This can be triggered at any time the sales man-

ager believes they must respond to specific marketplace developments.

Competitive  response  can  occur  for  a  number  of  reasons,  such  as  the

company recently lost several major tenders and must defend its posi-

tion  to  avoid  further  losses  or  there  could  be  an  increase  in  activity,

triggering a shortage of people and equipment, so the company wants to

raise prices.



Pipeline Review Alerts



Whenever  an  opportunity  is  entered  into  the  CRM  database,  the  majority  of  the

criteria can be checked and notices can be sent for the reviews. For example, if

the revenue is greater than $1,000,000, then the president must attend and approve

the bid strategy. In addition to the criteria, who must review and approve the tim-

ing for reviews must also predetermined. For

example,  any  opportunity  requiring  a  level  2

sales  plan  review  must  be  completed  six

months before tender submission. Table 13.1

is depicts a sample approval matrix.



u All in a Day’s Work



Not every part of the professional sales person’s job will filled

with thrills and excitement, but each step is value-added. Assess-

ments, plans, and reviews are part of pipeline management and

the salesperson’s responsibility. When consistently completed, it

becomes easier and better strategic decisions can be made; thus

managing the pipeline effectively will become more efficient. It is

the sales manager’s responsibility to establish the standard pro-

cedures required for the salespeople to manage their pipelines.

Here is a list requirements for when changing opportunity sales

stages:



•  1-Lead to 2-Confirmed Lead. Customer contact and a level 1



opportunity assessment completed and reviewed.



•  2-Confirmed  Lead  to  3-Sales  Opportunity  or  3-Disengage.



Level 2 strategic sales plan review.



•  3-Sales Opportunity to 4-Bid Proposal. Bid strategy review.



•  4-Bid  Proposal  to  5-Won,  Lost,  or  Withdrawn.  Outcome



analysis completed with the customer.



•  5-Won. Contract handover to contract owner and operations



team.



•  5-Won. Middle and project reviews completed with customer.



The time required for an opportunity to move from one stage to

the next is a good indicator of the health of the opportunity and

the skill level of the salesperson. If the opportunity is moving

slower than average or stalled, it suggests a possible problem

with the opportunity and needs to be investigated as part of the

pipeline management daily work.



Communication to Other Company

Departments



As discussed in this chapter, the pipeline is a

visualization of the salesperson’s and compa-

ny’s business. The visualization is based on the

project  management  techniques  originating

from the popular use of Gantt charts used by

project managers. The consolidated PMPV and

the PRFC are fantastic tools for sales manag-

ers  to  use  when  communicating  to  other

departments  in  the  company.  The  consoli-

dated pipeline is an invaluable resource plan-

ning tool to see when there will be peaks and

lows in demand assisting:



•  Operations will be able to better plan the



utilization of people and assets.



•  Production will be able to adjust produc-



tion-optimizing inventory levels.



•  Finance  will  be  able  to  manage  cash



flows and receivables better.



Sale’s Balance Sheet



561





As  just  discussed,  for  prior  key  opportunities,  a  certain  revenue  threshold,  or

issues identified as being strategic, management may also request that a specific

department such as finance, human resources, or legal attend selected reviews.

The  pipeline  review  alerts  can  be  easily  forwarded  to  other  staff  members  or  a

summary can be prepared on a monthly or quarterly basis summarizing the Level

1, 2 and 3 reviews and posted on the company management information portal.



When executives from headquarters are visiting the regions, the PMPV and reve-

nue forecast chart are effective ways to present a high level view of the region’s

performance  and  activity.  These  can  be  used  to  highlight  major  sales  successes

and  challenges.  The  summary  list  of  the  pipeline  review  alerts  can  be  used  to

adjust the timing of visits to facilitate reviews and high level customer contacts.



Pipeline management should be one of the first priorities of sales management.

It  provides  them  with  insights  into  their  marketplace  that  allows  prioritization

and when building or expanding a sales organization, the pipeline revenue fore-

cast can be used to justify the investment and expected return on the investment

(ROI).  It  is  common  to  make  such  requests  at  annual  planning  meetings.  Too

often, salespeople and sales management will claim they need more resources but

do not commit to the revenue the investment will bring. Armed with the PMPV,

pipeline revenue forecast, and a credible track record, the sales organization will

be able to optimize their sales efforts and results.



Coaching



As discussed at the start of this section, coaching is one of the key activities for a

sales  manager.  All  coaching  must  be  focused  on  developing  their  salesperson’s

skills to manage their business. The most significant input for the tailored coach-

ing activities are the salesperson’s pipeline review and forecast. The sales-

person’s pipeline review, forecast, and coaching activities should all be



connected, as shown in Fig. 13.12.



Pipeline Review



The  pipeline  review  is  a  regularly  scheduled  event  that  takes  place

between  the  salesperson  and  their  sales  manager.  The  frequency

depends on a number of factors; the minimum frequency should be once

a month and even bimonthly for new salespeople. The pipeline review is a struc-

tured  conversation  between  the  sales  manager  and  salesperson.  Some  sales

managers will hold pipeline review and forecast meetings at the same time. This

is an efficient way to accomplish both meetings, but is not effective and not rec-

ommended.  The  forecast  review  meeting  will  be  discussed  later  in  this  section.

The purpose of the pipeline review meeting is for the salesperson to:



•  Update action items from the last pipeline review meeting.



•  Discuss their prioritized pipeline sales activities for the next 30 days.



•  Schedule reviews.



•  Identify the support needed for their pipeline, which can be from the sales



manager or other staff in departments outside of sales.



•  Review their pipeline scorecard.



•  Propose coaching events.



The centerpiece for the discussion is the PMPV chart. Each of the topics for dis-

cussion in the pipeline review meeting will be discussed next.



Fig. 13.12

Pipeline management

coaching process.



562



CHAPTER 13   Pipeline Management



Chapter Highlight 13.3: It is How You Spend Your Time



Below are the results of a time study analysis of B2B salespeople. The study was part of a group project for an

MBA program completed by the author. The title of the study was “From Sales Rookie to Professional.”8 The study

was  conducted  by  the  study  group  team  members,  who  followed  their  assigned  salespeople  for  one  week  to

determine how they spent their time. For each observed activity, the amount of time, sales stage of the opportu-

nity, and type of activity were recorded. The activity was classified as either a marketing or sales activity. A market-

ing  activity  was  defined  as  a  meeting  with  an  objective  classified  as  building  awareness,  liking,  and  knowledge

during  the  prospecting  and  qualification  stages.  Sales  activities  were  classified  as  meetings  that  were  building

customer preference, conviction, closing (purchase), or liking during the sales meetings, presenting proposals, and

following up on sales stages of an opportunity. If the salesperson had an objective to build awareness and knowl-

edge  in  the  sales  meetings  or  later  stages.  it  was  classified  as  a  marketing  activity. The  average  deal  size  was

$55,000, and the average sales cycle was 63 days. The industries were from the energy, heavy construction, and

manufacturing fields. All the people were full-time salespeople.  The average amount of time spent selling (all activ-

ity that was identified as related to selling) was 4.3 hours per day.



In the top chart is the time distribution of

the  group  as  identified  as  below-average

performers—salespeople  missing  quota

by  more  than  15%.  It  shows  this  group

spent nearly 50% of their time doing mar-

keting activities, 25% doing sales activities

during  the  qualifying  and  sales  meeting

stages,  and  the  remaining  25%  of  their

time selling during the submission of pro-

posals stage. Very little time was spent in

the follow-up stage.



In the lower chart is the distribution of the

group identified as above-average perform-

ers.  This  group  was  meeting  or  beating

their  quotas.  They  were  only  spending

25%  of  their  time  doing  marketing  activi-

ties,  50%  selling  during  sales  meetings

and  submission  of  proposals,  and  25%

during the follow-up stage, as measured by

performance against quota.



What  was  discovered  was  that  the  sales-

people in the top chart were having many

one-on-one  marketing  meetings  with  po-

tential  customers.  They  would  visit  with

one  customer  at  a  time  to  try  to  build

awareness  and  a  positive  opinion  of  their

company.  Making  these  marketing  calls

took a lot of the salesperson’s time.



In contrast, the above-average performers

allowed  their  marketing  department  to

identify  leads;  they  spent  25%  of  their

qualifying leads, 50% doing sales activities

during  the  sales  meetings,  and  25%  of

their  time  in  the  follow-up  stages,  which

the  below-average  performers  did  not  do.

It was during the follow-up stages that the

above-average  performers  built  relation-

ships  with  their  customers  by  ensuring

that their company was delivering on the promises made during the pursuit stages of the opportunity. Also impor-

tant was that when they captured the success stories and asked for references and leads. All of this added to their

resources to sell again in the future. Earlier in this chapter, in Chapter Highlight 13.1: “Opportunity Lifecycle,” the

follow-up activities were emphasized as being a very important a source for new opportunities.



There were three conclusions from the study—nothing new, but certainly made a terrific learning activity for the

study group. It was concluded:



1.



Telling (marketing) is not selling. The below-average performers were not lazy. They spent a lot of time in front



Sale’s Balance Sheet



563





Chapter Highlight 13.3: It is How You Spend Your Time (cont.)



of customers, coming in early and going home late, just like the above-average performers. The difference was

how they spent their time. For the below-average performers, they fell into the trap or their comfort zone. Most

the salespeople in this study came from technical backgrounds and were proud of their company and offerings.

They were comfortable talking to their customers about their products and services rather than having a quali-

fication or sales discussion with the customer. As such, very few opportunities were dropped from their pipe-

line. They did not have the time to create high quality proposals, instead submitting proposals that were only

price quotes and did not differentiate their offer from the competition.



2.  Sales managers cannot do their job completely from their office. The sales manager must go with the salesper-

son to see what the salesperson is doing during the sales call with the customer. This is a requirement for any

coach.  In sports, the coach does not sit in the team locker room while the team is playing. As discussed in

Chapter 7 “Sales Call Skills,” the sales manager should conduct a “Level 2 Sales Call Effectiveness Review” on

a regular basis, targeting different sales activities and specific sales skills. Sales skills will be discussed in more

detail later in this chapter. The sales managers for the salespeople in the study were interviewed and asked

what  they  believed  were  the  contributing  factors  to  the  salespersons’  success  or  lack  of  thereof.  It  became

obvious that the majority of the sales managers had not observed their salespeople in front of customers.



3.  The follow-up stage is very important. Too many B2B salespeople consider the sales cycle to end once the order

or contract has been signed. This is a very sales-related perspective. For the customer, the sales cycle is just

the  beginning. Their  impression  of  your  company  and  you  will  be  formed  by  their  experience  of  using  your

products and services. It is not suggested that the salesperson take responsibility for the operation portion of

the product and service delivery, but they should follow up with the customer as described in Chapter 6 “Sales

Activities.”



Time for the salesperson is one of their most precious commodities. They must use time wisely and effectively in

order  to  achieve  their  objectives  in  the  competitive  B2B  marketplace.  Pipeline  management  is  a  critical  skill  the

professional salesperson must master in order to prioritize their activities to decide how to spend their time.



Updates on Action Items



This is a quick update on the previous pipeline review meeting action items. This

can take the form of highlights and lowlights. This can cover items such as the

results of any sales meetings, prospecting, new opportunities added to the pipe-

line, and sales stage changes.



Prioritized Sales Activities



As  discussed  earlier  in  this  section,  by  using  the  PMPV  chart  the  salesperson

should brief the sales manager on the high priority activities planned for the next

15 or 30 days depending on the frequency of the review meeting.



Scheduled Reviews



Confirm all scheduled reviews for the month, such as level 2 strategic sales plan

reviews, bid strategy reviews.



Support Needed



Any support from the sales manager, such as high level meetings, negotiations, or

other departments such production, operations, finance, marketing, etc., should

be discussed. One of the reasons for discussing this is that the sales manager will

be aware of the support needed for other salespeople and can gain better efficien-

cies  by organizing the support needed for multiple salespeople when possible.



Review of Pipeline Scorecard



The pipeline scorecard includes the key pipeline metrics that should reflect the

health of the salesperson’s pipeline and identify potential coaching activities. The



564



CHAPTER 13   Pipeline Management



pipeline scorecard needs to be appropriate for the tsalesperson’s specific types of

assignment, whether it be inside or outside sales. If outside sales, the salesperson

is  assigned  to  accounts,  territories,  or  a  combination  of  the  two.  The  scorecard

described below is for outside sales with account assignments; many of the factors

would be the same for the other types of assignments and can be used as a start-

ing  point  to  build  the  salesperson’s  pipeline  scorecard.  The  scorecard  factors

should be automatically updated in real-time from the CRM database and include

the factors discussed below.



Key performance areas for twelve month running average and last month’s aver-

age. The key performance areas are:



•  Sales stage count. This will show the composition of the pipeline by sales

stage.  The ideal pipeline can be calculated from historical data. By estab-

lishing a healthy pipeline, it is possible to determine how many opportuni-

ties in specific sales stages are needed to achieve a revenue target, as dis-

cussed earlier in the forecast section of this chapter.



•  Number  of  days  per  stage  and  total  sales  cycle.  This  will  show  the  sales

cycle: Is it accelerating, flat, or slowing? If it is slowing, which sales stages

are causing the sales cycle to increase.



•  Number of sales calls per sales stage as measured by the number of client

visit reports (CVR’s) entered into the CRM. The salesperson’s allocation of

their time is a very important factor for their sales success, as concluded in

one study on sales effectiveness. For more discussion about the study, see

Chapter Highlight 13.3: “It is How You Spend Your Time.”



•  Number of calls per opportunity grouped by sales stage 5-Withdrawn, 5-Won,



and 5-Lost.



•  Average deal size.



•  Average conversion rates from each sales stage.



By having this scorecard when holding the forecast meeting with your sales team,

the salesperson will be prepared to state their strategy.



Major customer successes and failures. This can include reference letters, suc-

cess stories, and major problems as perceived from the customer’s perspective.



Market  share  analysis.  This  is  computed  from  the  CRM  database  showing  the

analysis of the revenue split between competitors for awarded revenue.



Competitor  intelligence.  Any  significant  observations  the  salesperson  has

observed regarding the competition. This can be changes to sales strategies, major

efforts to secure specific accounts or markets segments, and changes to the level

of resources in the salesperson’s markets.



Revenue forecast summary. This is the monthly version of the revenue forecast

for the salesperson’s pipeline versus quota and actual. Figure 13.13 is an example

of  a  revenue  forecast,  showing  that  the  first  two  quarters  the  salesperson  will

make quota, but the last two quarters will need some intervention.



Listing  of  opportunities.  This  a  list  generated  from  the  CRM  system  showing

information for each opportunity. In addition to the standard list of fields, such

as company, opportunity name, revenue, and sales stage, the list should include:



•  Last CVR. When the customer was last contacted.



•  Total CVRs. How many visits made for this opportunity.



•  Bid  due  date.  When  the  bid  is  submitted.  If  not  a  bid,  then  the  expected



date for presenting the proposal.



•  Job start and job end.

Sales Stage 5 – Won



Sales Stage 2 – Confirmed Lead



Sales Stage 3 – Sales Opportunity



Sales Stage 4 – Bid Proposal



Sales Stage 5 – Lost



YTD Forecast



YTD Actual



Quota



Fig. 13.13

Salesperson PRFS. In this example, the

salesperson has made their Q1 quota and

appears to be on track to make the Q2

quota. However, there will be a shortfall

in Q3 and Q4 if the pipeline remains as it

is now. The salesperson can use their his-

torical data to determine their best strate-

gy for increasing prospecting activity,

market share, and the level of sell up per

won opportunity or a combination of these.



•  Level of opportunity (as discussed earlier in this chapter, this triggers what



reviews must be done and who must approve the bid strategy).



•  Number of days in each sales stage.



By  having  this  structured  and  focused  review,  the  salesperson’s  key  issues,  suc-

cesses, problems, and challenges will be highlighted, which will assist in selecting

coaching events that are value added for the salesperson.



Proposed Coaching Events



As  part  of  the  manager–salesperson  relationship  at  the  start  of  each  year  the

salesperson should prepare a training and development plan for the next twelve

months. In this plan they should target sales skills that are most relevant for them

to  achieve  their  objectives.  A  skill  is  a  specific  capability  the  salesperson  is

expected to master to certain level required for the assignments. The PMPV is an

excellent  tool  for  the  salesperson  to  highlight  proposed  coaching  and  strategic

pipeline  activities  with  their  manager.  See  Chapter  Highlight  13.4:  “You  Got

Skills, But Are You Competent?”



Revenue Forecast Meeting



It must be emphasized that the pipeline review meeting is not a revenue forecast

meeting. Too many times sales managers will hold only a revenue forecast meeting

and consider their job done for reviewing the pipeline. The pipeline review meet-

ing is a tool to develop your salespeople’s skills, help them prioritize sales activi-

ties,  and  be  proactive  when  corrective  actions  are  needed  to  ensure  that  the

pipeline will deliver the salesperson’s quota.



The revenue forecast meeting is equally for management as this forecast informa-

tion  is needed to run the company efficiently and plan cash management to run

the  company.    The  revenue  forecast  meeting  is  typically  held  at  the  end  of  the

month to provide management with an estimate of the next month’s revenue and

end-of-quarter forecast so management can update stakeholders of the company



566



CHAPTER 13   Pipeline Management



 Chapter Highlight: 13.4: You Got Skills, But Are You Competent?



What  does  it  take  to  be  successful  in  sales? This  is  a  question  many  salespeople  ask  their  sales  managers.

Fortunately for some, their companyies have invested a significant effort to understand what skills their salesforce

need to master to be successful with their customers in a competitive B2B marketplace. Each industry can require

some unique skills specific for their industry, but most skill sets are composed of the same core skills.



A salesperson can learn core B2B sales skills by reading this textbook, attending training (there are many forms

of training available today: classroom, web based, computer based, etc.), or on-the-job training. But this is only a

part  of  what  it  takes  to  be  successful. You  also  must  be  competent  in  your  ability  to  apply  the  skill  you  have

learned.  Depending on your sales assignment, you may require a different set of sales skills. In this textbook, the

core sales skills discussed are shown in the blue box. Within each skill there can be several elements or sub-skills.

For example, the sales call skill has six elements based on the need-

satisfaction process:



Marketing



Selling Business Value



Account Management



Opportunities Management



Communication



Prospecting



Sales Call



Handling Customer Objections



Strategic Sales Planning



Bidding



Pricing for Bids



Negotiations



Pipeline Management



•  Opening skills



•  Probing skills



•  Supporting skills



•  Closing skills



•  Planning



•  Evaluation



The level of competency a salesperson can have for each of these

skills is based on the application of the skills called a behavior.9

Behaviors are:



•  Observable



•  Measurable for a specific outcome or deliverable



•  Linked to the salesperson’s responsibilities



•  Transferable



•  Based on performance



There can be several competency levels, as follows:



•  None



•  Basic



•  Advanced



•  Expert



Most companies that have a defined sales competency model for the development of their staff will define what

level a sales position will need to have. There can be specific sales training as a requirement in addition to the

behavior. In too many companies, the sales manager may think that, because they have sent someone to sales

training, that person is now competent, which is incorrect. After the training, the sales manager needs to validate

that the salesperson can achieve a behavior using the skills they learned during training.



When approving a training and development plan, the best practice for a sales manager is to have the salesperson

identify a specific opportunity in their pipeline and when for that opportunity will they apply the skills they will

learn at the training session. Then in the monthly pipeline review for any upcoming training, the sales manager

and salesperson agree on what opportunity they will use for their case study materials in the training and which

specific deliverable the sales manager will review at the next coaching session after the training. On the next page

is an example of a pipeline management skill competency grid.



As part of the coaching process, the salesperson should identify the coaching events on their PMPV chart at each

pipeline review meeting. As a guideline, sales managers should target three to five hours of coaching per sales-

person per month; this amount of time is based on a study completed by the Sales Executive Council.10



An example PMPV with coaching opportunities is shown below. The colored circles are the approximate time for

the events. Yellow circles are level 2 sales call effectiveness reviews, green circles are training seminars, and red

are reviews.

pipeline on a continuous basis, keeping

ensuring that information is added to the

nity status. Use prioritization of pipeline

create the 12-month pipeline.

forms.

Flanking (bid low, sell up)



For each event, the salesperson and sales manager would agree on materials to be reviewed before the event

and the objectives for the event. After each event, there is a post coaching feedback session.



When this approach to sales skills coaching is taken, the sales manager will have a salesperson who has the

necessary skills and is competent using the skills.



568



CHAPTER 13   Pipeline Management





on its quarterly performance. Once a year, this is also done as part of the annual

planning process and every three years as part of the longer term strategy plan-

ning. A sales team that is competent with pipeline management is an invaluable

input for management.



The tools discussed in this chapter forecast based on historical conversion rates

of sales stages, average sales cycles, and market share performance will provide

much more accurate forecasts than conventional probability-based forecasts. This

combined  with  monthly  pipeline  review  meetings  following  a  standard  meeting

review  structure  with  each  salesperson  before  the  forecast  meeting  will  ensure

that the forecasts are accurate and achievable.



There  are,  however,  factors  that  can  increase  the  risks  of  forecasts,  as  listed  in

the sidebar “These Can’t Be Good.” 11 Opportunities that fall outside these param-

eters should be flagged and investigated during the pipeline review meeting. As

stated in the sidebar, certain factors should trigger flags that require an opportu-

nity  to  be  investigated  by  the  salesperson.  The  time  to  have  this  discussion  is

during the pipeline review meeting so the salesperson can investigate before the

forecast meeting.



Pick One Thing



Based on experiences from world-class sales managers, the most important factor

for ensuring an accurate revenue forecast is early sales cycle qualification.12 Why?

As  the  old  cliche  goes  “time  is  money,”  and  wasted  time  on  deals  that  will  not



u  These Can’t Be Good



 •  Timing: Time kills all deals.



Look at the opportunity age ver-

sus your average sales cycle

(i.e., win cycle). Also, if the

opportunity is sitting longer in

first two stages than your his-

torical won cycles for those

stages, then they are “stuck

opportunities” and should be

flagged as being at risk.



•  Average Deal Size ($): Review

the expected opportunity size

versus your average won deal

size. If your opportunity is 3x

greater than your average, it

should be treated differently than

your regular opportunities. Larger

deals have lower close rates and

longer sales cycles than smaller

deals, so if the sales rep is not

treating the particular opportunity

differently, then it’s at risk. Also,

if the expected deal size of a

given opportunity dropped by

more than 30% in later stages,

then it may be at risk.



•  Slippage: Opportunities that are

often pushed or slip beyond the

expected close date should be

flagged as being at risk. If a deal’s

close date has been pushed more



than three times, then it should

be flagged as being at risk.



•  Stalled Engagement: Opportu-

nities that are forecasted to

close soon should be actively

engaged by reps. If an opportu-

nity displays little to no rep

activity over the past two

weeks, it has likely stalled,

diminishing the chances that

this deal will close, so it should

be flagged as being at risk.



•  Stage: An opportunity in the

first stage of the sales cycle

does not belong in the sales

forecast, even if the rep marked

the expected close date in the

current selling period. Addition-

ally, make sure that for every

opportunity (at any stage), your

reps clearly know the next

steps the contact will take. Oth-

erwise, the opportunity must be

flagged as being at risk.



•  Negative  Velocity:  If  there  is

negative  velocity  (or  the  deal  is

stalling for a longer period in the

last  few  stages),  then  it  should

also be flagged as a deal at risk.



•  Title: By the time the opportu-

nity reaches the late stages,

your reps should be talking to



a contact who has the title of

your typical buyers. If your men-

tor still has to ask for permission

at this late stage from others

with authority at his company,

then the opportunity is at risk. If

the rep is not already talking to

these other executive sponsors

at this late stage, then this

opportunity must be flagged as

being at risk.



•  Lead Source: Figure out what



your win rates are for each lead

source. If an opportunity comes

from your low-probability

source, it should be flagged as

being at risk.



•  No Competitor: Typically, cus-

tomers will shop around to

compare products and prices.

If, during late stages in the buy-

ing process, your sales rep is

not aware of which competitors

his contact has looked at, then

that opportunity should be

flagged being at risk.



•  Late Random Additions: Look

closely at any large deals (that

are more than 2x the average

deal size) that were randomly

added at the last minute. These

should be flagged as at risk.



Sale’s Balance Sheet



569





close or you can’t win is lost. Better qualification early in the sales process will

eliminate opportunities that cannot be won. Therefore, the salesperson will have

more quality time to spend working the opportunities they can win. That time is

spent  applying  the  professional  sales  approach.  Have  a  high-quality  discussion

with the customers and prepare the best strategies to win more opportunities at

better pricing, then deliver on your promises during the implementation stage of

the  opportunity.  The  key  to  ensuring  better  qualification  is  monthly  pipeline

review meeting. Not meeting the numbers too often is equated with the pipeline

not  being  bid  enough.  In  fact,  the  real  issue  is  better  pipeline  management

through individual opportunities management will result in better revenue gen-

eration from the opportunities already in the pipeline. As it is the customer who

buys  and  the  revenue  comes  from  the  most  important  activity,  having  the  right

conversation  with  the  right  customer  about  the  right  thing  at  the  right  time  is

critical. When the salesperson has a full pipeline, then the question is: “What are

my priorities this month to make my forecast?” Answer the prioritization question

using pipeline management.



Professional salespeople treat their pipeline like a company balance sheet. They

manage it every day so that they can generate the best results. Keep your pipeline

healthy.



This ends the discussion on pipeline management. The next chapter will discuss

another visualization technique for another valuable sales asset in your relation-

ship with your customers. The next chapter focuses on relationship profiling.



570



CHAPTER 13   Pipeline Management



             Summary of Chapter Objectives



Pipeline management done well is a significant differentiator between professional salespeople and sales staff. Pipeline

management provides a visualization of the salesperson’s business, and when all the sales team’s pipelines are added

together, the pipeline becomes the sale’s balance sheet. Forecasted revenues from an accurate pipeline are a significant

advantage for sales management and the company’s executives.



1.  Understand the composition and how to create a sales pipeline.

A pipeline consists of opportunities at specific sales stages. It is the responsibility of the salesperson to enter opportuni-

ties into the CRM and then keep the sales stages up to date as the opportunity goes through the opportunity life cycle.

The pipeline is a visualization of the salesperson’s business. To create this view, the minimum data required is the cre-

ate date, bid due date, close date, and job start and job end dates. What is the expected revenue and what sales stage

is the opportunity in?



2.  Know how to manage a pipeline effectively and use it to prioritize your daily sales activities.

Pipeline management is a daily activity of the professional salesperson. It is the responsibility of the salesperson to keep

the estimated revenues, sales stage, and dates accurate. It is then possible for the salesperson to review their pipeline

and prioritize their sales activities following the opportunity management process. One of the keys to pipeline manage-

ment success is opportunity qualification early in the sales cycle. Early qualification identifies which opportunities are

confirmed, and then the salesperson has time to have discussions with the buying center members and decide if the

sales team can secure the opportunity. If the sales team is weak in any of the four opportunity assessment questions:

(Is there an opportunity? Can we compete? Can we win? Is it worth winning?), then the best sales strategy may be to

disengage and use the opportunity to manage the competition and gain a competitive advantage on other opportunities.

Strategic decisions regarding opportunity selection are risky when made without an accurate pipeline.



3.  Use the sales pipeline to communicate to others important dates and activities to support the management

of your pipeline.

The monthly pipeline review is a very important meeting for the salesperson and sales manager. The meeting is a focused

and comprehensive review of the salesperson’s sales responsibilities, progress, forecast, and development. The pipeline

review provides the sales manager the time to compare the salesperson’s performance against target’s and skill develop-

ment. The pipeline management prioritization view (PMPV) is a modified version of a Gantt chart a well known and

popular tool for project managers. The PMPV is perfect for visualizing pipeline strategies, key opportunity dates, and

revenue periods as well as highlighting key review dates’ support activities.



4.  Know which reports can be created from a pipeline for input to management for reporting and planning.

The PMPV is a great tool for management to see where the business and revenues were generated in the past and where

the business and revenues will come from in the future. The pipeline scorecard is a report used determine the health

of the pipeline and plan what corrective actions are needed to improve the pipeline before problems such as missed

forecasts occur. Changes in the customer base, market segments, and demand for products and services can be deter-

mined from the PMPV.  The pipeline revenue forecast chart (PRFC) is frequently used by management to prepare their

updates to company stakeholders. The PRFC used in conjunction with other conventional forecasting techniques and

intuition will provide the most thorough approach to revenue forecasting.



Sale’s Balance Sheet



571

in Chapter Highlight 13.4: You Got Skills, But Are You Competent? to help you select the skills most relevant to manag-

ing your pipeline.



2. On the same printout of your pipeline identify the key reviews for the next twelve months.



3.  For your pipeline calculate the key pipeline metrics listed below and discuss with your sales manager how each of

these factors could be improved.



4.  For your pipeline calculate your forecast using the technique described in this chapter to create the PRFS chart.



  •  How does this forecast compare to the forecast you make using your current techniques?

Relationship

Profiling



What Matters Most…



What matters most is not who you know, but how you are known to them. Rela-

tionship profiling is a high-level snapshot of how groups of customers, such as

an entire account or a department within a company, perceive the relationship

with  your  company.  The  relationship  profile  also  lists  selected  performance

indicators. As the heading states above, salespeople must understand how your

company  is  perceived  by  your  customers.1  In  Chapter  13  “Pipeline  Manage-

ment,” the pipeline was described as a visualization of the salesperson’s respon-

sibilities  and  a  balance  sheet  showing  the  opportunities  as  assets  capable  of

generating revenue. The relationship profile is very similar. It is a relationship

balance sheet at a given point in time. When the profile is weak like a balance

sheet, the salesperson or sales team can take actions to change the customer’s

perception  of  the  supplier’s  company  and  improve  the  relationship  over  time.

The relationship profile is updated periodically to determine if the relationship

is improving with time.



Relationship Profile

A relationship profile has two parts: the relationship profile parameters and your

company’s performance indicators for the customer. Each part and its measure-

ment areas are listed below.



Relationship Profile Parameters:



•



•



•



•



 Customer’s perception of your company’s value provided to them.



 Pricing sensitivity of the customer.



 Supplier’s role in the customer’s organization.



 Customer’s technology adoption profile.



14



Chapter Objectives

After reading this chapter,

you will:



n Describe the steps in

relationship profiling.



n Perform a relationship

analysis for your key

accounts, and identify

actions to evolve,

improve and/or maintain

the relationship.



Relationship Profiling



575





•



•



 Customer’s technical abilities to use your company’s services and products.



 Customer’s willingness to form external partnerships and share value.



Supplier’s Performance Indicators:



•



•



•



 Current business and contract models used.



 Level of profitability for your company.



 Your company’s market share.



The next section explains how to assess each relationship factor. A spreadsheet

and workshop poster are available for download from the textbook website, and

the  poster  is  included  in  the  appendix  of  this  chapter.  These  resources  should

assist you and your team when completing a relationship profile.



Relationship Profile Parameters



Perception of Your Company’s Value



Chapter 1 discussed the marketing strategies companies use to attain goals and

objectives. Based on the work of M. Porter, a company model can have one or more

of four strategies based on strategic target and advantage: broad cost, broad dif-

ferentiation, focus differentiation, and focus cost, as shown in Fig. 14.1. Market-

ing strategy can also include the high-level value proposition the customer associ-

ates with the company.



Strategic

Target



Strategic Advantage



Broad Cost



Focus Cost



Broad

Differentiation



Focus

Differentiation



Fig. 14.1

Generic marketing strategies.



We can use a consumer example to illustrate. When the average consumer thinks

of watches, different images come to mind. Typical perceptions are based on what

the consumer expects from a watch, so one consumer may think of a Rolex watch

in terms of value and prestige; another consumer may think of a Rolex watch as

expensive. Rolex probably wants its customers to connect its watches with value

and  prestige—not  with  cost.  So  Rolex  would  have  some  work  to  do  with  those

customers who think of its watches as expensive.



The same is true for your company. The customer’s perceptions of your company

are important for managing the relationship. You need to know why the customer

purchases your products or services over your competitors’s and how the customer

perceives  the  value  you  deliver.  Using  Porter’s  model,  select  one  of  the  market

strategies to describe how the customer perceives your company2:

Broad differentiation. Total service provider with a global reach and having the

resources  needed  to  effectively  satisfy  multiple  needs  and  requirements.  The

customer  buys  from  your  company  because  your  broad  differentiation  provides

them value.



Focused differentiation. Best provider. The customer buys from you because they

see your company as the best for a specific product or service.

Importance of Product and Service Differences



Focused cost. Low-cost provider of product or services the customer needs. The

customer buys from you because they believe you provide what they need cheaper

than anyone else.



Pricing Product Service Sensitivity



Buyer  behavior  was  discussed  earlier  in  Chapter  1  “Understanding  the  Role  of

Marketing and Sales.” Buyer behavior can be classified into four buyer types, as

shown in Fig. 14.2.3 In the upper left-hand corner are buyers who seek to buy at

the lowest price consistent with some minimum level of acceptable quality that

several suppliers can meet. These price buyers do not make feature benefit trade-

offs and cannot be convinced to pay more for the unique added value of superior

features, service, or supplier  reputation. Price buyers’ perception of value is the

lowest price, without hassles, and consistent products. These buyers know exactly

what they are buying and do not want any surprises. Figure 14.3 shows what each

of the three main buyer groups perceives as value.4



In  the  lower  right-hand  corner  of  Fig.  14.2  are  the  relationship  buyers,  who

already have a strong preference for one company, based on its unique reputation,

its unique features, or past experience with that company. If the prices of purchas-

ing from that company do not exceed what they are willing to pay, these buyers

will  purchase  from  that  company  even  if  there  are  cheaper  alternatives.  Their

perception of value is to buy the best total solution for their needs. They want to

deal with experts so they can focus on other issues. Although they know they are

paying a premium, they are nonetheless convinced it is worth it.



• Great prices and quality

• Their products work

Buyers



• No-hassle firm

• Consistent



• Good value for the money

Buyers

Relationship

Buyers



• Premium price but worth it

• Best total solution

• Experts

• Exactly what we need



What Matters Most…



577



Fig. 14.2

Buyer price, product, and service

sensitivity  classification.



Fig. 14.3

What the three main buyer seg-

ments say about value.











Although these two segments are frequently considered the two ends of a price–

quality  continuum,  Fig.  14.2  clearly  reveals  two  other  segments  that  do  not  fit

within that linear view of buyers.



Many customers are price sensitive when they make large expenditures or have

limited budgets, yet they are also sensitive to differences offered by various sup-

pliers. These value buyers, shown in the upper right-hand corner of Fig. 14.2, may

buy  a  relatively  high-priced  offering  but  will  do  so  only  after  carefully  checking

the prices and features of the alternatives and concluding that the added value is

worth the added cost.



Their opposites, represented in the lower left-hand corner, are convenience buy-

ers.  These  buyers  are  not  particularly  concerned  about  the  differences  among

brands. Any brand will do, but convenience buyers are also not particularly con-

cerned  about  cost.  Consequently,  they  buy  whatever  is  most  readily  available,

minimizing the search and evaluation of prices and features.5



The most common types of buyer behavior for most new B2B purchases are  price,

value, and relationship buyers. Convenience buyer behavior can be important in

B2B repurchases or when a customer has several contracts in place with different

suppliers and can buy from any supplier by simply placing an order to any of the

approved suppliers.



Buyer Migration



Conventional  economic  theory  suggests  that,  over  time,  natural  market  forces

create a migration of buyer behavior from loyalty and value to price. This happens

when products and services become more similar over time, differences between

suppliers become less obvious, and the deciding purchasing factor becomes price.

Figure 14.4 illustrates this migration.6



This migration is observed as markets mature, even in markets where a company

constantly introduces value-added products and services. In fact, some procure-

ment departments would like you to believe that price is the only discriminating

factor. Why does this happen in an industry that can benefit from more than just

low prices?



Here are three common reasons:



•  Lack of customer focus. This happens when a company’s sales staff fail to

explain the differentiating features and benefits that result in added value

for the customer. Consequently, the customer views all or a selected group

of companies and their offerings as the same. This lack of focus is particu-

578



CHAPTER 14  Relationship Profiling









Fig. 14.5

Levels of focus.



larly true when the customer does not have the time or internal expertise

to quantify the benefits of the added features. As a result, the customer sees

all  products  and  services  as  equal  and  uses  price  as  the  key  purchasing

factor. Figure 14.5 shows three different levels of focus and the main focal

points  for  each  level.  For  example,  when  sales  staff  talk  to  the  customer

about product features, they show a strong product focus. When they discuss

general benefits, they show an appli cation focus; when they discuss added

value  as  it  pertains  to  the  customer’s  specific  environment,  they  use  cus-

tomer focus. Customer focus, discussed more fully in Chapter 7 “Sales Call

Skills,” is the ideal focus for most sales staff.



•  Service does not justify a price premium over a competitor. See Chapter

Highlight 14.1 “Beware of Customer Satisfaction Surveys.” Many studies

have demonstrated that customer loyalty is created by superior service, not

just good service. When buyers select a supplier, they believe the purchase

includes good service. Only total customer satisfaction gives a supplier an

advantage over competitors. Later chapters discuss the importance of deliv-

ering on the promise during the execution stage of projects, which directly

impacts total customer satisfaction.



•  Poor price management practices. Such practices occur when a company

has variable pricing policies that are not sufficiently justified based on the

level  of  products  or  services  offered,  after-sales  support,  or  size  of  the

customer’s commitment. As a result, the company rewards price buyers by

giving them a better deal than the value or relationship buyers. Once the

value and relationship buyers discover this, they ask for the same prices

and behave like price buyers. This typically happens when companies try

to increase market share by lowering prices.



What Matters Most…



579





Chapter Highlight 14.1: Beware of Customer Satisfaction Survey



The Satisfaction Trap7



Many companies that use satisfaction sur-

veys  to  learn  how  happy  their  customers

are  with  their  products  or  services  often

mislead  themselves.  What  matters  is  not

what  customers  say  about  their  level  of

satisfaction,  but  whether  the  value  they

feel  they’ve  received  will  keep  them  loyal.

As tools for measuring the value a compa-

ny  delivers  to  its  customers,  satisfaction

surveys are imperfect. As tools for predict-

ing whether customers will purchase more

of  the  company’s  products  and  services,

they are grossly imperfect.



Satisfaction surveys have two principal problems. The first is that satisfaction scores have become an end in

themselves at many companies, but scores mean nothing unless the satisfaction they purport to measure

translates into purchases and profit. The second problem is that satisfaction surveys are often poorly con-

ceived and conducted. They measure the wrong activity or the wrong customers; they are easy to manipu-

late; they encourage companies and employees to invest time and money unproductively.



In many organizations, good satisfaction scores are considered a higher goal than profits and have a more

immediate effect on compensation. The automobile industry pioneered the use of satisfaction surveys and

probably spends more money on them than any other industry. When General Motors committed itself to

reversing the inroads of foreign competition by concentrating on customers, it tied a portion of management

bonuses to improvements in satisfaction scores. In the 1980s, satisfaction scores went up, as do most mea-

sures  included  in  management  bonus  calculations,  but  market  share  and  profits  continued  to  go  down.

Nevertheless, most automakers continue to track satisfaction scores with great statistical rigor and use them

in incentive and recognition programs for a wide range of employees. Today, as a result, more than 90% of

industry customers report that they are satisfied or very satisfied. But repurchase rates remain mired in the

30% to 40% range. How can that be? There are several reasons, all having to do with the second problem:

the way satisfaction surveys are designed and carried out.



Whenever rewards are based on satisfaction scores decoupled from repurchase loyalty and profits, the result

is unproductive behavior. Employees naturally seek the easiest ways to improve scores, not necessarily the

most profitable ways. One car dealership offered free auto detailing to any customer who agreed to return a

survey marked “very satisfied” in all categories. The dealer even provided a printed copy of the survey show-

ing how to check it off. At another dealership, a salesman pleaded with a customer to fill the form with favor-

able responses. “I’ll lose my job here if I don’t get high scores,” he said. Auto companies like to advertise

high marks on J. D. Powers and Associates satisfaction surveys, so they’ve also learned how to manipulate

the scores. Calling customers immediately after they’ve bought a car and asking about the experience is one

way to keep scores high, but probably won’t lead to increased loyalty.



A second reason surveys don’t work is that they almost never provide the information that managers need

to pick the investments that will maximize customer value and, in turn, cash flow. Early successes with sat-

isfaction programs are often a matter of picking low-hanging fruit. A car company’s surveys might identify

easily remedied sources of dissatisfaction, such as mechanics wearing dirty uniforms or customers not get-

ting their cars on time. Once a company has made the obvious improvements, however, it’s likely to find that

the next level of satisfaction enhancement requires a real investment. Is it worth $10 million to increase the

average satisfaction score from 85% to 90%? Is it worth $100 million? These questions are basic to deliver-

ing the best value, but satisfaction surveys cannot answer them.



A third drawback is that surveys ignore critical distinctions among customer segments. Compa nies should

pay less attention to what customers say and make a greater effort to track lifetime purchases. What makes

this effort so important is that it forces a company to channel its consumer satisfaction investments toward



580



CHAPTER 14  Relationship Profiling



Chapter Highlight 14.1: Beware of Customer Satisfaction Surveys (cont.)



customers  with  the  highest  potential  value,  whereas  satisfaction  research  conducted  broadly  across  the

entire  customer base—the correct approach statisti cally—will necessarily show the influence of unprofitable

customers. For example, a bank’s branch manager might hear many complaints about long teller lines, but

it’s perfectly possible that the branch’s most profitable customers do most of their business by phone, mail

and/or ATM. Investing in more tellers may inflate satisfaction scores but actually deflate profits by improving

service levels and increasing costs in areas that the best customers don’t care about.



The  Baby  Bells  are  another  example  of  companies  using  satisfaction  surveys  as  they  grope  for  the  right

management tools in an increasingly competitive environment. Most have developed surveys to help focus

their organizations on customer service, but few have built systems to analyze lifetime purchases and profits

from different types of customers. Those that do this analysis find that the top 10% of their customers are

worth 5 to 10 times as much in potential lifetime profits as the bottom 10%. Telephone companies that try

to  manage through increasingly refined customer  satisfaction systems are likely to suffer the auto makers’

fate. While they work at raising broad  satisfaction scores, competitors will lure away their best customers by

delivering outstanding value to precisely these most profitable segments. Diminished cash flows will then

make it more  difficult to deliver good value even to average  customers.



Yet another weakness of satisfaction surveys is that an increasing number of customers are tired of being

surveyed. A Cadillac dealer tells this story: “One of my customers cornered me at a charity board meeting

and told me, ‘I got a call after I picked up my car, asking if I was satisfied with the sales experience. Then I

got a call after the car was serviced, asking if I was satisfied with the service experience. Finally, someone

called to check if I was happy with the ownership experience. So when am I going to get a call asking if I’m

satisfied with the satisfaction survey experience?’” One leading auto company admits that customers can

get as many as six surveys in a year. Imagine how much this costs the company, to say nothing of customers’

wasted time.



Companies serious about measuring the value they deliver to customers do not rely solely on satisfaction

surveys. They recognize that satisfaction is an inherently unstable and temporary mental state and therefore

is tricky to measure. Instead they track repurchase loyalty to determine the true value of their products and

services relative to those of competitors. When customers don’t return for service, or when they buy anoth-

er brand—these are incontestable signs that they are unhappy with the value. In business after business,

60% to 80% of lost customers reported on a survey just prior to defecting that they were satisfied or very

satisfied. Some companies respond by trying to increase the sophistication of their satisfaction measures.

Most automakers have chosen this approach, but they still see 90% of their customers claiming to be satis-

fied and 40% coming back to buy again.



The exception is Lexus, a consistent winner of auto satisfaction awards, which refuses to consider surveys

the best measure of satisfaction. In the words of Dave Illingworth, the first general man ager of Lexus, “The

only meaningful measure of satisfaction is repurchase loyalty.” Illingworth knows that the gap between sat-

isfaction scores and repurchase loyalty can be enormous. Drive by a Lexus dealership and you’ll see a satel-

lite dish on the roof. It keeps the dealer in constant touch with Lexus headquarters and maintains a steady

flow  of  information  in  both  directions  about  customers’  auto  and  service  purchases.  Lexus  knows  which

customers are coming back for more and which are not, and it can analyze the differences between dealers

who are earning superior customer loyalty and those who are not.



In depending so heavily on broad-based satisfaction surveys, companies are letting too many defectors slip

through the cracks. There is a better way. To know how much companies can afford to spend to satisfy

specific customers, they need to measure the return on their investment. The only way to do that is to

study lifetime purchase patterns. But since they have to track purchase patterns to determine customer

profitability, why not simply use this information as their satisfaction index? Customers’ repeat purchase

loyalty  must  become  the  basic  yardstick  of  success.  Companies  can  avoid  the  satisfaction  trap  if  they

remember that what matters is not how satisfied you keep your customers, but how many satisfied and

profitable customers you keep.



What Matters Most…



581





Supplier’s Role



What matters most is not who you know but how you are known to them.8 One way

to determine how a customer perceives your company is to identify how you par-

ticipate with that customer in the five stages of the project management process,

as discussed in Chapter 2 “Value Drivers” and Chapter 9 “Strategic Sales Plan,”

Chapter Highlight 9.2: Just Take My Order, Please. In Fig. 14.6,9 the supplier role

is classified on three levels:



•  Trusted adviser



•  Problem solver



•  Vendor



The black line under each role indicates in which stages in the project life cycle

your company participates. The white curved line indicates the amount of execu-

tive involvement in each stage of the project life cycle. Typically, executive involve-

ment in the project life cycle is high in earlier stages and then gradually decreases

through  each  successive  stage  to  procurement,  when  it  is  lowest.  Executive

involvement increases again once the project starts and is high again when the

results are reviewed. Your company’s contact with customer management during

the project stages is a good indicator of your role in the account.



Trusted Adviser



If the customer considers you a trusted advisor, you are viewed as a stakeholder.

At this level, the customer believes you are interested in optimizing the perfor-

mance of their reservoirs. You have demonstrated your concern that they achieve

their goals and objectives. When viewed as a trusted advisor, you will be involved

in all five stages of the customer’s project life cycle.



Problem Solver



If the customer considers you a problem solver, you are viewed as a specialist.

This perception can apply to specific products or ser vices, or only to a specific

situation, such as your expertise in offshore development. When this is your role,

you are involved in the later part of the project selection stage (2) and in the

evaluation of options stage (3) as well as on through the review of results stage

(5).

582



CHAPTER 14  Relationship Profiling





Vendor



The  account  considers  your  company  as  a  vendor  if  the  only  time  you  become

involved in a project is when you receive a tender to bid during the procurement

process. If you win the contract, you participate in the execution. In this role the

vendor does not have contact at the executive level. With such limited exposure

to the customer’s project life cycle, you cannot have much influence.



Technology Adoption Profile



As  discussed  in  Chapter  1  “Understanding  the  Role  of  Marketing  and  Sales,”

technology adoption is a measurement of the customer’s willingness to adopt new

technologies  as  shown  in  Fig.  14.7.  The  first  group  to  adopt  new  products  is

described  as  innovators.  They  account  for  a  small  portion  of  the  purchasing

 population.  They  buy  new  technology  for  the  sake  of  wanting  to  test  it.  Some

organizations  have  groups  who  are  given  the  responsibility  of  testing  any  new

technology  before  it  becomes  widely  used  in  the  companies’  operations.  These

groups  and  individuals  are  sometimes  called  gatekeepers  in  the  buying  center.

These  groups  are  usually  good  targets  for  field-testing  campaigns  during  the

product development stage and in the market development stage of the product

life cycle.



Fig. 14.7

New technology adoption

life cycle. Pragmatists and  conservatives

represent the largest portion of the

 purchasing population and make up the

majority of the purchases in stage two of

the product life cycle.



s

t

n

e



i

l



C

%



Visionaries



Innovators



Pragmatists



Conservatives



Time to Adapt to Change



Laggards



After the new technology has been proven to work as advertised, the next group

to adopt the technology is visionaries. Visionaries, like innovators, buy into new

product concepts very early in their life cycle, but unlike innovators, they are not

technologists. They are people who find it easy to imagine, understand, and appre-

ciate  the  benefits  of  a  new  technology  and  to  relate  these  potential  benefits  to

their other concerns. Whenever they find a strong match, early adopters are will-

ing  to  base  their  buying  decisions  upon  it.  Because  visionaries  do  not  rely  on

well-established  references  when  making  these  buying  decisions,  preferring

instead to rely on their own intuition and vision, they are key to opening up any

high-tech market segment. Visionaries believe they will gain a competitive advan-

tage by adopting new technology early. They believe they can gain a huge cost or

revenue  benefit  from  the  new  technology.  These  two  groups—innovators  and

visionaries—are  usually  the  ones  who  make  purchases  of  new  products  during

stage one, the market development stage.



The next group to purchase a new product is the pragmatists. Pragmatists share

some  of  the  visionaries’  ability  to  relate  to  technology,  but  ultimately  they  are

driven by a strong sense of practicality. They know that many new products may

not prove viable or practicable, so they are content to wait and see whether other

companies successfully integrate the new technology before buying it. They want

to see well-established references before making substantial investments. Because

this group is the largest—accounting for roughly one-third of the whole adoption



What Matters Most…



583







life cycle—winning their business is key to recovering investment and develop-

ment costs of the new product.



The  next  group  to  purchase  is  the  conservatives.  Conservatives  share  all  the

concerns of the pragmatists, plus one major additional one. Whereas pragmatists

are comfortable with their ability to handle a new technology product once they

finally decide to purchase it, members of the conservatives are not. As a result,

conservatives wait until the product has become an established standard or has

gained  widespread  industry  approval  and  support.  Even  then,  conservatives

require  a  lot  of  proof  and,  therefore,  tend  to  buy  from  large,  well-established

companies. Like the pragmatists, this group comprises one-third of the total buy-

ing population in any given market segment. Securing a large portion of this group

is required if high levels of profitability are to be achieved.



The last groups to adopt new technology is the laggards. Although small in size,

the laggards are almost the same size as the innovators and visionaries percent-

age-wise. Laggards generally purchase new technology to protect investments and

to replace worn-out products.



For  relationship  profiling,  the  choices  are  listed  as  “visionaries,”  “pragmatists,”

and  “conservatives.”  Most    companies  have  departments  of  innovators,  or  gate-

keepers, whose responsibility is to test new technologies and approve their usage.



Technical Ability



Technical ability refers to the account’s internal ability to use your products and

services to their fullest potential—that is, whether the customer has the internal

technical expertise to extract full value from your offerings. Technical ability is

classified as strong, good, or weak. Strong technical ability describes companies

with an advanced level of technical capability. Strong companies sometimes have

specialists whose technical knowledge equals your own experts. Typically, vision-

ary  companies  have  strong  technical  ability.  Good  technical  ability  describes

companies that have internal technical departments to assist end-users in adopt-

ing and applying your company’s technologies in their operations. Weak companies

do not have the resources necessary to stay abreast of available technologies and

need assistance in using the products and services. For example, companies that

have invested in the E&P industry but who do not have the traditional E&P expe-

rience or structure are often classified as weak.



Customer’s Willingness to Form Advanced Business

Relationships



This measurement area is used to identify accounts that are potential targets for

participating in advanced business relationships. From an account management

perspective, this factor helps to identify which accounts would be more receptive

to value-added cross-selling proposals. There are three types of this profiling fac-

tor, as shown in Fig. 14.8 on the next page.10



Standard



A standard account is one that will only consider the basic contractual supplier–

customer relationship. For these accounts, the contractor provides a defined deliv-

erable based on a cost plus contractual framework. In this type of relationship, the

customer’s main objective is to get what he asked for typically through a tendering

process. Business drivers of the account include the quality of the service and cost

reduction. Examples of delivery metrics for this type of the relationship are system

availability  and  cost-reduction  targets.  After  entering  the  procurement  phase,

procurement  and  contract  departments  largely  handle  the  discussions.  The  key

ings for compliance

service

for business need

delivering business value

and/or results

achieve business value

cess
