Improved performance



control  mechanism  used  by  accounts  for  this  type  of  relationship  is  the  signed

contract and service review meetings to ensure that deliverables have been satis-

factorily delivered. For this type of business relationship, there is very minimum

risk for the contractor and very little potential to earn more profit than factored

into their contract for requested services.



Collaborative



A collaborative account is one that has already participated in a more cooperative

business relationship or is actively discussing prospects to test this type of rela-

tionship. The collaborative business relationship is where the contractor engages

the  contractor  to  find  the  best  solution  to  their  business  needs.  The  supplier  is

considered  to  be  very  much  part  of  the  project  team.  Decisions  are  taken  after

options have been openly discussed and evaluated between the business partners.

It is not usual that the deliverable to the customer includes some form of manage-

ment responsibility, typically of products or services provided in level 1.



Remuneration  goes  beyond  the  standard  cost-based  systems  and  includes  the

contractor risking a portion of his remuneration based on benchmarks for levels

of performance and results. If benchmarks are achieved, then contractors receive

bonuses; if they are missed, contractors will be assessed a penalty for not achiev-

ing benchmarks. The drivers for this type of business relationship in addition to

level  1  drivers  are  additional  business  considerations,  such  as  speed  to  market,

access to the contractor’s larger resource base of skills and resources, the need

for commercial flexibility, scalability, and rapid response.



Metrics for this type of relationship in addition to level 1 metrics include improve-

ments in performance, on-time project delivery, and user satisfaction. During the

selection of the supplier, the procurement and legal departments play less of a role

and  the  management  initiating  the  collaborative  approach  will  stay  involved

throughout the selection process. The key control process for this type of business

relationship includes regular operating review meetings for results delivered and

coordination issues.



What Matters Most…



585





Strategic



A strategic account is one that has already participated in a collaborative business

relationship and is discussing projects to use a strategic partnership relationship

or has already participated in a strategic partnership. In a strategic partnership,

the partners are far more dependent on one another for the delivery of business

value. Discussions revolve more around what the strategic partner will be respon-

sible for in terms of processes and business needs, leaving the details of what and

how this is done to the strategic partner.



In a strategic partnership, each partner works to ensure that the other is financially

successful. Remuneration is based on agreed upon business results. Risks can be

higher than the collaborative relationship depending on what the contractor wants

to invest in the relationship to achieve better results for the account; however, the

improved results should result in a significant benefit for the contractor. In this type

of relationship, the service provider typically takes responsibility for completing the

project of the business process. In this type of relationship, it is not usual to have

the contractor’s research and development focus on the customer’s specific needs

or  to  participate  in  joint  research  and  development.  The  client  utilizes  the  full

resources of the partner to maximize the value to their business.



Metrics  for  this  type  of  relationship  are  direct  corporate  value  drivers,  such  as

revenue, profit, and earnings per share. Strategic relationships are driven by busi-

ness  strategy;  as  such,  in  the  selection  of  the  strategic  business  partner,  the

executive of the business units who are dependent on the strategic partner will

be very active throughout the selection processes. In most cases a strategic part-

ner will not be a new provider, but a company that the account has worked with

previously. In these types of strategic relationships, the main control process is a

regular review meeting for value delivered.



Shown  in  Fig.  14.9  are  all  the  relationship  profile  parameters  discussed.  The

interpretation of the results will be discussed later in this chapter. The next sec-

tion will discuss the performance indicators.

Fig. 14.10

 Basic and advanced pricing models.



Performance Indicators



Business and Contractual Model



Your company can offer customers a full range of business and contractual models

for their various projects, and you classify the account according to their preferred

business  model.  For  this  profile  measurement  area,  you  can  use  three  business

model  classifications:  cost  plus  for  basic  models  and  performance  based  or

results based for advanced models. Each advanced model requires successively

higher levels of cooperation, trust, and alignment of operator and supplier objec-

tives. The ultimate goal for the advanced models is greater profitability for both

the customer and your company. Fig. 14.10 lists business and contractual models

commonly used today.11

Cost-plus basis



Basic Models



All basic models are classified as cost plus, and are used for products and services

charged at cost plus a mark-up based on quantities used. Two common variations

of the basic model are volume discounts and turnkeys.



Volume  discounts  recognize  that,  as  volume  efficiencies  are  gained,  part  of  the

cost  saved  is  shared  back  with  the  account  through  volume  discounts.  Service

bundling  offered  without  a  performance-  or  results-based  remuneration  compo-

nent in the contract falls in the basic category under the volume discount scheme.



For turnkey or lump sum contracts, the operator sets specifications and jobs are

bid by well or by project. The selected service company then executes the job on

demand. The incentive is to complete the job as quickly as possible to earn the

greatest  profit.  If  the  supplier  has  problems  completing  the  job  or  project,  the

contractor earns no more than the turnkey price.



Advanced Models



Advanced  models,  in  addition  to  the  product  and  service  fee,  include  a  risk  or

reward component as an incentive portion of the remuneration. The risk or reward

component can be based on the contractor’s performance, project results, or asset

value  creation.  The  main  difference  among  the  various  advanced  models  is  the

level of risk your company is willing to accept and the level of customer willing-

ness to outsource and share value creation.



Performance-based contracts include integrated services and processes. These busi-

ness models span a range of activities, from service execution—performing bundled

services—at the basic end, to product delivery—such as providing a completed well

or offshore platform—at the most sophisticated end. These types of projects usually

include  conceptual  design,  process  planning,  service  execution,  and  evaluation.

R&D



Typically,  results-based  contracts  include  engineered  solutions,  with  performance

incentives  when  established  benchmarks  are  bettered  or  surpassed,  or  discounts

from  the  market’s  going  rate  when  benchmarks  are  missed.  Benchmarks  may

include days to drill a well, daily production rates, and unproductive time.



Value-based contracts include joint value enhancement projects. For these con-

tracts,  the  benchmark  is  a  base  case  value  of  an  asset.  Such  base  case  value

measurements  are  NPV  or  profit  per  barrel.  Your  company’s  exposure  in  these

types of projects is limited, and profits are capped because your company should

not take an equity position in any project.13 Compared to other business models,

the expected profit from a value-based contract is greater for both the customer

and your company. One example of a successful value-based project was for an oil

company and service company in Venezuela.14



The    project  is  described  as  an  alliance,  which  is  a  long-term  relationship

between  two  companies  that  furthers  their  common  interests  over  a  specific

range of activities. Alliances differ from integrated services contracts. Under an

integrated services contract, the client assigns responsibility to the supplier to

reduce the client’s costs. In an alliance, the supplier accepts responsibility for

reducing the client’s cost, and the client accepts responsibility for ensuring the

supplier’s profit, often by assuring future business and sharing gain with the sup-

plier. The two sides work together to reduce costs and improve profitability for

all involved.15 Chapter Highlight 14.2: “Impossible Improvement of Profits for

Both  Companies—It  Doesn’t  Add  Up!”  demonstrates  how  an  alliance  can

improve profitability for both companies.



Integrated  services  and  alliances  are  not  mutually  exclusive.  An  integrated  ser-

vices project may be offered to an alliance partner, or may evolve into an alliance.

In fact, most alliances start out as tests for a certain period of time. If successful,

alliances may become self-renewing, or “evergreen.”16



Alliances can evolve over time from entry to focus to enhance to strategic levels.

Fig.  14.11  lists  the  features  of  each  type  of  alliance.  “Strategic  alliance”  often

describes alliances that are part of the partner companies’ strategies and implies



588



CHAPTER 14  Relationship Profiling



u	Alliances as a Marriage



In French, “alliance” means “mar-

riage.” Indeed, many alliances seem to

resemble marriages more than they

do other business relationships. One

study across 37 different industries

found that relationships between com-

panies grow or fail much like relation-

ships between people. The report

describes four phases of an alliance:

courtship, engagement, marriage, and

old marrieds. During courtship, each

side checks out the other and com-

pares them with others, looking for

the most attractive, compatible part-

ner. Compatibility is based on com-

mon values, principles, experiences,

resources, and hopes for the future.

After the engagement, plans are

drawn and the wedding closes the

deal. The agreement is given a name

and made public. Executives from

both sides are invited to “meet the

family.” The marriage phase begins as

the partners set up housekeeping

together and grow as a family. During

this phase, differences are discov-

ered—sometimes coming as a sur-



Interdependence: The partners need

each other, have complementary skills,

and could not achieve the same

results alone.



Investment: Each side shows tangible

signs of long-term commitment

by devoting financial or other resourc-

es to the relationship.



Information: Partners share data

required to make the alliance work.

These include objectives, technical

data and knowledge of conflict, trou-

ble spots, or changes.



Integration: Partners develop linkages

and shared ways of working together.

They build broad connections among

many people at different levels in the

organization. They become both teach-

ers and students.



Institutionalization: The alliance is

given a  formal status and extends

beyond the people who formed it.



Integrity: The partners behave honor-

ably in ways that justify and enhance

mutual trust. They do not abuse infor-

mation, nor do they undermine each

other.



prise to those who founded the alli-

ance—and the partners must work out

problems and develop techniques for

getting along. Trust is crucial, and indi-

vidual sacrifices are made for the good

of the relationship. As partners enter

the “old marrieds” phase, they can

reflect back and recognize changes not

anticipated at the start of the relation-

ship. Collaborating becomes more

effortless and routine. The study also

outlines the eight essential “I”s in an

alliance that make for a strong “you:”



Individual excellence: Each company is

strong and has something of value to

contribute. Motives for pursuing the rela-

tionships are positive: to pursue future

opportunities, not mask weaknesses.



Importance: The alliance fits with

major strategic objectives.



that the companies share their strategies openly. Few alliances between custom-

ers and suppliers have reached such a level of cooperation and openness, but that

is  the  goal  to  which  many  aspire.17  Selecting  the  right  alliance  partner  takes

careful consideration of all aspects of the relationship (see “Alliances as a Mar-

riage” sidebar18).



Profitability



Profitability is a measure of profit made at the account level. Determining this is

not always easy as account-level profitability is not readily available from financial

reports unless the location has only one or two major accounts. This measurement

includes  both  the  direct  operational  costs  and  revenues  generated  from  the

account plus the servicing costs.



Servicing costs include a portion of the overhead that is dedicated to meeting an

account’s needs. This includes the costs of support personnel, such as dedicated

in-house engineers, and interpretation and sales staff dedicated or shared among

accounts. See Chapter Highlight 14.3 “Dedicated Service Personnel” for a real

example. It also includes any ongoing and special carrying costs such as invoices

open beyond the standard terms and conditions, holding of inventories for future

work, etc. Profitability also depends upon the prevailing market conditions within

the region.



Here is the suggested classification scheme for account profitability:



•  Level 1, the best profitability at the region level, is greater than 20%



•  Level 2, average profitability at the region level, is between 10% and 19%



•  Level 3, below average profitability at the region level, is 10% or less.



What Matters Most…



589





Chapter Highlight 14.2: Impossible Improvement of Profits

for Both Companies—It Doesn’t Add Up!



Cooperative Spirit of Alliance Changes

the Way Problems Are Approached



The  quest  to  cut  costs  means  not  dwelling  on

contractor profit but cutting total project cost (see

diagram at right).19 To uncover where cuts can be

made, every process in the entire project must be

analyzed and examined for inefficiencies. Alliance

partners construct a description, called a process

map, for each process. A process map may be a

list of steps or a flowchart, as shown below. After

the total project is analyzed, individual processes

are retained only if they add value. Improvements

are made to the remaining processes, or entirely

new processes are developed, and the new pro-

cesses  are  remapped,  leading  to  continuous

improvement.20  Decisions  on  how  to  improve  a

process  come  from  the  alliance  partners,  and

team members have the power to make the nec-

essary  changes.  Where  does  an  alliance  begin

cutting  costs?  An  economics  professor  would

advise to cut first where there are the easiest and

biggest gains.

In today’s development-oriented oilfield, pumping services can often account for the majority of the cost of a

well.  Pumping  services  have  become  the  early  targets  of  companies  trying  to  increase  efficiency.  Process

mapping  can  show  where  redundant  efforts  are  undermining  efficiency.  For  example,  before  one  alliance,

stimulation engineers from both sides spent time designing a fracturing job. Soon after the start of the alliance

when engineers from both companies completed the exercise of mapping their fracture designs, results showed

the two processes to be duplicates.



In the alliance, fracturing jobs are designed jointly and then modeled by the service company engineer, freeing

the oil company engineer to spend time on other projects that add more value—in some cases, selection of

other wells to be stimulated (candidate recognition). In other cases, an optimal division of labor may assign

candidate  recognition  and  job  design  to  the  service  com-

pany  engineer,  leaving  the  oil  company  engineer  free  to

develop future growth opportunities. In a growing number

of alliances, the oil company no longer requires a represen-

tative on site for the job. The streamlined process is more

efficient,  but  trust  in  the  alliance  partner  is  crucial  to  the

success of such a scheme.

(Service Company)

Two fracture-design process maps at left

Chapter Highlight 14.3: Dedicated Service Personnel21



The Dedicated In-house Engineer Redefines Work: Success Depends on Building

New Kinds of Working Relationships Between Oil and Service Companies



The operator’s oil production was declining and needed to stimulate under-producing wells. This

made candidate selection critical for improving production. With candidate well selection as the

focus, a new contractor–operator relationship was used to improve the suc-

cess of the workover program. In the old way of working, the client might

say, “Here’s my work schedule. Be ready to roll Tuesday morning at 5.” Now

the client might say, “Work with your team to develop a completion plan for

this well that gives both companies the highest value.” Oil companies realize

there’s a benefit in trusting suppliers

to  meet  or  exceed  their  criteria  for

job performance.



“Historically, you placed

a great deal of emphasis

on the lowest possible

bid. All that changed

when you stopped

focusing on price alone

and placed more empha-

sis on the significant

few oil and gas fields,

technologies and rela-

tionships with key sup-

pliers that contribute to

performance.”



Given the large scope of responsibili-

ties the dedicated in-house program places on it's engi-

neers, training had to rise to the challenge. The dedicated

in-house  program  starts  with  engineers  who  have  mas-

tered the basics. Those picked for the program have typi-

cally had at least three years of experience in completion

and  fluids  engineering  and  have  demonstrated  both

entrepreneurial spirit and interpersonal skills. These skills

are  essential  as  the  job  requires  technical  proficiency,

business acumen, and diplomacy. When first installed in

the client office, the engineer must win the confidence of

oil  company  colleagues  and,  to  be  most  effective,  must

progress from being regarded as a guest to being one of the team. Foremost, the engineer must

be  able  to  work  within  the  oil  company  culture  to  develop  support  for  optimal  solutions.  One

dedicated in-house engineer said, “When I first arrived at the oil company’s office, the most skep-

tical production engineer was the first to let me design a fracturing job. When the job went off

without a hitch, that went a long way to winning the trust of everyone.”



—Oil Company Manager



Dedicated Service Personnel’s Main Responsibilities:



•  Work with the client on

 treatment design and

 evaluation.



•  Collect and evaluate post-



•  Identify and implement pro-



treatment data  (performance

versus expectations).



cess improvements with the

client.



•  Collect and evaluate data for

candidate recognition treat-

ment design.



•  Identify, coordinate, and

implement treatment or

design changes for improved

well perfor mance and eco-

nomics.



•  Provide benchmarks for con-



•  Develop general know-



tinuous improvement.



•  Document results and supply

benefits to the client and to

supplier technical support

and management.



•  Enlist appropriate  technical

support when necessary.



ledge of your company’s

offerings outside their spe-

cific offerings.



•  Identify, coordinate, and



implement additional servic-

es from your company based

on client needs.



What Matters Most…



591





Often, the after-sales costs are not included, which fails to provide a true picture

of the account-level profitability. This issue is addressed in the “Managing Custom-

ers for Profits, Not Just for Sales” sidebar.22



Market Share



Market  share  is  a  measure  of  your  portion  of  the  total  market  available  for  an

account. Categories are typically based on your company’s share of the total dol-

lars spent by the account for products and services you can provide by segment.

However, this measure must be relevant for the account. In some instances, the

measurement  is  your  company’s  share  of  the  total  number  of  rigs,  software

licenses or seats, etc. Not an exact number, the measure is an estimation using

one of three possible estimates: majority, split, or low market share, depending on

how it is measured.



u	Managing Customers for

Profits, Not Just for Sales



With real cost-plus pricing, profitability

could be uniform across customers

despite wide variations in both costs

and prices. But there is evidence that

prices seldom reflect the actual costs

in serving customers (though they

may be somewhat related to produc-

tion costs). In many businesses, the

 difference between the highest and

lowest prices realized in similar trans-

actions for the same product is as

much as 30%, not including quantity

 discounts.



Consider, for example, the relation-

ship between prices and total costs

in one month’s orders for a manufac-

turer of pipe resin (below). The diago-

nal line indicates a price level equal to

costs. If gross margins were the

same on all orders, the orders would

all lie along a line parallel to the diago-

nal line. Instead, they are widely dis-

persed. Nearly 13% of sales volume

resulted in losses of about a nickel a

pound, while about 4% of volume

generated an 8% profit. The rest fell

somewhere in between.



This pattern is not unusual. In a wide

variety of situations, you can consis-

tently observe a lack of correlation

between price and the cost to serve.



Some orders and customers generate

losses, and in general the dispersion

of profitability is wide.



592



CHAPTER 14  Relationship Profiling



Analyzing the Relationship Profile

The  previously  discussed  relationship  measurement  factors  are  presented  in  an

Excel spreadsheet format (Fig. 14.12) that provides insight into the current rela-

tionship and acts as a basis for identifying gaps between the current relationship

and the targeted relationship.23



Building an accurate relationship profile requires collaboration with everyone who

works on an account. First, the “Performance Indicators” section is completed for

each business unit working with the account; these indicators are then consoli-

dated  to  arrive  at  a  total  relationship  analysis.  Each  parameter  has  been  color

coded to highlight inconsistencies or gaps in the relationship with the account.



Relationship profiles are categorized as Level 1 (blue), Level 2 (green), or Level

3 (red). These levels represent the model relationships that allow you to discuss

the relationship strategically and to build a plan for migrating or strengthening

the relationship.



Relationship Migration Strategy



The relationship profile matrix is completed in preparation for planning a migra-

tion strategy, envisioning the type of relationship (Level 1, 2, or 3) you want to have

with the account and identifying the benefits for both the account and your com-

pany. The next step in planning a migration strategy is to answer the questions:



•  Management alignment



•  Long-term advantage



•  Sufficient resources: Budget, people, and equipment



•  Competitive threats



•  Additional costs



These questions must be answered for each of the accounts and then prioritized

at the region level to ensure the best use of your limited resources to serve the

market.



Management Alignment



Which  relationship  level  does  the  account’s  management  believe  extracts  the

greatest amount of value for meeting their objectives (Level 1, 2, or 3)? Efforts to

change the way companies interact require support from the account’s manage-

ment to avoid wasting resources on a strategy that is unlikely to succeed.



Long-Term Advantage



What  is  the  future  activity  level  of  the  customer?  Does  the  customer  warrant

dedicating  additional  resources  to  migrate  to  a  Level  1  or  2  relationship?  Is

there time to achieve what you want with the customer?



Sufficient Resources



Do you have the operational resources to commit to meeting the customer’s expec-

tations?  Answers  to  resource  questions  include  not  only  sufficient  products  or

tools, but also people to service the account (field, sales, and other support per-

sonnel). If the required resources are brought into your region, does the region

have a sufficient budget? Does the region have sufficient funds to purchase assets

or make other required investments?



What Matters Most…



593





Competitive Threats



Competitive  threats  may  require  building  barriers  to  entry  to  the  account  by

migrating the account to a Level 2 or 1 relationship. New competitors to the region

may focus on Level 3 accounts, as entry points to these accounts are easiest to

penetrate. This is especially true in mature markets, such as in the oilfield service

industry  in  mature  oilfields  where  the  technical  challenges  of  producing  hydro-

carbons have already been addressed or the fields are in the decline stage. At this

stage, asset management may focus heavily on reducing costs. In such an environ-

ment,  customers  may  be  persuaded  to  try  new  suppliers  under  the  promise  of

lower costs.



Additional Costs



Customer  relationship  strategies  involve  investment  from  your  company,  which

increases your costs. Migrating an account from a Level 3 relationship to a Level

2 relationship increases your costs as you provide value-added products and ser-

vices at that level. Migrating an account from Level 2 to Level 1 increases your

costs  for  support  through  increased  operations  costs  (dedicated  field  support

managers),  additional  sales  support,  or  dedicated  in-house  engineers.  Before

migrating these accounts, you must be sure that the increased cost to your com-

pany  is  matched  by  a  willingness  from  the  account  to  compensate  you  for  the

increased  investment—through  improved  efficiencies,  volume  of  work,  better

pricing, or the use of an advanced-level business and contract model.



Salespeople  must  be  careful  not  to  assume  that  migrating  all  relationships  to

higher levels is always appropriate. As resources at the region level may be lim-

ited, it is unrealistic to assume that all accounts can be Level 1 relationships. But

the above questions can assist in identifying which customers are the best candi-

dates for higher-level relationships. In addition, you still want to provide excellent

service to Level 3 accounts while keeping the costs of servicing those accounts as

low as possible. This trade-off may require taking assets and other resources from

Level 3 accounts and deploying them for Level 2 or 1 accounts that are willing to

compensate you for the additional resources.



Migration Path



The migration path for an account moves from Level 3 to 2 to 1. For example, if

your  company  wants  an  alliance-type  relationship  with  a  Level  3  account,  you

must first migrate the account from price buyer to value buyer (Level 2), then

to  relationship  buyer  (Level  1).  You  must  also  maintain  all  other  relationship

parameters at a level consistent with the level of the desired relationship. Fig-

ure 14.11 illustrates the migration path.



Migration path



Level 3 → Level 2 → Level 1



Migration demands



Minimum cost → Higher costs → High maintenance



Contracting model



Cost plus → Performance → Results → Value sharing



Fig. 14. 11

Account relationship  migration path.

Interpretation of Example Relationship Profiles



This  section  provides  an  analysis  for  each  of  the  accounts  listed  in  Fig.  14.12.

These are real relationship profile analyses for one supplier called Company A in

one  region  for  their  top  seven  accounts,  which  generated  80%  of  the  supplier’s

revenue. The analysis was used to make strategic decisions to improve Company

A’s competitive position in the region.



Account 1



This account buys from Company A because they believe they are the best-in-class

provider of this service. They recognize value in a supplier who follows a focused

differentiation strategy. The account is classified as a value buyer as they consider

more  than  just  price,  and  the  account  views  Company  A  as  a  problem  solver

because  of  their  high  level  of  technical  expertise.  This  account  tends  toward

pragmatism when buying new technology—that is, they wait to buy proven inte-

grated products and services even though they are strong technically. Company A

has had discussions with this account about possibly entering into an advanced

business relationship and using a results-based contractual framework; however,

only  bonus  malice  clauses  were  explored.  A  cost-plus  contract  is  in  place,  and

their profitability is at Level 1, or greater than 20%. In addition, Company A has

the majority of this account’s market share—all in all a pretty good scenario.



The account profile is consistent, as highlighted by the same color across most of

the profile parameters. One strategy aimed to implement an advanced-level con-

tractual  arrangement,  such  as  results-based  pricing.  Because  of  their  in-house

expertise, however, this account preferred to continue on a cost-plus basis.



The strategy followed with this account was to continue to support this customer’s

in-house experts with advanced technology seminars, VIP seminars highlighting suc-

cessful applications of new technology in their operations, and examples of advanced

business  relationships  from  other  accounts  to  create  awareness  of  other  options.

Company A also held regular service quality review meetings to highlight major oper-

ating successes and how they would continue to repeat these successes, and to pin-

point major operating failures and what they were doing to prevent their recurrence.



Account 2



Very different from Account 1, Account 2 purchases only from the low-cost pro-

vider, which is consistent with their pricing sensitivity. As Account 2 views Com-

pany A as being equal to other suppliers, they respond only to tenders. Account 2



What Matters Most…



595















is conservative in its adoption of new technology, which it believes only

complicates their operations. Their technical depth with regard to their

services is weak. This account has stated it does not want to participate

in advanced business model relationships. In general, this account falls

under  the  cost-plus  contractual  model.  Their  profitability  is  not  good

(Level 3), yet they do the majority of their work. This account opens the

work to tender every two years, and Company A has the contract.



Again, this account is consistent—red across all profile parameters. For

this account, the strategy would be to use the current

contract  to  demonstrate  value—in  other  words,  to

migrate  the  account  from  a  price  buyer  to  a  value

buyer.  The  first  step  would  be  to  conduct  in-house

training to raise the expertise of their users to a level

where  they  can  see  the  benefits  of  advanced  tech-

nologies.  If  they  do  not  succeed  in  changing  their

buying behavior, they may consider two options:



•  Raise prices for the next tender. As they were the

low bidder on this contract, there may be some gap

between Company A and competitor pricing.



•  Reduce their costs to service this account so

they  reach  Level  2  profitability.  This  two-tier

pricing strategy, discussed briefly in Chapter 1

“Understanding  the  Role  of  Marketing  and

Sales,”  is  covered  again  in  detail  in  Chapter  2

“Value Drivers.”



This  account  is  not  a  good  candidate  for  advanced

contractual  models  as  it  focuses  only  on  cost  and

views  Company  A  and  other  suppliers  equally.

Advanced-level contractual models are only appropri-

ate  for  accounts  that  focus  on  value  creation  and

relationship (Accounts 4 and 5 below).



Account 3



Account 3 is interesting. This account views Company

A as the best-in-class provider of the service—focused

differentiation. Although this account tells them their

selection  rests  on  price,  they  view  them  as  experts,

and  they  participate  in  problem  solving,  developing

bid specifications, etc. The account is pragmatic in the

adoption of new technology, and its technical depth is

good. They have never discussed other business mod-

els  with  this  account.  They  have  in  place  a  cost-plus

contractual model, their profitability is at Level 3, and

they have the majority of this account’s work.



u	The Good, the Bad, the Ugly



It is useful to think of customers in terms of two dimensions:

net price realized and cost to serve. To show graphically the

dynamics of the interplay between seller and buyer, you can

devise a simple matrix (below). The vertical axis is net price, low

to high, and the horizontal axis is cost to serve, low to high.

This categorization is useful for any marketer. The carriage trade

costs a great deal to serve but is willing to pay top dollar. (This

category would include the customers of your introductory

example, who placed small orders for high-cost custom plumb-

ing fixtures.) At the opposite extreme are bargain basement

customers—sensitive to price and relatively insensitive to ser-

vice and quality.

They can be served

more cheaply than

the carriage trade.



Cost to Serve



High



High



Low



Passive



Carriage

Trade



Low



Net

Price



Aggressive



Bargain

Basement



Serving passive cus-

tomers costs less

too, but they are

willing to accept

high prices. These

accounts generate highly profitable orders. There are various

reasons for their attitude. In some cases the product is too

insignificant to warrant a tough negotiating stance over price.

Other customers are insensitive to price because the product is

crucial to their operation. Still others stay with their current sup-

plier, more or less regardless of price, because of the prohibi-

tive cost of switching. As an example from another industry,

many major aircraft components cannot be changed without

recertifying the entire aircraft. And in some cases, vendor capa-

bility is so well matched to buyer needs that cost to serve is

low though the customer is receiving (and paying for) fine ser-

vice and quality.



Aggressive customers, on the other hand, demand (and often

receive) the highest product quality, the best service, and low

prices. Procter & Gamble, boasting an efficient procurement

function, has a reputation among its suppliers for paying the

least and getting the most. Aggressive buyers are usually power-

ful; their practice of buying in large quantities gives them lever-

age with suppliers in seeking price deals and more service. The

national accounts described in the second example at the begin-

ning of this article drove hard bargains with the capital equip-

ment supplier.



This account’s inconsistent profile represents a “Good

Buyer” profile, or what “The Good, the Bad, the Ugly”

sidebar refers to as an aggressive buyer.24 Their profit-

ability  is  low  because  the  cost  to  service  this  account  is  high.  Newly

assigned sales representatives to this account quickly learn that prices

must be tight if Company A wants the work, as this is what the account

contacts tell them. The customer wants and uses value-added services

and has the expertise to use it, but the customer also wants it as cheaply

as possible. So who decides on prices? They do.



596



CHAPTER 14  Relationship Profiling



For this account the strategy is to solicit support throughout the organization and

clearly  communicate  the  value  generated  by  the  team  (account  contacts  and

Company A staff). The next step in the strategy is to inform the supporters that

their revenues for the services provided are too low. They share that they cannot

continue to support this level of service at the current prices and that prices will

increase in the next contract period. They discuss this first with their supporters

to  get  their  feedback  on  how  to  proceed  with  the  price  increase  without  losing

the contract.



In  addition,  Company  A  begins  to  hold  discussions  with  account  management  to

determine their willingness to consider advanced-level business models, as perfor-

mance- or results-based contracts could be one way to justify increased prices.



Accounts 4 and 5



Accounts 4 and 5 are loyal clients based on their account profile parameters (see

the “Measures of Loyalty” sidebar25). These accounts believe they realize value by

building strong relationships with a few key suppliers, so they buy from broadly

differentiated  companies  such  as  Company  A.  As  expected,  these  accounts  are

relationship  buyers,  and  the  role  of  Company  A  is  that  of  stakeholder.  These

accounts are visionaries in their adoption of technology. Account 4 has good tech-

nical depth, and Account 5 is strong. Both have participated in integrated projects

with performance- and results-based remuneration components.



u	Measures of Loyalty



Broadly speaking, customer loyalty is

the feeling of attachment to or affec-

tion for a company’s people, products,

or services. These feelings manifest

themselves in many forms of customer

behavior. The ultimate measure of loy-

alty, of course, is share of purchases in

the category. In the automobile busi-

ness, it is share of garage. In the cloth-

ing industry, it is share of closet. And

in the restaurant business, as Taco Bell

president and CEO John Martin says, it

is “share of stomach.”



Unfortunately, such information is rare-

ly available at the individual customer

level, but there are alternative mea-

surements, which can be grouped into

three major categories.



Intent to Repurchase. At any time in

the customer relationship, it is possible

to ask customers about their future

intentions to repurchase a given prod-

uct or service. Although their respons-

es are simply indications of future

behavior and are not assurances, they

have very important benefits. First,

companies can capture this information

when they measure satisfaction, mak-

ing it relatively easy to link intentions

and satisfaction for analytical purposes.

The fact that intent to repurchase can

be measured at any time in the cus-

tomer relationship makes it especially



valuable in industries with a long repur-

chase cycle. Finally, intent to repur-

chase is actually a very strong indicator

of future behavior. Although this mea-

sure will generally overstate the proba-

bility of repurchase, the degree of

exaggeration is usually fairly consis-

tent, meaning that the future results

can be predicted fairly accurately. For

example, an average of 60% to 80%

of automobile customers queried 90

days after buying a car say they intend

to repurchase the same brand, and

35% to 40% actually do so three to

four years later.



Primary Behavior. Depending on the

industry, companies often have access

to information on various transactions

at the customer level and can measure

five categories that show actual repur-

chasing behavior: recency, frequency,

amount, retention, and longevity.

Although they are important measures

of actual behavior, they only provide a

glimpse of overall share and are most

useful as an indication of changes over

time. Moreover, sometimes they can

send the wrong message. For exam-

ple, the credit card industry has tradi-

tionally measured the willingness of

the consumer to pay the annual fee as

its prime measure of retention. During

the late 1980s and early 1990s, those

same companies saw that willingness

rise while actual “share of wallet”—



the degree to which customers used

their particular card for making pur-

chases when they had the option of

using a credit card—decreased. The

consumer was willing to pay the fee to

have the credit card available but often

did not use it. Therefore, recency, fre-

quency, and amount of purchase were

significantly better measures of loyalty.



Secondary Behavior. Customer refer-

rals, endorsements, and word of

mouth are extremely important forms

of consumer behavior for a company.

In most product and service catego-

ries, word of mouth is one of the most

important factors in acquiring new

customers. Frequently, it is easier for

a customer to respond honestly to a

question about whether he or she

would recommend the product or ser-

vice to another, than to a question

about whether he or she intended to

repurchase the product or service.

Such indications of loyalty, obtained

through customer surveys, are fre-

quently ignored because they are soft

measures of behavior that are difficult

to link to eventual purchasing behavior.

However, as secondary behavior signif-

icantly leverages the positive experi-

ences of a single customer, it

is very important to understand what

types of experiences create such

behavior.



What Matters Most…



597





Although these accounts are alike in many ways, the performance indicators are

very  different.  Account  4  has  a  cost-plus  contractual  agreement  with  Level  1

profitability. Company A’s  market share for this account is low because they do

only  their  exploration  work.  For  Account  5,  however,  they  have  an  alliance  in

place that is based on reducing lifting costs. Company A holds the majority of this

account’s work.



The  strategy  for  each  of  these  accounts  is  to  invest  in  the  relationships.  For

Account 4, they would discuss with management whether they would be support-

ive of migrating to an advanced business and contractual model for their develop-

ment work. But there are drawbacks for Company A in this example. Why?



Advanced  models  mean  that  Company  A  assumes  more  risk.  With  this  account,

local Company A’s  management was satisfied with the results and did not wish to

change the relationship as they viewed this account’s development work as low

profit.  In  fact,  management  views  any  move  as  risky  and  as  possibly  making  it

easier  for  competitors  to  gain  entry  into  the  exploration  work—the  wrong

approach for this relationship-focused account.



For Account 5, they would want to maintain the account at its current level in an

effort to ensure that this customer would turn away their competitors.



These fairly secure accounts could easily be taken for granted if they do not con-

tinuously focus on satisfying the account and providing exceptional service in all

areas of their business.



In this example, another strategy might be to take resources from Account 3 and

move them to Accounts 4 and 5 to give them superior service. They would take

resources  from  Account  3  rather  than  Account  2  as  Account  3  uses  higher-cost

value-added services, and sales support is high for the level of revenue generated

from  their  operations.  These  resources  could  be  used  to  develop  Account  4’s

market and further strengthen Account 5.



Account 6



Account 6 shows a profile typical of some large multinational accounts. Although

the account buys from Company A because they view them as best in their field, the

account is price sensitive and buys at only a small premium. Their role with Account

6  is  that  of  vendor.  The  account  is  technically  strong,  as  shown  by  its  visionary

technology adoption profile and strong technical depth. They have had brief discus-

sions  with  this  account  regarding  advanced-level  business  models  and  have  par-

ticipated in one integrated remote offshore project. Current contracts, however, are

cost plus, profitability is at Level 2, and this account splits the work among vendors.



Some very good competitors try equally hard to win, earn, and increase their share

of the market with Account 6. The account believes there are three leading oilfield

service  companies,  and  that  Company  A  is  one  of  those  three.  As  long  as  this

account  buys  from  any  of  the  three,  price  is  the  deciding  factor.  In  fact,  this

account has developed a tendering strategy to accept bids from the three leading

companies and then award a 50/50 split to two companies to obtain services at

the lowest price.



Two strategies can be considered for managing Account 6:



•  Change nothing. Account profitability is at Level 2, and it has been difficult

to get involved in their project cycle before the procurement stage. Account

6 management does not believe Company A’s staff or their competitors’ staff

offer  any  benefits,  and  they  believe  their  in-house  expertise  is  adequate.

The strategy of not working to improve the relationship, however, may prove

faulty, as at any time Account 6 may choose a competitor to do the work.



598



CHAPTER 14  Relationship Profiling



Chapter Highlight 14.4: Moving from Vendor to Stakeholder



When Low Bidder Isn’t Good Enough



An oil company operator in Kalimantan believed the company was restricted

by government  tendering requirements to select the low bidder for servicing

its  offshore  operations. The  company  believed  it  was  receiving  suboptimal

service and lack of initiative from the service industry to deliver  leading-edge

technology. With a change in the operator's top management came a demand

for more innovative and collaborative relationships with key suppliers. In their

words,  if  suppliers  were  not  willing  to  work  as  part  of  the  operator’s  team

they would find suppliers who were. They also wanted world-class perform-

ers in the field with the latest technologies to help them reduce their lifting

costs per barrel.



After  hearing  this  message,  Company  A’s  business  units  reevaluated  their

relationship  and  level  of  commitment  with  the  operator.  Although  two  of

Company A’s business units were already on the key supplier list, the operator was asking for significant

changes in the way the company was doing  business.



The results of the change were outstanding. The operator/Company A team pioneered SX drilling, and

the Operator achieved world-record results using slimhole testing tools and an advanced reservoir pres-

sure and sampling tool to slash testing costs. The operator credits these and other supplier innovations

that  reduced  total  well  costs  for  revitalizing  Indonesian  oil  and  gas  production  in  East  Kalimantan. The

graph below was part of a the operator’s presentation given to Indonesian authorities to justify awarding

multiyear contracts to Company A at significantly higher supplier costs per well. Despite higher supplier

costs,  the  presentation  demonstrates  that  the  operator  reduced  total  costs  significantly  over  previous

 low-bidder win contracts.



What happened?

First, the operator clearly explained the project objectives:



•   Lifting costs per barrel of oil produced below $3



•   Drilling time reduced by 20%



•   Dependable world-class service with minimal downtime from their suppliers



•   Open and direct communication on how to improve every aspect of the operation.



To meet these objectives, investments were made in technologies and product modifications, and one

field service manager and one in-house engineer were added. Regular service quality review meetings

were  held  to  review  major  operating  successes  and   failures  and  to  document  lessons  learned. The

operator was regularly updated on technology through visits to engineering centers.



What were the results?

The graph demonstrates the operator’s success, and

Company A’s  profitability improved 50% over previous

contracts.

Using Advanced Program

What Matters Most…



599











•  Work  to  understand  Account  6’s  value  drivers  and  determine  where  Com-

pany A can differentiate its value proposition from their competitors. Then,

they would bring to the region a recognized Company A industry expert to

participate in venues that raise this customer’s awareness to create a buy-

ing environment. (Other techniques for creating a buying environment are

discussed in Chapter 9 “Strategic Sales Plan.”)



By adopting the second strategy, they would seek to migrate Account 6 to a value

buyer after the account has recognized their differential value and begins to view

their role as a problem solver. If this strategy is unsuccessful, this account should

be treated as a Level 3 account, minimizing their costs to service the account and

applying a two-tier pricing approach.



They  may  also  want  to  try  to  understand  why  this  account  participated  in  one

integrated project but has not expressed a desire to extend the arrangement to

other projects. Salespersons may also want to spend time learning how to differ-

entiate themselves from the competitor’s representatives. A one- to two-year time

period can be expected to implement the strategy for Account 6.



Account 7



Account 7 is similar to Account 6 in three key ways: Company A’s  position, pricing

sensitivity, and the role of Company A in the account. However, Account 7 is con-

servative  in  the  adoption  of  technology,  and  its  technical  depth  is  weak.  This

account  does  not  wish  to  participate  in  advanced  business  models,  as  it  views

them  as  complicated  and  difficult  to  implement  due  to  governmental  require-

ments. Currently, Company A has basic contractual pricing agreements with this

account, and profitability is at Level 2. Account 7 also believes there are only three

acceptable  service  providers,  but  unlike  Account  6,  the  low  bidder  gets  all  the

work.  In  fact,  Account  7’s  contracting  procedures  require  that  two  suppliers  be

qualified for every major contract early in the project life cycle.



The best strategy for this account is similar to that used with Account 6: First focus

on understanding their value drivers and then work on differentiating Company A’s

value proposition from competitors. In Account 7, however, the task may be easier

as their technical depth is weak. Therefore, Company A account managers should

target  Account  7  end-users  for  training,  if  possible,  and  if  not,  concentrate  on  a

program that creates a buying environment and demonstrates how Company A ser-

vices can enable the account to better achieve their corporate or project objectives.



They would also take action to elevate their role to problem solver. For exam-

ple, they would consider injecting their technical expertise by   plac ing a dedi-

cated  in-house  engineer  in  their  organization  to  assist  them  in  using  their

supplier services to full potential. Chapter Highlight 14.4 shows the results of

a strategy that moved Company A from a vendor role to a stakeholder role.



In the appendix is a relationship profiling poster that can be used to facilitate a

sales team or account team workshop to complete a relationship profile.  A copy

of  the  poster  suitable  for  large  format  printing  can  be  downloaded  from  www.

b2bprofessionalsales.com



Customer relationship profiling is a valuable tool for assessing the status of the

customer relationship. A completed profile provides a snapshot in time for plan-

ning account development. Accurate profiling and analysis are essential to decid-

ing  which  actions  are  likely  to  better  align  the  account  and  build  a  profitable

relationship. Like a marriage, both parties must agree that advancing the rela-

tionship level will mutually benefit both companies. Where there is not manage-

ment  alignment  or  support  for  more  advanced  relationships,  you  must  ensure

that a basic relationship still delivers total customer satisfaction.



600



CHAPTER 14  Relationship Profiling



       Summary of Chapter Objectives



1.  Describe the steps in relationship profiling.



The relationship profile always stems from the customer’s  perspective relative to how the account views your company’s

individual business units. Ask several contacts in the account to gain a good understanding of the customer’s perspective.

Then, determine the seven account profile items and three performance indicators.



The seven account profile parameters are:



Market position. What is basis of the value your company brings to the account (see M. Porter’s work on competitive

marketing strategies)? Does the account perceive that your company delivers value because of a broad differentiation

strategy (usually meaning the account prefers fewer suppliers and stronger relationships with a few suppliers)? Or are

you viewed as a best-in-class provider of specific services because of a focus differentiation strategy? Do they see you as

a provider of the lowest-cost services?



Pricing sensitivity. How do you classify the account (as a price buyer, value buyer, or relationship buyer)? The answer

determines how sensitive this client is to price differences versus product differences.



Your company’s role in the account. Does the account view your company as a stakeholder in its business? Have you

demonstrated that you are a company that is genuinely interested in optimizing the performance of their reservoirs? Or

do they consider you to be problem solvers with technical expertise? Are you treated as a vendor without differentiation

from other suppliers, and does your first involvement with their projects occur at the bidding stage?



Technology adoption profile. How willingly does the account adopt new technology in its day-to-day operations?



Technical depth. Does the account have the internal expertise to get the full benefit from your company’s  products and

services?



Willingness  to  form  external  partnerships  and  share  value.  How  willing  is  the  account  to  make  the  remuneration

higher  than  cost-plus?  Is  the  account  willing  to  include  risk  or  reward  incentives  targeted  at  such  metrics  as  perfor-

mance, results, or joint value creation? These more advanced business relationships can be part of a standard cost-plus

contract or can take the form of an integrated services package. They can also be in the form of a specific engineered

solution, designed to deliver optimal well construction.



The three performance indicators are:



Current business and contract models used. This indicator describes the most common contractual model used by the

account today.



Profitability. This indicator measures total revenue minus the costs to deliver not only the ser vices but also any addi-

tional support costs. Profitability ranges are classified as Level 1 (best), Level 2 (average), and Level 3 (below average)

for the region.



Your company’s market share. This indicator measures your company’s  share of total dollars spent for specific services

or some other relevant market share indicator, such as percentage of rigs or software seats.



2.  Complete a relationship profile for your key accounts.



Account relationship profiles should be completed periodically and compared to individual buying center contact pro-

files  to  measure  progress  toward  the  targeted  relationship  or  desired  rating  in  the  account  profile  parameters  and

performance measures. After completing the relationship profile, make an account management plan for building or

maintaining the relationship. Before deciding to invest in migrating an account relationship, answer questions regard-

ing management alignment, long-term advantage, level of resources, competitive threats, and costs to migrate. Then

follow the plan to migrate the account or maintain the current account relationship by minimizing your costs and using

a two-tier pricing strategy, or in some cases, reallocating resources to another account that will benefit from the invest-

ment. Once completed, share your analysis with another one of your company’s representatives who knows the account

to see if they agree.



What Matters Most…



601







        Applying the Concepts



1.  Complete  a  relationship  profile  for  your  key  accounts.  The  spreadsheet  can  be  downloaded  from  http://www.



b2bprofessionalsales.com



2.  For any customer with whom you are involved that has a level of profitability at a Level 3, what should you (your



company, at either the region or corporate level) be doing about it?



3.  Do you have any accounts in the region that should ideally be at Level 1 who is not? If so, what should you be doing



to migrate them?

What Matters Most…



605



































































Planning



The Last Piece



It may sound strange to call planning the last piece of the puzzle. Most people will

tell you that is the first step—and they are correct in assuming that the salesperson

has all the skills needed to compete effectively as a professional salesperson. From

the  perspective  of  this  textbook,  this  is  that  last  step  in  the  development  of  your

professional B2B salesperson’s skills. After you have a solid grounding in the sales

skills,  then  you  are  ready  to  build  a  plan  in  which  you  identify  actions  needed  to

compete more effectively. The first chapter of this section discusses pipeline man-

agement, which is a critical part of any account or territory plan. The second chap-

ter examines relationship profiling. In B2B sales and especially in large B2B oppor-

tunities, the people involved in the buying decisions can be many, and the factors

influencing their decisions complex. No sales plan is complete without a relationship

strategy for your key customers. The execution of the plans depends on your ability

to manage the individual opportunities and win your targeted opportunities using all

the skills discussed in the first three sections of the textbook.



This chapter is about creating plans to support your sales efforts for your assigned

responsibilities. Most assignments for salespeople are either, account or territory

based or a combination of the two. The structure of this chapter will be to discuss

the account plan first and then the territory plan.



Account Plan

For account assigned salespeople, account management is the foundation for all

other  activities  in  the  customer  relationship  management  process.  The  goal  of

account management is to make the account–supplier relationship more efficient

and  profitable  for  both  companies  and  provide  total  customer   satisfaction.  The

account  plan  has  two  specific  objectives:  1)  position  your  company  to  enhance

your participation in future projects and 2) support your day-to-day activities with

the account. A plan is a proactive tool used to document the specific actions you

will take to accomplish your goals. You also use the plan to communicate respon-

sibilities  and  coordinate  account  management  activities  with  other  company

personnel and provide input into the resource planning process.



15



Chapter Objectives



After reading this chapter, you

will:



n  Describe the components of

an account management and

territory plans.



n  Know the tools for  creating



and managing a plan.



n  Create a sale plan for one



of your key accounts or terri-

tories.



Planning



607





Account  plans  contain  valuable  information  for  all  personnel  involved  with  a

particular account:



•  Marketing management can use the plans as a means to prioritize decisions

regarding all accounts and marketing programs at the local, regional and

global levels.



•  Operations management can use the plans to prioritize resource allocation



and make budgetary decisions on future projects.



•  Support and sales staff can prioritize their activities to bring the most value



to both the account and your company.



The account plan has four main parts that direct your activities:



•  Awareness



•  Situation report



•  Strategic plan



•  Execution and tracking



The next sections discuss each account plan part and the tools available for the

sales team in completing and managing the plan.



Account Awareness



Account awareness consists of two parts:



•  Account Corporate Profile



•  Account Organizational Information



The account awareness section of the report provides basic information about the

account that can be used to improve the common understanding of the account.

All company personnel involved with the account should review this section of the

report. Each component is discussed in more detail below.



Account Corporate Profile



Corporate-level information about an account is used to create an account profile

that contains specific information regarding the account business environment.



For  instructions  on  completing  the  account  corporate  profile,  review  Chapter  3

“Account  Corporate  Profile.”  The  account  corporate  profile  should  be  updated

annually, ideally after the account’s annual planning meeting. The account corpo-

rate profile is then used as input for the account plan.



Account Organizational Information



As part of the annual account planning process, the primary focuses of this section

of the plan are:



•  Organizational structure



•  Contact information



•  Relationship building



Information  about  the  account  organization  and  reporting  structure,  as  well  as

specific contact information, should be entered and updated in the company CRM

database by the salesperson responsible for managing the account. It is the sales-

person’s responsibility to keep the account information up to date and accurate.



608



CHAPTER 15  Planning



Organizational Structure



Organize information about each account according to the account’s business and

service unit structure, as described below:



•  Business  units:  revenue-generating  groups  within  the  account  that  are



responsible for generating profits for the company



•  Service units: support functions within the company that are managed as



cost centers.



The  business  and  service  units  are  an  important  part  of  the  account  planning

process. Identifying account units is like segmenting a large market into smaller

more manageable sub-segments that have similar needs and challenges that allow

you  to  take  a  more  focused  approach  to  an  individual  unit  in  support  of  selling

and servicing. The analysis section of the account plan compares your company’s

performance for different organizational units; the strategic plan section focuses

on identifying future account projects that originate from either a business unit

or service unit.



Having the account’s structural information in place enables you to understand

how  the  organizational  units  conduct  their  planning  process.  The  account  plan

should answer the following questions:



•  Are decisions made at the headquarters level or the business or service unit



level?



•  Who handles preparing plans, review and approval?



•  What is the budget level authority for each unit?



•  What is the decision-making process for selecting a service provider?



Understanding the account’s internal planning processes enables you to identify

the key contacts.



When  deciding  how  to  organize  the  account  for  your  plan  consider  the  points

below:



•  Business units are profit centers such as asset groups.



•  Service units are cost centers such as drilling departments.



A unit consists of a minimum of three levels:



Executive: High-level managers with approval level authority for major new pur-

chases,  initiatives,  or  projects  that  significantly  impact  the  account’s  business

drivers. The executives are strongly concerned with making the best investments

with the greatest return at an acceptable level of risk.



Management: Middle-level managers with the responsibility to execute company

strategies and who are held accountable to achieve the company’s stated objec-

tives. Management is concerned with achieving annual objectives.



Operations: First-level managers and employees responsible for day-to-day opera-

tions. This level has a high concern for ensuring that budgets are not exceeded,

and there are no major problems or delays in projects. Most buying center mem-

bers will come from the operations levels—users, evaluators, and decision-mak-

ers. Approvers commonly come from management and executive levels.



The Last Piece



609





The plan should include an organizational chart, starting with the headquarters

office on top and each business and service unit in the account. For each organi-

zational unit, include:



•  Name of the highest level executive for each unit.



•  City in which the unit is located.



•  Culture for each unit. Corporate culture is very important to recognize and

respect.  See  Chapter  Highlight  15.1:  Corporate  Culture:  A  Powerful

Force.  At  the  end  of  the  chapter  highlight  is  a  table  summarizing  what

salespeople need to accommodate better the specific culture of each unit.



Each company is unique, and an accurate description of how the account is orga-

nized and its culture is an important part of the account plan. This information is

used to brief anyone not familiar with the account who needs to contact people

in  the  account  or  make  decisions  regarding  the  account.  Figure  15.1  is  an

example of an organizational chart for an oil and gas company.



Additional information in the account plan for each business and service unit are:



•  Business Drivers. For those business drivers that are most important to the

unit, in many cases the executives at the unit level will establish key per-

formance indicators to monitor their key business drivers. Some of the more

common  business  drivers  for  an  energy  company  are  profit,  production,

total costs, lifting costs per barrel, reserves, and reserves replacement ratio

(RRR).  The best and most direct way to determine the important business

drivers  is  to  research  the  account’s  publications,  such  as  their  annual

report, and engage in discussions with the unit executives. It is important

to emphasize that the completed account profile identifies which business

drivers are important to the account. The business driver information iden-

tifies which business drivers are relevant for each unit. The business drivers

identified in the account profile may not be relevant for a specific unit, and

important business drivers at the unit level may not be part of the higher

level account drivers identified in the account profile.



•  Business Initiatives. Initiatives are the projects, programs, or plans a  unit



implements to address the business, drivers.



•  Critical Success Factors. Critical success factors are the things that have

to happen or resources that have to be in place to ensure the success of a

business initiative.



Fig. 15.1

Account organizational chart showing

business and service units.



610



CHAPTER 15  Planning



Chapter Highlight 15.1: Corporate Culture: A Powerful Force



Every organization has its unique style of working, which often contributes to its culture. The beliefs, ideologies,

principles, and values of an organization form its culture. The culture of the workplace controls the way employ-

ees behave amongst themselves as well as with people outside the organization.1 If a supplier wants to build

a strong and lasting business relationship, they need to be seen by the account as aligned and respecting the

company culture.



Several models have been proposed to explain organizational culture, one of them being the commonly used

model know as the Charles Handy model.2 Handy, born in 1932 in Ireland, is a well-known philosopher who has

specialized in organizational culture. According to Handy’s model, there are four types of culture that organiza-

tions follow. He linked these four cultures (power culture, role culture, task culture, and person culture) to four

Greek gods:



Power  =  Zeus (supreme god of the Olympians)



Role  =  Apollo (the god of order and rules)



Task  =  Athena  (the  warrior  goddess,  patroness  of  Odysseus,  arch  problem  solver  of  craftsmen



and pioneering captains)



Person  =  Dionysus (the god of existential culture)



Power



In some organizations, the power remains in the hands of only a few

people and only they are authorized to make decisions. They are the

ones who enjoy special privileges at the workplace. They are the most

important people at the workplace and are the major decision makers.

These individuals further delegate responsibilities to the other employ-

ees. In such a culture the subordinates have no option but to strictly

follow  their  superior’s  instructions. The  employees  do  not  have  the

liberty  to  express  their  views  or  share  their  ideas  in  an  open  forum

and have to follow what their superior says. The managers in such a

type  of  culture  can  sometimes  be  partial  to  someone  or  the  other

leading to major unrest among others. In summary:



•  Control radiates from the center.



•  Power is concentrated among a few.



•  Few rules and little bureaucracy are in place.



Task Culture



Organizations where teams are formed to achieve the targets or solve

critical  problems  follow  the  task  culture.  In  such  organizations  indi-

viduals with common interests and specializations come together to

form a team. There are generally four to five members in each team.

In such a culture every team member has to contribute equally and

accomplish tasks in the most innovative way. In summary:



•  People have clearly delegated authorities within a highly defined



structure.



•  Hierarchical bureaucracy is the norm.



•  Power derives from a person’s position.



•  Little scope exists for expert power.



Functional Departments



The Last Piece



611









Chapter Highlight 15.1: Corporate Culture a Powerful Force (cont.)



Person Culture



In certain organizations, the employees feel that they are more important than their organization. Such organiz-

tions follow a culture known as person culture. In a person culture, individuals are more concerned about their

own self rather than the organization. The organization in such a culture takes a back seat and eventually suffers.

Employees  just  come  to  the  office  for  the  sake  of  money  and  never  get  attached  to  the

organization. They are seldom loyal to the management and never decide in favor of the

organization. In this culture, one should always remember that the organization comes



first and everything else later.



In summary:



•  People believe themselves to be superior to the business.



•  The business is full of people with similar training, background, and expertise.



•  This culture is common in firms of professionals (e.g., accountants and lawyers).



 •  Power lies in each group of individuals.



Role Culture



Role culture is a culture where every employee has delegated roles and responsibilities according to his specializa-

tion,  educational  qualification,  and  interest  to  extract  the  best  out  of  him.  In  such  a  culture,  employees  decide

what best they can do and willingly accept the challenge. Every individual is accountable for something or other

and has to take ownership of the work assigned to him.

Power  comes  with  responsibility  in  such  a  work  culture.

In summary:



•    People  have  clearly  delegated  authorities  within



a highly defined structure.



•  Hierarchical bureaucracy is the norm.



•  Power derives from a person’s position.



•  Little scope exists for expert power.



The table below lists what salespeople must do to ensure that they are accepted by the specific corporate culture.

formers

numerous relation-

ships

and rules

ground

team

the vision

pared for change

you navigate the system
