mand



The  business  initiatives  and  associated  critical  success  factors  should  include

both current and future initiatives. It is with this information that the sales team

can begin to consider and propose different types of business relationships best

suited for each type of initiative. For example, in Fig. 15.2 for the IT Service Unit

it has two initiatives where the sales team will discuss a transactional relation-

ship for the “Roll out new hardware platform” emphasizing the customer’s main

decision factors of costs and timing. And for the initiative “Introduce central call

center”  the  sales  team  wants  to  suggest  an  enterprise  relationship  to  the  IT

Director  for  the  IT  Service  Unit.    The  different  types  of  business  relationships

were discussed in Chapter 10 “Competitive Bidding” and will be reviewed later

in this chapter again.



Fig. 15.2

Examples of unit drivers, initiatives, and

critical success factors.



Contact Information



All interactions with your accounts take place with individual contacts or a group

of  contacts,  whether  in  the  pursuit  of  an  opportunity,   delivery  of  a  service  or

product, or carrying out of other business and non-business activities. An impor-

tant role of account management is to ensure that contact information is current.

Detailed information is maintained for each account contact.  The type of informa-

tion includes contact details, alternate contact, position, education, manager (for

preparing organizational charts),  projects, and personal details such as national-

ity, marital status, and hobbies. This information makes it easier to target indi-

viduals for mail outs, invitations to seminars, and notices about upcoming events

that are appropriate to their position or interests. Individual contact details in the

modern CRM databases make it easy to manage relationship activities and share

information with other staff. Two other factors in the CRM contact database are

useful for conducting business with account contacts: social style and technology

adoption profiles. Both factors are described in Chapter 5 “Communication Skills”

and Chapter 1 “Understanding the Role of Marketing and Sales.” The social and

technology adoption profiles help you conduct business according to the account

contact’s preferences when making decisions.



Relationship Building



One of the goals of an account plan is to identify the people in the account that

you want to improve business relationships with, maintaining regular contact and



The Last Piece



613





	 u Guanxi: One Key to



Business Success



In China, closing a deal often

involves blending social pleasures

with practical matters.



developing personal relationships. Your goal is to improve

your understanding of your key contacts and alignment

with  you  and  your  company.  In  some  societies,  these

activities  are  essential  to  business  success  (see  the

“Guanxi: One Key to Business Success” sidebar).3



You hear about it all the time in



Key High-Level Contacts



Chinese business circles—“Guanxi,” which is arguably the

ingredient for business success in China. But just what is it?



Guanxi is a Mandarin Chinese term that describes the busi-

ness relationship between two or more parties. “Guan”

means “close together,” and “Xi” means “relation-

ship.” Guanxi is always built on trust that has developed over

time. It helps you keep loyal customers in the long run,

maintain a profitable business, and compete successfully

in a competitive marketplace. Almost all Chinese business-

people consider Guanxi to be one of their most important

sales and marketing tools. According to Asian e-business and

 marketing consultant Vivien Lee, you can create good Guanxi

by  following these simple rules:



1.  Call your customers often to say “Hello,”  discuss their



 business, and understand their needs.



2.  Provide professional advice and share your point of



view regarding the industry.



3.  Invite your customers to lunch, dinner, or a special

event, establishing close personal ties with them.



4.  Send them gifts on special occasions, such as New



Year’s Day or Christmas, just to remind them that you

are thinking of them.



5.  Follow up with e-mails or phone calls to update them

on what’s happening on your end of the business.



6.  Treat your customers as good friends, with trust and



sincerity.



This may sound complicated and time-consuming, but most

experts agree that if you’re doing business in China, you’d

better get your Guanxi smokin’ because without it, your busi-

ness will fly no farther than a Peking duck.



It is easy to maintain a good business relationship with

those account members with whom you deal directly as

part of providing products and services or members of

the  buying  center.  As  part  of  your  account  plan,  how-

ever, you must not forget to develop relationships with

high-level  managers—those  with  whom  you  may  not

have cause to interact with on a regular basis.



The account plan should identify key high-level contacts

in  the  customer’s  organization.  Key  high-level  contacts

are those who:



•  Initiate large projects.



•  Have  approval  authority  in  the  buying  decision



process.



•  Have  objectives  that  can  be  impacted  by  your



company.



An  active  key  high-level  contact  program  (HLCP)  is

essential to managing any account, and an ongoing rela-

tionship  with  key  high-level  contacts  makes  for  good

business. For more discussion on the benefits of a high-

level contact program, see the “Ongoing Business Rela-

tionships” sidebar.



Initiators of Large Projects



Large projects within any organization have a long plan-

ning period.  Corporate strategies dictate how high-level

managers  sanction  projects.  To  better  understand  how  major  projects  impact

high-level contact’s objectives, you should contact them annually or semi-annually,

and  your  meetings  with  them  should  be  well  planned  to  benefit  both  them  and

your company.



When a newly sanctioned project is revealed by an account, the salesperson enters

the project into the CRM opportunity database either as a lead or confirmed lead.

This information gives the sales team the earliest notification of a project so they

may initiate the pursuit phase of the opportunities management process. Ideally,

your company executive meets with the account’s high-level initiator of the project

to qualify the compelling event by  getting answers to the following questions, as

discussed in Chapter 6 “Sales Activities”:



•  Why does the account have to act?



•  How does this project fit into the account’s business strategy?



•  What are the initiator’s key issues and objectives for the project?



•  What is the measurable impact on the account’s business?



•  What are the consequences if the project is delayed or tabled?



•  What is the payback if the project is completed ahead of schedule?



614



CHAPTER 15  Planning



•  What is the priority for this project compared to others?



•  What are the key milestones for the project?



•  Who is responsible for implementing the project?



•  What is the project budget?



An effective account management program constantly stays focused on identifying

potential future projects and using lead time to better position your company to

win the project.



Approvers in the Buying Decision Process



Initiators  may  or  may  not  be  the  approvers  of  projects.  Projects  are  commonly

initiated at the headquarters level, then passed to a high-level manager in the

business or service unit that has a budget for implementing the project. Identify-

ing the approvers improves your ability to arrange a meeting to support your offer

late  in  the  procurement  process.  Chapter  9  “Strategic  Sales  Plan”  discusses

“executive credibility,” which is part of the opportunity assessment to determine

whether you can win the opportunity. The executive credibility factor considers

the strength of your relationships with key high-level contacts and other influen-

tial contacts in the buying center. Having strong executive credibility improves

your chances of securing opportunities.



High-Level Contacts Whose Objectives Your Company Can Impact



These are high-level contacts whose objectives you impact with your products and

services.  For projects that you have won, you should contact them as part of the

project  review  stage  if  they  do  not  participate  in  the  formal  operations  review

meetings.  It  is  a  good  account  management  practice  to  find  out  the  high-level

manager’s perception of your performance.



For  opportunities  in  the  pursuit  stage,  high-level  contacts  should  be  contacted

early  in  the  process—ideally  at  the  start  of  the  evaluation  of  options  stage.  By



u  Ongoing Business

Relationships



Ongoing business relationships make

good business sense. In your day-to-

day responsibilities as sales represen-

tatives, you are busy managing oppor-

tunities and supporting existing busi-

ness with your accounts. Much of

your contact is with the customers

you deal with daily. However, there are

times when you need to contact high-

er-level executives. This can be difficult

for several reasons. First, your relation-

ships revolve around the lower-level

contacts who may feel you are going

around them or avoiding them if you

visit the higher levels. Second, from a

practical point, it may be very difficult

for sales staff to get appointments

with higher-level contacts.



The solution is to establish ongoing busi-

ness relationships between your compa-

ny’s management and the account’s key

high-level contacts. The quality of the

relationship can be measured by how



easy it is for your management to

arrange a meeting with a specific high-

level contact. This is always easier if you

have an ongoing relationship.



Some of the key objectives for high-

level contacting are:



•  Early identification of the custom-

er’s projects and the compelling

event.



•  Understanding of the account’s



goals, objectives, and strategies and

how your company can participate.



•  Reqeusts for assistance to apply



influence in the buying  center when

your added-value products and ser-

vices are not given proper consider-



ation in the evaluation process.



•  Contact during the resolution of

concerns in the  procurement pro-

cess with the objective of demon-

strating your company’s commit-

ment to the success of the project.



•  The high-level contact program



should also include invitations to

social events that are of interest to

the high-level contact in an effort to

develop a relationship between the

high-level contact and your compa-

ny’s management.



The purpose of the HLCP as part of

the account plan is to ensure that the

salesperson identifies from the

account pipeline the strategic opportu-

nities that require relationships

between executives. Then schedule

the meetings in advance and ensure

that all the necessary preparations are

completed in time for the visit. For an

example of such planning, see Chapter

Highlight 15.3: Account Plan Calendar.



The Last Piece



615





contacting them at this time, you can establish their objectives for the project and

align with their goals. Once the project is in implementation, you follow up again.



Often, the manager whose objectives are impacted most is not part of the buying

center’s decision-making process. For example, in an oil company the exploration

vice  president’s  team  may  provide  specifications  for  products  and  services,  and

the procurement or drilling department makes the purchase. It is very important

that you make the exploration team aware of your value-added product and ser-

vices so they can apply influence on the drilling department during the procure-

ment process.



As part of the account plan, you identify key high-level contacts in the CRM data-

base. With this information, you generate an organizational chart that shows only

the  key  high-level  contacts,  which  is  useful  when  discussing  the  HLCP  in  the

account  plan  and  briefing  your  company’s  managers  before  making  high-level

contacts.



Buying Center



Every major project an account undertakes goes through an assigned buying center

to manage the selection of suppliers. Members of the  buying center are affected by

many factors, all of which must be carefully analyzed to win their support in the

buying process. The buying center roles were described in the opportunities man-

agement process, and the many factors affecting the selection process are  discussed

in more detail in Chapter 9 “Strategic Sales Plan.”



As part of the account planning process, you capture information about the buying

center for the strategic opportunities you plan to pursue in the next calendar year.

This information is entered into the opportunities organizational analysis in the

CRM opportunities database. The analysis at this level, however, is very basic, with

only the roles in the buying process being identified. The recommended procedure

is to complete a strategic sales plan for major opportunities. The level of analysis

is much more detailed than when a strategic sales plan is not completed.  In the

strategic sales, the plan has information  covering important data about the buying

center  and  other  critical  opportunity  factors  affecting  your  chances  of  securing

the project.



Account plan activities require your team to have a detailed understanding of the

factors that are critical to the influential buying center members when they choose

one  supplier  over  another.  Often,  these  factors  are  not  in  the  technical  require-

ments  described  in  tender  specification  documents.  Individual  buying  center

members  are  influenced  by  their  business  and  personal  agendas,  which  are

unknown to you unless you make an effort to learn what they are. As part of the

account plan, you must identify high-influence buying center members.  Then you

develop strong business relationships with them so that they are willing to share

their  agendas  with  you.  When  you  provide  your  products  and  services,  you  must

deliver not only the expected business results, but also win–win results for buying

center members.4



Despite  your  best  efforts  to  develop  strong  business  relationships,  some  buying

center  members  intentionally  try  to  block  the  selection  of  your  company  for

account projects. These buying center members are called “enemies.” As it is dif-

ficult to neutralize an enemy’s negative impact on the buying decision, you must

rely  on  supporters  and  mentors  within  the  account  to  overcome  the  enemy’s

negative influence. Such  situations are best planned for in advance and should be

included as an action in the account management strategic plan section.5



616



CHAPTER 15  Planning



Situation Report



The situation report of the account plan should include the following:



•  Account summary



•  A consolidated list of the account relationship profiles for each of your



company’s business units active with the account



•  Account-level competitive assessment



•  Account pipeline



Account Summary



The account summary is a table listing the current full year and next two years of

information for the factors below:



u  Where Do You Belong?



CapEx and OpEx Definitions



Capital  expenditures  (CapEx)  are  expenditures  creating  future

benefits. A capital expenditure is incurred when a business spends

money either to buy fixed assets or to add to the value of an exist-

ing asset with a useful life that extends beyond the tax year.



Operational  expenditures  (OpEx)  refer  to  expenses  incurred  in

the course of ordinary business, such as sales and general and

administrative  expenses  (and  excluding  cost  of  goods  sold

(COGS), taxes, depreciation, and interest).



CapEx and OpEx are also known as capital expenditure or capital

expense,  respectively  and  operating  expense,  operating  expen-

diture, and revenue expenditure, respectively.



Accounting  treatment:  Cannot  be  fully  deducted  in  the  period

when the expenditures were incurred. Tangible assets are depre-

ciated and intangible assets are amortized over time. Operating

expenses  are  fully  deducted  in  the  accounting  period  during

which they were incurred.



In  throughput  accounting,  money  spent  on  inventory  falls  under

CapEx. The money spent turning inventory into throughput is OpEx.



In real estate: Costs incurred for buying income-producing prop-

erty. Costs associated with the operation and maintenance of an

income-producing property.



Examples: Buying machinery and other equipment and acquiring

intellectual property assets like patents; wages, maintenance and

repair of machinery, utilities, rent, and SG&A expenses.



Account  Spend  Metric.  How  much  does  the

account  spend  for  your  company’s  product  and

services and your competitors’ products and ser-

vices? If this is reported or budgeted separately,

this  is  the  best  number  to  track.  However,  this

type of information is not easy or is even impos-

sible to know for some accounts. In those situa-

tions,  the  next  best  metric  is  either  CapEx  or

OpEx.  Most  companies  will  report  CapEx  and

OpEx in their annual reports and discuss future

expenditure trends, giving the sales team a good

indication of future spending with your company.

See  the  sidebar  “Where  Do  You  Belong?”6    to

determine which spending category your compa-

ny’s  product  and  services  are  part  of.  With  the

appropriate account spend metric, you can calcu-

late  an  accurate  market  share  capture    for  the

current  year  and  the  next  two  years.  For  more

examples  of  CapEx  and  OpEx,  see  Chapter  2

“Value Drivers.”



Sales.  Total  sales  to  the  account  for  the  current

year and your projected planned revenues for the

next two years.



Total  Market  Share.  Calculated  by  dividing  your

revenue by the account spend metric.



Coverage. What percentage of the account’s business and service units do you

have  business  with  for  the  current  year  and  your  projection  for  the  next  two

years?



Penetration. What percentage of your product and services lines have you sold to

the account for the current year and your projection for the next two years?



The account summary acts as the introduction to the plan and sets the expecta-

tions for how ambitious the plan is in terms of revenue growth. It is the revenue

growth that gives importance to the account plan. It is the execution of the plan

that will turn the ambitions into reality. The next part of the plan is the account

relationship profile and consolidated performance parameters.

Account Relationship Profile and

Consolidated Performance Parameters



The relationship profile was discussed in detail in Chapter 14 “Relationship Profil-

The  competitive  assessment  uses  the  four  relationship  parameters  to  evaluate  the

major competitors’ position in the account’s business and service units and includes

a  strengths  and  weaknesses  analysis.  The  competitive  assessment  and  account

relationship analysis are  completed at the same time. Fig. 15.4 shows the com-

petitive analysis section of the account relationship worksheet.

Account Pipeline



The next part of the situation report is the account pipeline.  The pipeline is a key

part of the account plan as it is the basis for establishing the objectives for the

components  of  the  strategic  plan  shown  in  Fig.  15.5.  Included  in  the  account

pipeline are the following:



•  Account PMPV



•  Account Pipeline Revenue Forecast Summary



618



CHAPTER 15  Planning

































•  Account Detailed Activity Analysis



•   Levels 1, 2, and 3 Classified Opportunities List



The  PMPV  and  “pipeline  revenue  forecast  summary”  were  dis-

cussed in Chapter 13 “Pipeline Management.” These charts are

created  using  the  same  steps  as  explained  in  the  pipeline

chapter, except these charts only include opportunities for

the account. The third part of the account pipeline sec-

tion is the detailed account activity discussed next.



Account Detailed Activity Analyses



The account detailed activity analyses are used to

understand the account’s future activity presented

in a monthly format for the current year and next

two years. There are two charts included in this sec-

tion. Figure 15.6 is the account detailed opportunity

analysis  and  Fig.  15.7  the  account  detailed  revenue

analysis. When presented in this format (stacked bar),

the left axis is market share as a percentage of the total

opportunities for each month in Fig. 15.6 and market share

as a percentage of the account’s total revenue in Fig. 15.7. For

each chart, the annual average for the current year, year one plan,

and year two plan is shown by the solid line. These charts make it easy to

see what the account’s expected activity level and spending will be for the next

two years, and the detail is shown behind for each month.



The two detailed analysis charts are used later in the strategic plan section to estab-

lish the account plan activities. The charts also provide input for operations manage-

ment for their annual budgeting process.



Level 1, 2, and 3 Opportunities List



The last part of the account pipeline is the opportunities classified as Level 1, 2,

and 3 opportunities as discussed in Chapter 13 “Pipeline Management.” These are

opportunities for the account that require executive-level approval for the strate-

gic  sales  plan,  bid,  and  negotiations  strategies.  The  dates  for  each  review  and

customer contact dates after bid submission should also be listed.

of the plan for the categories shown

in the figure.

Fig. 15.7

Strategic Plan



After  completing  the  awareness  and  situation  report  sections,  you  are  ready  to

complete  the  strategic  plan,  which  includes  the  categories  shown  in  Fig.  15.5.

Each part of the strategic plan uses the previous parts of the report to develop a

strategic  approach  to  managing  the  account.  The  strategic  plan  section  of  the

report is completed as described next.



Marketing Communications Plan



The marketing communications plan (MCP) is a very effective way to prospect

for new opportunities in the account. The MCP and prospecting were both dis-

cussed in detail in Chapter 1 “Understanding the Role of Marketing and Sales”

and Chapter 6 “Sales Activities.” The MCP includes activities for managing the

buyer’s readiness stages and collecting feedback from the account. The purpose

of the account marketing is to build awareness, knowledge, and liking within the

account  and  identify  early  the  need  to  do  business  development  for  confirmed

opportunities.



As  mentioned  earlier,  an  effective  marketing  communications  program  with  an

effective feedback program, you establish links with your account. Effective com-

munication  links  make  it  easier  and  more  efficient  for  your  customers  in  the

account  to  discuss  their  business  and  plans.  With  a  good  understanding  or  the

account’s business and plans, you will be able to identify many high-quality leads

that will result in confirmed leads and new business.



The main difference between a general MCP and an account-specific MCP is the

account program is designed to complement the account’s future business plans

as identified in the account pipeline. A best practice is to identify a mentor in the

account  to  help  your  create  a  marketing  calendar.  The  marketing  calendar

includes:



•  New product or service introductions. Before any investments are made to

bring  new  products  or  services  to  a  region,  there  should  be  a  marketing

event  to  announce  and  make  the  account  aware  of  the  new  product  or

services about to launch. This will make the account aware and have knowl-

edge of the application of the new product and services.



•  Scheduled  lunch  and  learns.  Create  a  12-month  schedule  for  30-minute

presentations and lunch. The presentations should be on topics of interest

as agreed upon with the mentor.



•  Training sessions. Complimentary training sessions on topics delivered by

you  or  someone  in  your  company  recognized  as  an  expert  on  the  topic.

These types of sessions are normally 1/2 day to 2-days.



620



CHAPTER 15  Planning



•  Industry and professional society events. A reminder of high profile events

and to ensure your in-house events for the account are not planned at the

same time, thereby avoiding scheduling conflicts.



•  Annual VIP seminars. These are courtesy meetings between the account’s

executive  levels  and  your  executives  at  which  the  goal  is  to  make  the

executives aware of your company’s solutions for their business issues.

These types of “like-rank” meetings are very impor-

tant  to  create  connections  between  the  account’s

executives  and  your  executives.  Then  when  “like-

rank” selling is required, the business relationship

already  exists.  See  the  sidebar  “When  Is  ‘Like-

Rank’ Selling Necessary?”7



u  When Is “Like-Rank” Selling Necessary?



A study focused on selling to senior executives conducted by

the Kenan-Flagler Business School at the University of North

Carolina found “like-rank” selling to be an important factor

when trying to build the seller’s trust and credibility with senior

executives in the buyer’s account.



Sixty-eight percent of the respondents to the first study rated

such a meeting a four or five, five being the highest level of

importance. Only 4% rated executive-to-executive selling the

least important. In meetings, respondents expect executives

from the sales organization to know the buyer’s business,

industry, and current key projects, as well as the significance

of the relationship in dollars and percentage of business.



Executives in both studies stated they do not want a “glad

hand” meeting. An executive-to-executive sales meeting must

reaffirm the seller’s commitment and highlight the strategic fit

for both companies. Executives favor suppliers with a similar

business philosophy and culture, and prefer to meet with “like-

rank” executives to affirm the value of the relationship.



On the other hand, more than one executive said, “If I need to

meet with the salesperson’s executive management, then

something is probably wrong.” In fact, sometimes the only rea-

son a buying executive wants to meet with a seller’s executive

of similar rank is to address a problem.



•  Public  relations  activities.  As  discussed  in  the

earlier  marketing  sections  of  the  textbook  and

in  the  networking  section  of  Chapter  6  “Sales

Activities,”  you  and  your  company  need  to  be

seen to support community events. Such events

should also be included in the marketing calen-

dar to make people aware of the event and time.



•  Account e-mail distribution. E-mail can be used

to send items of interest to people in the account

on  the  distribution  list  on  a  regular  basis.  The

content  can  be  success  stories,  new  product

introduction,  articles  of  interest  about  your

industry, etc. The key consideration is the mate-

rials  must  be  relevant  to  the  account.  E-mail

distribution lists were also discussed in the pros-

pecting section of Chapter 6 “Sales Activities.”



One of the key factors for the marketing calendar,

as discussed above, is to have input from the account. The calendar also needs to

be published and have wide distribution within the account. The marketing cal-

endar can be distributed as part of the account e-mail distribution list. Also, it is

recommended to print the calendar on high-quality paper so the salesperson can

distribute it when meeting new people in the account. This is a very effective way

to engage people in the account you have not worked with before.



Business Development



Business  development  is  a  term  widely  used  in  the  sales  and  marketing  com-

munities  and  can  mean  different  things  to  many  people.  To  avoid  confusion,

business development for the purpose of B2B professional sales is an activity by

the seller with the customer discussing specific options for a customer’s need

not considered by the customer before. For the seller, this is new business; for

the buyer it is a new approach. Something that is new always represents change,

and with change comes risk. Business development is different from marketing.

Marketing is meant to target the early stages of the buyer’s readiness stages as

discussed in Chapter 1 “Understanding the Role of Marketing and Sales” and as

shown  in  Fig.  15.8.  This  figure  is  from  Chapter  Highlight  13.3:  It  is  How  You

Spend Your Time, with the business development activity added. Business devel-

opment is best suited to address sales opportunities that are early in the cus-

tomer’s  project  management  process  when  the  account  is  still  considering

options for their project or initiative. Business development is a bridge between

marketing and sales in the early stages of a sales opportunity. Business develop-

ment activities can occur:



The Last Piece



621





Fig. 15.8

Business development activities.

Business development is not market-

ing and it supports sales. On the

bottom of the  chart: Line 1-Buyer

readiness stages Line 2-Customer

project management process

Line 3-Sales activities.



622



CHAPTER 15  Planning



•  Early in the evaluation of options.



•  Early in the implementation stage.



•  At the end of a project as part of the project review stage when the seller



makes recommendations for future projects.



Projects for which the account would consider an enterprise approach for their

project are an excellent time to pursue business development. You can leverage

the experts in your organization to engage in discussions regarding options for the

customer to consider for their project. Business development must complement

the competitive sales strategy. For example if the salesperson is expecting to use

a flanking competitive sales strategy, it may require a business development activ-

ity during the customer’s evaluation of options.



Examples of business development activities are:



•  Product champions or product line managers discussing applications of new



products or services for a customer’s specific application.



•  Customer’s visiting the seller’s engineering or research centers to discuss



specific technologies or processes for their needs.



•  Providing the customer with demonstrations using their examples or running

pilots  on  the  customer’s  projects.  A  caution,  however,  needs  to  be  made

whenever providing demonstrations or pilots. See the sidebar “How Will You

Know?”



In some situations, companies will schedule product champions or experts at a

location  and  ask  for  the  sales  staff  to  arrange  meetings  so  the  experts  can  fill

their time talking to customers. This is not an effective way to do business devel-

opment.  A  more  effective  and  cost-efficient  way  to  do  business  development  is

described next.



u  How Will You Know?



When customers are ready to try a

new product or service, they may ask

the supplier to run a demonstration

first so they can be confident the new

product or service will work in their

application. As discussed in Chapter 8

“Handling Customer Objections,” the

customer is expressing skepticism,

and this should be handled following

the steps for skepticism before agree-

ing to run demonstrations. If at the

end the customer must have a dem-

onstration, then ensure that you ask

the question, “How will you know if

the demonstration is successful?”

Once that is discussed and agreed to,

then tell the customer the price of the

product or service if success is

achieved; if success is not achieved,



indicate the pricing terms. If the cus-

tomer is not agreeable to paying for

success, then you are gaining much by

running the demonstration.



If you are in a situation where the cus-

tomer is considering the new product

or service and will be tendering the

work for the project, you must decide

first (before you begin the discussion)

if you want to hold this conversation

before or after the tender has been

awarded. This is impacted by all the

other factors that are considered

before selecting your competitive

sales strategy for an opportunity and

the type of tender you expect the cus-

tomer to issue.



If you decide to run the demonstration

in a tendering scenario and the cus-

tomer is providing you an opportunity



to prove the product, service, or pro-

cess so it can be included in the ten-

der then in most cases the tender,

must be a Type 1 Enterprise or Type 2

Consultative type of tender, where

you gain a competitive advantage for

the product or service. With Type 3

Transactional type tenders, you are in

most cases better to wait until you are

awarded the work and do the busi-

ness development during the imple-

mentation of the project, as shown in

Fig. 15.8.



In situations where it is a Type 1 or

Type 2 tender, you still must have the

customer define success and, at a

minimum if success is achieved, ask

the customer to provide a testimonial

describing the success and benefits

from the demonstration job.



Marketing before Business Development



Business development activities tend to be more costly and time consuming than

marketing  and  sales  activities.  It  is  not  unusual  for  companies  to  give  an  indi-

vidual the title business development manager, making them responsible for sup-

porting the introduction of new offerings internally and to the market. Marketing

activities can be held for many customers at one time whereas business develop-

ment is a one-to-one discussion about applying the products and services that the

customer has not tried before. One effective way to hold a business development

activity is to follow the process as detailed:



•  Product or service expert or champion arrives at your location and hosts

a  marketing  event  (for  example,  lunch  and  learn  or  presentation  at  a

conference).



•  After-marketing  event.  Make  it  easy  for  customers  to  ask  for  additional

information or time to discuss their specific project or need with the prod-

uct expert while still at the location.



•  Have customers complete a short form with their name, convenient time to

meet, a short description of their project, and any issues or challenges.



The  customer,  by  requesting  time  to  visit  with  the  expert,  is  seeking  business

development as the customer has invited your company to talk to them about your

product  and  services.  There  is  an  old  saying  in  sales:  “Customers  prefer  to  buy

rather than being sold to.”



Business development activities need to be scheduled to have the largest impact

on the account pipeline. In many companies, several salespeople will be given one

or two large account sales responsibilities. In those situations, management will

typically  set  a  day  aside  to  have  all  the  account  plans  presented  so  decisions

regarding marketing calendars and business development activities can be opti-

mized to have the largest impact on multiple key accounts.



The next section will examine how to maximize your position within the account’s

business and service units.

Account Development Strategies



One of the main objectives of the account plan is to identify areas where you can

develop or improve your position within the account. Account development strat-

egies are shown in Fig. 15.9. The account development strategies are described

by one or more of the following strategies:



•  Establish: Leverage your existing relationships in the account to introduce addi-

tional company products and services to your current  customers in the account.



•  Enter: Use your existing relationships in the account to expand your rela-

tionships and establish new company products and services in areas of the

account that you are not working currently.



•  Expand: Leverage your existing relationships within the account to expand your

current product and services to new business or service units within the account.



•  Improve:  Focus  on  your  current  business  to  improve  your  position  and



profitability with the account.



•  Exit: Withdraw from a specific business unit or service unit when strategic



or profitability targets cannot be achieved.



In  some  companies,  account  development  is  also  called  cross-selling  of  other

product or service lines to the account.8 However, cross-selling does not capture

the  situations  when  a  company  does  not  want  to  cross-sell  but  still  improve  its

position  with  the  account  that  is  captured  under  the  account  development

“Improve” strategy. Each of the four strategies will be described next.



Establish



To  be  better  established  for  an  account,  leverage  your  current  position  in  the

account to increase the total market share by establishing a position from which

you sell additional products and services to the account. To do this, you need to

identify  opportunities  where  the  account  would  accept  an  integrated  company

approach. The integrated approach has been successful many times in establishing

a presence in an account for a particular business line that previously had no busi-

ness  with  the  account.  For  the  account  to  accept  the  proposal,  there  must  be  a

clear  advantage  for  the  account  to  award  multiple  products  and  services  to  one

company.  These  advantages  can  be  better  logistics,  improved  operating  efficien-

cies, etc. The expand account development strategy requires marketing and busi-

ness development activities be completed before the opportunity you have targeted

to win and the advantages to be clearly described to the customer. The competitive

sales strategy for the targeted opportunity in most cases should be frontal.



624



CHAPTER 15  Planning





Expand



To expand, leverage your relationships from one account business or service unit

to  expand  your  current  products  and  services  to  a  new  business  or  service  unit

within the account. If you have a project with the customer but your market share

for the account’s business is low, you may choose an expansion account develop-

ment strategy. It is not unusual to have a large market share in one business unit

but minimal or none in another. In this situation, leverage the strength of your

relationships and  service delivery to identify supporters who can act as references

and generate leads to expanding your customer base within the account.



You should also use your presence in the account to gain a competitive advan-

tage  over  competitors.  The  advantage  comes  from  learning  of  opportunities

early in the opportunities management process and working with the account

to     address  a  compelling  event  or  participate  in  the  evaluation  of  options.  In

this situation, use a frontal or flanking competitive sales strategy, as discussed

in Chapter 4 “Opportunities Management” and Chapter 9 “Strategic Sales Plan.”



Enter



There are two ways to execute an enter account development strategy.  The first

is  to  wait  until  there  is  an  opportunity  for  the  new  account  business  or  service

unit needing products or services you are not currently selling them. This enter

account development strategy has a higher degree of success when you learn of

the opportunity early in the customer’s project management process, and there is

a degree of dissatisfaction with the incumbent supplier. This version of the enter

strategy can take a long time to develop. Another option that can be faster is to

use an enter strategy following a two-step approach:



•  Step 1 Expand Strategy. Create a presence in the targeted account busi-

ness or service unit following an expand strategy as described above. This

is  done  for  one  of  your  company’s  products  or  services  that  is  currently

active in another account business or service unit. This first win in the new

business  unit  or  service  unit  is  achieved  by  using  a  frontal  or  fragment

strategy depending on how you are evaluated against the account’s decision

criteria.



•  Step 2 Establish Strategy. Introduce the new product or service leveraging

your position from inside the business or service unit as described earlier

in the establish strategy.



From the account’s perspective, the two-stage approach is less risky than bringing

in  a  new  supplier  for  a  new  service  unless  there  is  a  problem  with  the  current

supplier.

ship or current business with this unit. There

blank and the “Market Share” field is

“None.” The other fields related to the spe-

cific unit are completed based on your

research for that unit.

The Last Piece



625























Chapter Highlight 15.2:  You Can Always Improve!



The professional salesperson should never be satisfied once business

has been secured with an account. Like all professionals you should

always be looking for ways to improve. There are four common ways

to improve the relationship with a current business or service unit:



•  Improving communication links



•  Pursuing  ong-term  relationship  strategies  for  key  buying  center



members



•  Improving profitability



•  Matching relationship levels with selling approaches



Improving communication links: Improving your communication links focuses on both the marketing communica-

tions  program  and  feedback  systems. The  salesperson’s  role  in  the  improvement  activities  is  to  impact  each  of

buyer-readiness stages, with the goal of reducing the customer’s negative reactions to your sales proposals.



As new key high-level executives and key buying center members enter into the account, the account plan should

have  a  contacting  strategy  that  quickly  introduces  and  moves  the  new  contacts  to  the  conviction  stage  of  the

buyer-readiness stage.



Communication improvement actions also include building better feedback systems between your company and

the  account  contacts  at  all  levels.  Improved  communications  result  in  better-informed  customers  in  the  account

and more accurate forecasts of the account’s future requirements and activities. Good communications also help

you learn what competitors are offering in terms of products, services, solutions, and prices, and how well com-

petitors are meeting the account’s needs. Through honest exchange between your company and the account, you

can  learn  how  well  you  are  meeting  their  needs  and  whether  you  are   fulfilling  promises  you  made  during  the

pursuit stages. Your level of  confidence in this information is directly related to the level of trust in the relationship.



Pursing  long-term  strategy  for  key  buying  center  members:  The  contacting  plan  is  contained  in  the  improve

account development discussion—not in the contacting plan of the strategic plan—because this long-term strategy

puts  the  emphasis  on  improving  rather  than  just  maintaining  relationships. To  improve  relationships  with  buying

centers, you have to focus on maintaining an ongoing business relationship with all the influential buying center

members who are actively involved in the buying center. The contact you have with the buying center members

during the pursuit phase of an opportunity is best described as brief, and there may be times when business is

not being pursued from certain groups within the account. Therefore, it is imperative to develop and coordinate an

ongoing business relationship with buying center members similar to the relationship you have with key high-level

contacts.



Key buying center members can span the complete range of buying alignment as discussed in Chapter 9 “Strategic

Sales Plan.” There should be a coordinated effort among account team members to ensure that buying center mem-

bers are included in your marketing communications and feedback programs. Special attention should be paid to:



•  Non-supporters, to ensure that they are contacted after the pursuit stage to resolve any of their outstanding



concerns.



•  Long-term strategies for dealing with enemies.



Improving profitability: In addition to improving the relationship between the account contacts and your com-

pany, the salesperson should also include discussions to identify ways to improve profitability for both companies.

This can happen by finding ways to improve efficiencies, sharing infrastructure when appropriate, increasing the

account’s  performance,  and  increasing  your  company’s  revenues. The  improved  profitability  effort  should  be  an

ongoing focus of the account plan. This is one of the goals of successful account management: improved profit-

ability for both the customer and your company.



Another  benefit  of  a  continuous  spotlight  on  improving  profitability  for  both  companies  is  the  ability  to  focus  on

trades that bring value to both during negotiations. This is much easier when you have a well-developed list of high-

quality trades, generated from your ideas on how to improve profitability for both companies. Trades were discussed

in more detail in Chapter 12 “Collaborative Negotiations.”



Improved  profitability  may  require  that  you  engage  in  discussions  about  changing  the  business  relationship,  as

described  in  Chapter  14  “Relationship  Profiling,”  and  moving  from  lower-level  to  higher-level  relationships  or,  in

some cases, from a higher level to a lower level.



Matching relationship levels with selling approaches: Chapter 14 “Relationship Profiling” examined several ways

to  evaluate  the  account  relationship. The  consolidated  business  unit  rela tionship  profiles  for  the  account  were

included in the situation report section of the account plan. The relationship levels range from Level 1, in which the

relationship  is  between  stakeholders,  to  Level  2,  where  the  relationship  is  that  of  a  problem  solver,  to  Level  3,

which is a standard vendor-customer relationship.



626



CHAPTER 15  Planning



Chapter Highlight 15.2: You Can Always Improve! (cont.)



However, not every project the account undertakes requires the same type of relationship and selling approach

during  the  pursuit  phase  of  an  opportunity.  As  the  account  project  mix  changes,  you  must  adjust  your  selling

approach, which requires that you stay informed of the customer’s project objectives and strategies. As discussed

in Chapter 14 “Relationship Profiling,” each successive higher-level relationship requires a higher level of invest-

ment  from  both  your  company  and  the  account. This  concept  was  first  introduced  in  the  competitive  bidding

chapter and is included here for your ease of reference.

The Level 1, 2, and 3 relationships correspond to a preferred selling approach for any given account project:9



•  Level 1–Enterprise Selling: The project is strategic to the account and your company. The planning horizon

occurs  well  in  advance  of  the  project,  and  corporate  capabilities  are  essential  for  delivering  the  results

promised to the customer. The ability to meet your commitments is far beyond any individual salesperson

and  requires  management’s  assistance  to  gain  corporate   commitment  and  look  for  opportunities  to  bring

added value by leveraging more of your company’s organization.



•  Level 2–Consultative Selling: The level of importance of the project and concern of the account for meeting

their objectives are high, and as such the account searches for the best possible solution. This relationship

is the most common type. It leverages your differentiated products and services, and specialized sales and

support staff. In this type of relationship the goal of account  management is to have a well-defined account

marketing  com munications  program  to  build  awareness  and  knowledge  of  your  company’s  products  and

services.



•  Level  3–Transactional  Selling:  For  these  projects  the  main  driver  is  to  control  costs  and  source  the  most

cost-effective   suppliers.  Standardization  and  efficient  purchasing  strategies  are  the  main  focus  of  the

account.  In  these  relationships,   your  objective  is  to  avoid  over-resourcing  the  project  and  driving  up  your

costs and profits down. Your focus is on gaining  efficiencies and reducing the cost of delivering your products

and services.



When there is a mismatch between the relationship and your selling approach, you:



•  Are at risk of wasting resources that are not wanted by the account, and



•  Expose your company to competitive threats.



For example, if the account has an ongoing field

development  that  they  consider  simple  and

straightforward but you recommend using more

expensive, advanced products and services, you

may  be  wasting  additional  support  services

needed  for  the  advanced  products  and   services

if you win the project. You may also be at risk of

losing the  project to a lower-cost supplier.



Alternatively,  if  the  account  is  starting  a  major

new  offshore  development  and  is  looking  for

innovative  ways  to  gain  efficiencies  but  you  do

not take a Level 1 approach, you may be exposed

to  a  competitor  who  recommends  forming  a

strategic  alliance.  The  figure  on  the  right  illus-

trates that there is a region of balance where the

relationship  level  and  selling  approach  meet  the

customer’s expectations for a given project. The

 bidirectional  arrow  suggests  that  you  may  have

to adapt your selling approach based on the cus-

tomer’s level of investment in the project.



It is critical that the account plan identify the best selling approach and enable you to migrate from one approach

to another as the relationship level changes. The plan must identify in advance that a repositioning from the current

relationship and selling strategy is required. To reposition successfully and minimize competitive threats, you must

be  proactive  well  before  the  procurement  stage  of  the  opportunities   management  process,  and  ideally  in  the

evaluation of options phase.



To do this, the salesperson must be well informed of the strategic importance and level of investment the account

intends to make in upcoming projects.



All  account  development  strategies  should  include  components  of  the  improve  strategy  in  each  of  the  expand,

establish, and diversify strategies. The improve strategy can be a stand-alone strategy for a specific business unit,

but components of the improve strategy should be shared across the  organization, such as key high-level contact

and  communication links.



The Last Piece



627





Improve



If  none  of  the  other  account  development  strategies  is  applicable  for  securing

more business in the account, then the plan must identify other ways to improve

your position and market share with the improve strategy. The improve strategy

is a very worthwhile strategy, and parts of the improve strategy are always appli-

cable  to  every  account  and  can  result  in  significant  revenue  improvement.  The

improve strategy discussion is presented in the form of a chapter highlight. This

will facilitate using the highlight as a handout when meeting with other account

team  members  to  discuss  ways  to  improve  the  relationship  with  your  accounts.

See Chapter Highlight 15.2: You Can Always Improve!



Exit



Sometimes an exit strategy is the most desirable account development strategy. If

the long-term prospects of an account are not sufficient to justify the resources

dedicated to the account, it may warrant an exit strategy. It is common to use an

exit  strategy  when  long-term  profitability  objectives  cannot  be  achieved.  Obvi-

ously, an exit strategy should be closely analyzed and discussed to be sure it is the

correct strategy. An exit strategy, when warranted, is targeted at specific business

or service units. When an exit strategy is chosen, a competitive disengage strategy

should be used, as discussed in Chapter 4 “Opportunities Management.”



The final step of the account development strategy is to add the account development

strategy for each of your company’s business units to the account relationship profile,

as shown in Fig. 15.10. When account development strategies are added to the account

relationship profile, it becomes more obvious for the establish strategy that existing

relationships can be leveraged to introduce the new customer business or service unit.

When using an expand strategy, you should try to determine the impact it will have on

market  share  for  the  account.  Each  account  development  strategy  should  also  be

tested against the competitors’ position, as described in the situation report section.



Each account development strategy—improve, expand, and diversify—should be

associated with specific opportunities from the account pipeline and incorporated

into the selling strategy for the specific opportunity. Each selling strategy should

be checked to be sure it is well balanced. When the account relationship strate-

gies  and  account  development  strategies  are  combined,  refer  to  them  as  the

account relationship and development strategy.



Contacting and Actions Plan



The contacting and actions plan identifies contacts the salesperson requires other

people to make, as listed below:



•  Contacts made in support of opportunities won



•  Contacts made in support of opportunities being pursued



•  Contact made to determine the account’s future plans



•  Actions and events planned to support current projects and opportunities



Contacts Made in Support of Opportunities Won



Projects are opportunities that are in the sales stage 5-Won and the account is in

the implementation stage of their project management process. Contacts in this

category are made with key high-level contacts in the account. The purpose can

be to review your company’s performance on current projects or to highlight your

company’s  contribution  to  the  project  objectives.  As  discussed  in  Chapter  4

“Opportunities Management” and Chapter 6 “Sales Activities,” you should docu-

ment best practices used during the project implementation phase in order to:



628



CHAPTER 15  Planning



Chapter Highlight 15.3: Account Plan Calendar



Planning Is the Key



All  activities  discussed  in  this  chapter  are  directed  toward  developing  an  account  plan  that  assists  in  day-to-day

operations and in securing current opportunities and potential projects. With a proactive approach to account plan-

ning, the relationship between the account and your company is more beneficial for both companies. Listed below

are  just  a  few  of  the  action  items  that  would  make  up  an  account  plan. With  today’s  CRM  software  and  tools,

account planning is an efficient process. Account management activities should happen around key events in the

customer’s  organization. The  only  way  to  ensure  that  the  appropriate  people  from  your  company  and  required

resources are available is to plan.



Review Account Plan



E



Review end of year two results and

update the year three plan after the

account’s  annual  strategy  meeting.

Plan  a  meeting  with  account  presi-

dent to discuss the focus of the year

three  plan.  Date:  December  10.

Also  present  updated  account  cor-

porate   profile  based  on  meeting

with the president. Set date for busi-

ness development activity.  Research

center visits in support of year three

potential project number 10.



Year-End Client Appreciation Party



D



Thank your clients for their business.



Performance Review



C



Two projects nearing completion (5 and 9)

and  one  active  (6).  Collect  significant

achievements  and  major  operating  suc-

cesses  from  different  divisions  within

Company  A.  Use  material  in  support  of

client  contacts  and  future  VIP  seminars.

Also  demonstrate  delivering  on  promises

made during evaluations and procurement

stages. Leverage best practices for poten-

tial  projects  (2  and  10)  in  second  half  of

year two and in year three.



B

High-Level Contact During Industry Conference



Two very important multi-division opportunities in the procurement stage (numbers 5

and  6).  Be  prepared  to  resolve  any  concerns  the  account  may  have  regarding  your

company’s commitment to their projects or any other unresolved concerns. Present

three papers at a national conference. Executives will also contact executives regard-

ing  the  outcome  of  the  recently  finished  project.  Introduce  new  products  and  ser-

vices as part of your business develop efforts and expand in the account (numbers 7

and 8).



A

VIP Seminar



Four opportunities (numbers 1, 2, 3, and

4) early in the evaluation of options. Use

VIP  seminar  to   create  awareness  in  cli-

ent’s organization for possible options for

their projects and establish another busi-

ness line in the account.



The Last Piece



629





•  Prevent failures from happening in the future, and



•  Promote  practices  that  result  in  major  successes,  as  recognized  by  the



customer.



Both  actions  demonstrated  that  your  company  takes  a  proactive  approach  in

customer satisfaction and stays focused on the customer’s expectations and objec-

tives. This will create a favorable impression with key high-level contacts.  Ideally,

documents  used  during  the  high-level  contact  should  be  brief  (one  page)  and

validated by a member of the executive team.



Chapter Highlight 15.3: Account Plan Calendar item

 illustrates a  scenario

where Company A has several active projects and high-level contacts will be made

to  review  Company  A’s  performance.  This  can  be  with  any  or  all  high-level  con-

tacts—initiators, approvers, or high-level contacts whose objectives are impacted

by the projects.



Contacts Made In Support Of Opportunities Being Pursued



Opportunities  being  pursued  are  opportunities  in  the  sales  stage:  2-Confirmed

Lead, 3-Sales Opportunity, or 4-Bid Proposal. Key high-level contacts should also

be  identified  that  support  opportunities  in  the  late  stages  of  the  pursuit  phase,

ideally in the resolution of concerns stage. This should happen after the buying

center has reviewed your proposal and that of the competitors. One advantage of

 making a high-level contact at this stage is that, during the procurement process,

some  accounts  impose  restrictions  on  communications  between  buying  center

members and the sales team. These restrictions, however, rarely apply to upper

management. By making a scheduled contact during this time, you can address

any concerns regarding your proposal, emphasize your corporate commitment, and

try to eliminate the concerns.



Contacts Made to Determine Account’s Future Plans



Each plan should include a regularly scheduled high-level contact or contacts to

determine  the  account’s  future  plans.  This  is  best  done  if  the  contact  can  be

scheduled after the account’s annual planning process.



For example, in Chapter Highlight 15.3: Account Plan Calendar, the salesperson

realized  the  account  team  was  aware  of  only  a  few  leads,  which  indicate  the

account’s activity was decreasing. The salesperson created a PMPV chart as shown

in Chapter Highlight 15.3: Account Plan Calendar. A high-level contact would be

appropriate  and  is  planned  (item

)  to  confirm  your  understanding  of  the

account’s future plans. The PMPV chart will be used to brief management before

contacting the account’s key high-level contacts.



Actions and Events Planned to Support Current Projects and Opportunities



In addition to the contacting plan, specific actions and events should be made for

key  buying  center  members  to  address  opportunities  in  the  pursuit  phase.  For

example, in Chapter Highlight 15.3: Account Plan Calendar, item

, a scheduled

VIP (executive level) seminar, targets four opportunities that are in the early part

of the accounts evaluation of options phase. Other possible activities that should

be  considered  are  engineering  center  visits,  technical  presentations,  and  site

visits to demonstrate your company’s capabilities—all meant to make the account

aware of the most appropriate solution for upcoming projects.



When required, contacts and events for buying center members can also be sched-

uled during the procurement stage. For example, it may be appropriate during the

bid proposal presentation to have a technical expert from the HQ marketing or



630



CHAPTER 15  Planning



engineering center present at the pre sentation. Such support should be identified

as part of the account plan, and the technical expert’s schedule should be checked

to ensure his or her availability. In other cases, it might be desirable to ensure

that specific company staff are available during negotiation meetings as part of

the advance planning activities.



Completing the Account Plan



The final step of completing the account plan is to write an executive overview

that:



•  Describes the overall goal of the account plan in terms of the:



  – Next two years’ revenue objectives.



  – Strategic business and account development objectives.



•  Identifies  the  critical  success  factors  for  the  plan  to  achieve  the  revenue



objectives. This can include such factors as:



– Must-win opportunities that represent a significant portion of the revenue



objectives.



– Establishment of a relationship with key buying center members or secur-



ing work for a key business or service unit.



The complete report should be stored in electronic format in the CRM database.

The complete report should include:



•  Executive Overview Account Summary



•  Account Corporate Profile



•  Account Organization Chart



•  Account  Business  and  Services  Unit  Summary  Table  (Drivers,  Initiatives,



and Critical Success Factors)



•  Account Pipeline



•  Consolidated Account Relationship, Account Development, and Competitive



Analysis worksheet



•  Account Plan Action Items, including:



  – Marketing calendar



  – Business development plan



  – Account development plan



–  Relationship  building  HCLP  assignments.  For  each  assignment  include



the requested contact, purpose, and dates to be completed.



– List of Level 1, 2, and 3 Opportunities. For each opportunity include key

contacts  as  discussed  in  the  contact  section  of  this  chapter  and  the

required review dates.



Execution and Tracking



The  company’s  CRM  system  should  be  used  to  execute  and  track  the  plan.  The

e-mail  function  can  be  used  to  keep  account  team  members  informed  on  the

progress  of  the  plan.  On  a  regular  basis  (normally  quarterly),  the  salesperson

should send a brief update on the plan’s progress against objectives and include

an  updated  PMPV  and  detailed  revenue  analysis  chart  for  the  account.  Also,  it

should list on one page the key contacts and Level 1, 2, and 3 Opportunity reviews

for the next quarter.



The Last Piece



631





Table 15.1

Comparison of account and territory plans.



Plan Section



Plan Components



Account



Territory



All  account  plans  should  be  reviewed  annually  at  the  headquarters  or  regional

level.  Typically  one  or  two  days  are  set  aside  each  year  to  have  account  plans

presented;  such  presentations  are  attended  by  the  executive  team  and  other

salespeople  presenting  plans.  This  makes  it  more  efficient  for  setting  priorities

and  assigning  resources.  It  also  makes  it  easier  to  identify  where  resources  for

marketing  events,  entertainment,  training,  HLCP  visits  by  company  executives,

etc., can be shared between accounts.



When several of your company business lines are working for a large account, the

salespeople  should  meet  to  discuss  their  plans.  They  should  look  for  ways  to

complement each other’s plans to form a cohesive, integrated company approach

to the account. An integrated approach will make the relationship more beneficial

for both companies.



Also, key parts of the account plan should be reviewed with mentors and execu-

tives in the account to acquire their feedback and support for the plan. Parts of

the plan to share with the account are the:



•  Account profile



•  Marketing calendar



•  Business development plan



•  Account development plan



Sharing the account plan with the account demonstrates to them that their busi-

ness is important to you and that you are actively working to win more of their

business.  It  is  not  unusual  for  the  account  to  give  very  valuable  feedback  when

you  share  the  plan  with  them,  and  don’t  be  surprised  if  they  recommend  other

opportunities for you to grow your business with them.



This concludes this section on the account plan. The next section is the terri-

tory plan.



Territory Plan

In this planning chapter, the account plan was presented first as there are many

similarities  between  the  account  plan  and  territory  plan.  Table  15.1  lists  the



632



CHAPTER 15  Planning



similarities  and  differences  between  the  two  types  of  plans.  In  many  situations

when a salesperson is successful in a territory, it is the result of securing several

key accounts. Knowing how to create an account plan will be very useful as the

salesperson tries to grow their company business with the account.



The main question to answer before creating the territory plan is: “What is the best

way to segment the territory to maximize your return on sales?” For the account

plan, the account was segmented in its business and service unit structure. After

you have your territory segmentation, then build your plan. Too many times territory

plans according to one study do not have the correct segmentation.10  The sidebar

“Get  Focused. Pick Your Segments” offers examples of common segmentation.11



The territory plan has the same four components as the account plan:



•  Awareness



•  Situation report



•  Strategic plan



•  Execution and tracking.



As in the account plan, the executive overview and plan critical success factors

are  completed  last.  The  first  part  of  the  territory  plan  to  be  completed  is  the

awareness sections, as described below.



Awareness



Territory awareness consists of three parts:



•  Territory profile



•  Territory segmentation map



•  Segment drivers, initiatives, and critical success factors



Territory Profile



The territory profile is the basic research that either the salesperson or the mar-

keting department has completed. It includes the most detailed information sup-

porting the territory summary as well as a section describing:



u Get Focused. Pick Your

Segments



Segmentation

by Size

B2B marketers

sometimes choose

to target potential business cus-

tomers based on their size. Size

might be measured in terms of the

number of employees or annual sales.

Larger companies may represent the

potential for more significant sales,

while smaller companies hold value

by virtue of the fact that there will be

many more of them to target as

potential customers. Size will also

become an issue in determining how

to best connect with these organiza-

tions and which individuals within the

organizations to target for communi-

cations. In small companies, for



example, this task will be much more

straight forward; in larger companies,

it can be challenging to identify the

key decision-makers accurately.



Segmentation by Industry

Industry segmentation may be used

by marketers who are selling products

with specific appeal in certain industry

segments. For instance, companies

that make specialized computer com-

ponents will want to identify compa-

nies that use the components and

then segment these companies into

a targeted group for communications

and outreach. Different industry seg-

ments may also have specific needs

and challenges that can be addressed

through key messaging in communica-

tions. Industry segmentation also

holds an advantage in that there are



a multitude of industry-specific trade

associations that can be used to con-

nect with these audiences.



Business Need Segmentation

Segmentation based on business

need allows marketers to identify and

connect with businesses that span

geographies, sizes and industries, but

share a common need addressed by

the marketer’s products or services.

For example, virtually every business

needs telephone systems and com-

puters. Marketers may choose to

combine segments to more narrowly

identify their audience, such as busi-

nesses that need accounting software

that are located within a certain radius

and are part of the energy utility

industry.



The Last Piece



633





•  Significant industry events and conferences



•  Active professional organizations and local chapters



•  Customer base information



•  Historical territory information such as market size (revenue)



The territory profile is sent to attendees of the annual territory meeting. It is also

used to brief and support personnel who will attend Level 1, 2, or 3 opportunity

reviews and brief company personnel not familiar with the territory before con-

tacting customers in the territory.



Territory Segmentation Map



The territory segmentation map is created to make a physical representation of

the territory. This can be based on geography, industry, company size, etc. It can

be either a graphic such as a map or a table listing. The segmentation map has to

complement the method you have used to segment your territory.



Customer Drivers, Initiatives, and Critical Success Factors



With the segmentation map you can also show or list the common business drivers,

major projects or initiatives, and critical success factors for the projects or initia-

tives in the same table for each territory segment. Fig. 15.2  showed two examples.



Situation Report



Situation report consists of three parts:



•  Territory summary



•  Territory pipeline



•  Territory consolidated segment relationship and competitor assessment



Territory Summary



The territory summary was discussed in Chapter 6 “Sales Activities” and includes

more than the account summary because of the added complexity a territory has

compared to an account. The territory summary includes the consolidated statis-

tics for all the territory segments for the current full year and next two years:



•  Total territory size in terms of market expenditures and number of customers.



•  Breakdown by territory segmentation expenditures and number of customers.



•  Number of competitors by territory segment.



•  Average sales size revenue, sales cycle days, and contract period by territory



segment.



•  Market share by territory segment.



•  Coverage % of customers you do business with in each territory segment.



•  Penetration % of your product and service lines you have sold in each ter-



ritory segment.



Territory Pipeline



Much like in the account plan, the pipeline is a key component. With the segmen-

tation set and your historical data collected and your territory pipeline, the rest

of  the  plan  will  develop.  What  is  critical  is  the  revenue  objectives  or  quota  the

salesperson has been assigned.  The revenue objectives will define how aggressive

your plan must be and on which territory segments you must focus. Equally impor-



634



CHAPTER 15  Planning



tant is the competitor analysis for each territory segment and what to expect from

the competition if your revenue growth occurs at the expense of a few competitors

or  many.  Answering  these  questions  can  only  come  after  the  salesperson  has

analyzed their territory pipeline for each territory segment.



The revenue growth can come from several areas:



•  Increase market share per territory segment. This can be achieved by win-

ning more of the proposals you submit or submitting more proposals.  The

first  option,  winning  more,  will  require  a  better  value  proposition.  The

second option requires either a shorter sales cycle or more efficient selling

that  results  in  spending  less  time  per  sale  or  the  addition  of  more  sales

resources. The additional resources could be:



–  Doing more of any of the prospecting activities discussed in Chapter

6 “Sales Activities.” Prospecting will be discussed later in this section.



–  Providin  inside  salespeople  with  responsibilities  to  free  the  outside

salesperson to spend more time with clients. For example, the inside

salespeople  could  write  proposals  and  follow  up  with  the  customer,

leaving more time for the outside salespeople to visit more confirmed

leads.



–  Add more outside salespeople.



•  Introduce new products or sell additional company product or service lines.



•  Increase revenue per sale. This can be achieved by selling more volume or



complimentary products and services or increasing prices.



•  Gain more business from your current accounts by using the account devel-

opment techniques described in the account plan section of this chapter.



The above techniques to increase market share also include reviewing the pipe-

line and ensuring that the salespeople are effectively managing their pipeline. For

example,  as  discussed  in  Chapter  13  “Pipeline  Management”  if  a  salesperson  is

not  qualifying  properly,  they  could  be  chasing  opportunities  they  cannot  win;

therefore, this is wasted effort. If the salesperson does a better job at qualifying

they can spend more time pursuing more opportunities they can win. As a result

the revenue increases. However, this sales effectiveness step is not part of a ter-

ritory plan but part of the salesperson’s professional development.



For each territory segment, know the critical pipeline factors, such as:



•  Market size



•  Average deal size



•  Sales cycle by stage



•  Conversion rate to the next sales cycle



•  Market share



Developing  the  plan  will  be  an  iterative  process  and,  by  running  different  sce-

narios  to  decide  on  the  best  approach  to  achieve  the  plan  revenue  objectives

based  on  effort,  outcome,  and  confidence,  the  strategy  will  be  successful.  Each

option  must  be  evaluated  in  terms  of  risk  and  competitor  response.    The  next

section relationship analysis, development plan, and competitor analysis, all vital

inputs for determining how confident you are in the various options you consider

will succeed.

In  the  account  plan  section,  the  relationship  analysis  was  done  on  one  spread-

sheet  for  each  business  and  service  unit.  For  the  territory  plan,  perform  this

analysis  for  each  territory  segment  using  your  perceptions  based  on  customer

feedback for the relationship profile parameters as shown in Fig. 15.11. In this

example, there are four territory segments:



•  Large Accounts. Accounts with more than $1 billion annual sales.



•  Medium Accounts. $500,000 to $1 billion annual sales.



•  Small Business. Less than $500,000 annual sales.



•  Government. Government departments run as non-profit entities.



Also in this example, the salesperson for the purpose of their territory plan has

classified  Account  A  as  a  major  account  and  will  treat  the  account  as  a  stand-

alone segment in the territory.



It is clear from the analysis in Fig. 15.11 that the salesperson wants to position their

company in the territory as a value-added supplier. The salesperson is targeting a

level 2 consultative selling relationship for all the territory segments except for the

large accounts, for which they are targeting an enterprise selling relationship.
