CHAPTER 15  Planning















































































Competitor Analysis



The competitor analysis is completed the same way as described in the account

plan section of this chapter. However in a territory plan it is not unusual to have

two or more significant competitors with whom you are competing.  In this situa-

tion,  add  the  competition’s  information  on  additional  lines  in  the  competitive

analysis section of the analysis, as shown in Fig. 15.12. In this example, the small

business  territory  segment  has  Competitors  1,  2,  and  3,  and  the  large,  medium

accounts, government, and account A only have one main competitor each.



It  is  important  to  highlight  that,  when  completing  this  competitive  assessment,

the  salesperson  must  review  the  detailed  competitor  information  and  reports

provided as part of the marketing department’s competitor intelligence program.

Competitor  intelligence  is  not  a  one-time  assessment,  but  an  ongoing  effort  of

monitoring your competitors. The entire sales and marketing organization must

learn  as  much  as  possible  about  your  competitors.  There  are  many  competitor

information sources you can use, such as:



•  Customers



•  Competitor’s pricing behavior and sales strategies they use in the territory



and globally



•  Competitor’s websites



•  Competitor’s speeches and investor reports



•  Competitor’s presentation at conferences



•  Competitor’s annual report



The competitor analysis in the territory plan is used to compare the competitor’s

position in the territory segment to your company’s position. With this analysis,

you can determine if the competitor is better positioned than your company and

how difficult it will be to execute your territory development strategy.



The next section of the territory plan is the strategic plan, which lists the details

of how the revenue growth in the territory will be achieved by executing specific

actions based on the revenue objective and situational analysis.



Strategic Plan



The strategic plan consists of:



•  Territory prospecting plan



•  Business development activities



•  Territory development strategies



•  Contacting plan



The territory strategic plan has the same components as the account plan and was

shown earlier in Fig. 15.5. Each section of the strategic plan will be discussed below.



Territory Prospecting Plan



The territory prospecting plan follows the guidelines explained in Chapter 6 “Sales

Activities” in the prospecting section. This is a very important part of the territory

plan and should result in sufficient high-quality leads being added to the territory

pipeline  so  that  ultimately  a  sufficient  portion  will  be  converted  to  sales  stage

5-Won opportunities. Specifically, the prospecting plan will consist of:



The Last Piece



637





•  Corporate  Marketing  Communications  Plan  (MCP). Which parts of the

corporate MCP will be leveraged in the territory? In addition to the corpo-

rate MCP, there is also a territory MCP.



•  Territory MCP. As discussed in the account plan, the territory MCP includes

a  marketing  calendar.  The  territory  marketing  calendar  lists  the  specific

territory marketing events the salesperson will conduct or for which they

will  request  support  from  management  for  resources  to  hold  a  specific

event. The types of events that make up a territory MCP are the same as

described in the account plan “marketing calendar” section.



•  Current Accounts. How much time or resources are planned for prospecting

in current accounts from which you already have a business, as described in

Chapter 6 “Sales Activities” prospecting in current accounts section?



•  Referrals.  What  is  the  territory  referrals  program?  Which  prospecting

activities  produce  the  most  referrals?  Which  territory  segments  and

accounts provide the best referrals? These are referrals that result in con-

firmed leads and new business.



•  References.  What  is  the  territory  reference  program?  Are  the  references

sufficient for the types support materials needed for successful sales calls?

Is there an active program to capture successes and ask for releases from

customers? This topic was discussed in Chapter 6 “Sales Activities” refer-

ences and follow-up sections.



•  Networking.  What  are  the  planned  networking  activities  as  described  in



Chapter 6 “Sales Activities” networking section?



•  Conferences. How many conferences are in the territory?  Of these confer-



ences what are the dates and your company’s participation?



•  Cold Calling. What is the cold calling program?  Who has the responsibility

for cold calling? How many cold calls does it take to add a lead to the pipe-

line  and  what  is  the  conversion  of  cold  call  leads  compared  to  the  other

prospecting  activities?  For  more  discussion  of  cold  calling,  see  Chapter  6

“Sales Activities” cold calling section.



•  E-mail Marketing. This is the same as the e-mail distribution discussed in

the account plan section and Chapter 6 “Sales Activities” e-mail marketing

section.



•  Tracking. For the purpose of the territory plan, the results from your track-

ing  system  are  key.  The  tracking  of  the  prospecting  plan  identifies  which

prospecting  activities  generate  the  most  and  best  quality  leads.  It  should

also  include  a  library  in  which  referrals  and  references  are  stored  and

shared.



Territory Business Development Plan



The  territory  business  development  plan  creation  process  is  the  same  as  the

account business development plan process. Each business development plan will

target specific opportunities from the territory, such as opportunities:



•  Early in the customer’s planning process,



•  After award and during the implementation phase, and



•  In the review stage of the customer’s project management process.



See Fig. 15.8 for information on business development activities.



638



CHAPTER 15  Planning



Chapter Highlight 15.4: Developing the Territory

Establish



The  idea  of  using  a  territory  develop-

ment strategy to improve revenues is

based  on  leveraging  the  relationships

you have with your current customers

in the territory segments you are sell-

ing  to  now.  Leveraging  this  means

your  current  or  active  customers  as

shown  in  the  figure  on  the  right  will

provide  you  with  referrals  and  refer-

ences  for  new  business.  The  new

business  can  come  from  a  new  terri-

tory segment by selling company busi-

ness lines that are not being sold cur-

rently or improving your position with

your  current  customers.  Or  you  can

use  a  combination  of  the  strategies

called enter, when your strategy is to sell something new in a new territory segment. Each strategy is explained below.



Improve



Expand



Territory Segment



Enter



Exit



s

s

e

n

i

s

u

B



e

v

i

t

c

A



Active



New



Establish



To be more well-established within a territory segment, leverage your current position in the territory segment to

increase total market share by establishing a position and selling additional products and services to current custom-

ers in the territory. To do this, you need to be proactive and identify opportunities where the accounts in the territory

segment would accept an integrated company approach. The integrated approach has been successful many times

in establishing a presence in a territory for a particular business line that previously had no business.  For customers

to accept the proposal, there must be a clear advantage for the customer to award multiple products and services

to one company. These advantages can be better logistics, improved operating efficiencies, etc. The expand territory

development strategy requires marketing and business development activities to be completed before you make a

proposal for the opportunity you have targeted to win. You should also clearly describe the advantages to the cus-

tomer. The competitive sales strategy for the targeted opportunity in most cases should be frontal.



Summary: Establish a territory strategy. Sell more of your company’s offerings in the current territory segment.



Expand



Leverage  your  business  relationships  from  one  territory  segment  to  expand  your  sales  to  territory  segment  you

have no sales. If you have sales in one territory segment but your market share is low, you may choose an expand

strategy. It is not unusual to have a large market share in one territory segment but none in another. In this situa-

tion,  you  leverage  the  strength  of  your  relationships  and   service  delivery  to  identify  supporters  in  your  current

customers organizations who can act as references and generate leads to expand into other territory segments.



Summary: Expand territory strategy. Sell current offering to more territory segments.



Enter



There are two ways to execute an enter territory development strategy. The first is to wait until there is an oppor-

tunity  in  the  new  territory  segment  requesting  the  products  or  services  you  are  not  selling  in  the  territory. This

version of the enter development strategy has a higher degree of success when you learn of the opportunity early

in the customer’s project management process and there is a degree of dissatisfaction with the incumbent supplier.

This version of the enter strategy can take a long time to develop. Another option that can be faster is to use an

enter strategy following a two-step approach:



•  Step 1 Expand Strategy. Create a presence in the targeted territory segment following an expand strategy

as described above.  This is done for one of your company’s products or services that is currently being sold

in a one or more of the territory segments. This first win in the new segment is achieved by using a frontal

or fragment strategy depending on how you are evaluated against the customer’s decision criteria.



•  Step 2 Establish Strategy. Introduce the new product or service, leveraging your position from inside the



territory segment as described earlier in the establish strategy.



From the customer’s perspective, the two-stage approach is less risky than bringing in a new supplier for a new

service unless there is a problem with the current supplier.



Summary: Sell a product you are not selling today to a territory segment you are not in.



The Last Piece



639











Chapter Highlight 15.4: Developing the Territory (cont.)



Improve



If none of the other territory development strategies is applicable for securing more business in the territory, then

the plan must address ways to improve your position and market share with the improve strategy.  The improve

strategy  is  a  very  worthwhile  strategy,  and  parts  of  it  are  always  applicable  to  every  territory  and  can  result  in

significant revenue improvement. The improve strategy discussion is presented in the form of a chapter highlight

to facilitate using it as a handout when meeting with other sales team members when looking for ways to improve

the relationship with your current accounts. See Chapter Highlight 15.2:  You Can Always Improve!



Summary: Focus on your current customer’s.



Exit



Sometimes  an  exit  strategy  is  the  only  viable  option.  If  the  long-term  prospects  for  a  territory  segment  are  not

sufficient to justify the resources dedicated to the territory segment, it may warrant an exit strategy. It is common

to use an exit strategy when long-term profitability objectives cannot be achieved. Obviously, an exit strategy should

be closely analyzed and discussed to be sure it is the correct strategy. An exit strategy, when warranted, is targeted

at specific territory segment. When an exit strategy is chosen, a competitive sales disengage strategy should be

used, as discussed in Chapter 4 “Opportunities Management.”



Summary: Disengage.



Bringing the Analysis Together



The territory development strategy is added to the to the territory relationship profile as shown in Fig. 15.13. When

development strategies are added to the relationship profile, it becomes more obvious how much effort the devel-

opment  strategy  will  take  to  implement  from  a  relationship  aspect. This  was  discussed  in  detail  in  Chapter  14

“Relationship Profiling.” Competitive assessment is also part of the relationship profile. Each territory development

strategy should also be evaluated against the competitors’ position to predict their reaction to your strategy.



Each territory development strategy should have a specific opportunity from the territory pipeline that will be used

to execute the strategy. When the territory relationship strategies and development strategies are combined, you

refer to it as the territory relationship and development strategy.



Territory Development Plan



In a territory, the development plan is a summary of how the salesperson is plan-

ning  to  increase  market  share.  In  a  territory  plan,  the  territory  development

strategies are labeled the same as in the account plan but with a slightly different

meaning, as described below:



•  Establish:  Sell  more  of  your  company’s  offerings  in  the  current  territory



segment.



•  Enter: Sell a product you are not selling today to a territory segment you



are not in.



•  Expand: Sell current offering to more territory segments.

competitive assessment and develop-

ment plan.

Cost Plus

































•  Improve: Focus on your current customers.



•  Exit: Disengage.



In  the  example  in  Fig.  15.13,  the  salesperson  is  planning  to  expand  into  more

accounts in the large accounts segment, improve their position in the medium and

small business accounts, and enter into the government segment. The salesperson

has identified Account A from the medium accounts segment to be a key account

and  has  chosen  to  create  a  separate  development  strategy.  The  salesperson

believes they can leverage their current position in the account for whom they are

selling a single product line. For the plan, they have identified a significant oppor-

tunity for which they can expand and introduce another one of their company’s

product lines.



The execution of the territory development strategies again are slightly different

from the account development strategies and are presented in Chapter Highlight

15.4:  Developing  the  Territory.  When  developing  a  specific  account,  such  as

Account A shown in Fig. 15-11, the account development strategies are executed

the same way as described in the account plan section of this chapter.



The last section of the strategic plan is specific contacts to people in customer

organizations in support of the strategic plan and the securing of strategic oppor-

tunities identified in the plan.



Contacting Plan



The contacting plan is prepared the same way as described in the account plan

and consists of the requested contacts the salesperson requires other people to

make.



The  contacting  and  additional  support  plan  identifies  who  and  when  support  is

needed for the following:



•  Contacts made in support of opportunities won



•  Contacts made in support of opportunities being pursued



•  Contact made to determine the account’s future plans



•  Support at prospecting and business development activities



All  contacting,  CVR’s,  and  scheduling  of  activities  should  be  managed  through

the  company’s  CRM  system,  making  it  easy  to  associate  specific  contacts  and

activities with the appropriate opportunity, account, and territory segment. With

the  contacting  plan  completed,  the  last  step  is  to  create  the  territory  plan,  as

described below.



Completing the Territory Plan

The final step of completing the territory plan is to write an executive overview that:



•  Describes the overall goal of the territory plan in terms of:



  – Next two years’ revenue objectives.



  – Strategic business and account development objectives.



•  Identifies  the  critical  success  factors  for  the  plan  to  achieve  the  revenue



objectives. This can include such factors as:



– Must-win opportunities that represent a significant portion of the reve-



nue objectives.



– Establishment of a relationship with key buying center members or secur-



ing work for a key business or service unit.



The Last Piece



641





The complete report should be stored in an electronic format in the CRM data-

base. The complete report should include the following:



•  Executive overview



•  Territory summary



•  Territory profile



•  Territory segmentation map



•  Customer drivers, initiatives, and critical success factors



•  Territory pipeline



•  Consolidated territory relationship, competitive analysis and development



worksheet



•  Territory plan action items, including:



  – Territory prospecting plan.



  – Business development plan.



  – Territory development plan.



– Relationship-building HCLP assignments. For each assignment include



the requested contact, purpose, and dates to be completed.



– List of Level 1, 2, and 3 opportunities. For each opportunity include

key contacts as discussed in the contact section of this chapter and

the required review dates.



Execution and Tracking



The company’s CRM system should be used to execute and track the plan.  The

e-mail  function  can  be  used  to  keep  territory  team  members  informed  on  the

progress  of  the  plan.    On  a  regular  basis  (normally  quarterly),  the  salesperson

should send a brief update on the plan’s progress against objectives and include

an updated PMPV and territory detailed revenue analysis chart.  Also, it should

list on one page the key contacts and Level 1, 2, and 3 opportunity reviews for the

next quarter.



All  territory  plans  should  be  reviewed  annually  at  the  headquarters  or  regional

level.  Typically  one  or  two  days  are  set  aside  each  year  to  have  territory  and

account  plans  presented,  and  the  presentations  are  attended  by  the  executive

team and other salespeople presenting plans. Having all the plans’ requirements

at one time makes it more efficient for setting priorities and assigning resources.

It also makes it easier to identify where resources for marketing events, entertain-

ment, training, HLCP visits by company executives, etc., can be shared between

territories.



In addition, key parts of the territory plan should be reviewed with mentors and

executives from key customers in the territory to gather their feedback and sup-

port for the plan. Parts of the plan to share with the key customers are the:



•



•



Territory profile



Prospecting plan



•  Business development plan



Sharing  the  territory  plan  with  the  key  customers  demonstrates  to  the  customer

that their business is important to you and that you are actively working on how to

win more of their business. It is not unusual for the customer to give valuable feed-

back  when  you  share  the  plan  with  them.  Don’t  be  surprised  if  they  recommend

other opportunities for you to grow your business with them or in the territory.



642



CHAPTER 15  Planning









This concludes this section of the territory plan and planning chapter.  There were

many previous chapters referenced in this chapter, which further highlights why

the  planning  chapter  is  at  the  end  of  the  textbook.  Making  the  best  plans  to

achieve your objectives requires understanding how all the pieces of the profes-

sional  sales  puzzle  fit  together  for  the  complete  picture  of  what  it  takes  to  be

consistently successful in sales. The chapter summary is next.



The Last Piece



643





Summary of Chapter Objectives



This chapter was about creating plans for key accounts and territories. Creating a successful plan in the competitive B2B

environment requires the salesperson to master all the earlier sales skills discussed in this textbook. It is the execution

of  the  plan  that  requires  the  salesperson  and  their  teams  to  use  all  the  B2B  professional  sales  skills:  sales  call  skills,

strategic sales plans, bidding and negotiation skills. The other skill that is as important as any single factor is pipeline

management. For any plan to be successful and focused, the sales team must have a well-defined pipeline from which to

plan and prioritize their activities with their customers. When the salesperson shares parts of their plans with their cus-

tomers, it also strengthens the relationship between your company and the account and customers in your territories. A

completed plan is an investment made to support your sales efforts and identifies where you need the support of your

company’s management, support, and operations staff to be successful.



1.  Describe the components of an account and territory plan.



The account and territory plans consist of four sections: awareness, situation report, strategic plan, and execution and

tracking. The awareness section is the introduction to the plan and can be used to brief staff who support or review the

plan or be part of the team reviewing Level 1, 2, or 3 opportunities from the plan. The first part of the awareness  section

is the account corporate or territory profile. Instructions for completing an account corporate profile were detailed in

Chapter  3  “Account  Corporate  Profile,”  and  the  territory  research  was  described  in  Chapter  6  “Sales  Activities.”  The

next part of the awareness section is the account organization information or territory segmentation map. The account

organization includes how the account is organized in terms of business and service units and how decisions are made

in  the  account.  The  territory  segmentation  is  critical  to  establishing  as  it  defines  how  the  territory  is  organized  and

where  the  plan  will  focus.  The  organization  section  for  both  the  account  and  territory  plans  also  includes  individual

contact information, including information regarding key high-level contacts that you include in the high-level contact

program as part of your ongoing business relationship effort. The last section is a discussion of the key buying centers

that handle the selection of suppliers for the opportunities you are trying to secure. The focus of this section is to iden-

tify high-influence members of the buying  center and select an appropriate relationship strategy for them.



The next component is the situation report, which has three parts: a relationship profile, competitor assessment, and

pipeline  at  the  heart  of  both  plans.  The  consolidated  relationship  profile  identifies  where  your  company  has  strong

relationships with the account or in the territory that can be leveraged to improve your market share. Instructions for

completing a relationship profile were covered in Chapter 14 “Relationship Profiling.” The competitive assessment is

completed in a process similar to that used to complete the account relationship profile. In fact, the competitive assess-

ment  is  part  of  the  relationship  profile,  which  makes  it  easier  when  deciding  on  account  and  territory  development

strategies  in  the  strategic  plan  section  of  the  report.  In  addition  to  market  share,  role,  and  profitability  factors,  the

competitive assessment lists the key competitor for each account business and service unit or territory segment. The

competitive assessment also lists the competitor’s strengths and weaknesses.



One of the most important parts of the plan is the pipeline. The PMPV and revenue charts are powerful tools to be used

to analyze and identify strategic opportunities for setting revenue objectives and can also be used to provide information

needed for the annual budgeting process.



The  next  section  of  the  plan  is  the  strategic  plan.  This  section  includes  the  account  marketing  calendar  or  territory

prospecting plan, business development plan, account development plan, and contacting plan.



The two goals of the account marketing calendar and territory prospecting plan are to create awareness and establish

an effective feedback mechanism for customers. Ideally, the plans use all the marketing and prospecting tools to migrate

users and buyers from the awareness level of the buyer-readiness stage to the preference level. The feedback mechanism

completes the communication process.  Without an effective listening system, you may never determine how the account

perceives your product and services. Together, the marketing and prospecting plans and feedback mechanism form the

communication links, and most relationships are only as good as their communications links.



In the account and territory development part of the strategic plan, you select one or more of the development strate-

gies: improve, establish, expand, enter, or exit. The improve development strategy is typically part of all account and

territory development strategies. The improve development strategy includes improving communication links, developing

a long-term strategy for key buying center members, identifying a strategy for improving profitability for both your cus-

tomers  and  your  company,  and  matching  relationship  levels  with  preferred  selling  approaches.  Your  selling  approach



644



CHAPTER 15  Planning







must match the level of relationship, and there may be multiple levels active within any given account or territory and

for different types of projects. You can use three selling approaches—transactional, consultative, and enterprise—as

determined for each of the Level 3, Level 2, and Level 1 relationships, respectively. The plans help you recognize when

to migrate from your current level of relationship, as identified in the relationship profile, to a higher- or lower-level

relationship. Your chance of success when changing your selling approach improves if you are aware of needed changes

for the best approach well in advance of the sales activities. Key to recognizing when this is the case is to be involved

with your customers in the account and territory early in the opportunities management process. The account and ter-

ritory development strategy combined with the relationship strategy forms the account and territory relationship and

development strategy.



The last part of the account plan is the execution and tracking section. This part leverages the many features of the

CRM database, which allows you to enter planned actions into the database, assign them to specific company staff, and

track their progress. The ability to enter the account management action plans into the CRM database also allows the

account team to avoid conflicts in planned account activities.



2.  Know the tools for creating and managing the account plan.



The main tool for creating the account plan is your company’s CRM account, contact, opportunity, and activities data-

base. You use all the information when formulating your strategic plan based on the current and future account and

territory projects and your competitive position by creating strategic sales plans. The relationship profile tool can be

easily attached to the account attachments database and shared with other team members.



3.  Create an account plan for key accounts.



The best way to learn is by doing. See the Applying the Concepts section.



         Applying the Concepts



1.  Following the guidelines provided in this chapter, prepare an account or territory plan for your area of responsibility.

The Relationship and Account Development Strategy worksheets can be downloaded from the www.b2bprofessionalsales.

com website.



2.  What major marketing communications event have you planned for your account or territory? Should there be others?



3.  For your key account or territory, what is the most frequently used development strategy?



The Last Piece
